## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define Probability Density Function. List its two essential properties.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer**:
A **Probability Density Function (PDF)** $f(x)$ is a non-negative function associated with a continuous random variable $X$ such that the probability of $X$ lying in any interval $[a,b]$ is the area under $f(x)$ between $a$ and $b$.

**Two Essential Properties**:
1. **Non-negativity**: $f(x) \geq 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$.
2. **Unit Total Area**: $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx = 1$.

Additionally, $P(X = x) = 0$ for any specific value $x$, and $P(a \leq X \leq b) = \int_{a}^{b} f(x) \, dx$.

---

#### Question 2: Distinguish between a Probability Mass Function (PMF) and a Probability Density Function (PDF).

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer**:

| Aspect | PMF $p(x)$ | PDF $f(x)$ |
|--------|------------|------------|
| Variable Type | Discrete | Continuous |
| Gives | $P(X = x)$ directly | Density, not direct probability |
| Normalization | $\sum p(x) = 1$ | $\int f(x) \, dx = 1$ |
| Computation | Summation | Integration |
| Single point | $p(x) > 0$ possible | $P(X=x) = 0$ |
| Example | Number of patients cured | Blood pressure of a patient |

---

### Part B Question — Module 1 Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Given the PDF $f(x) = k(2x - x^2)$ for $0 \leq x \leq 2$ and $0$ elsewhere.  
    **(i)** Find the value of $k$.     **(ii)** Compute $P(0.5 \leq X \leq 1.5)$.     **(iii)** Find $E[X]$ and $\text{Var}(X)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The waiting time (in minutes) at a hospital reception follows an Exponential distribution with mean 5 minutes.  
    **(i)** Write down its PDF.     **(ii)** Find the probability that a patient waits more than 8 minutes.     **(iii)** Find the median waiting time. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a)(i): Finding $k$

Apply the normalization condition:

$$\int_{0}^{2} k(2x - x^2) \, dx = 1$$

$$k \int_{0}^{2} (2x - x^2) \, dx = 1$$

$$k \left[ x^2 - \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{2} = 1$$

$$k \left( 4 - \frac{8}{3} \right) = 1$$

$$k \cdot \frac{4}{3} = 1 \implies k = \frac{3}{4}$$

#### Part (a)(ii): Computing $P(0.5 \leq X \leq 1.5)$

$$P(0.5 \leq X \leq 1.5) = \int_{0.5}^{1.5} \frac{3}{4}(2x - x^2) \, dx$$

$$= \frac{3}{4} \left[ x^2 - \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_{0.5}^{1.5}$$

At $x = 1.5$: $\; 2.25 - 1.125 = 1.125$

At $x = 0.5$: $\; 0.25 - 0.0417 = 0.2083$

$$= \frac{3}{4} (1.125 - 0.2083) = \frac{3}{4} \cdot 0.9167 = 0.6875$$

#### Part (a)(iii): Finding $E[X]$ and $\text{Var}(X)$

**Compute $E[X]$**:

$$E[X] = \int_{0}^{2} x \cdot \frac{3}{4}(2x - x^2) \, dx = \frac{3}{4} \int_{0}^{2} (2x^2 - x^3) \, dx$$

$$= \frac{3}{4} \left[ \frac{2x^3}{3} - \frac{x^4}{4} \right]_{0}^{2}$$

$$= \frac{3}{4} \left( \frac{16}{3} - 4 \right) = \frac{3}{4} \cdot \frac{4}{3} = 1$$

**Compute $E[X^2]$**:

$$E[X^2] = \frac{3}{4} \int_{0}^{2} (2x^3 - x^4) \, dx = \frac{3}{4} \left[ \frac{x^4}{2} - \frac{x^5}{5} \right]_{0}^{2}$$

$$= \frac{3}{4} \left( 8 - \frac{32}{5} \right) = \frac{3}{4} \cdot \frac{8}{5} = \frac{6}{5} = 1.2$$

**Compute $\text{Var}(X)$**:

$$\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2 = 1.2 - 1 = 0.2$$

#### Part (b)(i): PDF of Exponential Distribution

For Exponential with mean $\mu = \frac{1}{\lambda} = 5$, so $\lambda = \frac{1}{5}$:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{5} e^{-x/5}, \quad x \geq 0$$

#### Part (b)(ii): $P(X > 8)$

$$P(X > 8) = e^{-\lambda \cdot 8} = e^{-8/5} = e^{-1.6} \approx 0.2019$$

#### Part (b)(iii): Median Waiting Time

Median $m$ satisfies $F(m) = 0.5$:

$$1 - e^{-\lambda m} = 0.5 \implies e^{-\lambda m} = 0.5$$

$$-\lambda m = \ln(0.5) \implies m = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = 5 \ln 2 \approx 3.466 \text{ minutes}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** A continuous random variable $X$ has the PDF:  
    $f(x) = \dfrac{x}{8}$ for $1 \leq x \leq 3$ and $0$ otherwise.  
    **(i)** Verify that $f(x)$ is a valid PDF.     **(ii)** Find the CDF $F(x)$.     **(iii)** Compute $P(X \geq 2)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The cholesterol level (mg/dL) of adult males in a city follows a Normal distribution with mean $\mu = 200$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 25$.  
    **(i)** Write the PDF.     **(ii)** Find the probability that a randomly selected male has cholesterol between 180 and 220 mg/dL.     **(iii)** Find the value $x$ such that $P(X < x) = 0.95$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a)(i): Verify Validity

**Check 1 (Non-negativity)**: For $1 \leq x \leq 3$, $f(x) = x/8 > 0$. ✓

**Check 2 (Total area)**:

$$\int_{1}^{3} \frac{x}{8} \, dx = \frac{1}{8} \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} \right]_{1}^{3} = \frac{1}{8} \left( \frac{9}{2} - \frac{1}{2} \right) = \frac{1}{8} \cdot 4 = \frac{1}{2} \neq 1$$

⚠️ **Correction**: This function as given is NOT a valid PDF. The corrected PDF should be $f(x) = \frac{x}{4}$ for $1 \leq x \leq 3$. For KTU purposes, students are expected to identify this error.

**Using the corrected PDF** $f(x) = \frac{x}{4}$:

$$\int_{1}^{3} \frac{x}{4} \, dx = \frac{1}{4} \cdot 4 = 1 \checkmark$$

#### Part (a)(ii): Find CDF $F(x)$

For $x < 1$: $F(x) = 0$

For $1 \leq x \leq 3$:

$$F(x) = \int_{1}^{x} \frac{t}{4} \, dt = \frac{1}{4} \left[ \frac{t^2}{2} \right]_{1}^{x} = \frac{x^2 - 1}{8}$$

For $x > 3$: $F(x) = 1$

$$F(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 1 \\ \frac{x^2 - 1}{8}, & 1 \leq x \leq 3 \\ 1, & x > 3 \end{cases}$$

#### Part (a)(iii): Compute $P(X \geq 2)$

$$P(X \geq 2) = 1 - P(X < 2) = 1 - F(2)$$

$$F(2) = \frac{4 - 1}{8} = \frac{3}{8}$$

$$P(X \geq 2) = 1 - \frac{3}{8} = \frac{5}{8}$$

#### Part (b)(i): PDF of Normal Distribution

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{25\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{(x-200)^2}{2 \cdot 625}} = \frac{1}{25\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{(x-200)^2}{1250}}$$

#### Part (b)(ii): $P(180 \leq X \leq 220)$

$$z_1 = \frac{180 - 200}{25} = -0.8, \quad z_2 = \frac{220 - 200}{25} = 0.8$$

$$P(180 \leq X \leq 220) = \Phi(0.8) - \Phi(-0.8) = 2\Phi(0.8) - 1$$

$$\Phi(0.8) \approx 0.7881$$

$$P(180 \leq X \leq 220) = 2(0.7881) - 1 = 0.5762$$

#### Part (b)(iii): Find $x$ such that $P(X < x) = 0.95$

From standard normal table: $z_{0.95} \approx 1.645$

$$x = \mu + z \cdot \sigma = 200 + 1.645 \cdot 25 = 200 + 41.125 = 241.125 \text{ mg/dL}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Students often confuse PMF and PDF — remember, PDF gives *density*, not probability. You MUST integrate over an interval.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Forgetting to verify BOTH conditions (non-negativity AND unit area) for a valid PDF. If either fails, it's not a PDF.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Writing $P(X = x) = f(x)$ — this is **WRONG**. The correct statement is $P(a \leq X \leq b) = \int_a^b f(x) \, dx$.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Mixing up mean formulas for discrete ($E[X] = \sum x \cdot p(x)$) and continuous ($E[X] = \int x \cdot f(x) \, dx$).
> - **Common Mistake 5**: In Normal distribution problems, not standardizing properly — always convert to $Z = (X - \mu)/\sigma$ before using tables.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **PDF Definition**: $f(x)$ for continuous $X$; area under curve = probability.
- [x] **Two Validity Conditions**: $f(x) \geq 0$ and $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx = 1$.
- [x] **Single-point probability is ZERO**: $P(X = x) = 0$.
- [x] **Probability over interval**: $P(a \leq X \leq b) = \int_{a}^{b} f(x) \, dx$.
- [x] **CDF**: $F(x) = P(X \leq x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t) \, dt$.
- [x] **PDF from CDF**: $f(x) = \dfrac{d}{dx} F(x)$.
- [x] **Mean**: $E[X] = \int x \cdot f(x) \, dx$.
- [x] **Variance**: $\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2$.
- [x] **Normal Distribution**: Symmetric, bell-shaped, parameterized by $\mu$ and $\sigma$.
- [x] **Exponential Distribution**: Memoryless, used for waiting times and survival.
- [x] **Standard Normal**: $Z = (X - \mu)/\sigma$; use Z-tables for probability.
- [x] **Survival Function**: $S(x) = 1 - F(x) = P(X > x)$ — important in medical/biostatistics.
- [x] **Median**: $F(m) = 0.5$.
- [x] **PMF vs PDF**: Discrete vs continuous — summation vs integration.
- [x] **Biostat applications**: Heights, BP, drug levels, recovery rates, survival times.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Binomial, Poisson and Normal Distributions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT416 - BIOSTATISTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Binomial, Poisson and Normal Distributions # 📘 MODULE 1: BINOMIAL, POISSON AND NORMAL DISTRIBUTIONS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is a Probability Distribution? A **probability distribution** is a mathematical function (or rule) that assigns a probability to every possible outcome of a random experiment. In biostatistics, we use these distributions to model the **uncertainty in biological, medical, and health-related data** — for example, the number of patients who recover, the number of bacterial colonies in a culture, or the distribution of blood pressure readings in a population. > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** A probability distribution is a mathematical description of a random phenomenon in terms of the probabilities of events. For a discrete random variable $X$, it is given by $P(X = x_i) = p_i$, where $p_i \geq 0$ and $\sum p_i = 1$. For a continuous random variable, it is described by a probability density function $f(x)$ such that $P(a \leq X \leq b) = \int_a^b f(x)\,dx$. ### 1.2 The Big Three Discrete & Continuous Distributions The three distributions in this module form the **backbone of inferential biostatistics**: | Distribution | Type | Core Use in Biostatistics | |--------------|------|---------------------------| | **Binomial** | Discrete | Number of successes in $n$ independent trials | | **Poisson** | Discrete | Number of rare events in a fixed interval | | **Normal** | Continuous | Continuous measurements (height, BP, weight) | --- ### 1.3 Binomial Distribution — Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** A discrete random variable $X$ follows a **Binomial distribution** with parameters $n$ and $p$ if it represents the number of successes in $n$ independent Bernoulli trials, each with probability of success $p$. We write $X \sim B(n, p)$. **Probability Mass Function (PMF):** $$P(X = x) = \binom{n}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n-x}, \quad x = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n$$ **🎯 Conceptual Analogy — "The Coin Toss Medical Trial":** Imagine a doctor testing a new drug on 10 patients. Each patient independently either *recovers* (success, probability $p = 0.7$) or *does not recover* (failure, probability $1 - p = 0.3$). The number of recoveries among the 10 patients is a Binomial random variable. Just like flipping a coin 10 times, except now the coin is "biased" with probability $p$. --- ### 1.4 Poisson Distribution — Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** A discrete random variable $X$ follows a **Poisson distribution** with parameter $\lambda > 0$ if it represents the number of events occurring independently in a fixed interval of time or space, at a constant average rate $\lambda$. We write $X \sim P(\lambda)$. **Probability Mass Function (PMF):** $$P(X = x) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}, \quad x = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$ **🎯 Conceptual Analogy — "Rare Events in a Hospital Ward":** Count the number of patients who arrive at the emergency room in a 1-hour interval. On average, 3 patients arrive per hour. Some hours 1 arrives, some hours 5 arrive, but the **average rate** is fixed. This is the **Poisson process** — events happen independently, one at a time, at a constant average rate. Other examples: number of bacteria per microscopic field, number of mutations per gene, number of asthma attacks per week. > [!TIP] > **Key Insight:** Poisson distribution is a **limiting case of the Binomial distribution** when $n \to \infty$, $p \to 0$, and $np = \lambda$ remains constant. This is the famous **Poisson approximation to the Binomial**. --- ### 1.5 Normal Distribution — Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** A continuous random variable $X$ follows a **Normal (Gaussian) distribution** with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$ if its probability density function (PDF) is the classic "bell curve." We write $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. **Probability Density Function (PDF):** $$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right), \quad -\infty < x < \infty$$ **🎯 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bell Curve of Human Traits":** Measure the heights of 1,000 adult males. Most will be clustered around the average (say, 170 cm). Very few will be 140 cm or 200 cm. Plot the frequencies — you get a **symmetric bell-shaped curve**. Blood pressure, cholesterol levels, IQ scores, and measurement errors all follow this distribution. It is often called the **"most important distribution in statistics"** because of the **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)**. > [!NOTE] > **Special Case — Standard Normal Distribution:** When $\mu = 0$ and $\sigma = 1$, we have $Z \sim N(0,1)$. Any Normal variable can be converted to $Z$ using the **Z-transformation** (or standardization): > $$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$ --- ### 1.6 Why Are These Three Distributions So Important in Biostatistics? > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** These three distributions are interconnected through classical limit theorems: > 1. **Binomial** → models binary outcomes (success/failure) > 2. **Poisson** → models rare events; is a limiting case of Binomial > 3. **Normal** → approximates Binomial (for large $n$) and Poisson (for large $\lambda$); and is the limit of the sample mean of i.i.d. random variables (CLT) This hierarchy makes them the **foundation of all parametric statistical inference** in medical and biological research. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Binomial Distribution — Detailed Theoretical Analysis ### 2.1.1 Assumptions (Bernoulli Trials) A random experiment qualifies as a **Binomial experiment** if it satisfies ALL four conditions: 1. **Fixed number of trials:** The number of trials $n$ is fixed in advance. 2. **Two mutually exclusive outcomes:** Each trial results in either "success" or "failure." 3. **Constant probability:** The probability of success $p$ remains the same for every trial. 4. **Independence:** The outcome of one trial does not influence another. > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid:** Think **"S-F-C-I"** → **S**uccess/Failure, **F**ixed $n$, **C**onstant $p$, **I**ndependence. ### 2.1.2 Structural Properties of Binomial Distribution - The distribution is **unimodal** (one peak) when $(n+1)p$ is not an integer. - If $(n+1)p$ is an integer, the distribution is **bimodal** with peaks at $x = (n+1)p$ and $x = (n+1)p - 1$. - The distribution is **skewed right** if $p < 0.5$, **skewed left** if $p > 0.5$, and **symmetric** if $p = 0.5$. - As $n$ increases, the Binomial approaches the Normal distribution (De Moivre–Laplace Theorem). ### 2.1.3 Mean, Variance, and Skewness $$E(X) = np$$ $$\text{Var}(X) = np(1-p) = npq, \quad \text{where } q = 1 - p$$ $$\text{Standard Deviation: } \sigma = \sqrt{npq}$$ $$\text{Skewness: } \gamma_1 = \frac{q - p}{\sqrt{npq}}$$ --- ## 2.2 Poisson Distribution — Detailed Theoretical Analysis ### 2.2.1 Assumptions (Poisson Process) A random experiment qualifies as a **Poisson process** if: 1. Events occur **independently** of one another. 2. The **average rate** $\lambda$ is constant over time/space. 3. Two events cannot occur at **exactly the same instant**. 4. Events are **countable** (discrete outcomes). ### 2.2.2 Why Poisson Distribution is the "Law of Rare Events" Poisson distribution arises naturally as a **limiting case of the Binomial** when: - The number of trials $n \to \infty$ - The probability of success $p \to 0$ - The product $np = \lambda$ (a finite constant) ### 2.2.3 Mean, Variance, and Skewness $$E(X) = \lambda$$ $$\text{Var}(X) = \lambda$$ $$\text{Standard Deviation: } \sigma = \sqrt{\lambda}$$ $$\text{Skewness: } \gamma_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Remarkable Property of Poisson:** Mean equals Variance. This is a unique identifying signature. If you see a count dataset where $\bar{x} \approx s^2$, suspect Poisson. --- ## 2.3 Normal Distribution — Detailed Theoretical Analysis ### 2.3.1 Properties of the Normal Distribution 1. **Symmetric** about the mean $\mu$: $f(\mu + x) = f(\mu - x)$. 2. **Bell-shaped** and **asymptotic** to the horizontal axis (never touches zero). 3. **Mean = Median = Mode = $\mu$** (all coincide at the center). 4. **Inflection points** occur at $x = \mu \pm \sigma$. 5. **Total area under the curve** equals 1: $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1$. ### 2.3.2 The Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7 Rule) For any Normal distribution $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$: | Range | Probability of Falling Within | |-------|------------------------------| | $\mu \pm 1\sigma$ | **68.27%** | | $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ | **95.45%** | | $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ | **99.73%** | > [!NOTE] > **Biostatistical Application:** When measuring patient biomarkers (e.g., blood glucose), values outside $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ are often flagged as "clinically unusual" — a cornerstone of laboratory reference ranges. ### 2.3.3 Mean, Variance, and Skewness/Kurtosis $$E(X) = \mu$$ $$\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2$$ $$\text{Standard Deviation: } \sigma$$ $$\text{Skewness: } \gamma_1 = 0 \quad \text{(perfectly symmetric)}$$ $$\text{Kurtosis: } \gamma_2 = 3 \quad \text{(mesokurtic)}$$ ### 2.3.4 Standard Normal Distribution & Z-Scores For $Z \sim N(0, 1)$, the PDF simplifies to: $$\phi(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-z^2 / 2}$$ **Z-transformation** to convert any Normal variable to standard Normal: $$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma} \sim N(0,1)$$ --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Distribution | PMF / PDF | Mean $\mu$ | Variance $\sigma^2$ | Skewness | MGF / Characteristic Function | |---|--------------|-----------|------------|---------------------|----------|-------------------------------| | 1 | **Binomial** $B(n,p)$ | $P(X=x)=\binom{n}{x}p^x q^{n-x}$ | $np$ | $npq$ | $\dfrac{q-p}{\sqrt{npq}}$ | $M_X(t)=(pe^t+q)^n$ | | 2 | **Poisson** $P(\lambda)$ | $P(X=x)=\dfrac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^x}{x!}$ | $\lambda$ | $\lambda$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda}}$ | $M_X(t)=e^{\lambda(e^t-1)}$ | | 3 | **Normal** $N(\mu,\sigma^2)$ | $f(x)=\dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-(x-\mu)^2/2\sigma^2}$ | $\mu$ | $\sigma^2$ | $0$ | $M_X(t)=e^{\mu t+\sigma^2 t^2/2}$ | ### 2.5 Key Limiting Theorems (High-Yield for KTU) | Theorem | Statement | Engineering/Medical Use | |---------|-----------|--------------------------| | **Poisson Limit of Binomial** | $B(n,p) \to P(\lambda)$ when $n \to \infty$, $p \to 0$, $np = \lambda$ | Approximating rare-event counts | | **Normal Limit of Binomial** | $B(n,p) \approx N(np, npq)$ when $n \geq 30$, $np \geq 5$, $nq \geq 5$ | Hypothesis testing on proportions | | **Normal Limit of Poisson** | $P(\lambda) \approx N(\lambda, \lambda)$ when $\lambda \geq 10$ | Approximating large count data | | **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)** | $\bar{X}_n \xrightarrow{d} N\!\left(\mu, \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)$ as $n \to \infty$ | Foundation of all large-sample inference | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse the parameters. Binomial has TWO parameters $(n, p)$; Poisson has ONE parameter $(\lambda)$; Normal has TWO parameters $(\mu, \sigma^2)$. Mixing them up is the #1 mistake in board exams. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Biomedical Engineering > [!TIP] > **Engineering Connections (KTU 2024 NEP context):** > - **Binomial** → Quality control of diagnostic test kits (defective vs. non-defective). > - **Poisson** → Modeling the arrival of patients in an ICU; modeling the number of errors in DNA sequences. > - **Normal** → Signal noise in biomedical instrumentation (ECG, EEG); measurement error analysis in calibration. > - **CLT** → Justifies the use of $Z$-tests and $t$-tests in clinical trials. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Binomial PMF (From First Principles) ### Step 1: Define a Single Bernoulli Trial A single trial has probability of success $p$ and probability of failure $q = 1 - p$. ### Step 2: Probability of a Specific Sequence For a specific sequence with $x$ successes and $(n - x)$ failures (in a fixed order), the probability is: $$P(\text{that specific sequence}) = p^x \cdot q^{n-x}$$ This uses the **multiplication rule** for independent events. ### Step 3: Count the Number of Such Sequences The number of ways to arrange $x$ successes among $n$ trials is the **binomial coefficient**: $$\binom{n}{x} = \frac{n!}{x!\,(n-x)!}$$ ### Step 4: Apply the Addition Rule Since the sequences are **mutually exclusive**, we add their probabilities: $$P(X = x) = \binom{n}{x} p^x q^{n-x}$$ This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Mean of the Binomial Distribution Let $X_i$ be the Bernoulli indicator for the $i$-th trial: $$X_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if success (probability } p) \\ 0 & \text{if failure (probability } q) \end{cases}$$ Then $X = \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$. ### Step 1: Find $E(X_i)$ $$E(X_i) = 1 \cdot p + 0 \cdot q = p$$ ### Step 2: Apply Linearity of Expectation $$E(X) = E\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E(X_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p = np$$ Therefore, $E(X) = np$. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation of the Variance of the Binomial Distribution ### Step 1: Find $\text{Var}(X_i)$ $$\text{Var}(X_i) = E(X_i^2) - [E(X_i)]^2 = p - p^2 = p(1-p) = pq$$ (since $X_i^2 = X_i$ for a 0-1 variable) ### Step 2: Apply the Independence Property Since the trials are independent, the variance of the sum is the sum of variances: $$\text{Var}(X) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}(X_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} pq = npq$$ Therefore, $\text{Var}(X) = npq$. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation of the Poisson PMF (As a Limit of Binomial) ### Step 1: Start with the Binomial PMF $$P(X = x) = \binom{n}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n-x}$$ ### Step 2: Substitute $p = \lambda / n$ (so that $np = \lambda$) $$P(X = x) = \binom{n}{x} \left(\frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^x \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n-x}$$ ### Step 3: Expand the Binomial Coefficient $$\binom{n}{x} = \frac{n!}{x!\,(n-x)!} = \frac{n(n-1)(n-2)\cdots(n-x+1)}{x!}$$ ### Step 4: Separate the Terms That Will Be Limits $$\begin{aligned} P(X = x) &= \frac{n(n-1)\cdots(n-x+1)}{x!} \cdot \frac{\lambda^x}{n^x} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n-x} \\[4pt] &= \frac{1}{x!} \cdot \frac{n(n-1)\cdots(n-x+1)}{n^x} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n-x} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 5: Take the Limit as $n \to \infty$ $$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n(n-1)\cdots(n-x+1)}{n^x} = 1$$ $$\lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n} = e^{-\lambda}$$ $$\lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{-x} = 1$$ ### Step 6: Combine the Limits $$P(X = x) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}$$ This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is a high-yield KTU derivation** — board examiners frequently ask for the "Poisson as a limit of Binomial." --- ## 3.5 Derivation of the Mean and Variance of the Poisson Distribution ### Step 1: Mean We use the fact that $E(X) = \sum_{x=0}^{\infty} x \cdot P(X = x)$: $$E(X) = \sum_{x=0}^{\infty} x \cdot \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!} = e^{-\lambda} \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^x}{(x-1)!}$$ Let $k = x - 1$: $$E(X) = e^{-\lambda} \cdot \lambda \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^k}{k!} = e^{-\lambda} \cdot \lambda \cdot e^{\lambda} = \lambda$$ Therefore, $E(X) = \lambda$. $\blacksquare$ ### Step 2: Variance We use $\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2$: $$E(X^2) = E[X(X-1)] + E(X)$$ Computing $E[X(X-1)]$: $$E[X(X-1)] = \sum_{x=0}^{\infty} x(x-1) \cdot \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!} = e^{-\lambda} \lambda^2 \sum_{x=2}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{x-2}}{(x-2)!} = \lambda^2$$ Therefore: $$\text{Var}(X) = \lambda^2 + \lambda - \lambda^2 = \lambda$$ So $\text{Var}(X) = \lambda$. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.6 Derivation of the Mean and Variance of the Normal Distribution ### Step 1: Mean $E(X) = \mu$ By definition: $$E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(x-\mu)^2/2\sigma^2}\,dx$$ Use substitution $z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$, so $x = \mu + \sigma z$ and $dx = \sigma\,dz$: $$E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\mu + \sigma z) \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2} \cdot \sigma\,dz$$ $$\begin{aligned} E(X) &= \mu \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2}\,dz + \sigma \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{z}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2}\,dz \\[4pt] &= \mu \cdot 1 + \sigma \cdot 0 = \mu \end{aligned}$$ (The second integral vanishes because the integrand is an odd function.) Therefore, $E(X) = \mu$. $\blacksquare$ ### Step 2: Variance $\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2$ $$\text{Var}(X) = E[(X - \mu)^2] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (x - \mu)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(x-\mu)^2/2\sigma^2}\,dx$$ Using the same substitution $z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$: $$\text{Var}(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\sigma z)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2} \cdot \sigma\,dz$$ $$\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2 \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{z^2}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2}\,dz$$ The integral $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z^2 \cdot \phi(z)\,dz = 1$ (the second moment of the standard Normal). Thus: $$\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2 \cdot 1 = \sigma^2 \quad \blacksquare$$ --- ## 3.7 Standardization (Z-Transformation) — Step by Step Given $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, we want to find a new variable $Z \sim N(0, 1)$. ### Step 1: Subtract the Mean Centering: $Y = X - \mu \implies Y \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$. ### Step 2: Divide by Standard Deviation Scaling: $Z = Y / \sigma = (X - \mu)/\sigma$. ### Step 3: Verify $Z \sim N(0, 1)$ - $E(Z) = (E(X) - \mu)/\sigma = 0$ ✓ - $\text{Var}(Z) = \text{Var}(X)/\sigma^2 = 1$ ✓ Therefore: $$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma} \sim N(0, 1) \quad \blacksquare$$ --- ## 3.8 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Binomial **Problem:** In a clinical trial, the probability that a patient recovers after a new drug is $p = 0.8$. If 6 patients are treated, find the probability that exactly 4 recover. **Solution:** $X \sim B(6, 0.8)$, find $P(X = 4)$. $$P(X = 4) = \binom{6}{4} (0.8)^4 (0.2)^2$$ $$\begin{aligned} \binom{6}{4} &= 15 \\ (0.8)^4 &= 0.4096 \\ (0.2)^2 &= 0.04 \\ P(X = 4) &= 15 \times 0.4096 \times 0.04 = 0.2458 \end{aligned}$$ Therefore, $P(X = 4) = 0.2458$. --- ## 3.9 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Poisson **Problem:** The number of emergency admissions to a hospital per hour follows a Poisson distribution with mean $\lambda = 3$. Find the probability that exactly 5 admissions occur in a given hour. **Solution:** $X \sim P(3)$, find $P(X = 5)$. $$P(X = 5) = \frac{e^{-3} \cdot 3^5}{5!}$$ $$\begin{aligned} e^{-3} &= 0.04979 \\ 3^5 &= 243 \\ 5! &= 120 \\ P(X = 5) &= \frac{0.04979 \times 243}{120} = \frac{12.099}{120} = 0.1008 \end{aligned}$$ Therefore, $P(X = 5) \approx 0.1008$. --- ## 3.10 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Normal **Problem:** The serum cholesterol level in a population is Normally distributed with mean $\mu = 200$ mg/dL and standard deviation $\sigma = 20$ mg/dL. Find the probability that a randomly selected person has cholesterol between 180 and 220 mg/dL. **Solution:** Convert to Z-scores: $$Z_1 = \frac{180 - 200}{20} = -1.0, \quad Z_2 = \frac{220 - 200}{20} = 1.0$$ $$P(180 \leq X \leq 220) = P(-1 \leq Z \leq 1) = \Phi(1) - \Phi(-1)$$ Using symmetry, $\Phi(-1) = 1 - \Phi(1)$: $$P(-1 \leq Z \leq 1) = 2\Phi(1) - 1 = 2(0.8413) - 1 = 0.6826$$ Therefore, $P(180 \leq X \leq 220) \approx 0.6826$ (consistent with the 68% rule). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Flowchart: The Three Distributions & Their Relationships ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Discrete Random Variable"] --> B{"Countable
Outcomes?"} B -- Yes --> C["Discrete Distributions"] B -- No --> D["Continuous Distributions"] C --> E["Binomial B np"] C --> F["Poisson P lambda"] D --> G["Normal N mu sigma^2"] E -- "n large, p fixed" --> H["Approximated by Normal"] E -- "n large, p small,
np = lambda" --> F F -- "lambda large" --> H G --> I["Standardized to Z 0 1"] H --> I I --> J["Used for Hypothesis
Testing and CI"] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Binomial Distribution Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Bernoulli Trial 1
p success, q fail"] --> D["Sum X = X1 + X2 + ... + Xn"] B["Bernoulli Trial 2
p success, q fail"] --> D C["Bernoulli Trial n
p success, q fail"] --> D D --> E["X follows B n p"] E --> F["Mean = np"] E --> G["Variance = npq"] E --> H["PMF = C n x p^x q^n-x"] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Poisson Distribution Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Poisson Process"] --> B["Constant rate lambda"] A --> C["Independent events"] A --> D["No simultaneous events"] B --> E["X follows P lambda"] C --> E D --> E E --> F["Mean = lambda"] E --> G["Variance = lambda"] E --> H["PMF = e^-lambda lambda^x / x!"] E --> I["Used in rare event modeling
ICU arrivals, mutations"] ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram: Normal Distribution Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Continuous Random Variable X"] --> B["N mu sigma^2"] B --> C["Symmetric Bell Curve"] B --> D["Mean = Median = Mode = mu"] B --> E["Inflection points at mu +- sigma"] B --> F["Standardize Z = X-mu/sigma"] F --> G["Z follows N 0 1"] G --> H["68 percent within 1 sigma"] G --> I["95 percent within 2 sigma"] G --> J["99.7 percent within 3 sigma"] ``` ## 4.5 Comparative Decision Diagram: Which Distribution to Use? ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Start: Identify Random Variable"] --> Q1{"Is the variable
discrete or continuous?"} Q1 -- "Discrete count of successes
in n trials" --> Binom["Use Binomial B n p"] Q1 -- "Discrete count of rare
events in interval" --> Pois["Use Poisson P lambda"] Q1 -- "Continuous symmetric
bell-shaped data" --> Norm["Use Normal N mu sigma^2"] Binom --> Q2{"Is n large and p small?"} Q2 -- "Yes, np = lambda" --> Pois Q2 -- "No" --> Norm Q2 -- "n large, p not small" --> Norm Pois --> Q3{"Is lambda large?"} Q3 -- "Yes, lambda >= 10" --> Norm ``` > [!TIP] > **Use this decision tree during the KTU exam** to quickly identify which distribution a problem is asking about. It is a high-yield visual summary. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** ### Q1. Define the Binomial distribution. State its mean and variance. **Model Answer:** A random variable $X$ is said to follow a Binomial distribution with parameters $n$ and $p$ if it represents the number of successes in $n$ independent Bernoulli trials, each with constant probability of success $p$. Its PMF is: $$P(X = x) = \binom{n}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n-x}, \quad x = 0, 1, \ldots, n$$ Mean: $E(X) = np$, Variance: $\text{Var}(X) = np(1-p)$. --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** ### Q2. State any three properties of the Normal distribution. **Model Answer:** 1. The Normal curve is **symmetric** about the mean $\mu$; therefore mean = median = mode. 2. It is **bell-shaped** and asymptotic to the horizontal axis. 3. Approximately **68.27%** of observations lie within $\mu \pm 1\sigma$, **95.45%** within $\mu \pm 2\sigma$, and **99.73%** within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ (Empirical Rule). 4. The total area under the curve equals 1. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** ### 📝 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Binomial & Poisson Focus **Q(a)** **[7 Marks]** Derive the mean and variance of the Binomial distribution $B(n, p)$. **Model Solution:** Let $X_i$ be the indicator of success in the $i$-th trial. Then $X_i = 1$ with probability $p$ and $X_i = 0$ with probability $q = 1 - p$. The total number of successes is $X = \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$. **Mean:** $$E(X_i) = 1 \cdot p + 0 \cdot q = p$$ By linearity of expectation: $$E(X) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E(X_i) = np$$ **Variance:** $$E(X_i^2) = 1^2 \cdot p + 0^2 \cdot q = p$$ $$\text{Var}(X_i) = E(X_i^2) - [E(X_i)]^2 = p - p^2 = p(1-p) = pq$$ Since the trials are independent: $$\text{Var}(X) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}(X_i) = npq$$ Therefore, $E(X) = np$ and $\text{Var}(X) = npq$. $\blacksquare$ --- **Q(b)** **[7 Marks]** The number of asthma attacks in a chronic patient per week follows a Poisson distribution with mean $\lambda = 2$. Find (i) the probability of exactly 3 attacks in a week, and (ii) the probability of at most 1 attack in a week. **Model Solution:** $X \sim P(2)$, so $P(X = x) = \dfrac{e^{-2} \cdot 2^x}{x!}$. **(i) Probability of exactly 3 attacks:** $$P(X = 3) = \frac{e^{-2} \cdot 2^3}{3!} = \frac{(0.1353)(8)}{6} = \frac{1.0824}{6} = 0.1804$$ **(ii) Probability of at most 1 attack:** $$P(X \leq 1) = P(X = 0) + P(X = 1)$$ $$P(X = 0) = \frac{e^{-2} \cdot 2^0}{0!} = e^{-2} = 0.1353$$ $$P(X = 1) = \frac{e^{-2} \cdot 2^1}{1!} = 2e^{-2} = 0.2707$$ $$P(X \leq 1) = 0.1353 + 0.2707 = 0.4060$$ **Final Answer:** (i) $P(X = 3) \approx 0.1804$, (ii) $P(X \leq 1) \approx 0.4060$. --- ### 📝 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Normal Distribution & Approximations **Q(a)** **[7 Marks]** Define the Normal distribution. State and prove the empirical rule (68-95-99.7) using the standard normal distribution. **Model Solution:** A continuous random variable $X$ follows a Normal distribution $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ if its PDF is: $$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(x-\mu)^2/2\sigma^2}, \quad x \in (-\infty, \infty)$$ **Empirical Rule Proof Sketch:** Using $Z = (X - \mu)/\sigma \sim N(0,1)$ and standard normal tables: - $P(-1 \leq Z \leq 1) = 2\Phi(1) - 1 = 2(0.8413) - 1 = 0.6827 \approx 68.27\%$ - $P(-2 \leq Z \leq 2) = 2\Phi(2) - 1 = 2(0.9772) - 1 = 0.9545 \approx 95.45\%$ - $P(-3 \leq Z \leq 3) = 2\Phi(3) - 1 = 2(0.9987) - 1 = 0.9973 \approx 99.73\%$ Therefore, the empirical rule holds. $\blacksquare$ --- **Q(b)** **[7 Marks]** The systolic blood pressure of adult males is Normally distributed with mean $\mu = 120$ mmHg and standard deviation $\sigma = 10$ mmHg. Find (i) the probability that a randomly selected male has BP between 110 and 130 mmHg, and (ii) the BP value $x$ such that only 5% of males exceed it. **Model Solution:** $X \sim N(120, 100)$. **(i) Probability of BP between 110 and 130:** $$Z_1 = \frac{110 - 120}{10} = -1.0, \quad Z_2 = \frac{130 - 120}{10} = 1.0$$ $$P(110 \leq X \leq 130) = P(-1 \leq Z \leq 1) = 2(0.8413) - 1 = 0.6826$$ **(ii) Find $x$ such that $P(X > x) = 0.05$:** This means $P(X \leq x) = 0.95$, so we need $z$ such that $\Phi(z) = 0.95$. From tables, $z = 1.645$. $$x = \mu + z\sigma = 120 + 1.645 \times 10 = 120 + 16.45 = 136.45 \text{ mmHg}$$ **Final Answer:** (i) $P(110 \leq X \leq 130) \approx 0.6826$, (ii) $x \approx 136.45$ mmHg. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing parameters:** Students often write the Poisson PMF with $e^{\lambda}$ instead of $e^{-\lambda}$ in the numerator. **Always check the sign of the exponent.** > > 2. **Forgetting the binomial coefficient:** Many students write $P(X = x) = p^x q^{n-x}$ without the $\binom{n}{x}$ factor. This is a fatal error worth 3–4 marks. > > 3. **Mixing up standard deviation with standard error:** In CLT, the standard deviation of the sample mean is $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$, not $\sigma$. Examiners specifically look for this. > > 4. **Skipping the substitution step in Z-transformation:** Always show $Z = (X - \mu)/\sigma$ explicitly and substitute numerical values, not just state the formula. > > 5. **Using Normal approximation without checking validity:** For $B(n, p) \approx N(np, npq)$, you MUST verify $np \geq 5$ and $nq \geq 5$. Examiners deduct marks if this is missing. > > 6. **Unit errors in biostatistics:** Always include units (mg/dL, mmHg) in your final answer for clinical realism. Examiners award grace marks for this. > > 7. **Skewness direction:** "Skewed right" means tail is on the **right** (positive skew), which occurs when $p < 0.5$ for Binomial. Students often invert this. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **🔥 Last-Minute Revision Flashcard — Binomial, Poisson & Normal** - ✅ **Binomial $B(n, p)$** → discrete, $n$ trials, 2 outcomes, $E(X) = np$, $\text{Var}(X) = npq$. - ✅ **Poisson $P(\lambda)$** → discrete, rare events, $E(X) = \text{Var}(X) = \lambda$, signature property: mean = variance. - ✅ **Normal $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$** → continuous, bell-shaped, $E(X) = \mu$, $\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2$, mean = median = mode. - ✅ **Z-score formula** → $Z = (X - \mu)/\sigma \sim N(0, 1)$. - ✅ **Empirical Rule** → 68% (1$\sigma$), 95% (2$\sigma$), 99.7% (3$\sigma$). - ✅ **Poisson as limit of Binomial** → $n \to \infty$, $p \to 0$, $np = \lambda$. - ✅ **Normal approximation to Binomial** → valid when $n \geq 30$, $np \geq 5$, $nq \geq 5$. - ✅ **Normal approximation to Poisson** → valid when $\lambda \geq 10$. - ✅ **CLT** → $\bar{X}_n \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2/n)$ for large $n$. - ✅ **PMF of Binomial** → $P(X=x) = \binom{n}{x}p^x q^{n-x}$ (NEVER forget the binomial coefficient). - ✅ **PMF of Poisson** → $P(X=x) = e^{-\lambda}\lambda^x / x!$ (NEVER forget $e^{-\lambda}$, not $e^{\lambda}$). - ✅ **PDF of Normal** → $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(x-\mu)^2/2\sigma^2}$. - ✅ **Standard normal** → $\phi(z) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2}$, total area = 1. - ✅ **Additivity** → $X_1 \sim N(\mu_1, \sigma_1^2)$, $X_2 \sim N(\mu_2, \sigma_2^2)$ independent ⟹ $X_1 + X_2 \sim N(\mu_1 + \mu_2, \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2)$. - ✅ **Skewness** → Binomial: $(q-p)/\sqrt{npq}$; Poisson: $1/\sqrt{\lambda}$; Normal: $0$. - ✅ **Biomedical examples** → Binomial: drug response (yes/no); Poisson: ICU arrivals, mutations; Normal: BP, height, weight. > 🎯 **Good luck on your KTU 2024 ESE! Master these three distributions and you have the foundation of all inferential biostatistics.**

topics.title: Joint Probability Density Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT416 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Joint Probability Density Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Joint Probability Density Function (Joint PDF)** of two continuous random variables $X$ and $Y$, denoted as $f_{X,Y}(x,y)$ or simply $f(x,y)$, is a non-negative bivariate function that completely describes the simultaneous probabilistic behaviour of $X$ and $Y$. For any two-dimensional region $R$ in the $xy$-plane, the probability that the random point $(X,Y)$ falls inside $R$ is obtained by evaluating the **double integral** of the joint PDF over that region.

$$P((X,Y) \in R) = \iint\limits_{R} f(x,y) \, dx \, dy$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Fundamental Properties of a Valid Joint PDF:**
> 1. **Non-negativity:** $f(x,y) \geq 0$ for all $(x,y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$
> 2. **Total Unit Probability:** $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x,y) \, dx \, dy = 1$
>
> If a function violates even one of these, it is **not** a valid joint PDF.

The **Joint Cumulative Distribution Function (Joint CDF)** $F(x,y) = P(X \leq x, Y \leq y)$ is related to the joint PDF through partial differentiation:

$$f(x,y) = \frac{\partial^2 F(x,y)}{\partial x \, \partial y}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 Biostatistics syllabus, the joint PDF is the cornerstone for deriving marginal distributions, conditional distributions, covariance, and correlation — all of which appear in Part A and Part B questions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> **The 3D Mountain Landscape Analogy**

Imagine a vast 3D mountain landscape where the horizontal plane is the $xy$-plane and the **height** of the mountain at any point $(x,y)$ represents the probability density $f(x,y)$.

- **Probability** is the **volume under the mountain**, not the height itself. Just as a wide, low hill can hold the same volume as a tall, narrow peak.
- The **total volume** of the entire mountain must equal exactly **1** (100% probability).
- A **Marginal PDF** is the *shadow* the mountain casts when light is shined parallel to the $y$-axis (giving $f_X(x)$) or the $x$-axis (giving $f_Y(y)$). The shadow collapses one dimension of information.
- **Independence** means the mountain has a special *separable* shape — like a roof tile — that is the product of a curve in the $x$-direction and a curve in the $y$-direction.

**Biostatistics Context:** In a clinical study, let $X$ = systolic blood pressure (mmHg) and $Y$ = cholesterol level (mg/dL) of patients. The joint PDF $f(x,y)$ tells you how *likely* a randomly selected patient has both a particular BP **and** a particular cholesterol value simultaneously — something the individual marginals cannot capture.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** A joint PDF is a *recipe* for probabilities in two dimensions. Want a probability? Integrate the recipe over the region of interest.

---

## 1.3 Bivariate Extension (for general awareness)

The concept extends naturally to $n$ random variables. For $n$ continuous random variables $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$, the **joint PDF** $f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ satisfies:

$$f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \geq 0, \quad \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\cdots\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x_1, \ldots, x_n) \, dx_1 \cdots dx_n = 1$$

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Joint PDF Concepts

The joint PDF framework rests on **four pillars** that the KTU examiner frequently tests:

### Pillar 1 — The Constant Normalization Step
When given a function with an unknown constant $k$ (e.g., $f(x,y) = k(x+y)$ over a region), you must always find $k$ first by enforcing the unit-probability condition. This is **Step Zero** in almost every KTU numerical problem.

### Pillar 2 — Marginal PDFs (Projection of the Mountain)
- The **marginal PDF of $X$** is obtained by integrating the joint PDF over **all possible values of $Y$**.
- The **marginal PDF of $Y$** is obtained by integrating the joint PDF over **all possible values of $X$**.

This is mathematically "collapsing" the 2D information onto a 1D axis.

### Pillar 3 — Conditional PDFs (Slicing the Mountain)
- The conditional PDF $f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y)$ describes the distribution of $X$ **given that** $Y$ has taken a specific value $y$.
- It is derived as a ratio: joint divided by the marginal of the conditioning variable.

### Pillar 4 — Independence (The Separability Test)
Two continuous random variables $X$ and $Y$ are **independent** if and only if their joint PDF factorizes into the product of their marginals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The independence test is **bidirectional** — you can test it from joint CDFs, joint PDFs, or even the joint PMF. In Module 1, the PDF version is mandatory.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Domain / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Joint PDF definition | $P((X,Y) \in R) = \displaystyle\iint_R f(x,y) \, dx \, dy$ | $R \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ |
| 2 | Non-negativity | $f(x,y) \geq 0$ | For all $(x,y)$ |
| 3 | Normalization | $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x,y) \, dx \, dy = 1$ | Over entire plane |
| 4 | Joint CDF | $F(x,y) = P(X \leq x, Y \leq y)$ | Monotonic non-decreasing |
| 5 | PDF from CDF | $f(x,y) = \dfrac{\partial^2 F(x,y)}{\partial x \, \partial y}$ | Where continuous |
| 6 | Marginal of $X$ | $f_X(x) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x,y) \, dy$ | Eliminates $y$ |
| 7 | Marginal of $Y$ | $f_Y(y) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x,y) \, dx$ | Eliminates $x$ |
| 8 | Conditional of $X$ given $Y = y$ | $f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y) = \dfrac{f(x,y)}{f_Y(y)}$ | $f_Y(y) > 0$ |
| 9 | Conditional of $Y$ given $X = x$ | $f_{Y \mid X}(y \mid x) = \dfrac{f(x,y)}{f_X(x)}$ | $f_X(x) > 0$ |
| 10 | Independence condition | $f(x,y) = f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y)$ for all $(x,y)$ | Necessary & sufficient |
| 11 | Multiplication rule | $f(x,y) = f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y) \cdot f_Y(y)$ | Always valid |
| 12 | Expected value of $g(X,Y)$ | $E[g(X,Y)] = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(x,y) f(x,y) \, dx \, dy$ | If integral exists |
| 13 | $E[XY]$ | $E[XY] = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} xy \, f(x,y) \, dx \, dy$ | Existence required |
| 14 | Covariance | $\text{Cov}(X,Y) = E[XY] - E[X]\cdot E[Y]$ | $\vert \text{Cov} \vert$ bounded |
| 15 | Correlation coefficient | $\rho_{X,Y} = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y}$ | $-1 \leq \rho \leq 1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol caution:** In KTU answer scripts, examiners expect the limits of integration to match the support of the function. Writing $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}$ for a function that is zero outside $[0,1]$ wastes time and earns no penalty, but writing **wrong limits** loses marks.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Biostatistics Utility

| Field | Application of Joint PDF |
|---|---|
| **Clinical Trials** | Modelling joint response of two biomarkers (e.g., BP and glucose) to a drug |
| **Epidemiology** | Joint distribution of age and disease severity in a population |
| **Medical Imaging** | Joint distribution of pixel intensity in paired MRI/CT scans |
| **Pharmacokinetics** | Joint distribution of drug concentration in plasma and tissue |
| **Genetic Studies** | Joint distribution of two correlated gene expression levels |
| **Signal Processing** | Joint time-frequency distributions of biomedical signals (ECG, EEG) |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: The Canonical KTU-Style Problem

> **Problem Statement:**
> The joint PDF of two random variables $X$ and $Y$ is given by:
> $$f(x,y) = x + y, \quad 0 \leq x \leq 1, \quad 0 \leq y \leq 1$$
> and $f(x,y) = 0$ otherwise.
>
> Find:
> (a) Verify that $f(x,y)$ is a valid joint PDF.
> (b) Find the marginal PDFs $f_X(x)$ and $f_Y(y)$.
> (c) Test whether $X$ and $Y$ are independent.
> (d) Find the conditional PDF $f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y)$.
> (e) Compute $P(X > 0.5, Y > 0.5)$.

---

### (a) Verification of Validity

**Step 1:** Check non-negativity.
For $0 \leq x \leq 1$ and $0 \leq y \leq 1$, both $x$ and $y$ are non-negative, hence $f(x,y) = x + y \geq 0$. ✓

**Step 2:** Check unit total probability.

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x,y) \, dx \, dy = \int_0^1 \int_0^1 (x+y) \, dx \, dy$$

$$= \int_0^1 \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} + xy \right]_{x=0}^{x=1} dy$$

$$= \int_0^1 \left( \frac{1}{2} + y \right) dy$$

$$= \left[ \frac{y}{2} + \frac{y^2}{2} \right]_0^1 = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

Hence, $f(x,y) = x + y$ on $[0,1] \times [0,1]$ is a valid joint PDF.

---

### (b) Marginal PDFs

**Marginal of $X$** — integrate $f(x,y)$ over all $y$:

$$f_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x,y) \, dy = \int_0^1 (x+y) \, dy$$

$$= \left[ xy + \frac{y^2}{2} \right]_0^1 = x + \frac{1}{2}, \quad 0 \leq x \leq 1$$

**Marginal of $Y$** — integrate $f(x,y)$ over all $x$:

$$f_Y(y) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x,y) \, dx = \int_0^1 (x+y) \, dx$$

$$= \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} + xy \right]_0^1 = \frac{1}{2} + y, \quad 0 \leq y \leq 1$$

---

### (c) Test of Independence

If $X$ and $Y$ were independent, then $f(x,y) = f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y)$ for all $(x,y)$.

$$f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y) = \left(x + \frac{1}{2}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2} + y\right)$$

$$= \frac{1}{4} + xy + \frac{x}{2} + \frac{y}{2}$$

We compare this to $f(x,y) = x + y$.

Setting $x = 0.5, y = 0.5$:
- LHS: $f(0.5, 0.5) = 0.5 + 0.5 = 1$
- RHS: $f_X(0.5) \cdot f_Y(0.5) = (0.5 + 0.5)(0.5 + 0.5) = 1 \times 1 = 1$

Setting $x = 0, y = 0$:
- LHS: $f(0, 0) = 0 + 0 = 0$
- RHS: $f_X(0) \cdot f_Y(0) = (0 + 0.5)(0.5 + 0) = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25$

Since $0 \neq 0.25$, the equality fails.

$$\therefore f(x,y) \neq f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y) \implies X \text{ and } Y \text{ are NOT independent.}$$

---

### (d) Conditional PDF of $X$ given $Y = y$

By the definition of conditional PDF:

$$f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y) = \frac{f(x,y)}{f_Y(y)} = \frac{x + y}{\frac{1}{2} + y}, \quad 0 \leq x \leq 1, \; 0 \leq y \leq 1$$

**Sanity check:** Does this conditional PDF integrate to 1 over $x$?

$$\int_0^1 \frac{x + y}{\frac{1}{2} + y} \, dx = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{2} + y} \int_0^1 (x + y) \, dx = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{2} + y} \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} + xy \right]_0^1 = \frac{\frac{1}{2} + y}{\frac{1}{2} + y} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

---

### (e) Probability Calculation: $P(X > 0.5, Y > 0.5)$

$$P(X > 0.5, Y > 0.5) = \int_{0.5}^{1} \int_{0.5}^{1} (x + y) \, dy \, dx$$

**Inner integral (over $y$):**

$$\int_{0.5}^{1} (x + y) \, dy = \left[ xy + \frac{y^2}{2} \right]_{0.5}^{1}$$

$$= \left(x \cdot 1 + \frac{1}{2}\right) - \left(0.5x + \frac{0.25}{2}\right) = x + \frac{1}{2} - 0.5x - 0.125$$

$$= 0.5x + 0.375$$

**Outer integral (over $x$):**

$$\int_{0.5}^{1} (0.5x + 0.375) \, dx = \left[ 0.25 x^2 + 0.375 x \right]_{0.5}^{1}$$

$$= (0.25 + 0.375) - (0.25 \cdot 0.25 + 0.375 \cdot 0.5)$$

$$= 0.625 - (0.0625 + 0.1875) = 0.625 - 0.25 = 0.375$$

$$\therefore P(X > 0.5, Y > 0.5) = 0.375 = \frac{3}{8}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: How the Joint PDF Connects to All Derived Quantities

The matrix below summarizes the **causal relationships** you must know for any KTU problem on this topic.

| Starting Object | Operation | Resulting Object | Typical KTU Question |
|---|---|---|---|
| Joint PDF $f(x,y)$ | $\int dy$ | Marginal $f_X(x)$ | "Find marginal of $X$" |
| Joint PDF $f(x,y)$ | $\int dx$ | Marginal $f_Y(y)$ | "Find marginal of $Y$" |
| Joint PDF $f(x,y)$ | $\int dx \int dy$ over region | Probability $P(\text{event})$ | "Find $P(X+Y<1)$" |
| Joint $+$ Marginals | Comparison | Independence verdict | "Are $X,Y$ independent?" |
| Joint $\div$ Marginal | Ratio | Conditional PDF | "Find $f_{X \mid Y}$" |
| Joint $g(X,Y) = XY$ | Double integral | $E[XY]$ | "Find covariance" |
| $E[XY] - E[X]E[Y]$ | Arithmetic | $\text{Cov}(X,Y)$ | "Find correlation coefficient" |

---

## 3.3 Bonus Derivation: $E[X]$, $E[Y]$, $\text{Cov}(X,Y)$, and $\rho$ for the Example

We have $f_X(x) = x + \frac{1}{2}$ and $f_Y(y) = y + \frac{1}{2}$ on $[0,1]$.

**Compute $E[X]$:**

$$E[X] = \int_0^1 x \left(x + \frac{1}{2}\right) dx = \int_0^1 \left(x^2 + \frac{x}{2}\right) dx = \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{4} = \frac{7}{12}$$

By symmetry (since $X$ and $Y$ have identical marginals):

$$E[Y] = \frac{7}{12}$$

**Compute $E[XY]$:**

$$E[XY] = \int_0^1 \int_0^1 xy(x+y) \, dx \, dy = \int_0^1 \int_0^1 (x^2 y + x y^2) \, dx \, dy$$

$$= \int_0^1 \left[ \frac{x^3 y}{3} + \frac{x^2 y^2}{2} \right]_0^1 dy = \int_0^1 \left(\frac{y}{3} + \frac{y^2}{2}\right) dy$$

$$= \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Compute Covariance:**

$$\text{Cov}(X,Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y] = \frac{1}{3} - \frac{7}{12} \cdot \frac{7}{12} = \frac{1}{3} - \frac{49}{144} = \frac{48 - 49}{144} = -\frac{1}{144}$$

**Compute Variances:**

$$\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2$$

$$E[X^2] = \int_0^1 x^2 \left(x + \frac{1}{2}\right) dx = \int_0^1 \left(x^3 + \frac{x^2}{2}\right) dx = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{6} = \frac{5}{12}$$

$$\text{Var}(X) = \frac{5}{12} - \left(\frac{7}{12}\right)^2 = \frac{5}{12} - \frac{49}{144} = \frac{60 - 49}{144} = \frac{11}{144}$$

By symmetry, $\text{Var}(Y) = \frac{11}{144}$.

**Compute Correlation Coefficient:**

$$\rho_{X,Y} = \frac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\sqrt{\text{Var}(X)\text{Var}(Y)}} = \frac{-\frac{1}{144}}{\sqrt{\frac{11}{144} \cdot \frac{11}{144}}} = \frac{-\frac{1}{144}}{\frac{11}{144}} = -\frac{1}{11}$$

$$\rho_{X,Y} = -\frac{1}{11} \approx -0.0909$$

This **negative but weak** correlation is consistent with the fact that $X$ and $Y$ are not independent but are only mildly linearly related.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Joint PDF Relationships

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **functional interactions** between the joint PDF, its marginals, conditionals, and derived statistical quantities. This is the "engine schematic" of the joint PDF concept.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["JOINT PDF f of x and y"] --> B["MARGINAL OF X f_X x"]
    A --> C["MARGINAL OF Y f_Y y"]
    A --> D["PROBABILITY P over region R"]

    B --> E["CONDITIONAL OF Y GIVEN X f_Y_given_X"]
    C --> F["CONDITIONAL OF X GIVEN Y f_X_given_Y"]

    A --> G["INDEPENDENCE TEST"]
    B --> G
    C --> G
    G --> H{"Is f equals f_X times f_Y ?"}
    H --> I["YES X and Y Independent"]
    H --> J["NO X and Y Dependent"]

    A --> K["MOMENTS E X E Y E XY"]
    K --> L["COVARIANCE Cov X Y"]
    K --> M["VARIANCE Var X Var Y"]
    L --> N["CORRELATION RHO X Y"]
    M --> N

    E --> O["MULTIPLICATION RULE"]
    F --> O
    O --> A
```

## 4.2 Schematic Description (Reading the Diagram)

1. **Central Node (A):** The Joint PDF $f(x,y)$ is the *source* of all probabilistic information.
2. **Top Branch (B, C):** Marginal PDFs are obtained by integrating the joint PDF over the "unwanted" variable.
3. **Right Branch (D):** Direct double integration of the joint PDF over a region $R$ gives probabilities.
4. **Middle Branch (E, F):** Conditional PDFs are derived by dividing the joint PDF by the appropriate marginal.
5. **Bottom-Left Branch (G, H, I, J):** The independence test is a comparison: does the joint equal the product of marginals? This determines whether $X$ and $Y$ are independent or dependent.
6. **Bottom-Right Branch (K, L, M, N):** Moments of $X$ and $Y$ (and the cross-moment $E[XY]$) are extracted from the joint PDF, leading to covariance and finally the correlation coefficient.
7. **Feedback Loop (O → A):** The multiplication rule (joint = conditional × marginal) confirms that the joint PDF can be reconstructed from conditionals and marginals — a self-consistency check.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU answer scripts:** Always draw a similar hierarchical diagram (even a hand-drawn one) before starting a 14-mark question. It earns method marks even if the final numerical answer is partially wrong.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**
**Define the joint probability density function of two continuous random variables. State its two fundamental properties.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **joint probability density function (joint PDF)** of two continuous random variables $X$ and $Y$ is a non-negative bivariate function $f(x,y)$ such that for any region $R$ in the $xy$-plane:

$$P((X,Y) \in R) = \iint\limits_R f(x,y) \, dx \, dy$$

**The two fundamental properties are:**

1. **Non-negativity:** $f(x,y) \geq 0$ for all $(x,y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$.
2. **Unit total probability:** $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x,y) \, dx \, dy = 1$.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for property 1, 1 mark for property 2)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**
**If the joint PDF of $X$ and $Y$ is $f(x,y) = 2$ for $0 < x < 1$, $0 < y < 1$, $x + y > 1$, and zero otherwise, find $P(X + Y < 1.5)$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The region of integration is the triangle bounded by $x \in (0,1)$, $y \in (0,1)$, and $x+y < 1.5$, intersected with $x + y > 1$.

$$P(X+Y < 1.5) = \int\int_{R} 2 \, dx \, dy$$

This is the area of the region multiplied by 2. The region $R$ (for $x + y > 1$ within the unit square and below the line $x+y = 1.5$) is a quadrilateral. The complementary area (where $x + y \geq 1.5$) is a triangle with legs $0.5$.

Area of complementary triangle $= \frac{1}{2}(0.5)(0.5) = 0.125$.
Area of $R$ $= 1 - 0.125 = 0.875$.

$$P(X+Y < 1.5) = 2 \times 0.875 = 1.75$$

> [!WARNING]
> Since probabilities cannot exceed 1, this answer indicates a miscalculation. The correct interpretation: the constraint $0 < x < 1$, $0 < y < 1$, $x + y > 1$ defines a triangular region of area 0.5. The condition $x + y < 1.5$ within this triangle is automatically satisfied for $(x,y) \in (0,1)^2$ except a small corner. Re-check region. **The actual answer is $P = 1 - 2 \times 0.125 = 0.75$** when properly integrated.

$$P(X+Y < 1.5) = 1 - 2(0.125) = 0.75$$

*(1 mark setup, 1 mark integration, 1 mark final answer)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part B questions carry 14 marks and offer an internal choice between two alternatives. The model pattern below mirrors the exact KTU paper layout.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
> The joint PDF of two random variables $X$ and $Y$ is given by:
> $$f(x,y) = k(x + y), \quad 0 \leq x \leq 1, \quad 0 \leq y \leq 1$$
> and $f(x,y) = 0$ otherwise.

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Find the value of the constant $k$, and hence obtain the marginal PDFs $f_X(x)$ and $f_Y(y)$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Find $k$ using the unit-probability condition.**

$$\int_0^1 \int_0^1 k(x+y) \, dx \, dy = 1$$

$$k \int_0^1 \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} + xy \right]_0^1 dy = k \int_0^1 \left(\frac{1}{2} + y\right) dy = k \left[ \frac{y}{2} + \frac{y^2}{2} \right]_0^1$$

$$= k \left(\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\right) = k(1) = 1 \implies \boxed{k = 1}$$

**Step 2: Find $f_X(x)$** (integrate $f(x,y)$ over $y \in [0,1]$):

$$f_X(x) = \int_0^1 (x+y) \, dy = \left[ xy + \frac{y^2}{2} \right]_0^1 = x + \frac{1}{2}, \quad 0 \leq x \leq 1$$

**Step 3: Find $f_Y(y)$** (integrate $f(x,y)$ over $x \in [0,1]$):

$$f_Y(y) = \int_0^1 (x+y) \, dx = \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} + xy \right]_0^1 = \frac{1}{2} + y, \quad 0 \leq y \leq 1$$

*(3 marks for finding $k$, 2 marks each for marginals)*

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Test whether $X$ and $Y$ are independent. If not, find the conditional PDF $f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y)$ and compute $P(X > 0.5 \mid Y = 0.5)$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Test independence.**

$$f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y) = \left(x + \frac{1}{2}\right)\left(y + \frac{1}{2}\right) = xy + \frac{x}{2} + \frac{y}{2} + \frac{1}{4}$$

Since $f(x,y) = x + y \neq xy + \frac{x}{2} + \frac{y}{2} + \frac{1}{4}$, the variables are **NOT independent**.

**Step 2: Find conditional PDF $f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y)$.**

$$f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y) = \frac{f(x,y)}{f_Y(y)} = \frac{x + y}{y + \frac{1}{2}}, \quad 0 \leq x \leq 1, \; 0 \leq y \leq 1$$

**Step 3: Compute $P(X > 0.5 \mid Y = 0.5)$.**

Substitute $y = 0.5$:

$$f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid 0.5) = \frac{x + 0.5}{0.5 + 0.5} = \frac{x + 0.5}{1} = x + 0.5, \quad 0 \leq x \leq 1$$

$$P(X > 0.5 \mid Y = 0.5) = \int_{0.5}^{1} (x + 0.5) \, dx = \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} + 0.5x \right]_{0.5}^{1}$$

$$= \left(\frac{1}{2} + 0.5\right) - \left(\frac{0.25}{2} + 0.25\right) = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625$$

$$\boxed{P(X > 0.5 \mid Y = 0.5) = 0.625 = \frac{5}{8}}$$

*(2 marks independence test, 2 marks conditional PDF, 3 marks probability calculation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
> The joint PDF of $X$ and $Y$ is:
> $$f(x,y) = \frac{3}{2}(x^2 + y^2), \quad 0 \leq x \leq 1, \quad 0 \leq y \leq 1$$
> and zero otherwise.

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Verify that $f(x,y)$ is a valid joint PDF, and find the marginal PDFs of $X$ and $Y$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Check non-negativity.** For $0 \leq x,y \leq 1$, $x^2 + y^2 \geq 0$, so $f(x,y) \geq 0$. ✓

**Step 2: Check unit total probability.**

$$\int_0^1 \int_0^1 \frac{3}{2}(x^2 + y^2) \, dx \, dy = \frac{3}{2} \int_0^1 \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} + xy^2 \right]_0^1 dy$$

$$= \frac{3}{2} \int_0^1 \left(\frac{1}{3} + y^2\right) dy = \frac{3}{2}\left[\frac{y}{3} + \frac{y^3}{3}\right]_0^1 = \frac{3}{2}\left(\frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{3} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3: Marginal of $X$.**

$$f_X(x) = \int_0^1 \frac{3}{2}(x^2 + y^2) \, dy = \frac{3}{2}\left[x^2 y + \frac{y^3}{3}\right]_0^1 = \frac{3}{2}\left(x^2 + \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{3x^2}{2} + \frac{1}{2}, \quad 0 \leq x \leq 1$$

**Step 4: Marginal of $Y$.**

$$f_Y(y) = \int_0^1 \frac{3}{2}(x^2 + y^2) \, dx = \frac{3}{2}\left(y^2 + \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{3y^2}{2} + \frac{1}{2}, \quad 0 \leq y \leq 1$$

*(2 marks verification, 2.5 marks each marginal)*

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Determine whether $X$ and $Y$ are independent. Also find $E[X]$, $E[XY]$, $\text{Cov}(X,Y)$, and the correlation coefficient $\rho_{X,Y}$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Test independence.**

$$f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y) = \left(\frac{3x^2}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\right)\left(\frac{3y^2}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\right) \neq \frac{3}{2}(x^2 + y^2)$$

Therefore, $X$ and $Y$ are **NOT independent**.

**Step 2: Find $E[X]$.**

$$E[X] = \int_0^1 x \cdot f_X(x) \, dx = \int_0^1 x\left(\frac{3x^2}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\right) dx = \int_0^1 \left(\frac{3x^3}{2} + \frac{x}{2}\right) dx$$

$$= \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{3}{8} + \frac{1}{4} = \frac{5}{8}$$

By symmetry, $E[Y] = \frac{5}{8}$.

**Step 3: Find $E[XY]$.**

$$E[XY] = \int_0^1 \int_0^1 xy \cdot \frac{3}{2}(x^2 + y^2) \, dx \, dy = \frac{3}{2}\int_0^1 \int_0^1 (x^3 y + xy^3) \, dx \, dy$$

$$= \frac{3}{2}\int_0^1 \left[\frac{x^4 y}{4} + \frac{x^2 y^3}{2}\right]_0^1 dy = \frac{3}{2}\int_0^1 \left(\frac{y}{4} + \frac{y^3}{2}\right) dy$$

$$= \frac{3}{2}\left(\frac{1}{8} + \frac{1}{8}\right) = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{4} = \frac{3}{8}$$

**Step 4: Find $\text{Cov}(X,Y)$.**

$$\text{Cov}(X,Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y] = \frac{3}{8} - \frac{5}{8} \cdot \frac{5}{8} = \frac{3}{8} - \frac{25}{64} = \frac{24 - 25}{64} = -\frac{1}{64}$$

**Step 5: Find $\text{Var}(X)$ and correlation.**

$$E[X^2] = \int_0^1 x^2\left(\frac{3x^2}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\right) dx = \int_0^1 \left(\frac{3x^4}{2} + \frac{x^2}{2}\right) dx = \frac{3}{10} + \frac{1}{6} = \frac{9 + 5}{30} = \frac{14}{30} = \frac{7}{15}$$

$$\text{Var}(X) = \frac{7}{15} - \left(\frac{5}{8}\right)^2 = \frac{7}{15} - \frac{25}{64} = \frac{448 - 375}{960} = \frac{73}{960}$$

By symmetry, $\text{Var}(Y) = \frac{73}{960}$.

$$\rho_{X,Y} = \frac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\sqrt{\text{Var}(X)\text{Var}(Y)}} = \frac{-\frac{1}{64}}{\frac{73}{960}} = -\frac{960}{64 \cdot 73} = -\frac{15}{73} \approx -0.2055$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{X,Y} = -\frac{15}{73}}$$

*(2 marks independence, 2 marks $E[X]$ and $E[XY]$, 3 marks covariance and correlation)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Joint PDF Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to find the constant $k$ first.** If the joint PDF contains an unknown constant, the very first step **must** be normalization. Skipping this makes all subsequent answers (marginals, conditionals, probabilities) numerically wrong — examiners deduct **2–3 marks** for this.
>
> 2. **Using wrong limits of integration.** The limits must match the support (region where $f > 0$). Writing $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}$ for a function supported on $[0,1]$ does not lose marks, but using the wrong *finite* limits does.
>
> 3. **Confusing joint with marginal probabilities.** A common trap: writing $P(X > 0.5)$ when the question asks for $P(X > 0.5, Y > 0.5)$. Always re-read the question carefully.
>
> 4. **Independence test performed wrongly.** The test $f(x,y) = f_X(x) f_Y(y)$ must hold for **all** $(x,y)$. Showing it holds at one point is insufficient; one counterexample is enough to prove dependence.
>
> 5. **Conditional PDF normalization not checked.** A good KTU student always verifies that $\int f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y) \, dx = 1$. Examiners award method marks for this verification.
>
> 6. **Mixing up E[XY] with E[X]·E[Y].** These are equal **only** when $X$ and $Y$ are independent. If the variables are dependent, $\text{Cov}(X,Y) \neq 0$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Joint PDF (Module 1)**

- [ ] **Definition:** Joint PDF $f(x,y)$ is a non-negative function whose double integral over any region gives the probability of $(X,Y)$ falling in that region.
- [ ] **Two Properties:** $f(x,y) \geq 0$ everywhere; $\iint f(x,y) \, dx \, dy = 1$ over the entire plane.
- [ ] **Normalization first:** Always find the constant $k$ before doing anything else.
- [ ] **Marginal of $X$:** Integrate the joint PDF over all $y$-values — collapses the $y$-dimension.
- [ ] **Marginal of $Y$:** Integrate the joint PDF over all $x$-values — collapses the $x$-dimension.
- [ ] **Independence Test:** $f(x,y) = f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y)$ must hold for **all** $(x,y)$.
- [ ] **Conditional PDF:** $f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y) = f(x,y) / f_Y(y)$, defined only when $f_Y(y) > 0$.
- [ ] **Multiplication Rule:** $f(x,y) = f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y) \cdot f_Y(y)$ — always true.
- [ ] **Covariance:** $\text{Cov}(X,Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y]$. Zero covariance does **not** imply independence for non-Gaussian variables.
- [ ] **Correlation:** $\rho_{X,Y} = \text{Cov}(X,Y) / (\sigma_X \sigma_Y)$ lies in $[-1, 1]$.
- [ ] **Always verify:** The conditional PDF must integrate to 1 over its support.
- [ ] **Limits matter:** Match integration limits to the support of the joint PDF.
- [ ] **Independence implies:** $E[XY] = E[X] E[Y]$, $\text{Cov}(X,Y) = 0$, marginal = conditional, but the converse is **not** always true.
- [ ] **Symmetry shortcut:** If $f(x,y) = f(y,x)$, then $f_X(x) = f_Y(x)$ and $E[X] = E[Y]$, $\text{Var}(X) = \text{Var}(Y)$ — a huge time-saver in exams.

---

> **End of Notes — Joint Probability Density Function | PEEBT416 - Biostatistics | Module 1**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Marginal and Conditional Distributions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT416 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Marginal and Conditional Distributions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Joint Distribution Recap (Prerequisite Foundation)

Before exploring marginal and conditional distributions, we must establish the **joint probability distribution** of two random variables. Let $(X, Y)$ be a **bivariate random variable**.

- **Discrete Case:** The joint probability mass function (PMF) is $f(x, y) = P(X = x, Y = y)$.
- **Continuous Case:** The joint probability density function (PDF) is $f(x, y)$, where $P[(X, Y) \in A] = \iint_{A} f(x, y) \, dx \, dy$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Biostatistics deals heavily with bivariate data (e.g., *Age vs. Blood Pressure*, *Dose vs. Recovery Time*, *Height vs. Weight*). The joint distribution captures how two variables *co-vary*, but often a researcher wants to study each variable in *isolation* (marginal) or *given a fixed value of the other* (conditional).

---

### 1.2 Marginal Distribution — The "Projection" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (Discrete Case):** If $(X, Y)$ is a discrete bivariate random variable with joint PMF $f(x, y)$, then the **marginal probability mass function of $X$** is obtained by summing the joint PMF over all possible values of $Y$:
> $$g(x) = \sum_{\text{all } y} f(x, y)$$
> Similarly, the **marginal PMF of $Y$** is:
> $$h(y) = \sum_{\text{all } x} f(x, y)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (Continuous Case):** If $(X, Y)$ is a continuous bivariate random variable with joint PDF $f(x, y)$, then the **marginal density of $X$** is obtained by integrating the joint PDF over the range of $Y$:
> $$g(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x, y) \, dy$$
> Similarly, the **marginal density of $Y$** is:
> $$h(y) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x, y) \, dx$$

**Marginal Distribution Validation Check:** A marginal must be a valid probability distribution. For discrete: $\sum_x g(x) = 1$. For continuous: $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(x) \, dx = 1$.

---

### 1.3 Conditional Distribution — The "Slice" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** The **conditional probability mass/density function of $X$ given $Y = y$** is defined as:
> $$f(x \mid y) = \frac{f(x, y)}{h(y)}, \quad \text{provided } h(y) > 0$$
> where $f(x,y)$ is the joint PMF/PDF and $h(y)$ is the marginal of $Y$. Analogously, $f(y \mid x) = \frac{f(x,y)}{g(x)}$.

This is the direct biostatistical extension of the elementary conditional probability formula $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$.

---

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Imagine a Hospital Outpatient Clinic:**

| Concept | Real-World Analogy |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Joint Distribution $f(x,y)$** | A 2D table counting patients by *(Age, Blood Group)* together. |
| **Marginal of Age $g(x)$** | Ignore the Blood Group column and just total up the Age row. You get the *overall age distribution* of all patients combined. |
| **Conditional $f(\text{Blood Group} \mid \text{Age} = 40)$** | Look **only** at the row where Age = 40, then renormalize (divide by the row total) to make it sum to 1. This gives the *blood group distribution restricted to 40-year-olds*. |
| **Independence** | Whether you know the patient's age tells you *nothing* extra about their blood group. The conditional equals the marginal. |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Intuition:** *Marginal* = collapse one dimension (projection). *Conditional* = fix one variable and renormalize (slice).

---

### 1.5 KTU 2024 Course Outcome Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This topic directly addresses:
> - **CO1:** *Understand the fundamental concepts of probability and statistical distributions applied to biological data.*
> - **CO2:** *Apply bivariate distribution techniques to model relationships between biomedical variables.*
> - **Bloom's Level:** L3 (Apply) and L4 (Analyze).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Flow — From Joint to Marginal to Conditional

The mathematical logic proceeds in **three structured steps**:

**Step 1 — Aggregation (Joint → Marginal):**
- The marginal distribution ignores one variable by *summing* (discrete) or *integrating* (continuous) over all its possible values.
- This is essentially *marginalizing out* the unwanted variable.
- The marginal represents the *unconditional* behavior of one variable.

**Step 2 — Normalization (Joint + Marginal → Conditional):**
- The conditional distribution is the joint distribution *renormalized* so that it sums/integrates to 1 along the remaining axis.
- The denominator $h(y)$ is the *normalizing constant* that ensures $\sum_x f(x \mid y) = 1$.
- This represents *updated belief* about $X$ after learning the value of $Y$.

**Step 3 — Independence Check:**
- If $f(x \mid y) = g(x)$ for all $x$ and $y$ (i.e., the conditional does not depend on $y$), then $X$ and $Y$ are **statistically independent**.
- Algebraically, $X \perp Y \iff f(x, y) = g(x) \cdot h(y)$ for all $x, y$.

---

### 2.2 The Multiplication Rule (Bridge Between Concepts)

The joint PMF/PDF can always be decomposed as:

$$f(x, y) = g(x) \cdot f(y \mid x) = h(y) \cdot f(x \mid y)$$

This is the **probability multiplication rule** extended to random variables. It is the *single most important identity* linking joint, marginal, and conditional distributions.

---

### 2.3 The Law of Total Probability (Random Variable Form)

For any partition $\{B_i\}$ of the sample space:

$$g(x) = \sum_{i} f(x \mid y_i) \cdot P(Y = y_i) \quad \text{(discrete)}$$

$$g(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x \mid y) \cdot h(y) \, dy \quad \text{(continuous)}$$

This is used to compute marginals from conditionals — a frequent KTU exam pattern.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Board Examination 2024 Scheme**

| # | Concept | Discrete Formula | Continuous Formula |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Joint PMF / PDF | $f(x,y) = P(X=x, Y=y)$ | $f(x,y) \geq 0$, $\iint f = 1$ |
| 2 | Marginal of $X$ | $g(x) = \sum_{y} f(x,y)$ | $g(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x,y) \, dy$ |
| 3 | Marginal of $Y$ | $h(y) = \sum_{x} f(x,y)$ | $h(y) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x,y) \, dx$ |
| 4 | Conditional $X \mid Y$ | $f(x \mid y) = \dfrac{f(x,y)}{h(y)}$ | $f(x \mid y) = \dfrac{f(x,y)}{h(y)}$ |
| 5 | Conditional $Y \mid X$ | $f(y \mid x) = \dfrac{f(x,y)}{g(x)}$ | $f(y \mid x) = \dfrac{f(x,y)}{g(x)}$ |
| 6 | Multiplication Rule | $f(x,y) = g(x) \cdot f(y \mid x)$ | $f(x,y) = g(x) \cdot f(y \mid x)$ |
| 7 | Independence Test | $f(x,y) = g(x) \cdot h(y)$ $\forall x,y$ | $f(x,y) = g(x) \cdot h(y)$ $\forall x,y$ |
| 8 | Total Probability | $g(x) = \sum_y f(x \mid y) h(y)$ | $g(x) = \int f(x \mid y) h(y) \, dy$ |
| 9 | Normalization Check | $\sum_x f(x \mid y) = 1$ | $\int f(x \mid y) \, dx = 1$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse $\sum$ with $\int$ in the marginal formulas. Always check whether the joint distribution is **discrete** (sum) or **continuous** (integral). The KTU question stem will explicitly say "discrete" or "continuous."

---

### 2.5 Real-World Biostatistical Utility

- **Marginal distributions** are used in **epidemiological surveys** to report the *overall prevalence* of a disease across the entire population (ignoring strata like age, gender).
- **Conditional distributions** power **diagnostic medicine** — e.g., $P(\text{Disease} \mid \text{Positive Test})$ is computed using conditionals via Bayes' theorem.
- **Independence testing** underpins the **Chi-square test of independence** in Module 2, where one tests whether two categorical biomedical variables (e.g., smoking status and lung disease) are independent.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Discrete Bivariate Distribution

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** The joint PMF of $(X, Y)$ is given by $f(x, y) = \dfrac{xy}{18}$ for $x = 1, 2, 3$ and $y = 1, 2$. Find the (a) marginal distributions of $X$ and $Y$, (b) conditional distribution of $X$ given $Y = 2$, and (c) determine if $X$ and $Y$ are independent.

**Step 1 — Verify the joint PMF sums to 1 (sanity check).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{x=1}^{3} \sum_{y=1}^{2} f(x,y) &= \sum_{x=1}^{3} \sum_{y=1}^{2} \frac{xy}{18} \\
&= \frac{1}{18} \left[ (1)(1) + (1)(2) + (2)(1) + (2)(2) + (3)(1) + (3)(2) \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{18} [1 + 2 + 2 + 4 + 3 + 6] = \frac{18}{18} = 1 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The joint PMF is valid.

---

**Step 2 — (a) Marginal PMF of $X$: $g(x) = \sum_{y=1}^{2} f(x, y)$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(1) &= f(1,1) + f(1,2) = \frac{1}{18} + \frac{2}{18} = \frac{3}{18} = \frac{1}{6} \\
g(2) &= f(2,1) + f(2,2) = \frac{2}{18} + \frac{4}{18} = \frac{6}{18} = \frac{1}{3} \\
g(3) &= f(3,1) + f(3,2) = \frac{3}{18} + \frac{6}{18} = \frac{9}{18} = \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

So, $g(1) = \tfrac{1}{6}, \quad g(2) = \tfrac{1}{3}, \quad g(3) = \tfrac{1}{2}$. Verification: $\tfrac{1}{6} + \tfrac{1}{3} + \tfrac{1}{2} = 1 \checkmark$

---

**Step 3 — (a) Marginal PMF of $Y$: $h(y) = \sum_{x=1}^{3} f(x, y)$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h(1) &= f(1,1) + f(2,1) + f(3,1) = \frac{1}{18} + \frac{2}{18} + \frac{3}{18} = \frac{6}{18} = \frac{1}{3} \\
h(2) &= f(1,2) + f(2,2) + f(3,2) = \frac{2}{18} + \frac{4}{18} + \frac{6}{18} = \frac{12}{18} = \frac{2}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

So, $h(1) = \tfrac{1}{3}, \quad h(2) = \tfrac{2}{3}$. Verification: $\tfrac{1}{3} + \tfrac{2}{3} = 1 \checkmark$

---

**Step 4 — (b) Conditional PMF of $X$ given $Y = 2$: $f(x \mid Y=2) = \dfrac{f(x, 2)}{h(2)}$**

We use $h(2) = \tfrac{2}{3}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(1 \mid 2) &= \frac{f(1, 2)}{h(2)} = \frac{2/18}{2/3} = \frac{2}{18} \times \frac{3}{2} = \frac{1}{6} \\
f(2 \mid 2) &= \frac{f(2, 2)}{h(2)} = \frac{4/18}{2/3} = \frac{4}{18} \times \frac{3}{2} = \frac{1}{3} \\
f(3 \mid 2) &= \frac{f(3, 2)}{h(2)} = \frac{6/18}{2/3} = \frac{6}{18} \times \frac{3}{2} = \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Verification: $\tfrac{1}{6} + \tfrac{1}{3} + \tfrac{1}{2} = 1 \checkmark$ (A valid conditional distribution.)

---

**Step 5 — (c) Independence Test**

Check $f(x, y) \stackrel{?}{=} g(x) \cdot h(y)$ for all $(x, y)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(1) \cdot h(1) &= \frac{1}{6} \times \frac{1}{3} = \frac{1}{18} = f(1, 1) \quad \checkmark \\
g(1) \cdot h(2) &= \frac{1}{6} \times \frac{2}{3} = \frac{2}{18} = f(1, 2) \quad \checkmark \\
g(3) \cdot h(2) &= \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} = \frac{6}{18} = f(3, 2) \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $f(x, y) = g(x) \cdot h(y)$ holds for **all** pairs, $X$ and $Y$ are **independent**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Continuous Bivariate Distribution

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** The joint PDF of $(X, Y)$ is $f(x, y) = \dfrac{1}{8}(x + y)$ for $0 \leq x \leq 2, \, 0 \leq y \leq 2$. Find the marginal density of $X$ and the conditional density of $Y$ given $X = 1$.

**Step 1 — Verify total probability is 1.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\iint f(x,y) \, dx \, dy &= \int_{0}^{2} \int_{0}^{2} \frac{1}{8}(x + y) \, dx \, dy \\
&= \frac{1}{8} \int_{0}^{2} \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} + xy \right]_{x=0}^{x=2} \, dy \\
&= \frac{1}{8} \int_{0}^{2} (2 + 2y) \, dy \\
&= \frac{1}{8} \left[ 2y + y^2 \right]_{0}^{2} = \frac{1}{8}(4 + 4) = 1 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 2 — Marginal density of $X$: $g(x) = \int_{0}^{2} \frac{1}{8}(x + y) \, dy$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(x) &= \frac{1}{8} \int_{0}^{2} (x + y) \, dy \\
&= \frac{1}{8} \left[ xy + \frac{y^2}{2} \right]_{y=0}^{y=2} \\
&= \frac{1}{8} \left( 2x + 2 \right) = \frac{x + 1}{4}, \quad 0 \leq x \leq 2
\end{aligned}
$$

Verification: $\int_0^2 \frac{x+1}{4} \, dx = \frac{1}{4} \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} + x \right]_0^2 = \frac{1}{4}(2 + 2) = 1 \checkmark$

---

**Step 3 — Conditional density of $Y$ given $X = 1$: $f(y \mid x=1) = \dfrac{f(1, y)}{g(1)}$**

First compute $g(1) = \frac{1 + 1}{4} = \frac{1}{2}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(y \mid x=1) &= \frac{f(1, y)}{g(1)} = \frac{\frac{1}{8}(1 + y)}{1/2} = \frac{1}{8}(1 + y) \times 2 = \frac{1 + y}{4}, \quad 0 \leq y \leq 2
\end{aligned}
$$

Verification: $\int_0^2 \frac{1+y}{4} \, dy = \frac{1}{4}\left[y + \frac{y^2}{2}\right]_0^2 = \frac{1}{4}(2 + 2) = 1 \checkmark$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Random Variable | Marginal $g(x)$ | Conditional $f(x \mid y)$ | Independence $f(x,y) \stackrel{?}{=} g(x) h(y)$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Discrete (Ex 3.1) | $g(1)=\tfrac{1}{6}, g(2)=\tfrac{1}{3}, g(3)=\tfrac{1}{2}$ | $f(1 \mid 2)=\tfrac{1}{6}, f(2 \mid 2)=\tfrac{1}{3}, f(3 \mid 2)=\tfrac{1}{2}$ | **Yes, independent** |
| Continuous (Ex 3.2) | $g(x) = \frac{x+1}{4}$ for $0 \leq x \leq 2$ | $f(y \mid x=1) = \frac{1+y}{4}$ for $0 \leq y \leq 2$ | $f(x,y) = \frac{x+y}{8} \neq g(x) h(y)$; **Not independent** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Architecture Flow — Joint, Marginal, Conditional

This block diagram maps how the three types of distributions interrelate within a bivariate random variable framework.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Bivariate Random Variable X and Y] --> B[Joint Distribution f of X and Y]
    B --> C[Sum or Integrate over Y axis]
    B --> D[Sum or Integrate over X axis]
    C --> E[Marginal of X g of x]
    D --> F[Marginal of Y h of y]
    B --> G[Fix Y equal to y0 and normalize]
    B --> H[Fix X equal to x0 and normalize]
    G --> I[Conditional of X given Y equals y0]
    H --> J[Conditional of Y given X equals x0]
    E --> K{Test f x y equals g x times h y}
    F --> K
    K --> L[Yes: X and Y are Independent]
    K --> M[No: X and Y are Dependent]
    I --> N[Used in Bayesian Inference]
    J --> N
    N --> O[Application: Diagnostic Testing in Biostatistics]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read:** Begin at the top with the joint distribution. Two pathways (left/right) lead to the two marginals by *summing* or *integrating* out the other variable. Two other pathways lead to the conditionals by *fixing* one variable and *renormalizing*. The marginals are then tested against the joint for the independence criterion.

---

### 4.2 Decision Tree — Step-by-Step Problem Solving Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Read the Problem] --> Q{Is the joint discrete or continuous}
```
