> **Problem:** In a city, **40%** of residents are reported to have a particular blood type. A researcher tests **200 residents** and finds **90** have that blood type. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the true proportion differs from 40%.

**Step 1: Hypotheses**

$$H_0: P = 0.40 \quad H_1: P \neq 0.40$$

**Step 2: Sample Proportion**

$$p = \frac{90}{200} = 0.45$$

**Step 3: Critical Value** — Two-tailed, $Z_{\text{crit}} = \pm 1.96$.

**Step 4: Compute Z**

$$Z = \frac{p - P_0}{\sqrt{\frac{P_0(1 - P_0)}{n}}} = \frac{0.45 - 0.40}{\sqrt{\frac{0.40 \times 0.60}{200}}}$$

$$Z = \frac{0.05}{\sqrt{\frac{0.24}{200}}} = \frac{0.05}{\sqrt{0.0012}} = \frac{0.05}{0.03464} = 1.443$$

**Step 5: Decision**

$$\vert Z_{\text{calc}} \vert = 1.443 < 1.96 = \vert Z_{\text{crit}} \vert$$

**Do not reject $H_0$**.

**Step 6: Conclusion**

At the 5% level, there is no significant evidence that the proportion of residents with that blood type differs from 40%.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture: Hypothesis Testing Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PROBLEM IDENTIFICATION\nPopulation Parameter of Interest"] --> B["STEP 1: FORMULATE HYPOTHESES\nH0 vs H1"]
    B --> C["STEP 2: SELECT TEST\nZ-test, t-test, or Proportion Test"]
    C --> D["STEP 3: FIX SIGNIFICANCE LEVEL\nAlpha = 0.05 or 0.01"]
    D --> E["STEP 4: DETERMINE CRITICAL VALUE\nFrom Z or t Distribution Table"]
    E --> F["STEP 5: COMPUTE TEST STATISTIC\nFrom Sample Data"]
    F --> G{"STEP 6: DECISION RULE\nIs |Calculated| > |Critical|?"}
    G -- YES --> H["REJECT H0\nResult is Statistically Significant"]
    G -- NO --> I["DO NOT REJECT H0\nInsufficient Evidence"]
    H --> J["CONCLUDE IN CONTEXT\nof Original Problem"]
    I --> J
```

### 4.2 Type I and Type II Error Relationship Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REALITY["REALITY (Unknown Truth)"]
        R1["H0 is TRUE"]
        R2["H0 is FALSE"]
    end
    subgraph DECISION["OUR DECISION"]
        D1["Reject H0"]
        D2["Fail to Reject H0"]
    end
    R1 -->|Reject H0| ERR1["TYPE I ERROR\nProbability = Alpha\nFALSE POSITIVE"]
    R1 -->|Accept H0| COR1["CORRECT\nProbability = 1 - Alpha"]
    R2 -->|Reject H0| COR2["CORRECT DECISION\nPOWER = 1 - Beta\nTRUE POSITIVE"]
    R2 -->|Accept H0| ERR2["TYPE II ERROR\nProbability = Beta\nFALSE NEGATIVE"]
```

### 4.3 Test Selection Logic Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["What are you testing?"] --> S1["Population Mean"]
    S --> S2["Population Proportion"]
    S1 --> Q1{"Is Sigma KNOWN?"}
    Q1 -- YES AND n greater than 30 --> T1["USE Z-TEST"]
    Q1 -- NO AND n less than 30 --> Q2{"Is population\nNORMAL?"}
    Q2 -- YES --> T2["USE t-TEST\nu = n minus 1"]
    Q2 -- NO --> T3["USE NON-PARAMETRIC\nor Sign Test"]
    S2 --> T4["USE Z-TEST FOR PROPORTION\nnP greater than 5 and nQ greater than 5"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### **PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the following terms with respect to hypothesis testing:**
**(a)** Null Hypothesis  
**(b)** Type I Error  
**(c)** Level of Significance

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Null Hypothesis ($H_0$):** It is a statistical hypothesis which states that there is **no significant difference** between a population parameter and a sample statistic, or between two population parameters. It is the hypothesis that is assumed to be true unless proven otherwise. The null hypothesis always contains the equality sign ($=$, $\leq$, or $\geq$).

**(b) Type I Error:** A Type I error occurs when a **true null hypothesis is rejected**. In other words, it is the error of concluding that a difference or effect exists when in reality it does not. The probability of committing a Type I error is denoted by the Greek letter **$\alpha$** (alpha), also called the level of significance.

**(c) Level of Significance ($\alpha$):** It is the **maximum probability of rejecting a true null hypothesis** (committing a Type I error) that a researcher is willing to accept. Common choices in KTU problems are $\alpha = 0.05$ (5%) and $\alpha = 0.01$ (1%).

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Distinguish between a one-tailed and a two-tailed test. When would you use each?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | One-Tailed Test | Two-Tailed Test |
|---|---|---|
| **Form of $H_1$** | $\mu > \mu_0$ or $\mu < \mu_0$ | $\mu \neq \mu_0$ |
| **Direction** | Pre-specified (right or left) | Both directions |
| **Critical Region** | Entirely in one tail ($\alpha$) | Split between two tails ($\alpha/2$ each) |
| **Critical Value (at 5%)** | $\pm 1.645$ | $\pm 1.96$ |
| **Used when** | Researcher has a directional hypothesis (e.g., "drug is *better*") | Researcher only checks for *any* difference |

A one-tailed test is used when the alternative hypothesis specifies a **directional** claim (greater than or less than), while a two-tailed test is used when the alternative simply states "different from," without specifying the direction.

---

### **PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice**

---

**Q3(A).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the procedure for testing a hypothesis with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A random sample of size 25 from a population has mean 52 and standard deviation 8. Test whether the sample comes from a population with mean 50 at 5% level of significance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **procedure for testing a hypothesis** consists of the following six steps:

1. **Formulate the Hypotheses:** State the null hypothesis $H_0$ and the alternative hypothesis $H_1$ about the population parameter.
2. **Select the Appropriate Test Statistic:** Choose Z-test, t-test, or chi-square test depending on the sample size, known/unknown standard deviation, and population distribution.
3. **Fix the Level of Significance ($\alpha$):** Set the acceptable probability of Type I error, typically 0.05.
4. **Determine the Critical Value:** From the relevant statistical table (Z-table, t-table) corresponding to $\alpha$ and the test type.
5. **Compute the Test Statistic:** Substitute the sample values into the chosen formula.
6. **Make the Decision:** If $\vert \text{calculated} \vert > \vert \text{critical} \vert$, reject $H_0$; otherwise, do not reject $H_0$.

**Example:** Suppose a manufacturer claims that the average weight of a product is 500g. A sample of 36 items gives a mean of 495g with $\sigma = 12$g. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$.

$H_0: \mu = 500$g, $H_1: \mu \neq 500$g. $Z = \frac{495 - 500}{12/\sqrt{36}} = \frac{-5}{2} = -2.5$. Critical value $= \pm 1.96$. Since $\vert -2.5 \vert > 1.96$, reject $H_0$.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Hypotheses**

$$H_0: \mu = 50 \quad H_1: \mu \neq 50$$

This is a **two-tailed test**.

**Step 2: Given Data**

$n = 25$, $\bar{X} = 52$, $s = 8$, $\mu_0 = 50$, $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Step 3: Test Selection**

Since $n = 25 < 30$ and the population standard deviation is **unknown** ($s$ given), we use the **t-test**.

**Step 4: Degrees of Freedom**

$$\nu = n - 1 = 25 - 1 = 24$$

**Step 5: Critical Value**

From the t-table, for $\nu = 24$ and a two-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.05$:

$$t_{\text{critical}} = \pm 2.064$$

**Step 6: Compute the Test Statistic**

$$t = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}} = \frac{52 - 50}{8 / \sqrt{25}} = \frac{2}{8 / 5} = \frac{2}{1.6} = 1.25$$

**Step 7: Decision**

$$\vert t_{\text{calc}} \vert = 1.25 < 2.064 = \vert t_{\text{crit}} \vert$$

The calculated value does not lie in the critical region $\Rightarrow$ **Do not reject $H_0$**.

**Step 8: Conclusion**

At the 5% level of significance, there is **insufficient evidence** to conclude that the sample mean (52) differs significantly from the population mean (50). The sample may reasonably have been drawn from a population with mean 50.

---

**Q3(B).** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Define Type I and Type II errors. Explain the concepts of level of significance and power of a test. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A random sample of 400 items from a large consignment shows that 80 are defective. Test at 5% level of significance whether the proportion of defective items in the consignment is 0.20. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

- **Type I Error:** Rejecting $H_0$ when it is true. Probability = $\alpha$.
- **Type II Error:** Failing to reject $H_0$ when it is false. Probability = $\beta$.
- **Level of Significance ($\alpha$):** The pre-fixed maximum probability of Type I error.
- **Power of the Test ($1 - \beta$):** The probability of correctly rejecting a false $H_0$. Higher power is desirable. Power depends on $\alpha$, sample size $n$, and the true parameter value.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Hypotheses**

$$H_0: P = 0.20 \quad H_1: P \neq 0.20$$

Two-tailed test, $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Step 2: Given Data**

$n = 400$, $x = 80$ (defective), $P_0 = 0.20$.

**Step 3: Sample Proportion**

$$p = \frac{80}{400} = 0.20$$

**Step 4: Critical Value**

For a two-tailed test: $Z_{\text{crit}} = \pm 1.96$.

**Step 5: Compute Z**

$$Z = \frac{p - P_0}{\sqrt{\frac{P_0(1 - P_0)}{n}}} = \frac{0.20 - 0.20}{\sqrt{\frac{0.20 \times 0.80}{400}}} = \frac{0}{\sqrt{0.0004}} = 0$$

**Step 6: Decision**

$$\vert Z_{\text{calc}} \vert = 0 < 1.96 \Rightarrow \text{Do not reject } H_0$$

**Step 7: Conclusion**

At the 5% level of significance, the data provides **no evidence** that the proportion of defective items differs from 0.20.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Never** write "Accept $H_0$" — the correct phrase is **"Do not reject $H_0$"** (KTU strict marking).
> - Always specify whether the test is **one-tailed** or **two-tailed** in Step 1.
> - State the **decision rule** ($\text{reject if } \vert T \vert > \vert T_{\text{crit}} \vert$) **before** computing the statistic.
> - For t-tests, **always state the degrees of freedom** explicitly.
> - Conclusion must be in **plain English context**, not just "reject $H_0$".

---

### **TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST**

- **Hypothesis:** A testable statement about a population parameter.
- **$H_0$** = Null Hypothesis (status quo, contains $=$); **$H_1$** = Alternative (research claim, contains $\neq$, $<$, or $>$).
- **Type I Error** ($\alpha$): Rejecting a true $H_0$ (False Positive).
- **Type II Error** ($\beta$): Failing to reject a false $H_0$ (False Negative).
- **Power** $= 1 - \beta$: Probability of correctly rejecting false $H_0$.
- **Six Steps**: Hypotheses → Test Choice → Significance Level → Critical Value → Compute Statistic → Decision.
- **Z-test** is used when $\sigma$ is known OR $n \geq 30$: $Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$.
- **t-test** is used when $\sigma$ is unknown AND $n < 30$: $t = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}}$, $\nu = n - 1$.
- **Z-test for proportion**: $Z = \frac{p - P_0}{\sqrt{P_0(1 - P_0)/n}}$.
- **Critical values at $\alpha = 0.05$**: Two-tailed $= 1.96$, One-tailed $= 1.645$.
- **Decision Rule**: Reject $H_0$ if $\vert T_{\text{calc}} \vert > \vert T_{\text{crit}} \vert$.
- **p-value Rule**: Reject $H_0$ if $p \leq \alpha$.
- **Always conclude** in the context of the original problem statement.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Population Mean
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT416 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Single Population Mean

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In **Biostatistics**, the study of a **Single Population Mean** refers to the statistical inference procedures applied to estimate, test hypotheses, and construct confidence intervals for the **true mean (μ)** of a single underlying population, using data drawn from a random sample.

A **population** is the entire group of individuals, items, or measurements about which we seek information (e.g., all diabetic patients in Kerala). A **sample** is a subset selected from the population. The **sample mean (x̄)** acts as the **point estimator** of the unknown **population mean (μ)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *Single Population Mean Inference* involves procedures for drawing conclusions about the unknown arithmetic mean of a single population, employing either the **Z-distribution** (when population standard deviation σ is known) or the **Student's t-distribution** (when σ is unknown and estimated from the sample).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a hospital wants to know the **average systolic blood pressure (SBP)** of all 50,000 patients visiting its OPD. Measuring every patient is impractical, expensive, and time-consuming. So, the doctor randomly selects **120 patients**, records their SBPs, and calculates the **average (x̄)** of these 120 readings.

The questions that follow are statistical:
- How close is this **sample mean (x̄ = 138 mmHg)** to the true **population mean (μ)**?
- Can we claim with 95% confidence that the true mean lies between, say, **135 mmHg and 141 mmHg**?
- If the textbook says normal SBP is 120 mmHg, is 138 mmHg **significantly higher**, or is it just random chance?

This entire reasoning process — estimating μ, building a confidence interval, and testing claims about μ — is the essence of **Single Population Mean** inference.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Plain English Meaning |
|------|--------|----------------------|
| Population Mean | μ | The true average of the entire population (usually unknown) |
| Sample Mean | x̄ | The average calculated from the observed sample |
| Population Std. Dev. | σ | The true spread of the population (often unknown) |
| Sample Std. Dev. | s | Spread estimated from the sample |
| Sample Size | n | Number of observations in the sample |
| Standard Error | SE = σ/√n | Variability of the sample mean |
| Level of Significance | α | Probability of rejecting H₀ when it is true (Type I error) |
| Degrees of Freedom | df = n − 1 | Number of independent values free to vary |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Board Exam:**
> - Use **Z-test** ⟹ σ is **known** OR n ≥ 30 (large sample, by CLT)
> - Use **t-test** ⟹ σ is **unknown** AND n < 30 (small sample, population must be approximately Normal)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) — The Backbone

The **Central Limit Theorem** states that for a population with finite mean μ and finite standard deviation σ, the sampling distribution of the sample mean x̄ approaches a **Normal distribution** as the sample size n becomes large (n ≥ 30), regardless of the population's original distribution.

**Implications:**
1. The mean of x̄ equals μ (unbiased estimator).
2. The standard deviation of x̄ (Standard Error) is σ/√n.
3. The standardized form Z = (x̄ − μ) / (σ/√n) follows a **standard normal distribution N(0, 1)**.

### 2.2 The Logic of Inference — Step-by-Step

1. **Formulate Hypotheses** (for testing):
   - Null Hypothesis H₀: μ = μ₀ (no significant difference)
   - Alternative H₁: μ ≠ μ₀ (two-tailed) or μ > μ₀ / μ < μ₀ (one-tailed)

2. **Choose Test Statistic**:
   - **Z-statistic** when σ is known
   - **t-statistic** when σ is unknown

3. **Compute the Test Statistic** from sample data.

4. **Determine the Critical Region** (or p-value) using α.

5. **Decision Rule**:
   - If test statistic falls in the rejection region → Reject H₀
   - Otherwise → Fail to reject H₀

6. **Conclusion** in the context of the problem.

### 2.3 Confidence Interval (CI) for Single Mean

A **(1 − α) × 100% Confidence Interval** for μ is a range of plausible values constructed from the sample such that, if the experiment were repeated many times, the interval would contain the true μ in (1 − α) × 100% of the cases.

**Intuition:** A 95% CI does NOT mean "there is a 95% probability that μ lies in this specific interval." Rather, it means we are 95% confident in our procedure of constructing such intervals.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Scenario | Test Statistic | (1−α)×100% Confidence Interval | Critical Value |
|---|----------|----------------|--------------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | σ known / Large sample (n ≥ 30) | $Z = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$ | $\bar{x} \pm Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $Z_{\alpha/2}$ from std. normal |
| 2 | σ unknown, small sample (n < 30), Normal pop. | $t = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}}$ | $\bar{x} \pm t_{\alpha/2,\, n-1} \cdot \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $t_{\alpha/2,\, n-1}$ from t-table |
| 3 | σ unknown, large sample (n ≥ 30) | $Z \approx \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}}$ | $\bar{x} \pm Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $Z_{\alpha/2}$ (s replaces σ) |
| 4 | Sample size estimation | — | $n = \left(\dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2$ | E = margin of error |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Critical Values (Memorize for KTU Exam):**
> - $Z_{0.05/2} = 1.96$ ⟹ 95% CI
> - $Z_{0.01/2} = 2.576$ ⟹ 99% CI
> - $Z_{0.10/2} = 1.645$ ⟹ 90% CI
> - For t, ALWAYS consult the t-table with df = n − 1.

### 2.5 Engineering & Biomedical Utility

- **Pharmaceutical Trials:** Testing whether a new drug reduces mean blood pressure compared to a placebo.
- **Quality Control:** Verifying whether the mean diameter of manufactured tablets meets specification.
- **Public Health:** Estimating mean cholesterol levels in a regional population.
- **Clinical Reference Ranges:** Establishing "normal" ranges for lab parameters (Hb, glucose, BP).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Confidence Interval for μ (σ known)

We start with the standard normal pivot:
$$Z = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \sim N(0, 1)$$

For a (1 − α) confidence level, we require:
$$P\left(-Z_{\alpha/2} \leq Z \leq Z_{\alpha/2}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

Substituting the expression for Z:
$$P\left(-Z_{\alpha/2} \leq \frac{\bar{x} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \leq Z_{\alpha/2}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

Multiplying throughout by (σ/√n) (a positive quantity):
$$P\left(-Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \leq \bar{x} - \mu \leq Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

Subtracting x̄ from all parts:
$$P\left(-\bar{x} - Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \leq -\mu \leq -\bar{x} + Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

Multiplying by −1 (reversing inequality signs):
$$P\left(\bar{x} - Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \leq \mu \leq \bar{x} + Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

Therefore, the **(1 − α) × 100% CI for μ** is:
$$\boxed{\bar{x} \pm Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}}$$

The term $E = Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ is called the **Margin of Error**.

### 3.2 Derivation of Sample Size Formula

We want the margin of error E not to exceed a specified value. Equating:
$$E = Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

Solving for n:
$$\sqrt{n} = \frac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}$$

Squaring both sides:
$$\boxed{n = \left(\frac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2}$$

Always round **up** to the next integer to ensure the desired precision.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for Choosing Z or t

| Condition on σ | Condition on n | Distribution to Use | Test Statistic |
|----------------|----------------|---------------------|----------------|
| σ Known | Any n | Normal (Z) | $Z = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$ |
| σ Unknown | n ≥ 30 (CLT) | Normal approx. (Z with s) | $Z \approx \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}}$ |
| σ Unknown | n < 30, Normal pop. | t (df = n−1) | $t = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}}$ |
| σ Unknown | n < 30, Non-Normal pop. | **Cannot use t** | Use non-parametric test |

### 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example — KTU Board Style

**Problem:** A researcher claims that the mean fasting blood glucose level of diabetic patients is 130 mg/dL. A random sample of 36 patients yields x̄ = 138 mg/dL with s = 12 mg/dL. Test at α = 0.05 whether the true mean differs from 130 mg/dL. Also construct a 95% CI.

**Step 1 — Hypotheses (Two-tailed):**
$$H_0: \mu = 130 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \mu \neq 130$$

**Step 2 — Test Selection:**
σ unknown, but n = 36 ≥ 30, so use **Z-test** (by CLT).

**Step 3 — Compute Test Statistic:**
$$Z = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}} = \frac{138 - 130}{12 / \sqrt{36}}$$

$$\sqrt{36} = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{12}{6} = 2$$

$$Z = \frac{8}{2} = 4.0$$

**Step 4 — Critical Value:**
For two-tailed α = 0.05, $Z_{0.025} = 1.96$.

**Step 5 — Decision Rule:**
Reject H₀ if $\vert Z \vert > 1.96$.
Since $\vert 4.0 \vert = 4.0 > 1.96$, **Reject H₀**.

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**
There is sufficient evidence at the 5% level to conclude that the mean fasting blood glucose level of diabetic patients is significantly different from 130 mg/dL.

**Step 7 — 95% Confidence Interval:**
$$\bar{x} \pm Z_{0.025} \cdot \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = 138 \pm 1.96 \cdot 2 = 138 \pm 3.92$$

$$CI = (134.08, \ 141.92) \text{ mg/dL}$$

Since 130 is **not** in this interval, this confirms the rejection of H₀.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Inference Workflow for Single Population Mean

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Research Question] --> B{Is population SD sigma known?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is sample size n greater or equal 30?}
    B -- No --> D{Is sample size n greater or equal 30?}
    C -- Yes --> E[Use Z test with sigma]
    C -- No --> F[Use Z test with sigma and small n]
    D -- Yes --> G[Use Z test with sample SD s by CLT]
    D -- No --> H[Is population approximately Normal?]
    H -- Yes --> I[Use t test df equals n minus 1]
    H -- No --> J[Use non parametric method]
    E --> K[Formulate H0 and H1]
    F --> K
    G --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Choose significance level alpha]
    L --> M[Compute test statistic value]
    M --> N[Determine critical value from Z or t table]
    N --> O{Does statistic fall in rejection region?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Reject H0]
    O -- No --> Q[Fail to reject H0]
    P --> R[Construct confidence interval for verification]
    Q --> R
    R --> S[Write biostatistical conclusion]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Estimation vs Testing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[INPUT DATA LAYER]
        P1[Population with unknown mean mu]
        P2[Random sample size n]
        P3[Sample observations x1 x2 ... xn]
    end

    subgraph ComputationLayer[STATISTICAL COMPUTATION LAYER]
        C1[Compute sample mean xbar]
        C2[Compute sample variance s squared]
        C3[Compute standard error SE]
    end

    subgraph DecisionLayer[INFERENCE DECISION LAYER]
        D1[Estimation Pathway: CI equals xbar plus or minus critical value times SE]
        D2[Testing Pathway: Statistic equals xbar minus mu0 over SE]
        D3[Compare with critical value or p value]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[OUTPUT LAYER]
        O1[Confidence Interval lower and upper bound]
        O2[Decision: Reject or Fail to reject H0]
        O3[Biomedical Interpretation]
    end

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> C1
    P3 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    D1 --> O1
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
    O2 --> O3
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "Standard Error of the Mean." How does it differ from the standard deviation of the population?*

**Model Answer:**
The **Standard Error of the Mean (SEM)** is the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the sample mean. It quantifies how much the sample mean x̄ is expected to vary from sample to sample due to random sampling.

$$SEM = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Difference:**

| Feature | Standard Deviation (σ) | Standard Error of Mean (SEM) |
|---------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| Measures variability of | Individual observations in the population | The sample mean x̄ across samples |
| Denominator | n (or n−1 for sample) | √n |
| Decreases with larger n | No, it is a property of the population | Yes, it shrinks as n increases |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
*State the Central Limit Theorem and mention its significance in estimating a single population mean.*

**Model Answer:**
**Statement:** The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) states that, regardless of the shape of the parent population, the sampling distribution of the sample mean x̄ approaches a **Normal distribution** with mean μ and standard deviation σ/√n as the sample size n becomes sufficiently large (commonly n ≥ 30).

**Significance:**
1. It justifies the use of the Z-test for large samples even when the original population is skewed.
2. It enables construction of confidence intervals for μ using the Normal approximation.
3. It bridges non-normal real-world data with tractable Normal-based inference.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** A random sample of 49 patients is drawn from a population with mean blood pressure μ and known standard deviation σ = 10 mmHg. The sample mean is computed as x̄ = 142 mmHg.
**(i)** Construct a **95% confidence interval** for the true mean blood pressure μ.
**(ii)** Test at the 5% level whether μ differs significantly from **140 mmHg**. [7 Marks]

**(b)** A pharmaceutical company wishes to estimate the mean recovery time of patients on a new drug. The standard deviation from past studies is σ = 4 days. Find the **minimum sample size** required to estimate the true mean recovery time within a margin of error of **±1 day**, at 95% confidence. [7 Marks]

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a)(i) — 95% CI:**
$$CI = \bar{x} \pm Z_{0.025} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = 142 \pm 1.96 \cdot \frac{10}{\sqrt{49}}$$

$$\sqrt{49} = 7 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{10}{7} = 1.4286$$

$$CI = 142 \pm 1.96 \times 1.4286 = 142 \pm 2.80$$

$$\boxed{CI = (139.20, \ 144.80) \text{ mmHg}}$$

**Interpretation:** We are 95% confident that the true mean blood pressure of the population lies between 139.20 and 144.80 mmHg.

---

**Part (a)(ii) — Hypothesis Test:**

**Hypotheses:**
$$H_0: \mu = 140 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \mu \neq 140 \quad \text{(Two-tailed, } \alpha = 0.05\text{)}$$

**Test Statistic:**
$$Z = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} = \frac{142 - 140}{10 / \sqrt{49}} = \frac{2}{1.4286} = 1.40$$

**Critical Value:** $Z_{0.025} = 1.96$

**Decision:** $\vert 1.40 \vert = 1.40 < 1.96$ → **Fail to reject H₀**.

**Conclusion:** There is insufficient evidence at the 5% level to conclude that the mean blood pressure differs from 140 mmHg. Note that 140 also lies inside the CI from part (i), which corroborates the decision.

---

**Part (b) — Sample Size:**

Given: σ = 4 days, E = 1 day, $Z_{0.025} = 1.96$.

$$n = \left(\frac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{1.96 \times 4}{1}\right)^2 = (7.84)^2 = 61.4656$$

Rounding up:
$$\boxed{n = 62 \text{ patients}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the procedure for testing the mean of a normally distributed population when the population standard deviation is **unknown** and sample size is **small (n < 30)**. Mention the test statistic and its sampling distribution. [7 Marks]

**(b)** The following data represent the cholesterol levels (mg/dL) of 9 randomly selected patients:
**180, 210, 195, 220, 205, 190, 230, 215, 200.**
Test at α = 0.05 whether the mean cholesterol level is **greater than 200 mg/dL**. Assume cholesterol is approximately normally distributed. [7 Marks]

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Small-Sample t-test Procedure:**

When σ is unknown and the sample size is small (n < 30), we estimate σ by the sample standard deviation s. The relevant test statistic is:

$$t = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}}, \quad df = n - 1$$

**Assumptions:**
1. The population is approximately Normal.
2. Samples are drawn randomly and independently.
3. σ is unknown and estimated by s.

**Sampling Distribution:** Under H₀, the statistic t follows **Student's t-distribution** with (n − 1) degrees of freedom. The t-distribution is bell-shaped, symmetric about 0, with heavier tails than the standard Normal — making it more conservative for small samples.

**Procedure:**
1. Set up H₀ and H₁.
2. Compute x̄ and s.
3. Calculate the t-statistic.
4. Compare $\vert t \vert$ with $t_{\alpha/2, n-1}$ (two-tailed) or $t_{\alpha, n-1}$ (one-tailed).
5. Reject H₀ if statistic falls in rejection region.

---

**Part (b) — One-Sample t-test:**

**Hypotheses:**
$$H_0: \mu = 200 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \mu > 200 \quad \text{(Right-tailed, } \alpha = 0.05\text{)}$$

**Step 1 — Sample Mean:**
$$\bar{x} = \frac{180 + 210 + 195 + 220 + 205 + 190 + 230 + 215 + 200}{9} = \frac{1845}{9} = 205.0$$

**Step 2 — Sample Standard Deviation:**
$$s^2 = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n - 1}$$

| $x_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ |
|-------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 180 | −25 | 625 |
| 210 | 5 | 25 |
| 195 | −10 | 100 |
| 220 | 15 | 225 |
| 205 | 0 | 0 |
| 190 | −15 | 225 |
| 230 | 25 | 625 |
| 215 | 10 | 100 |
| 200 | −5 | 25 |
| **Sum** | | **1950** |

$$s^2 = \frac{1950}{9 - 1} = \frac{1950}{8} = 243.75$$

$$s = \sqrt{243.75} = 15.612$$

**Step 3 — Test Statistic:**
$$t = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}} = \frac{205 - 200}{15.612 / \sqrt{9}} = \frac{5}{15.612 / 3} = \frac{5}{5.204} = 0.9608$$

**Step 4 — Critical Value:**
For right-tailed test with df = 8 at α = 0.05: $t_{0.05, 8} = 1.860$.

**Step 5 — Decision:**
Since $t = 0.9608 < 1.860$, **Fail to reject H₀**.

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**
At the 5% significance level, there is insufficient evidence to conclude that the mean cholesterol level of patients is greater than 200 mg/dL.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to round up** sample size calculations. Always use ceiling — fractional patients don't exist.
> 2. **Confusing σ with s**: If σ is given in the problem, NEVER replace it with s. If s is given, treat σ as unknown.
> 3. **Two-tailed vs One-tailed critical values**: For a right-tailed test at α = 0.05, use $t_{0.05}$, NOT $t_{0.025}$.
> 4. **df Calculation**: In t-tests, df = n − 1, not n. Using the wrong df will fetch 0 marks.
> 5. **Not stating the conclusion in the problem's context** — the final answer must interpret the result in biomedical/clinical terms, not just "reject H₀".
> 6. **Calculation errors in (xᵢ − x̄)²** — these are worth partial credit; show the table clearly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Point estimator** of μ is the sample mean x̄; it is **unbiased** (E(x̄) = μ).
- **Standard Error**: $\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ — decreases as n increases.
- **Z-test** applies when σ is known OR n ≥ 30.
- **t-test** applies when σ is unknown AND n < 30 with Normal population.
- **Critical values** (memorize): 1.96 (95%), 2.576 (99%), 1.645 (90%).
- **Confidence Interval**: $\bar{x} \pm \text{Critical Value} \times SE$ — width shrinks with larger n.
- **Sample size formula**: $n = \left(\dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2$ — always round **up**.
- **Hypothesis Testing Steps**: State H₀, H₁ → Choose test → Compute statistic → Compare with critical value → Conclude in context.
- **CLT** guarantees Normality of x̄ for large n, regardless of population shape.
- For **two-tailed test** at α, use $Z_{\alpha/2}$; for **one-tailed**, use $Z_{\alpha}$.
- **df for t-test** = n − 1 always.
- Always cross-verify CI and hypothesis test: if μ₀ is **outside** the CI, H₀ is rejected.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Difference Between Two Population Means
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT416 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Difference Between Two Population Means

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Biostatistics, the **Difference Between Two Population Means** refers to the formal statistical procedure of testing the hypothesis that the arithmetic means ($\mu_1$ and $\mu_2$) of two distinct populations differ significantly from each other, based on random samples drawn from these populations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> The inferential procedure of comparing two population means involves drawing two independent random samples (sizes $n_1$ and $n_2$) and testing the null hypothesis $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2$ (or $H_0: \mu_1 - \mu_2 = 0$) against an appropriate alternative hypothesis, using either the **Normal (Z) test** for large samples ($n \geq 30$) or the **Student's t-test** for small samples ($n < 30$).

The three standard scenarios encountered in KTU examinations are:

| Scenario | Sample Sizes | Population Variances | Appropriate Test |
|----------|--------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Case 1 | $n_1, n_2 \geq 30$ | Known or Assumed | **Z-test (Large Sample)** |
| Case 2 | $n_1, n_2 < 30$ | Assumed Equal ($\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2$) | **Pooled t-test** |
| Case 3 | $n_1, n_2 < 30$ | Assumed Unequal | **Welch's t-test** |
| Case 4 | Paired / Dependent | Any | **Paired t-test** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Recipes" Intuition

Imagine you are a **quality control scientist** at a pharmaceutical company in Kerala. You have developed two formulations of a new drug (Drug A and Drug B), and you want to know: *"Does the average blood-pressure reduction caused by Drug A differ significantly from that of Drug B?"*

- **Population 1** = The *theoretical* infinite set of all patients who could take Drug A.
- **Population 2** = The *theoretical* infinite set of all patients who could take Drug B.
- You cannot test every patient (the population is infinite), so you test **two small groups** (samples).
- The **difference of sample means** ($\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$) is your "best guess" of the true population difference ($\mu_1 - \mu_2$).
- But because you are only looking at a *sample*, your guess will naturally have a small error — this error is measured by the **Standard Error of the Difference**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Intuition:** A small difference between two sample means ($\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$) can either be a *real effect* OR just random sampling noise. The hypothesis test mathematically distinguishes between these two competing explanations.

## 1.3 Core Symbols and Notation

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $\mu_1, \mu_2$ | True population means (unknown constants) |
| $\bar{x}_1, \bar{x}_2$ | Sample means (computed from data) |
| $\sigma_1^2, \sigma_2^2$ | True population variances |
| $s_1^2, s_2^2$ | Sample variances (unbiased estimators) |
| $n_1, n_2$ | Sample sizes |
| $\alpha$ | Level of significance (commonly **0.05**) |
| $S_p^2$ | Pooled variance estimator |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Step by Step

The procedure for testing $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2$ follows a universal 5-step framework in KTU-style problems:

1. **State the Hypotheses:** Formulate the null hypothesis $H_0$ and the alternative hypothesis $H_1$ (two-tailed or one-tailed).
2. **Select the Test Statistic:** Decide whether Z-test, pooled t-test, or paired t-test applies.
3. **Compute the Sampling Distribution:** The difference $\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2$ is itself a random variable with mean $\mu_1 - \mu_2$ and a calculable standard deviation called the **Standard Error of the Difference (SED)**.
4. **Calculate the Test Value:** Standardize the observed difference $(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2)$ using the appropriate formula.
5. **Decision Rule:** Compare the test value with the critical value (from Z or t tables). Reject $H_0$ if the test value falls in the rejection region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this works (The "Why"):** The Central Limit Theorem guarantees that the difference $(\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2)$ is approximately normally distributed for large $n$, and exactly t-distributed for small $n$ when populations are normal. This allows us to use known probability tables to make decisions.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet** — Memorize this table. It contains every formula needed to solve KTU 2024 Scheme questions on this topic.

### Case 1: Large Sample Test (Z-test, $n_1, n_2 \geq 30$)

| Quantity | Formula |
|----------|---------|
| Test Statistic (Z) | $Z \;=\; \dfrac{(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) - (\mu_1 - \mu_2)}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}}$ |
| Standard Error (SED) | $SE(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}$ |
| Confidence Interval (95%) | $(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) \pm 1.96 \times SE$ |
| Confidence Interval (99%) | $(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) \pm 2.58 \times SE$ |

> When population variances are *unknown*, replace $\sigma_i^2$ with sample variances $s_i^2$.

### Case 2: Small Sample — Pooled t-test ($n_1, n_2 < 30$, $\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2$)

| Quantity | Formula |
|----------|---------|
| Pooled Variance | $S_p^2 \;=\; \dfrac{(n_1 - 1) s_1^2 + (n_2 - 1) s_2^2}{n_1 + n_2 - 2}$ |
| Test Statistic (t) | $t \;=\; \dfrac{(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) - (\mu_1 - \mu_2)}{S_p \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n_1} + \dfrac{1}{n_2}}}$ |
| Degrees of Freedom | $\nu \;=\; n_1 + n_2 - 2$ |

### Case 3: Small Sample — Welch's t-test (Unequal Variances)

| Quantity | Formula |
|----------|---------|
| Test Statistic (t) | $t \;=\; \dfrac{(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2)}{\sqrt{\dfrac{s_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{s_2^2}{n_2}}}$ |
| Degrees of Freedom (Welch–Satterthwaite) | $\nu \;=\; \dfrac{\left( \dfrac{s_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{s_2^2}{n_2} \right)^2}{\dfrac{(s_1^2/n_1)^2}{n_1 - 1} + \dfrac{(s_2^2/n_2)^2}{n_2 - 1}}$ |

### Case 4: Paired t-test (Dependent Samples)

| Quantity | Formula |
|----------|---------|
| Mean Difference | $\bar{d} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i}{n}$ where $d_i = x_{1i} - x_{2i}$ |
| Standard Deviation of Differences | $s_d \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum d_i^2 - n\bar{d}^{\,2}}{n - 1}}$ |
| Test Statistic (t) | $t \;=\; \dfrac{\bar{d}}{s_d / \sqrt{n}}$ |
| Degrees of Freedom | $\nu \;=\; n - 1$ |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering and Biomedical Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "Difference Between Two Population Means" test is one of the most applied procedures in:
> - **Pharmaceutical R&D:** Comparing mean efficacy of two drug formulations.
> - **Clinical Trials:** Comparing mean recovery times of a treatment group vs. a control group.
> - **Quality Engineering:** Comparing mean tensile strength of two production batches.
> - **Agricultural Biotechnology:** Comparing mean crop yield of two fertilizer treatments.
> - **Public Health:** Comparing mean BP / cholesterol levels of two demographic groups.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Error of the Difference

Let $\bar{X}_1$ and $\bar{X}_2$ be the sample means of two **independent** random samples of sizes $n_1$ and $n_2$ drawn from populations with means $\mu_1, \mu_2$ and variances $\sigma_1^2, \sigma_2^2$.

**Step 1: Mean of the difference.**

$$E(\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2) = E(\bar{X}_1) - E(\bar{X}_2) = \mu_1 - \mu_2$$

**Step 2: Variance of the difference (using independence).**

For independent random variables, $\text{Var}(A - B) = \text{Var}(A) + \text{Var}(B)$.

$$\text{Var}(\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2) = \text{Var}(\bar{X}_1) + \text{Var}(\bar{X}_2) = \frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}$$

**Step 3: Standard Error (square root of variance).**

$$SE(\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2) = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}$$

**Step 4: Standardized test statistic.** Since $(\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2)$ is normally distributed (by the Central Limit Theorem for large $n$):

$$Z = \frac{(\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2) - (\mu_1 - \mu_2)}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}} \sim N(0, 1)$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Pooled Variance Estimator

When $n_1, n_2 < 30$ and we **assume** $\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2 = \sigma^2$, the sample variances $s_1^2$ and $s_2^2$ are both unbiased estimators of the common $\sigma^2$. A *better* (lower-variance) estimator is their weighted average.

**Step 1: Each sample variance is unbiased.**

$$E(s_i^2) = \sigma^2 \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2$$

**Step 2: Construct the weighted average using weights $(n_i - 1)$.**

$$S_p^2 = \frac{(n_1 - 1) s_1^2 + (n_2 - 1) s_2^2}{n_1 + n_2 - 2}$$

**Step 3: Verify unbiasedness.**

$$E(S_p^2) = \frac{(n_1 - 1) \sigma^2 + (n_2 - 1) \sigma^2}{n_1 + n_2 - 2} = \frac{(n_1 + n_2 - 2)\sigma^2}{n_1 + n_2 - 2} = \sigma^2$$

Hence $S_p^2$ is an unbiased estimator of the common population variance $\sigma^2$.

**Step 4: Form the t-statistic.** Replacing $\sigma^2$ with $S_p^2$ gives a statistic that follows Student's t-distribution with $n_1 + n_2 - 2$ degrees of freedom.

$$\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) - (\mu_1 - \mu_2)}{\sqrt{S_p^2 \left( \dfrac{1}{n_1} + \dfrac{1}{n_2} \right)}} \\
&= \frac{(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) - (\mu_1 - \mu_2)}{S_p \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n_1} + \dfrac{1}{n_2}}}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Worked-Out KTU-Style Numerical Example (Large Sample Z-test)

> **Problem:** A researcher claims that the mean systolic blood pressure (SBP) of males in a city ($\mu_1$) differs from that of females ($\mu_2$). Two independent random samples yielded:
> - Males: $n_1 = 100$, $\bar{x}_1 = 128$ mmHg, $\sigma_1 = 15$ mmHg
> - Females: $n_2 = 80$, $\bar{x}_2 = 122$ mmHg, $\sigma_2 = 12$ mmHg
> - Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed).

**Step 1: Hypotheses.**

$$H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \mu_1 \neq \mu_2$$

**Step 2: Critical value (two-tailed, $\alpha = 0.05$).**

$$Z_{0.025} = \pm 1.96$$

**Step 3: Compute the Standard Error.**

$$\begin{aligned}
SE &= \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{15^2}{100} + \frac{12^2}{80}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{225}{100} + \frac{144}{80}} \\
&= \sqrt{2.25 + 1.80} \\
&= \sqrt{4.05} \\
&= 2.0125
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute the test statistic.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) - 0}{SE} \\
&= \frac{128 - 122}{2.0125} \\
&= \frac{6}{2.0125} \\
&= 2.9814
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Decision.** Since $\vert Z_{\text{calc}} \vert = 2.98 > 1.96 = Z_{\text{crit}}$, we **reject $H_0$**.

**Step 6: Conclusion.** At the **5% level of significance**, there is sufficient evidence to conclude that the mean SBP of males differs significantly from that of females.

**Step 7: 95% Confidence Interval for $\mu_1 - \mu_2$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
CI &= (\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) \pm 1.96 \times SE \\
&= 6 \pm 1.96 \times 2.0125 \\
&= 6 \pm 3.9445 \\
&= (2.0555, \; 9.9445) \text{ mmHg}
\end{aligned}$$

Since the interval does *not* contain 0, this confirms our rejection of $H_0$.

## 3.4 Worked-Out Example (Small Sample Pooled t-test)

> **Problem:** Two groups of 8 patients each are given Drug A and Drug B. The reduction in BP (mmHg) is measured:
> - Drug A: $\bar{x}_1 = 14$, $s_1 = 2.5$
> - Drug B: $\bar{x}_2 = 11$, $s_2 = 2.0$
> - Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the two drugs differ in mean efficacy (assume equal variances).

**Step 1: Hypotheses.**

$$H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \mu_1 \neq \mu_2$$

**Step 2: Degrees of freedom.**

$$\nu = n_1 + n_2 - 2 = 8 + 8 - 2 = 14$$

**Step 3: Critical value (two-tailed, $\alpha = 0.05$, df = 14).**

$$t_{0.025, \, 14} = 2.145$$

**Step 4: Compute the pooled variance.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_p^2 &= \frac{(n_1 - 1) s_1^2 + (n_2 - 1) s_2^2}{n_1 + n_2 - 2} \\
&= \frac{(8 - 1)(2.5)^2 + (8 - 1)(2.0)^2}{14} \\
&= \frac{7 \times 6.25 + 7 \times 4.00}{14} \\
&= \frac{43.75 + 28.00}{14} \\
&= \frac{71.75}{14} \\
&= 5.125
\end{aligned}$$

So $S_p = \sqrt{5.125} = 2.2638$.

**Step 5: Compute the test statistic.**

$$\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{S_p \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n_1} + \dfrac{1}{n_2}}} \\
&= \frac{14 - 11}{2.2638 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{8} + \dfrac{1}{8}}} \\
&= \frac{3}{2.2638 \times \sqrt{0.25}} \\
&= \frac{3}{2.2638 \times 0.5} \\
&= \frac{3}{1.1319} \\
&= 2.6503
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Decision.** Since $\vert t_{\text{calc}} \vert = 2.65 > 2.145 = t_{\text{crit}}$, we **reject $H_0$**.

**Step 7: Conclusion.** The mean BP reduction produced by Drug A is significantly higher than that of Drug B at the 5% level.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Tree: Choosing the Correct Test

The following block diagram maps the analytical decision flow that a KTU student must follow when confronted with a "difference of two means" problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START: Two Independent Samples?]) --> checkA{Samples Paired or Dependent?}
    checkA -- YES --> pairedPath[Use PAIRED t-test]
    pairedPath --> pairedF[Compute mean of differences d-bar and s-d]
    pairedF --> pairedOut[Test t = d-bar / s-d over sqrt of n, df = n minus 1]
    checkA -- NO --> checkB{Both n greater than or equal 30?}
    checkB -- YES --> zpath[Use LARGE SAMPLE Z-test]
    zpath --> zknow{Population Variances Known?}
    zknow -- YES --> zform1[SE = sqrt sigma1-sq by n1 plus sigma2-sq by n2]
    zknow -- NO --> zform2[SE = sqrt s1-sq by n1 plus s2-sq by n2]
    zform1 --> zout[Z calc = x1-bar minus x2-bar over SE]
    zform2 --> zout
    checkB -- NO --> checkC{Variances Assumed Equal?}
    checkC -- YES --> tpooled[Use POOLED t-test]
    tpooled --> poolF[Sp-sq = n1 minus 1 times s1-sq plus n2 minus 1 times s2-sq, all divided by n1 plus n2 minus 2]
    poolF --> tpooledOut[t = x1-bar minus x2-bar over Sp times sqrt of 1 by n1 plus 1 by n2, df = n1 plus n2 minus 2]
    checkC -- NO --> twelch[Use WELCH t-test]
    twelch --> twelchOut[t = x1-bar minus x2-bar over sqrt of s1-sq by n1 plus s2-sq by n2, df by Welch-Satterthwaite]
    zout --> finalA([STEP 5: Compare with critical value and Conclude])
    pairedOut --> finalA
    tpooledOut --> finalA
    twelchOut --> finalA
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow: The Five-Step Hypothesis Test Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[STEP 1: State H0 and H1] --> B[STEP 2: Select Test Statistic]
    B --> C[STEP 3: Compute Standard Error]
    C --> D[STEP 4: Calculate Test Value]
    D --> E{STEP 5: Compare with Critical Value}
    E -- Reject H0 --> F[Conclusion: Significant Difference Exists]
    E -- Do not Reject H0 --> G[Conclusion: No Significant Difference]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram: Sources of Variation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pop1[Population 1 with mean mu1 and variance sigma1-sq] --> samp1[Sample 1 size n1]
    pop2[Population 2 with mean mu2 and variance sigma2-sq] --> samp2[Sample 2 size n2]
    samp1 --> diff[Observed Difference x1-bar minus x2-bar]
    samp2 --> diff
    diff --> se[Standard Error of Difference]
    se --> z[Standardized Z or t Statistic]
    z --> crit[Compare with Critical Value]
    crit --> out[Inference about mu1 minus mu2]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q:** Define the term *Standard Error of the Difference between two means*. State its formula for two independent large samples.

**Model Answer:**

The **Standard Error of the Difference (SED)** between two sample means is a measure of the variability (or sampling fluctuation) of the difference $(\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2)$ around its expected value $(\mu_1 - \mu_2)$. It quantifies the precision of the difference as an estimator.

For two independent large samples, the formula is:

$$SE(\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2) = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}$$

A *small* SED indicates that the difference $(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2)$ is a precise estimate of the true population difference, whereas a *large* SED implies that the difference is subject to considerable sampling variation.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Distinguish between the **pooled t-test** and the **paired t-test**. When is each applied?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Pooled t-test | Paired t-test |
|---------|---------------|---------------|
| Sample Type | Two **independent** samples | Two **dependent / matched** samples |
| Data Structure | Separate observations in two groups | Each subject measured twice (before–after) |
| Null Hypothesis | $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2$ | $H_0: \mu_d = 0$ (mean difference is zero) |
| Test Statistic | Uses $S_p$ (pooled standard deviation) | Uses $s_d$ (SD of differences) |
| Degrees of Freedom | $n_1 + n_2 - 2$ | $n - 1$ |
| Example | Comparing BP of male vs. female groups | Comparing BP of the *same* patients before and after a drug |

**Application Rule:**
- Use the **pooled t-test** when the two samples are drawn from *different* individuals (e.g., treatment vs. control groups).
- Use the **paired t-test** when measurements are taken on the *same* individual under two conditions, or on naturally matched pairs (twins, before–after studies).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q:** Two independent random samples drawn from two normal populations yielded the following data:

| Sample | Size | Mean | Standard Deviation |
|--------|------|------|---------------------|
| I | $n_1 = 10$ | $\bar{x}_1 = 62$ | $s_1 = 8$ |
| II | $n_2 = 12$ | $\bar{x}_2 = 55$ | $s_2 = 6$ |

Test at the **5% level of significance** whether the two population means are equal, assuming that the population variances are equal.

#### Part (a) (7 Marks): Formulate hypotheses, compute the pooled variance and standard error.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the hypotheses.**

$$H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \mu_1 \neq \mu_2 \quad \text{(two-tailed)}$$

**Step 2 — Significance level and degrees of freedom.**

$$\alpha = 0.05, \quad \nu = n_1 + n_2 - 2 = 10 + 12 - 2 = 20$$

**Step 3 — Critical value (from t-table, two-tailed, df = 20).**

$$t_{0.025, \, 20} = 2.086$$

**Step 4 — Compute the pooled variance.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_p^2 &= \frac{(n_1 - 1) s_1^2 + (n_2 - 1) s_2^2}{n_1 + n_2 - 2} \\
&= \frac{(10 - 1)(8)^2 + (12 - 1)(6)^2}{20} \\
&= \frac{9 \times 64 + 11 \times 36}{20} \\
&= \frac{576 + 396}{20} \\
&= \frac{972}{20} \\
&= 48.6
\end{aligned}$$

So $S_p = \sqrt{48.6} = 6.9712$.

**Step 5 — Compute the Standard Error of the Difference.**

$$\begin{aligned}
SE &= S_p \sqrt{\frac{1}{n_1} + \frac{1}{n_2}} \\
&= 6.9712 \times \sqrt{\frac{1}{10} + \frac{1}{12}} \\
&= 6.9712 \times \sqrt{0.1000 + 0.0833} \\
&= 6.9712 \times \sqrt{0.1833} \\
&= 6.9712 \times 0.4281 \\
&= 2.9843
\end{aligned}$$

#### Part (b) (7 Marks): Compute the test statistic, take the decision, and state the conclusion.

**Model Solution (continued):**

**Step 6 — Compute the t-statistic.**

$$\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{SE} \\
&= \frac{62 - 55}{2.9843} \\
&= \frac{7}{2.9843} \\
&= 2.3458
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Decision rule.**

Since $\vert t_{\text{calc}} \vert = 2.3458 > 2.086 = t_{\text{crit}}$, the calculated value falls in the **rejection region**.

**Step 8 — Conclusion.**

We **reject $H_0$** at the 5% level of significance. There is sufficient statistical evidence to conclude that the mean of Population I is **significantly different** from the mean of Population II.

**Step 9 — 95% Confidence Interval (bonus, for full credit).**

$$\begin{aligned}
CI &= (\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) \pm t_{0.025,20} \times SE \\
&= 7 \pm 2.086 \times 2.9843 \\
&= 7 \pm 6.2253 \\
&= (0.7747, \; 13.2253)
\end{aligned}$$

Since the interval does *not* contain 0, this confirms the rejection of $H_0$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q:** The following data represent the blood sugar levels (mg/dL) of 8 patients **before and after** administering a new drug. Test at the 5% level whether the drug has a significant effect on blood sugar.

| Patient | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Before ($x_1$) | 180 | 165 | 190 | 175 | 200 | 170 | 185 | 160 |
| After ($x_2$) | 170 | 160 | 175 | 165 | 185 | 160 | 170 | 150 |

#### Part (a) (7 Marks): Compute the differences and the test statistic.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the hypotheses.** Let $d_i = x_{1i} - x_{2i}$ (Before − After). A positive $d$ means sugar *decreased* after the drug.

$$H_0: \mu_d = 0 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \mu_d \neq 0 \quad \text{(two-tailed)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the differences $d_i$ and their squares.**

| Patient | Before | After | $d_i$ | $d_i^2$ |
|---------|--------|-------|--------|----------|
| 1 | 180 | 170 | 10 | 100 |
| 2 | 165 | 160 | 5 | 25 |
| 3 | 190 | 175 | 15 | 225 |
| 4 | 175 | 165 | 10 | 100 |
| 5 | 200 | 185 | 15 | 225 |
| 6 | 170 | 160 | 10 | 100 |
| 7 | 185 | 170 | 15 | 225 |
| 8 | 160 | 150 | 10 | 100 |
| **Sum** | | | $\sum d_i = 90$ | $\sum d_i^2 = 1100$ |

**Step 3 — Compute the mean of differences.**

$$\bar{d} = \frac{\sum d_i}{n} = \frac{90}{8} = 11.25$$

**Step 4 — Compute the standard deviation of differences.**

Using the computational formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
s_d &= \sqrt{\frac{\sum d_i^2 - n\bar{d}^{\,2}}{n - 1}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{1100 - 8 \times (11.25)^2}{8 - 1}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{1100 - 8 \times 126.5625}{7}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{1100 - 1012.5}{7}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{87.5}{7}} \\
&= \sqrt{12.5} \\
&= 3.5355
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the test statistic.**

$$\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{\bar{d}}{s_d / \sqrt{n}} \\
&= \frac{11.25}{3.5355 / \sqrt{8}} \\
&= \frac{11.25}{3.5355 / 2.8284} \\
&= \frac{11.25}{1.2500} \\
&= 9.000
\end{aligned}$$

#### Part (b) (7 Marks): Take the decision and state the conclusion.

**Model Solution (continued):**

**Step 6 — Degrees of freedom and critical value.**

$$\nu = n - 1 = 8 - 1 = 7, \quad t_{0.025, \, 7} = 2.365$$

**Step 7 — Decision rule.**

Since $\vert t_{\text{calc}} \vert = 9.00 \gg 2.365 = t_{\text{crit}}$, the calculated value falls deep within the rejection region.

**Step 8 — Conclusion.**

We **reject $H_0$** at the 5% level of significance. There is very strong statistical evidence that the drug **significantly reduces** blood sugar levels in patients.

**Step 9 — Practical interpretation.**

The mean reduction of **11.25 mg/dL** is highly significant; clinically, the drug is effective.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the square root in the SE formula** — a single missing radical costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Using the wrong degrees of freedom** — for pooled t-test it is $n_1 + n_2 - 2$, NOT $n_1 + n_2$.
> 3. **Choosing the wrong test** — using the independent t-test on paired data inflates the SE and leads to a wrong "do not reject" decision. Always check if the problem says "same patients" or "before–after".
> 4. **Not stating the hypotheses explicitly** — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for missing $H_0$ / $H_1$ statements.
> 5. **Comparing $\vert t_{\text{calc}} \vert$ with $t_{\text{crit}}$ but not stating the decision in words** — always end with a *conclusion in plain English*.
> 6. **Misreading the t-table** — for df = 14, two-tailed at $\alpha = 0.05$, the value is **2.145**, not 2.977 (which is the one-tailed value).
> 7. **Rounding too early** — carry at least 4 decimal places in intermediate steps to avoid compounding errors in the final answer.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1: Difference Between Two Population Means**

- The **null hypothesis** is always $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2$ (or $H_0: \mu_1 - \mu_2 = 0$); the **alternative** may be $\neq$, $>$, or $<$.
- The **Standard Error of the Difference** is the cornerstone: $SE = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2/n_1 + \sigma_2^2/n_2}$.
- **Large sample rule:** If $n_1 \geq 30$ AND $n_2 \geq 30$, use the **Z-test** with critical value **1.96** (at $\alpha = 0.05$).
- **Small sample + equal variances:** use **Pooled t-test**, $S_p^2 = [(n_1-1)s_1^2 + (n_2-1)s_2^2] / (n_1+n_2-2)$, df $= n_1 + n_2 - 2$.
- **Small sample + unequal variances:** use **Welch's t-test** with the Welch–Satterthwaite df formula.
- **Paired/dependent data:** ALWAYS use the **paired t-test** on the differences $d_i = x_{1i} - x_{2i}$, with df $= n - 1$.
- The **confidence interval** for $\mu_1 - \mu_2$ is constructed as $(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) \pm (\text{critical value}) \times SE$. If it contains 0, we *do not* reject $H_0$.
- A **p-value** less than $\alpha$ also leads to rejection of $H_0$ (modern KTU pattern).
- The **t-distribution** approaches the **Z-distribution** as $\nu \to \infty$; for $\nu \geq 30$, t and Z critical values are nearly identical.
- Always end your solution with a **clear, non-technical conclusion sentence** — this is what KTU examiners reward most in the final 1–2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hypothesis Testing for Categorical Data: Fisher Exact Test, Chi-Square Distribution, Goodness of Fit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT416 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hypothesis Testing for Categorical Data: Fisher Exact Test, Chi-Square Distribution, Goodness of Fit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Hypothesis Testing for Categorical Data** is a branch of inferential statistics that evaluates whether observed frequency counts in one or more categories conform to a hypothesized distribution, or whether two categorical variables are independent. Because the response variable is *categorical* (nominal or ordinal) rather than numerical, the test statistics are based on **frequency comparisons** rather than means and variances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Syllabus Triad (Module 2 Focus):**
> 1. **Chi-Square (χ²) Distribution** — the parent distribution governing the test statistic.
> 2. **Goodness-of-Fit Test** — tests whether sample data match a *theoretical* distribution (e.g., Mendelian 9:3:3:1, Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, Poisson arrivals).
> 3. **Fisher's Exact Test** — exact probability computation for **2×2 contingency tables**, especially when expected cell counts are small (typically < 5).

The general null hypothesis for categorical data takes the form:

$$H_0: \text{The observed categorical distribution matches the expected distribution (or the variables are independent)}.$$

$$H_1: \text{The observed categorical distribution does NOT match the expected distribution (or the variables are associated)}.$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Hospital Blood-Type Audit**
> Imagine a blood bank that *expects* 40% of donors to be O+, 30% A+, 20% B+, 10% AB+. A new month begins, and the technician tallies 500 donors. The observed counts are 175 O+, 160 A+, 110 B+, 55 AB+. Intuitively, the A+ count *looks* too high and AB+ *looks* too low. But how confident can we be that this is not just random variation?
>
> - The **Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test** asks: *"If the expected ratio were truly correct, how often would we see a deviation this large purely by chance?"*
> - The **Chi-Square Test of Independence** asks: *"Are two classifications (say, smoking status and lung disease) related, or are they independent?"*
> - **Fisher's Exact Test** is used when the sample is too small for χ² to be valid (e.g., a rare-disease trial with 8 patients total). Instead of approximating, it computes the **exact probability** of the observed 2×2 table under the null.

In essence, categorical data testing answers a single question: **"Is what we observed explainable by chance, or is there a real effect?"**

### 1.3 Key Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> - **Observed Frequency (Oᵢⱼ):** Count actually obtained in cell (i, j) of the contingency table.
> - **Expected Frequency (Eᵢⱼ):** Count we *would* expect if H₀ were true, computed from marginal totals.
> - **Contingency Table:** Cross-tabulation of two categorical variables.
> - **Degrees of Freedom (df):** Number of independent cells that can vary once margins are fixed.
> - **Yates' Correction:** A continuity correction for 2×2 tables to improve the χ² approximation.

Explicit metrics / constants used in this module:
- **Significance level (α):** Commonly **0.05** or **0.01**.
- **Small-sample threshold for χ² validity:** Expected frequency **≥ 5** in every cell.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Chi-Square (χ²) Distribution — Theoretical Foundation

The **χ² distribution with k degrees of freedom** is the distribution of the sum of the squares of k independent standard normal random variables. It is **asymmetric, non-negative**, and **right-skewed**, becoming more symmetric as df increases.

$$\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} Z_i^2 \quad \text{where} \quad Z_i \sim N(0,1)$$

**Key properties** of the χ² distribution with df = ν:
- **Mean:** E(χ²) = ν
- **Variance:** Var(χ²) = 2ν
- **Mode:** ν − 2 (for ν ≥ 2)
- **Support:** [0, ∞)
- **Asymptotic behavior:** As ν → ∞, χ² approaches the Normal distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why χ² for categorical data?**
> Each term (Oᵢ − Eᵢ)²/Eᵢ is itself approximately χ² with 1 df when the null is true. Summing them yields χ² with df equal to the number of independent cells.

### 2.2 Karl Pearson's Chi-Square Statistic

The unified chi-square statistic for all categorical tests:

$$\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{r}\sum_{j=1}^{c} \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$$

where **Oᵢⱼ** = observed frequency and **Eᵢⱼ** = expected frequency under H₀.

**Computational steps (How the test works):**
1. State H₀ and H₁.
2. Compute the expected frequency for each cell.
3. Calculate (O − E)² for each cell.
4. Divide by E.
5. Sum all cells to obtain χ²_stat.
6. Determine df and the critical value χ²_crit at level α.
7. **Decision rule:** Reject H₀ if χ²_stat > χ²_crit (right-tailed test).

### 2.3 Expected Frequency Formula (for r×c Tables)

$$E_{ij} = \frac{(\text{Row Total}_i)(\text{Column Total}_j)}{n}$$

For a one-way goodness-of-fit table with k categories and a hypothesized proportion pᵢ:

$$E_i = n \cdot p_i$$

### 2.4 Degrees of Freedom

- **Goodness-of-Fit test:** df = k − 1 (where k = number of categories).
- **Test of Independence (r×c table):** df = (r − 1)(c − 1).
- **2×2 Fisher's exact test:** df is *not* used; exact p-value is computed.

### 2.5 Fisher's Exact Test (2×2 Tables)

When expected cell counts are small, χ² is unreliable. Fisher derived the **exact hypergeometric probability** of obtaining the observed (or more extreme) table conditional on the marginal totals being fixed.

For the 2×2 layout:

|  | Column 1 | Column 2 | Row Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| Row 1 | a | b | a + b |
| Row 2 | c | d | c + d |
| **Col Total** | a + c | b + d | n |

The exact probability of this *specific* table under H₀:

$$P = \frac{\binom{a+b}{a}\binom{c+d}{c}}{\binom{n}{a+c}} = \frac{(a+b)!(c+d)!(a+c)!(b+d)!}{n! \cdot a! \cdot b! \cdot c! \cdot d!}$$

For a **two-tailed test**, sum probabilities of all tables whose probability is ≤ the observed table's probability.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Notation / Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| Chi-Square Statistic | $\chi^2 = \sum \dfrac{(O-E)^2}{E}$ | Sum over all cells |
| Expected Cell Frequency | $E_{ij} = \dfrac{(\text{Row}_i)(\text{Col}_j)}{n}$ | Independence test |
| Expected GoF Frequency | $E_i = n \cdot p_i$ | pᵢ from H₀ |
| Degrees of Freedom (GoF) | df = k − 1 | k = # categories |
| Degrees of Freedom (Independence) | df = (r − 1)(c − 1) | r rows, c columns |
| Fisher's Exact P (one-tail) | $P = \dfrac{(a+b)!(c+d)!(a+c)!(b+d)!}{n!\,a!\,b!\,c!\,d!}$ | Hypergeometric |
| Yates' Corrected χ² | $\chi^2_c = \sum \dfrac{(\vert O - E \vert - 0.5)^2}{E}$ | 2×2 table continuity |
| Mean of χ² distribution | E(χ²) = ν | ν = df |
| Variance of χ² distribution | Var(χ²) = 2ν | ν = df |
| Critical value lookup | Reject H₀ if χ²_stat > χ²_{α, ν} | Right-tailed |

### 2.7 Engineering & Real-World Utility

Categorical hypothesis testing underpins:
- **Clinical Trials:** Comparing treatment response rates (success/failure) between drug and placebo.
- **Genetics:** Testing Mendelian inheritance ratios (9:3:3:1, 1:2:1).
- **Quality Control:** Verifying that defect-type counts match a hypothesized distribution.
- **Epidemiology:** Testing association between exposure and disease in case-control studies.
- **Bioassay Validation:** Confirming assay results fit a Poisson or Normal distribution.
- **Marketing Analytics:** Testing independence between demographic segments and product preferences.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Goodness-of-Fit Statistic (Conceptual)

We wish to test whether a sample of n observations follows a hypothesized distribution with probabilities p₁, p₂, …, p_k.

Under H₀, the count in category i is a Binomial random variable, X_i ∼ B(n, p_i), with mean E(X_i) = n·p_i and variance Var(X_i) = n·p_i(1 − p_i). The standardized residual is:

$$Z_i = \frac{X_i - np_i}{\sqrt{np_i(1-p_i)}}$$

Squaring and summing:

$$\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{(X_i - np_i)^2}{np_i(1-p_i)}$$

When p_i is small, (1 − p_i) ≈ 1, simplifying to:

$$\chi^2 \approx \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$$

This is **Pearson's chi-square statistic** (1900). For large n, this converges in distribution to χ²_{k−1} (one df is lost because ∑p_i = 1 fixes one probability).

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Goodness-of-Fit (Mendelian Dihybrid Cross)

**Problem:** In a dihybrid cross of pea plants, Mendel predicted a phenotypic ratio of **9:3:3:1** (Round-Yellow : Round-Green : Wrinkled-Yellow : Wrinkled-Green). An experimenter observes **800** offspring with the following distribution: Round-Yellow = 452, Round-Green = 138, Wrinkled-Yellow = 138, Wrinkled-Green = 72. Test at α = 0.05 whether the data fit the 9:3:3:1 ratio.

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**
$$H_0: \text{Ratio is 9:3:3:1} \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \text{Ratio is not 9:3:3:1}$$

**Step 2 — Expected Frequencies (E_i = n·p_i):**

| Category | p_i | Expected E_i = 800·p_i |
|---|---|---|
| Round-Yellow | 9/16 = 0.5625 | 450 |
| Round-Green | 3/16 = 0.1875 | 150 |
| Wrinkled-Yellow | 3/16 = 0.1875 | 150 |
| Wrinkled-Green | 1/16 = 0.0625 | 50 |
| **Total** | 1.0000 | **800** |

**Step 3 — Compute (O − E)²/E for each cell:**

$$\text{Cell 1: } \frac{(452 - 450)^2}{450} = \frac{4}{450} = 0.0089$$

$$\text{Cell 2: } \frac{(138 - 150)^2}{150} = \frac{196}{150} = 1.3067$$

Wait — let me re-check: 138 − 150 = −12, (−12)² = 144, 144/150 = 0.96.

$$\text{Cell 2: } \frac{(138 - 150)^2}{150} = \frac{144}{150} = 0.9600$$

$$\text{Cell 3: } \frac{(138 - 150)^2}{150} = \frac{144}{150} = 0.9600$$

$$\text{Cell 4: } \frac{(72 - 50)^2}{50} = \frac{484}{50} = 9.6800$$

**Step 4 — Sum to obtain χ²_stat:**

$$\chi^2_{\text{stat}} = 0.0089 + 0.9600 + 0.9600 + 9.6800 = 11.6089$$

**Step 5 — Degrees of freedom and critical value:**

$$\text{df} = k - 1 = 4 - 1 = 3$$

From χ² table at α = 0.05, df = 3:

$$\chi^2_{0.05, 3} = 7.815$$

**Step 6 — Decision:**

$$\chi^2_{\text{stat}} = 11.6089 > 7.815 = \chi^2_{\text{crit}} \implies \text{Reject } H_0$$

**Conclusion:** At the 5% significance level, the data do **not** fit the 9:3:3:1 ratio. The deviation is primarily driven by the Wrinkled-Green category (contribution = 9.68, which alone exceeds the critical value).

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Test of Independence (2×2)

**Problem:** A case-control study investigates the link between smoking and lung disease. The 2×2 table is:

|  | Disease | No Disease | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| Smoker | 60 | 30 | 90 |
| Non-Smoker | 20 | 50 | 70 |
| **Total** | 80 | 80 | 160 |

Test at α = 0.05 whether smoking and lung disease are independent.

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**

$$H_0: \text{Smoking and disease are independent} \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \text{They are associated}$$

**Step 2 — Expected Frequencies (Eᵢⱼ = (Row Total × Col Total) / n):**

$$E_{11} = \frac{90 \times 80}{160} = 45 \quad E_{12} = \frac{90 \times 80}{160} = 45$$

$$E_{21} = \frac{70 \times 80}{160} = 35 \quad E_{22} = \frac{70 \times 80}{160} = 35$$

**Step 3 — Compute (O − E)²/E:**

$$\frac{(60-45)^2}{45} = \frac{225}{45} = 5.0000$$

$$\frac{(30-45)^2}{45} = \frac{225}{45} = 5.0000$$

$$\frac{(20-35)^2}{35} = \frac{225}{35} = 6.4286$$

$$\frac{(50-35)^2}{35} = \frac{225}{35} = 6.4286$$

**Step 4 — Sum:**

$$\chi^2_{\text{stat}} = 5.0000 + 5.0000 + 6.4286 + 6.4286 = 22.8571$$

**Step 5 — df and critical value:**

$$\text{df} = (2-1)(2-1) = 1 \quad \chi^2_{0.05, 1} = 3.841$$

**Step 6 — Decision:**

$$22.8571 \gg 3.841 \implies \text{Reject } H_0$$

**Conclusion:** Strong evidence that smoking and lung disease are **not independent** (they are associated).

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Fisher's Exact Test (Small-Sample 2×2)

**Problem:** A small pilot trial of a new drug vs. placebo on 18 patients yields:

|  | Drug | Placebo | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| Improved | 8 | 2 | 10 |
| Not Improved | 1 | 7 | 8 |
| **Total** | 9 | 9 | 18 |

Here, the expected count for the (Not Improved, Placebo) cell is (8×9)/18 = 4, but one cell has an observed count of 1. Use **Fisher's exact test** to assess whether the drug improves outcomes (one-sided p-value).

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**

$$H_0: \text{Drug and improvement are independent} \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \text{Drug increases improvement rate}$$

**Step 2 — Compute exact one-sided probability:**

$$P = \frac{(a+b)!\,(c+d)!\,(a+c)!\,(b+d)!}{n!\,a!\,b!\,c!\,d!}$$

$$P = \frac{(10)!\,(8)!\,(9)!\,(9)!}{(18)!\,(8)!\,(2)!\,(1)!\,(7)!}$$

$$P = \frac{3628800 \times 40320 \times 362880 \times 362880}{6402373705728000 \times 40320 \times 2 \times 1 \times 5040}$$

Cleaner approach using combination form:

$$P = \frac{\binom{10}{8}\binom{8}{1}}{\binom{18}{9}} = \frac{45 \times 8}{48620} = \frac{360}{48620} = 0.0074$$

**Step 3 — For one-sided p-value, sum probabilities of all tables at least as extreme:**

A more extreme table would be (9, 1; 0, 8):

$$P_{\text{extreme}} = \frac{\binom{10}{9}\binom{8}{0}}{\binom{18}{9}} = \frac{10 \times 1}{48620} = 0.000206$$

$$p_{\text{one-sided}} = 0.0074 + 0.000206 = 0.0076$$

**Step 4 — Decision at α = 0.05:**

$$p = 0.0076 < 0.05 \implies \text{Reject } H_0$$

**Conclusion:** Strong evidence that the new drug improves patient outcomes.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Test to Use?

| Scenario | Test | df | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| One categorical variable vs. known distribution, n large | GoF χ² | k − 1 | Eᵢ ≥ 5 |
| Two categorical variables, r×c table, n large | Independence χ² | (r−1)(c−1) | Eᵢⱼ ≥ 5 |
| 2×2 table, any cell Eᵢⱼ < 5 | Fisher's Exact | — | Compute exact p |
| 2×2 table, all Eᵢⱼ ≥ 5 but n < 40 | Yates' Corrected χ² | 1 | Conservative |
| Large n, many categories | Standard χ² | k − 1 or (r−1)(c−1) | Verify df ≥ 1 |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Chi-Square Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Categorical Variables] --> B[State H0 and H1]
    B --> C{Is sample 2x2 with small expected counts?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Use Fisher's Exact Test]
    C -- No --> E[Is data 1 categorical variable vs theoretical distribution?]
    E -- Yes --> F[Use Goodness-of-Fit Chi-Square]
    E -- No --> G[Use Test of Independence Chi-Square]
    F --> H[Compute Observed O_i and Expected E_i = n times p_i]
    G --> I[Compute Expected E_ij = RowTotal times ColTotal / n]
    H --> J[Calculate chi-square statistic = sum of O minus E squared / E]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Determine degrees of freedom]
    K --> L{df = k minus 1 OR r minus 1 times c minus 1}
    L --> M[Find chi-square critical value at alpha]
    M --> N{chi-square stat greater than chi-square critical?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Reject H0: Significant Association]
    N -- No --> P[Fail to Reject H0: No Evidence]
    D --> Q[Compute exact hypergeometric probability]
    Q --> R{p-value less than alpha?}
    R -- Yes --> O
    R -- No --> P
    O --> S[End: Report conclusion with p-value]
    P --> S
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Test Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Categorical Hypothesis Test] --> B{Number of Variables?}
    B -- One --> C{Hypothesized Proportions Given?}
    B -- Two --> D{Expected Frequencies all greater than or equal to 5?}
    C -- Yes --> E[Goodness-of-Fit Test]
    C -- No --> F[Homogeneity Test]
    D -- Yes --> G[Chi-Square Test of Independence]
    D -- No --> H{2x2 Table?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Fisher's Exact Test]
    H -- No --> J[Pool Adjacent Categories or Increase Sample]
    E --> K[chi-square with df equals k minus 1]
    F --> L[chi-square with df equals c minus 1]
    G --> M[chi-square with df equals r minus 1 times c minus 1]
    I --> N[Hypergeometric exact p-value]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Chi-Square Distribution Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Chi-Square Distribution Properties] --> B[Support: zero to infinity]
    A --> C[Mean: nu where nu is df]
    A --> D[Variance: 2 times nu]
    A --> E[Right-Skewed for small df]
    A --> F[Approaches Normal as df increases]
    A --> G[Critical region is RIGHT tail]
    A --> H[Used for categorical frequency comparisons]
    A --> I[Assumptions: Independence, Expected greater than or equal to 5, Random sample]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block — Worked-Example Summary Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Three Worked Examples] --> B[Example 1: Goodness-of-Fit]
    A --> C[Example 2: Test of Independence]
    A --> D[Example 3: Fisher's Exact Test]
    B --> B1[Mendel 9 colon 3 colon 3 colon 1 ratio with 800 plants]
    B --> B2[chi-square stat equals 11.61, df equals 3]
    B --> B3[Reject H0 since 11.61 greater than 7.815]
    C --> C1[Smoking versus Lung Disease 2x2]
    C --> C2[chi-square stat equals 22.86, df equals 1]
    C --> C3[Reject H0 since 22.86 greater than 3.841]
    D --> D1[Drug Trial with n equals 18, small cell]
    D --> D2[p-value equals 0.0076 one-sided]
    D --> D3[Reject H0 since 0.0076 less than 0.05]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*Define the chi-square goodness-of-fit test. State its assumptions and the formula for expected frequencies.*

**Model Answer:**

The chi-square goodness-of-fit test, formulated by **Karl Pearson (1900)**, assesses whether an observed frequency distribution of a single categorical variable significantly deviates from a hypothesized theoretical distribution. It is used when we wish to test if sample data conform to a known model (e.g., Mendelian ratios, Poisson process, equal proportions).

**Assumptions:**
1. Data must be categorical (nominal/ordinal).
2. Observations are independent.
3. Expected frequency in each category should be **≥ 5**.
4. Sample is a random draw.

**Formula for expected frequency:**

$$E_i = n \cdot p_i$$

where n = total sample size, p_i = hypothesized probability for category i.

**Test statistic:**

$$\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$$

with df = k − 1.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model)]**
*When is Fisher's exact test preferred over the chi-square test of independence? Write the formula for the exact probability of a 2×2 table.*

**Model Answer:**

Fisher's exact test is preferred over the chi-square test of independence in the following situations:

1. The contingency table is **2×2**.
2. One or more **expected cell frequencies are less than 5** (the chi-square approximation is unreliable).
3. The **sample size is small** (typically n < 40).
4. The marginal totals are treated as **fixed** (conditioning on them), which is appropriate for sampling schemes such as case-control studies.

The exact probability of a 2×2 table with cell counts a, b, c, d and total n is:

$$P = \frac{(a+b)!\,(c+d)!\,(a+c)!\,(b+d)!}{n!\,a!\,b!\,c!\,d!}$$

Equivalently, using combinations:

$$P = \frac{\binom{a+b}{a}\,\binom{c+d}{c}}{\binom{n}{a+c}}$$

For a one-sided p-value, sum P over all tables at least as extreme as observed; for a two-sided p-value, sum P over all tables whose probability is ≤ the observed probability.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Students **commonly lose marks** in the following ways:
> 1. **Forgetting to state H₀ and H₁ explicitly** (loses 1 mark).
> 2. **Incorrect df calculation** — confusing GoF (k−1) with independence (r−1)(c−1) (loses 1–2 marks).
> 3. **Not verifying the Eᵢ ≥ 5 assumption** before applying chi-square (loses 1 mark).
> 4. **Using Fisher's test for a 5×5 table** — it is *only* for 2×2 layouts (loses 2 marks).
> 5. **Concluding "accepted H₀"** instead of "failed to reject H₀" (KTU 2024 strictly follows the latter; loses 0.5 mark).
> 6. **Forgetting to write the conclusion in context** of the problem (loses 1 mark).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** Explain the chi-square test of independence with its hypotheses, formula, and degrees of freedom. Under what conditions is this test valid? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a clinical trial, the following data were obtained on the response to two treatments:

|  | Improved | Not Improved | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| Drug A | 40 | 20 | 60 |
| Drug B | 25 | 35 | 60 |
| **Total** | 65 | 55 | 120 |

Test at the 5% level of significance whether the response is independent of the treatment given. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

The chi-square test of independence, developed by **Karl Pearson**, is used to determine whether two categorical variables (X and Y) are associated or independent in a population. The data are presented in an r×c contingency table.

**Hypotheses:**

$$H_0: \text{The two variables are independent (no association)}$$

$$H_1: \text{The two variables are associated (not independent)}$$

**Formula:**

$$\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{r}\sum_{j=1}^{c} \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$$

where the expected frequency is:

$$E_{ij} = \frac{(\text{Row Total}_i)(\text{Column Total}_j)}{n}$$

**Degrees of freedom:**

$$df = (r - 1)(c - 1)$$

**Conditions for validity:**
1. Sample must be random.
2. Observations must be independent.
3. All expected frequencies should be **≥ 5**.
4. Data should be frequencies (counts), not percentages.
5. The total sample size n should be reasonably large (typically n ≥ 20 for a 2×2 table; for larger tables, n ≥ 50).

If Eᵢⱼ < 5 in any cell, either (a) increase sample size, (b) combine categories, or (c) use Fisher's exact test (only for 2×2).

**Decision rule:** Reject H₀ if χ²_stat > χ²_{α, (r−1)(c−1)}.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**
