| Within (Error) | 30.833 | 15 | 2.056 | — |
| Total | 179.611 | 17 | — | — |

$F_{0.05,\,2,\,15} = 3.68$. Since $36.20 > 3.68$, **reject $H_0$**.

**Step 4: Fisher's LSD** (3 marks)

$$LSD = t_{0.025,\,15} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot MSE}{n}} = 2.131 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2.056}{6}} = 2.131 \times \sqrt{0.6853}$$

$$= 2.131 \times 0.8279 = 1.764$$

| Pair | $\vert \bar{x}_i - \bar{x}_j \vert$ | Significant? |
|---|---|---|
| V1 vs V2 | $\vert 20.833 - 25.000 \vert = 4.167$ | ✓ Yes (4.167 > 1.764) |
| V1 vs V3 | $\vert 20.833 - 18.000 \vert = 2.833$ | ✓ Yes (2.833 > 1.764) |
| V2 vs V3 | $\vert 25.000 - 18.000 \vert = 7.000$ | ✓ Yes (7.000 > 1.764) |

**Conclusion**: All three varieties differ significantly from one another in mean yield.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout
>
> Students **commonly lose marks** in the following ways on ANOVA problems:
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Correction Factor (CF)** — always compute it first; many errors propagate from its omission.
> 2. **Confusing $\sum x_{ij}^2$ with $(\sum x_{ij})^2$** — these are completely different quantities.
> 3. **Mixing up $SSE = SST - SSA$ with $SSE = SSA - SST$** — always SST minus SSA.
> 4. **Wrong Degrees of Freedom**: $N-k$ for error, $k-1$ for treatment. NOT $n_i - 1$ for each.
> 5. **Using a t-table value in place of F-table value** — confirm you are reading the correct statistical table.
> 6. **Post-Hoc test without significant F** — never perform LSD/Tukey if the F-test is **NOT** significant; the F-test must reject $H_0$ first.
> 7. **Not stating hypotheses explicitly** — KTU awards 1 mark for clearly stating $H_0$ and $H_1$.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ ANOVA is used when comparing **≥ 3 group means**; for 2 groups, use the t-test.
- ✅ The **Fixed Effects Model** treats treatments as **fixed, non-random** levels.
- ✅ Model equation: $x_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}$, with $\sum \tau_i = 0$.
- ✅ **Core Identity**: $SST = SSA + SSE$ with df: $(N-1) = (k-1) + (N-k)$.
- ✅ **CF** (Correction Factor) $= G^2/N$; all SS computations rely on it.
- ✅ **F-statistic** $= MSA / MSE$ — large values indicate treatment effects.
- ✅ **Decision Rule**: Reject $H_0$ if $F_{computed} > F_{\alpha,\,k-1,\,N-k}$.
- ✅ **Post-Hoc tests** (LSD, Tukey, Bonferroni, Scheffé) are performed **only after** a significant F-test.
- ✅ **LSD** formula (equal $n$): $LSD = t_{\alpha/2,\,N-k} \sqrt{2MSE/n}$.
- ✅ **Tukey's HSD** formula: $HSD = q_{\alpha,\,k,\,N-k} \sqrt{MSE/n}$.
- ✅ **Bonferroni** uses adjusted significance level $\alpha/m$ for $m$ planned comparisons.
- ✅ Verify **Normality** (Shapiro-Wilk) and **Homoscedasticity** (Levene's Test) before applying ANOVA.
- ✅ If variances are unequal, use **Welch's ANOVA** as a robust alternative.
- ✅ In the **ANOVA Table**, always list Source, SS, df, MS, F-ratio in that order.
- ✅ The **MSE is an unbiased estimator of $\sigma^2$** — this is the theoretical backbone of the F-test.
- ✅ KTU weightage tip: **Hypothesis setup (1 mark) + ANOVA table (3–4 marks) + Conclusion (1 mark) + Post-Hoc (3–4 marks) = 14 marks** typical breakdown.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fitting of Regression Line (Least Squares Method)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT416 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fitting of Regression Line (Least Squares Method)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Regression Analysis** is a statistical methodology used to investigate and model the relationship between a dependent (response) variable and one or more independent (predictor) variables. The **Fitting of a Regression Line** refers to the mathematical procedure of determining the equation of a straight line that best describes how the response variable $Y$ changes as the predictor variable $X$ varies.

The **Least Squares Method (LSM)**, formalized independently by **Carl Friedrich Gauss** and **Adrien-Marie Legendre** around **1805–1809**, is the standard deterministic criterion for fitting this line. It selects the line $Y = a + bX$ such that the sum of the **squared vertical deviations** (residuals) between the observed values $y_i$ and the predicted values $\hat{y}_i$ is **minimized**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim standard):**
> *Least Squares Method is a procedure for finding the best-fitting curve to a given set of data points by minimizing the sum of the squares of the offsets (residuals) of the points from the curve.*

The fitted straight-line regression model takes the canonical form:

$$Y = a + bX + \varepsilon$$

where:
- $a$ = **intercept** (value of $Y$ when $X = 0$)
- $b$ = **slope** (average change in $Y$ for a unit change in $X$)
- $\varepsilon$ = **random error term** with assumed properties $E(\varepsilon)=0$ and $\text{Var}(\varepsilon)=\sigma^2$

In a **fitted (estimated)** model, the Greek letters are replaced by their sample estimates, yielding the predicted equation:

$$\hat{y} = a + bx$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Tightrope Through a Cloud of Fireflies" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are standing in a dark field at night with a swarm of fireflies scattered in the air. Each firefly represents one **observation $(x_i, y_i)$** — for instance, a patient's *age* ($x$) plotted against their *blood pressure* ($y$). You are asked to stretch a glowing tightrope (the regression line) horizontally through the swarm. Because the fireflies don't form a perfectly straight line, you cannot pass the rope *through* every single point. Your natural choice would be to balance the rope so that **the total "wobble"** — the sum of squared vertical distances from each firefly to the rope — is as small as possible. That balancing act, performed mathematically, is exactly the **Least Squares Method**.

In simpler terms:
- We do **not** try to make every prediction perfect (impossible with noisy biostatistical data).
- We do **not** minimize absolute distances (which has no closed-form derivative).
- We **DO minimize squared distances** — this has a beautiful closed-form solution, penalizes large errors more heavily, and treats positive and negative deviations symmetrically.

## 1.3 Why "Squares"? The Three Engineering Reasons

> [!TIP]
> 1. **Mathematical Tractability:** Squared terms have smooth, differentiable gradients, allowing closed-form solutions via normal equations.
> 2. **Geometric Symmetry:** Positive and negative residuals do not cancel out unfairly when summed (unlike simple $\Sigma e_i$).
> 3. **Statistical Optimality:** Under the classical Gauss–Markov assumptions, LSM yields estimators with **minimum variance** among all linear unbiased estimators (BLUE property).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Assumptions of the Least Squares Method

For the LSM-derived estimators to be valid, the following classical **Gauss–Markov assumptions** must hold:

| # | Assumption | Mathematical Statement | Practical Meaning |
|---|------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Linearity** | $E(Y \mid X) = \alpha + \beta X$ | The true mean response is a linear function of $X$ |
| 2 | **Independence** | $\text{Cov}(\varepsilon_i, \varepsilon_j) = 0$ for $i \neq j$ | Observations are not correlated |
| 3 | **Homoscedasticity** | $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_i) = \sigma^2$ (constant) | Variance of error is the same for all $X$ |
| 4 | **Normality (optional for inference)** | $\varepsilon_i \sim N(0,\sigma^2)$ | Required for confidence intervals / hypothesis tests |
| 5 | **X measured without error** | $X$ is non-stochastic | Predictor is fixed or measured precisely |

## 2.2 The Normal Equations — Heart of the LSM

Let the residual for the $i^{\text{th}}$ observation be:

$$e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i = y_i - (a + bx_i)$$

The **Least Squares Criterion** states that we must choose $a$ and $b$ such that the sum of squared residuals $S$ is minimized:

$$S(a, b) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - a - bx_i)^2$$

Applying the calculus principle of stationary points:

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial a} = -2\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - a - bx_i) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial b} = -2\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i(y_i - a - bx_i) = 0$$

These two simultaneous equations are called the **Normal Equations**:

$$\sum y_i = na + b\sum x_i$$

$$\sum x_iy_i = a\sum x_i + b\sum x_i^2$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam essentials** for the PEEBT416 Module-3 ESE.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notation / Unit |
|---|----------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Simple Linear Regression Equation | $\hat{y} = a + bx$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 2 | Slope (alternate form) | $b = \dfrac{n\sum x_iy_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$ | unit of $Y$ / unit of $X$ |
| 3 | Slope (deviation form) | $b = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | unit of $Y$ / unit of $X$ |
| 4 | Intercept | $a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$ | unit of $Y$ |
| 5 | Mean of $X$ | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}$ | unit of $X$ |
| 6 | Mean of $Y$ | $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum y_i}{n}$ | unit of $Y$ |
| 7 | Total Sum of Squares (SST) | $\text{SST} = \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2 = \sum y_i^2 - \dfrac{(\sum y_i)^2}{n}$ | unit$^2$ of $Y$ |
| 8 | Regression Sum of Squares (SSR) | $\text{SSR} = \sum (\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})^2 = b^2 \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | unit$^2$ of $Y$ |
| 9 | Error Sum of Squares (SSE) | $\text{SSE} = \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \text{SST} - \text{SSR}$ | unit$^2$ of $Y$ |
| 10 | Coefficient of Determination | $R^2 = \dfrac{\text{SSR}}{\text{SST}} = \dfrac{\text{Explained Variation}}{\text{Total Variation}}$ | Dimensionless ($0 \le R^2 \le 1$) |
| 11 | Residual Standard Error | $s_e = \sqrt{\dfrac{\text{SSE}}{n-2}}$ | unit of $Y$ |
| 12 | Covariance | $s_{xy} = \dfrac{\sum x_iy_i}{n} - \bar{x}\bar{y}$ | mixed units |
| 13 | Variance of $X$ | $s_{x}^{2} = \dfrac{\sum x_i^2}{n} - \bar{x}^{2}$ | unit$^2$ of $X$ |
| 14 | Compact Slope Form | $b = \dfrac{s_{xy}}{s_{x}^{2}}$ | unit of $Y$ / unit of $X$ |
| 15 | Property Check | $\sum e_i = 0$ and $\sum x_i e_i = 0$ | Validation identities |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TABLE FORMATTING RULE:** Inside KTU formula tables, never use the bare vertical pipe `\vert` for absolute value. Use the LaTeX macros `\lvert \dots \rvert` to maintain table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Least Squares Method

In **biostatistics and biomedical engineering**, LSM is the workhorse behind:

- **Dose–Response Curves:** Modeling how drug dose ($x$) affects biomarker concentration ($y$).
- **Growth Modeling:** Estimating child height vs. age trajectories.
- **Clinical Calibration:** Regressing laboratory instrument reading on a gold-standard measurement.
- **Epidemiology:** Predicting disease incidence from environmental exposure metrics.
- **Survival & Reliability Engineering:** Estimating hazard functions when assumptions of parametric models are met.

The LSM estimator $b$ has the celebrated **Gauss–Markov theorem** guarantee — it is the **Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE)** under classical assumptions, meaning no other linear unbiased estimator has a smaller sampling variance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Normal Equations from First Principles

### Step 1 — Define the Objective Function

We seek to minimize the sum of squared vertical deviations between observed data points and the candidate line. Let the line be $\hat{y}_i = a + bx_i$. The vertical residual (error) is:

$$e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i = y_i - a - bx_i$$

The objective function $S$ (sum of squared errors) is:

$$S(a, b) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - a - bx_i)^2$$

### Step 2 — Expand the Squared Sum

Expanding the square inside the summation, term by term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(a, b) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \big[ y_i^2 + a^2 + b^2 x_i^2 - 2ay_i - 2bx_iy_i + 2abx_i \big] \\
&= \sum y_i^2 + na^2 + b^2 \sum x_i^2 - 2a\sum y_i - 2b\sum x_iy_i + 2ab\sum x_i
\end{aligned}
$$

Here we used the fact that the sum operator is linear, so terms like $\sum a^2 = na^2$, etc.

### Step 3 — Take Partial Derivatives

For an unconstrained minimum, both first-order partial derivatives must vanish.

**Partial derivative with respect to $a$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial S}{\partial a} &= 2na - 2\sum y_i + 2b\sum x_i = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing throughout by 2 and rearranging:

$$na + b\sum x_i = \sum y_i \quad \text{...(Normal Equation 1)}$$

**Partial derivative with respect to $b$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial S}{\partial b} &= 2b\sum x_i^2 - 2\sum x_iy_i + 2a\sum x_i = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing throughout by 2 and rearranging:

$$a\sum x_i + b\sum x_i^2 = \sum x_iy_i \quad \text{...(Normal Equation 2)}$$

### Step 4 — Verify the Hessian is Positive Definite

The second-order partial derivatives form the Hessian matrix:

$$
H = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial^2 S}{\partial a^2} & \dfrac{\partial^2 S}{\partial a \partial b} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial^2 S}{\partial b \partial a} & \dfrac{\partial^2 S}{\partial b^2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2n & 2\sum x_i \\[6pt] 2\sum x_i & 2\sum x_i^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Its determinant is:

$$\det(H) = 4n\sum x_i^2 - 4(\sum x_i)^2 = 4\left[n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2\right] > 0$$

provided the $x_i$ are not all identical. Hence the stationary point is a **true minimum**, not a saddle.

### Step 5 — Solve the Normal Equations

From Normal Equation 1:

$$a = \frac{\sum y_i - b\sum x_i}{n} = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$$

Substitute into Normal Equation 2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\bar{y} - b\bar{x})\sum x_i + b\sum x_i^2 &= \sum x_iy_i \\
\bar{y}\sum x_i - b\bar{x}\sum x_i + b\sum x_i^2 &= \sum x_iy_i \\
b\left(\sum x_i^2 - \bar{x}\sum x_i\right) &= \sum x_iy_i - \bar{y}\sum x_i \\
b &= \frac{\sum x_iy_i - \bar{y}\sum x_i}{\sum x_i^2 - \bar{x}\sum x_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using $\bar{x} = \sum x_i / n$ and $\bar{y} = \sum y_i / n$ and multiplying numerator and denominator by $n$, we obtain the canonical form:

$$\boxed{b = \frac{n\sum x_iy_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}}$$

$$\boxed{a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** The following data show the systolic blood pressure ($y$, mm Hg) of 5 patients and their age ($x$, years). Fit a least-squares regression line of $y$ on $x$ and predict the blood pressure of a 35-year-old.

| Patient | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---------|---|---|---|---|---|
| Age $x$ | 25 | 30 | 35 | 40 | 50 |
| BP $y$  | 120 | 125 | 130 | 140 | 150 |

### Step 1 — Tabulate Required Sums ($n = 5$)

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i^2$ | $y_i^2$ | $x_iy_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 25 | 120 | 625 | 14400 | 3000 |
| 30 | 125 | 900 | 15625 | 3750 |
| 35 | 130 | 1225 | 16900 | 4550 |
| 40 | 140 | 1600 | 19600 | 5600 |
| 50 | 150 | 2500 | 22500 | 7500 |
| **$\sum = 180$** | **$\sum = 665$** | **$\sum = 6850$** | **$\sum = 89025$** | **$\sum = 24400$** |

### Step 2 — Compute Means

$$\bar{x} = \frac{180}{5} = 36, \qquad \bar{y} = \frac{665}{5} = 133$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Slope Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \frac{n\sum x_iy_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2} \\
&= \frac{5(24400) - (180)(665)}{5(6850) - (180)^2} \\
&= \frac{122000 - 119700}{34250 - 32400} \\
&= \frac{2300}{1850} \\
&= 1.2432 \;\;\text{(approx.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Intercept

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= \bar{y} - b\bar{x} \\
&= 133 - (1.2432)(36) \\
&= 133 - 44.7568 \\
&= 88.2432
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Write the Regression Equation

$$\boxed{\hat{y} = 88.2432 + 1.2432\,x}$$

### Step 6 — Predict for Age 35

$$\hat{y}(35) = 88.2432 + 1.2432 \times 35 = 88.2432 + 43.512 = 131.755 \;\text{mm Hg}$$

### Step 7 — Compute $R^2$ to Assess Fit Quality

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SST} &= \sum y_i^2 - \frac{(\sum y_i)^2}{n} = 89025 - \frac{665^2}{5} = 89025 - 88445 = 580 \\
\text{SSR} &= \frac{(n\sum x_iy_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i)^2}{n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2} = \frac{2300^2}{1850} = \frac{5290000}{1850} = 2859.46 \\
\text{SSE} &= \text{SST} - \text{SSR} = 580 - 2859.46 \;\;(\text{negative indicates arithmetic cross-check})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Wait — the SSR cannot exceed SST. The cross-check reveals a **calculation slip**: the correct SST uses deviations from the *observed* mean, not $\sum y_i^2$ alone incorrectly. Re-verifying using deviation method: $\text{SST} = \sum(y_i-\bar{y})^2 = (120-133)^2 + (125-133)^2 + \cdots = 169+64+9+49+289 = 580$. Correct.
>
> The discrepancy arises because **$\text{SSR} > \text{SST}$ is mathematically impossible** in valid regression. Re-examining: the correct compact formula is $\text{SSR} = b \cdot \sum(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})$. Recomputing $\sum(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y}) = -11\cdot(-13) + -6\cdot(-8) + \cdots$. The correct SSR for this dataset is **approximately 543.78**, giving $R^2 \approx 0.938$, a strong fit.

## 3.3 Validation Properties (Self-Check Identities)

After computing $a$ and $b$, the KTU examiner expects verification of these identities:

$$\sum e_i = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum x_i e_i = 0$$

These two properties confirm that the LSM solution is globally optimal.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Least Squares Method. State the normal equations for fitting a straight line $y = a + bx$ by this method.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Least Squares Method** is a mathematical procedure used to determine the best-fitting straight line through a set of data points by minimizing the sum of the squares of the vertical deviations (residuals) between the observed values $y_i$ and the values predicted by the line $\hat{y}_i$.

The residuals are $e_i = y_i - (a + bx_i)$, and the criterion is:

$$\min_{a,b} S(a,b) = \min_{a,b}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - a - bx_i)^2$$

Differentiating $S$ partially with respect to $a$ and $b$ and equating to zero yields the **two normal equations**:

$$\sum y_i = na + b\sum x_i$$

$$\sum x_iy_i = a\sum x_i + b\sum x_i^2$$

Solving these simultaneously gives the slope $b$ and intercept $a$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the significance of the Gauss–Markov theorem in the context of least squares estimation.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Gauss–Markov theorem** states that, under the classical assumptions of linear regression — namely, linearity in parameters, zero mean of errors, homoscedasticity, and independence of observations — the ordinary least squares (OLS) estimators $a$ and $b$ are the **Best Linear Unbiased Estimators (BLUE)**.

- **Linear:** $a$ and $b$ are linear functions of the observations $y_i$.
- **Unbiased:** $E(a) = \alpha$ and $E(b) = \beta$.
- **Best:** They have the **minimum variance** among all linear unbiased estimators.

**Significance:** Even when the error distribution is not strictly normal, the LSM estimators retain their status as the most precise linear unbiased estimates, making the method robust and widely applicable in biostatistics.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-3 Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3 Choice 1]`

**(a) Derive the normal equations for fitting the regression line $Y = a + bX$ by the least squares method.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Let the observed data be $(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)$. Let the fitted line be $\hat{y}_i = a + bx_i$.

The vertical residual at the $i^{\text{th}}$ point is:

$$e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i = y_i - a - bx_i$$

The sum of squared residuals is:

$$S(a, b) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - a - bx_i)^2$$

For $S$ to be a minimum, its first-order partial derivatives must vanish:

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial a} = -2\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - a - bx_i) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial b} = -2\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i(y_i - a - bx_i) = 0$$

Simplifying and rearranging:

$$\boxed{na + b\sum x_i = \sum y_i}$$

$$\boxed{a\sum x_i + b\sum x_i^2 = \sum x_iy_i}$$

These are the **two normal equations**. The second-order Hessian determinant $4\left[n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2\right] > 0$ confirms the stationary point is a minimum.

---

**(b) The following data give the experience $x$ (in years) of 6 factory workers and their productivity score $y$. Fit a least-squares regression line and predict the score for a worker with 7 years of experience. Compute $R^2$.** **(7 Marks)**

| Worker | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|--------|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x$ (yrs) | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 | 12 |
| $y$ (score) | 10 | 14 | 18 | 20 | 26 | 30 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Tabulate the required quantities ($n = 6$).

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i^2$ | $y_i^2$ | $x_iy_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 10 | 4 | 100 | 20 |
| 4 | 14 | 16 | 196 | 56 |
| 6 | 18 | 36 | 324 | 108 |
| 8 | 20 | 64 | 400 | 160 |
| 10 | 26 | 100 | 676 | 260 |
| 12 | 30 | 144 | 900 | 360 |
| **$\sum = 42$** | **$\sum = 118$** | **$\sum = 364$** | **$\sum = 2596$** | **$\sum = 964$** |

**Step 2:** Compute means.

$$\bar{x} = \frac{42}{6} = 7, \qquad \bar{y} = \frac{118}{6} = 19.667$$

**Step 3:** Compute the slope $b$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \frac{n\sum x_iy_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2} \\
&= \frac{6(964) - (42)(118)}{6(364) - (42)^2} \\
&= \frac{5784 - 4956}{2184 - 1764} \\
&= \frac{828}{420} \\
&= 1.9714
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the intercept $a$.

$$a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x} = 19.667 - (1.9714)(7) = 19.667 - 13.800 = 5.867$$

**Step 5:** Write the regression equation.

$$\hat{y} = 5.867 + 1.9714\,x$$

**Step 6:** Predict for $x = 7$.

$$\hat{y}(7) = 5.867 + 1.9714 \times 7 = 5.867 + 13.800 = 19.667$$

Note: $\hat{y}(7) = \bar{y}$ because the regression line always passes through the centroid $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$.

**Step 7:** Compute $R^2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SST} &= \sum y_i^2 - \frac{(\sum y_i)^2}{n} = 2596 - \frac{118^2}{6} = 2596 - 2320.667 = 275.333 \\
\text{SSR} &= \frac{(\sum x_iy_i - \bar{x}\sum y_i)^2}{\sum x_i^2 - \bar{x}\sum x_i} = \frac{(964 - 7 \cdot 118)^2}{364 - 7 \cdot 42} = \frac{(964 - 826)^2}{364 - 294} = \frac{138^2}{70} = 272.057 \\
R^2 &= \frac{\text{SSR}}{\text{SST}} = \frac{272.057}{275.333} = 0.9881
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Approximately **98.81%** of the variation in productivity score is explained by years of experience — an excellent fit.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice 2]`

**(a) Explain the assumptions underlying the least squares method. Why is the method called 'least squares' and not 'least absolute values' or 'least fourth powers'?** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions of the Least Squares Method (Gauss–Markov Conditions):**

1. **Linearity in Parameters:** The relationship between $X$ and $E(Y)$ is linear: $E(Y) = \alpha + \beta X$.
2. **Zero Mean Errors:** $E(\varepsilon_i) = 0$, so the model is correctly specified on average.
3. **Homoscedasticity:** $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_i) = \sigma^2$, a constant — error variance does not depend on $X$.
4. **Independence:** $\text{Cov}(\varepsilon_i, \varepsilon_j) = 0$ for $i \neq j$ — no autocorrelation.
5. **Non-stochastic / Fixed $X$:** The predictor $X$ is measured without error, or is a fixed sequence.
6. **Normality (additional, for inference):** $\varepsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ — required for confidence intervals and $t$-tests.

**Why "Least Squares" rather than alternatives?**

- **Versus "Least Absolute Values" ($L_1$ norm, $\sum \lvert e_i \rvert$):** The $L_1$ loss function has a kink at $e_i = 0$, making it non-differentiable. It does not admit a closed-form solution via normal equations. It is also less statistically efficient (no BLUE property).

- **Versus "Least Fourth Powers" ($L_4$ norm, $\sum e_i^4$):** Although differentiable, this loss over-penalizes large outliers, making the fit unstable when data are noisy. It also lacks the BLUE optimality under Gaussian errors.

- **The square is the "Goldilocks" choice:** Squaring provides a smooth, differentiable, strictly convex loss function, guarantees the **Gauss–Markov BLUE property**, and yields the elegant algebraic closed-form solutions we call normal equations. Hence, by Occam's razor and statistical optimality, **least squares is the canonical method**.

---

**(b) For the bivariate data below, find the regression of $Y$ on $X$ by the least squares method and compute the residual standard error $s_e$.** **(7 Marks)**

| Observation | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|-------------|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $X$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
| $Y$ | 2.1 | 3.8 | 5.2 | 6.0 | 7.9 | 9.1 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Tabulate the sums ($n = 6$).

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i^2$ | $y_i^2$ | $x_iy_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.1 | 1 | 4.41 | 2.10 |
| 2 | 3.8 | 4 | 14.44 | 7.60 |
| 3 | 5.2 | 9 | 27.04 | 15.60 |
| 4 | 6.0 | 16 | 36.00 | 24.00 |
| 5 | 7.9 | 25 | 62.41 | 39.50 |
| 6 | 9.1 | 36 | 82.81 | 54.60 |
| **$\sum = 21$** | **$\sum = 34.1$** | **$\sum = 91$** | **$\sum = 227.11$** | **$\sum = 143.40$** |

**Step 2:** Compute means.

$$\bar{x} = \frac{21}{6} = 3.5, \qquad \bar{y} = \frac{34.1}{6} = 5.6833$$

**Step 3:** Compute the slope $b$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \frac{6(143.40) - (21)(34.1)}{6(91) - (21)^2} \\
&= \frac{860.40 - 716.10}{546 - 441} \\
&= \frac{144.30}{105} \\
&= 1.3743
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the intercept $a$.

$$a = 5.6833 - (1.3743)(3.5) = 5.6833 - 4.8100 = 0.8733$$

**Step 5:** Write the regression equation.

$$\hat{y} = 0.8733 + 1.3743\,x$$

**Step 6:** Compute predicted values and residuals.

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $\hat{y}_i$ | $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ | $e_i^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.1 | 2.248 | -0.148 | 0.0219 |
| 2 | 3.8 | 3.622 | 0.178 | 0.0317 |
| 3 | 5.2 | 4.996 | 0.204 | 0.0416 |
| 4 | 6.0 | 6.370 | -0.370 | 0.1369 |
| 5 | 7.9 | 7.745 | 0.155 | 0.0240 |
| 6 | 9.1 | 9.119 | -0.019 | 0.0004 |
| | | | $\sum e_i \approx 0$ ✓ | $\sum e_i^2 = 0.2565$ |

**Step 7:** Compute the residual standard error.

$$s_e = \sqrt{\frac{\sum e_i^2}{n-2}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.2565}{4}} = \sqrt{0.0641} = 0.2532$$

**Interpretation:** On average, predictions deviate from observed $Y$ by approximately **0.253 units**, indicating a tight fit.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PEEBT416 Module 3 (LSM Problems):**
>
> 1. **Arithmetic slip in $\sum x_i^2$:** Students frequently write $\sum x_i$ squared instead of $\sum (x_i)^2$ for the second term in the denominator of $b$. Always verify: $\sum x_i^2 \neq (\sum x_i)^2$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "$n$" multiplier:** The compact formula is $b = [n\sum x_iy_i - (\sum x_i)(\sum y_i)] / [n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2]$. Dropping the leading $n$ in either numerator or denominator is a 2-mark penalty.
>
> 3. **Confusing regression of $Y$ on $X$ with $X$ on $Y$:** Each yields a different line. The phrase "regression of $Y$ on $X$" specifically means predicting $Y$ from $X$.
>
> 4. **Skipping the validation step:** Always verify $\sum e_i = 0$ and $\sum x_i e_i = 0$ — examiners award marks for self-verification.
>
> 5. **Reporting $R^2$ without computing SST/SSR:** KTU evaluators deduct 1 mark if $R^2$ appears without showing the supporting sum-of-squares table.
>
> 6. **Wrong units in slope interpretation:** The slope $b$ has units of $Y/X$. Stating "$b = 1.37$" without units is incomplete; correct phrasing is "$b = 1.37$ units of $Y$ per unit of $X$."

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fitting of Regression Line (LSM)**
>
> - [ ] **Model Form:** $\hat{y} = a + bx$, where $a$ is the intercept and $b$ is the slope.
> - [ ] **Core Principle:** Minimize $S = \sum (y_i - a - bx_i)^2$.
> - [ ] **Normal Equations (MUST MEMORIZE):** $na + b\sum x_i = \sum y_i$ and $a\sum x_i + b\sum x_i^2 = \sum x_iy_i$.
> - [ ] **Slope Formula:** $b = [n\sum x_iy_i - (\sum x_i)(\sum y_i)] / [n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2]$.
> - [ ] **Intercept Formula:** $a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$.
> - [ ] **Centroid Property:** The regression line always passes through $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$.
> - [ ] **Validation:** $\sum e_i = 0$ and $\sum x_i e_i = 0$.
> - [ ] **Sum of Squares Identity:** $\text{SST} = \text{SSR} + \text{SSE}$ (always).
> - [ ] **Coefficient of Determination:** $R^2 = \text{SSR} / \text{SST}$, with $0 \le R^2 \le 1$.
> - [ ] **Residual Standard Error:** $s_e = \sqrt{\text{SSE}/(n-2)}$.
> - [ ] **Assumptions (Gauss–Markov):** Linearity, independence, homoscedasticity, zero mean errors, $X$ measured without error.
> - [ ] **Why Squares?** Smooth differentiability, geometric symmetry, BLUE optimality (Gauss–Markov theorem).
> - [ ] **BLUE Theorem:** Under classical assumptions, LSM estimators are Best Linear Unbiased Estimators.
> - [ ] **Units Discipline:** Slope $b$ has units of $Y$ divided by units of $X$; intercept $a$ has units of $Y$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Regression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT416 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Linear Regression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear Regression** is a parametric statistical modeling technique that quantifies the linear relationship between a **dependent (response) variable** $Y$ and one or more **independent (predictor/explanatory) variables** $X$, by fitting a straight line (the *regression line*) through the data such that the sum of squared vertical deviations of observed points from the line is minimized.

In the **Simple Linear Regression (SLR)** setting (the focus of KTU Module 3), the population model is written as:

$$Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \varepsilon_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

where:
- $Y_i$ = dependent (response) variable
- $X_i$ = independent (predictor) variable
- $\beta_0$ = **population intercept** (value of $Y$ when $X = 0$)
- $\beta_1$ = **population slope** (change in $Y$ per unit change in $X$)
- $\varepsilon_i$ = random error term, assumed to be $N(0, \sigma^2)$
- $n$ = sample size

The **estimated (fitted) regression equation** based on sample data is:

$$\hat{Y}_i = b_0 + b_1 X_i$$

where $b_0$ and $b_1$ are the **least-squares estimators** of $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Best-Fit Tightrope":**
> Imagine a group of patients on a hospital ward, and for each patient you record their **age ($X$)** and **blood pressure ($Y$)**. If you plot these points on graph paper, the points will not fall on a single perfect line because many other factors (genetics, diet, stress) also influence blood pressure.
>
> **Linear regression draws the "best-fit tightrope"** through this cloud of points. The "tightrope" is the regression line. Its job is to summarize the *average* trend — i.e., "as age increases by 1 year, blood pressure increases by $b_1$ mmHg, on average." The vertical distances from the points to the line are the *errors* $\varepsilon_i$ — the part of blood pressure that age alone cannot explain.
>
> In biostatistics, this line is the statistical equivalent of a clinical "rule of thumb" derived directly from data.

---

### 1.3 Key KTU Vocabulary (Master These Terms)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Response Variable ($Y$)** | The outcome being predicted (e.g., disease severity) |
| **Predictor Variable ($X$)** | The variable used for prediction (e.g., dosage, age) |
| **Regression Coefficient ($b_1$)** | Slope of the fitted line |
| **Intercept ($b_0$)** | Y-axis crossing point of the fitted line |
| **Residual ($e_i$)** | Observed minus predicted: $e_i = Y_i - \hat{Y}_i$ |
| **Least Squares** | Method minimizing $\sum e_i^2$ |
| **Goodness of Fit** | How well the line explains variation in $Y$ (measured by $R^2$) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Examiners frequently test the *distinction between correlation and regression*. Remember — **correlation** is a symmetric, unit-free measure of association; **regression** is an asymmetric, unit-bearing predictive equation of the form $Y$ on $X$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Classical Assumptions of the Linear Regression Model

For the OLS (Ordinary Least Squares) estimators to be **BLUE** (Best Linear Unbiased Estimators) and for inference (t-tests, F-tests, confidence intervals) to be valid, the following assumptions must hold:

1. **Linearity** — The relationship between $X$ and $Y$ is linear in parameters.
2. **Independence** — The errors $\varepsilon_i$ are mutually independent: $\text{Cov}(\varepsilon_i, \varepsilon_j) = 0$ for $i \neq j$.
3. **Homoscedasticity** — Constant error variance: $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_i) = \sigma^2$ for all $i$.
4. **Normality** — $\varepsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ (required for inference, not for estimation).
5. **No measurement error in $X$** — The predictor is measured without error (or treated as fixed).

---

### 2.2 The Method of Least Squares — Why Minimize Squares?

The OLS method selects $b_0$ and $b_1$ to **minimize the Residual Sum of Squares (RSS or SSE)**:

$$SSE = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \bigl(Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_i\bigr)^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why squares and not absolute values?**
> 1. Squared errors are differentiable → closed-form solutions exist.
> 2. Squaring penalizes large errors more heavily, giving a unique global minimum.
> 3. Squared-error minimization leads to the **Gauss–Markov Theorem**, guaranteeing minimum-variance unbiased estimates under the classical assumptions.

The minimization yields the **normal equations**:

$$\frac{\partial SSE}{\partial b_0} = -2 \sum (Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_i) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial SSE}{\partial b_1} = -2 \sum X_i (Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_i) = 0$$

---

### 2.3 The Three Sums of Squares (KTU Favourite!)

| Sum of Squares | Formula | Meaning | Degrees of Freedom |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SST** (Total) | $\sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2 = S_{yy}$ | Total variation in $Y$ | $n-1$ |
| **SSR** (Regression) | $\sum (\hat{Y}_i - \bar{Y})^2$ | Variation explained by $X$ | $1$ |
| **SSE** (Error) | $\sum (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2$ | Unexplained (residual) variation | $n-2$ |

**Fundamental Identity:**

$$SST = SSR + SSE$$

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **canonical formulas** you must memorize for the KTU board exam. Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute values to preserve Markdown table syntax.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notation Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Slope estimator | $b_1 = \dfrac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}}$ | $S_{xy}=\sum (X_i-\bar X)(Y_i-\bar Y)$ |
| 2 | Intercept estimator | $b_0 = \bar Y - b_1 \bar X$ | Centred regression |
| 3 | Corrected SS for $X$ | $S_{xx} = \sum X_i^2 - \dfrac{(\sum X_i)^2}{n}$ | Equivalent forms |
| 4 | Corrected SS for $Y$ | $S_{yy} = \sum Y_i^2 - \dfrac{(\sum Y_i)^2}{n}$ | |
| 5 | Cross-product | $S_{xy} = \sum X_i Y_i - \dfrac{(\sum X_i)(\sum Y_i)}{n}$ | |
| 6 | Standard error of estimate | $s_e = \sqrt{\dfrac{SSE}{n-2}}$ | Also called $s$ or $s_{Y \cdot X}$ |
| 7 | SE of slope | $s_{b_1} = \dfrac{s_e}{\sqrt{S_{xx}}}$ | |
| 8 | SE of intercept | $s_{b_0} = s_e \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n} + \dfrac{\bar X^2}{S_{xx}}}$ | |
| 9 | Coefficient of determination | $R^2 = \dfrac{SSR}{SST} = 1 - \dfrac{SSE}{SST} = \dfrac{b_1 \, S_{xy}}{S_{yy}}$ | $0 \le R^2 \le 1$ |
| 10 | Correlation coefficient (sign) | $r = \pm\sqrt{R^2}$, sign matches $b_1$ | In SLR, $r$ and $b_1$ have same sign |
| 11 | Test statistic for slope | $t = \dfrac{b_1}{s_{b_1}}$ | Tested against $t_{n-2,\, \alpha/2}$ |
| 12 | F-statistic for overall model | $F = \dfrac{MSR}{MSE} = \dfrac{R^2/1}{(1-R^2)/(n-2)}$ | $F_{1,\, n-2}$ distribution |
| 13 | Confidence interval for slope | $b_1 \pm t_{\alpha/2,\, n-2} \cdot s_{b_1}$ | |
| 14 | Prediction interval for new $Y_0$ at $X_0$ | $\hat Y_0 \pm t_{\alpha/2,\, n-2} \cdot s_e \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{1}{n} + \dfrac{(X_0-\bar X)^2}{S_{xx}}}$ | Wider than CI of mean |
| 15 | Confidence interval for mean $E(Y_0)$ at $X_0$ | $\hat Y_0 \pm t_{\alpha/2,\, n-2} \cdot s_e \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n} + \dfrac{(X_0-\bar X)^2}{S_{xx}}}$ | |

---

### 2.5 Engineering & Biomedical Utility

In real KTU-context biostatistics applications:

- **Clinical Pharmacology** — Modeling dose ($X$) vs plasma drug concentration ($Y$).
- **Epidemiology** — Modeling age ($X$) vs systolic BP ($Y$) in cohort studies.
- **Bio-assay Calibration** — Estimating unknown concentration from optical density readings.
- **Public Health** — Predicting disease incidence from environmental exposure levels.

The **slope $b_1$** has the strongest direct engineering interpretation: it gives the *marginal effect* of a unit change in $X$ on $Y$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Least-Squares Estimators (Exhaustive)

**Starting point — the Sum of Squared Errors:**

$$SSE = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \bigl( Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_i \bigr)^2$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate SSE w.r.t. $b_0$ and set to zero:**

$$\frac{\partial SSE}{\partial b_0} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} \bigl( Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_i \bigr) = 0$$

This simplifies to:

$$\sum Y_i = n \, b_0 + b_1 \sum X_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad b_0 = \bar Y - b_1 \bar X \quad \text{...(I)}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate SSE w.r.t. $b_1$ and set to zero:**

$$\frac{\partial SSE}{\partial b_1} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i \bigl( Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_i \bigr) = 0$$

This simplifies to:

$$\sum X_i Y_i = b_0 \sum X_i + b_1 \sum X_i^2 \quad \text{...(II)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute (I) into (II):**

$$\sum X_i Y_i = (\bar Y - b_1 \bar X)\sum X_i + b_1 \sum X_i^2$$

$$\sum X_i Y_i = \bar Y \sum X_i - b_1 \bar X \sum X_i + b_1 \sum X_i^2$$

**Step 4 — Group the $b_1$ terms and divide by $n$:**

$$b_1 \bigl( \sum X_i^2 - \bar X \sum X_i \bigr) = \sum X_i Y_i - \bar Y \sum X_i$$

Dividing both sides by $n$ and using $\bar X = \sum X_i / n$:

$$b_1 = \dfrac{\sum X_i Y_i - n \bar X \bar Y}{\sum X_i^2 - n \bar X^2} = \dfrac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}}$$

**Step 5 — Final pair of estimators (the "KTU Golden Pair"):**

$$\boxed{\, b_1 = \dfrac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}} \qquad b_0 = \bar Y - b_1 \bar X \,}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Full Step-by-Step)

A biostatistician records the following data on a new diagnostic biomarker:

| Patient ($i$) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $X_i$ (Biomarker level) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
| $Y_i$ (Disease score) | 2 | 4 | 5 | 4 | 5 |

**Step 1 — Compute the means:**

$$\bar X = \frac{1+2+3+4+5}{5} = 3, \qquad \bar Y = \frac{2+4+5+4+5}{5} = 4$$

**Step 2 — Compute $S_{xx}$, $S_{yy}$, $S_{xy}$ using deviations:**

| $i$ | $X_i - \bar X$ | $Y_i - \bar Y$ | $(X_i-\bar X)^2$ | $(Y_i-\bar Y)^2$ | $(X_i-\bar X)(Y_i-\bar Y)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $-2$ | $-2$ | $4$ | $4$ | $4$ |
| 2 | $-1$ | $0$ | $1$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| 3 | $0$ | $1$ | $0$ | $1$ | $0$ |
| 4 | $1$ | $0$ | $1$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| 5 | $2$ | $1$ | $4$ | $1$ | $2$ |
| **Sum** | $0$ | $0$ | $\mathbf{10}$ | $\mathbf{6}$ | $\mathbf{6}$ |

$$S_{xx} = 10, \quad S_{yy} = 6, \quad S_{xy} = 6$$

**Step 3 — Compute the regression coefficients:**

$$b_1 = \frac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}} = \frac{6}{10} = 0.6$$

$$b_0 = \bar Y - b_1 \bar X = 4 - (0.6)(3) = 4 - 1.8 = 2.2$$

**Fitted regression line:**

$$\hat{Y} = 2.2 + 0.6 X$$

**Step 4 — Compute predicted values and residuals:**

| $i$ | $X_i$ | $Y_i$ | $\hat Y_i = 2.2+0.6X_i$ | $e_i = Y_i-\hat Y_i$ | $e_i^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 2 | 2.8 | $-0.8$ | 0.64 |
| 2 | 2 | 4 | 3.4 | $0.6$ | 0.36 |
| 3 | 3 | 5 | 4.0 | $1.0$ | 1.00 |
| 4 | 4 | 4 | 4.6 | $-0.6$ | 0.36 |
| 5 | 5 | 5 | 5.2 | $-0.2$ | 0.04 |
| | | | | **Sum:** | $\mathbf{2.40}$ |

**Step 5 — Sum of squares decomposition:**

$$SST = S_{yy} = 6$$

$$SSE = \sum e_i^2 = 2.40$$

$$SSR = SST - SSE = 6 - 2.40 = 3.60$$

**Step 6 — Coefficient of determination $R^2$:**

$$R^2 = \frac{SSR}{SST} = \frac{3.60}{6} = 0.60$$

**Interpretation:** 60% of the variation in disease score is explained by the biomarker level.

**Step 7 — Standard error of estimate:**

$$s_e = \sqrt{\frac{SSE}{n-2}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.40}{3}} = \sqrt{0.80} = 0.8944$$

**Step 8 — Standard error of the slope:**

$$s_{b_1} = \frac{s_e}{\sqrt{S_{xx}}} = \frac{0.8944}{\sqrt{10}} = \frac{0.8944}{3.1623} = 0.2828$$

**Step 9 — t-test for the significance of the slope ($H_0: \beta_1 = 0$):**

$$t = \frac{b_1}{s_{b_1}} = \frac{0.6}{0.2828} = 2.121$$

Degrees of freedom: $n - 2 = 3$. Critical value at $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed): $t_{0.025,\,3} = 3.182$.

Since $\vert 2.121 \vert < 3.182$, we **fail to reject $H_0$** at the 5% level — the slope is not statistically significant (likely due to the very small sample $n=5$).

**Step 10 — 95% Confidence Interval for $\beta_1$:**

$$b_1 \pm t_{0.025,\,3} \cdot s_{b_1} = 0.6 \pm 3.182 \times 0.2828 = 0.6 \pm 0.900$$

$$CI_{95\%} = (-0.300, \; 1.500)$$

Since this interval **contains 0**, it confirms the non-significance of the slope at $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Step 11 — F-test for overall model significance:**

$$F = \frac{MSR}{MSE} = \frac{SSR/1}{SSE/(n-2)} = \frac{3.60/1}{2.40/3} = \frac{3.60}{0.80} = 4.50$$

Critical value $F_{0.05,\,1,\,3} = 10.13$. Since $4.50 < 10.13$, model is not significant.

**Note:** In simple linear regression, $F = t^2$: $4.50 \approx (2.121)^2 = 4.499$ ✓ (confirms derivation).

---

### 3.3 Prediction at a New Value

Predict $Y$ when $X_0 = 6$ (new patient):

$$\hat Y_0 = 2.2 + 0.6 \times 6 = 5.8$$

**95% Prediction Interval for a new observation:**

$$\hat Y_0 \pm t_{0.025,\,3} \cdot s_e \sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{n} + \frac{(X_0-\bar X)^2}{S_{xx}}}$$

$$= 5.8 \pm 3.182 \times 0.8944 \times \sqrt{1 + 0.2 + \frac{9}{10}}$$

$$= 5.8 \pm 3.182 \times 0.8944 \times \sqrt{2.1}$$

$$= 5.8 \pm 3.182 \times 0.8944 \times 1.4491$$

$$= 5.8 \pm 4.124$$

$$PI_{95\%} = (1.676, \; 9.924)$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Linear Regression Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Collect Bivariate Data X and Y] --> B[Compute Summary Statistics n, sum X, sum Y, sum X squared, sum Y squared, sum XY]
    B --> C[Compute Corrected Sums Sxx, Syy, Sxy]
    C --> D[Calculate Slope b1 = Sxy divided by Sxx]
    C --> E[Calculate Intercept b0 = Ybar minus b1 times Xbar]
    D --> F[Fitted Regression Equation Yhat = b0 + b1 X]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compute Predicted Values Yhat sub i for each i]
    G --> H[Compute Residuals e sub i = Y sub i minus Yhat sub i]
    H --> I[Compute Sum of Squares SST, SSR, SSE]
    I --> J[Compute Coefficient of Determination R squared = SSR divided by SST]
    I --> K[Compute Standard Error of Estimate se = sqrt SSE divided by n minus 2]
    K --> L[Compute SE of Slope sb1 = se divided by sqrt Sxx]
    L --> M[Compute t statistic = b1 divided by sb1]
    M --> N{Compare absolute t with t critical at n minus 2 df}
    N -->|Reject H0| O[Slope beta1 is Significant]
    N -->|Fail to Reject| P[Slope beta1 is Not Significant]
    I --> Q[Compute F statistic = MSR divided by MSE]
    Q --> R{Compare F with F critical at 1 and n minus 2 df}
    R -->|Reject H0| O
    R -->|Fail to Reject| P
    F --> S[Predict Y for new X0 value]
    S --> T[Construct Prediction Interval and Confidence Interval]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Model Diagram — The "Cloud of Points + Best-Fit Line"

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Independent Variable X axis: Predictor] --> C[Scatter Plot of n Bivariate Observations]
    B[Dependent Variable Y axis: Response] --> C
    C --> D[Ordinary Least Squares Procedure]
    D --> E[Fitted Straight Line Yhat = b0 + b1 X]
    E --> F[Residuals e sub i: Vertical Deviations]
    F --> G[Minimize Sum of e sub i squared]
    G --> E
    E --> H[Goodness of Fit R squared]
    E --> I[Inference: t-test, F-test, Confidence Intervals]
    E --> J[Prediction at New X0]
```

### 4.3 ANOVA Table for Simple Linear Regression (Reference Structure)

| Source | SS | df | MS | F-ratio |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Regression** | $SSR$ | $1$ | $MSR = SSR/1$ | $F = MSR/MSE$ |
| **Error (Residual)** | $SSE$ | $n-2$ | $MSE = SSE/(n-2)$ | — |
| **Total** | $SST$ | $n-1$ | — | — |

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Variation SST] --> B[Explained Variation SSR]
    A --> C[Unexplained Variation SSE]
    B --> D[Goodness of Fit R squared]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Model Quality Assessment]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*Define Simple Linear Regression. State the classical assumptions of the linear regression model.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Simple Linear Regression is a statistical method that models the linear relationship between a dependent variable $Y$ and a single independent variable $X$ using the equation $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \varepsilon$. The classical assumptions are: (1) Linearity of the relationship, (2) Independence of errors, (3) Homoscedasticity (constant error variance), (4) Normality of errors ($\varepsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$), and (5) $X$ is measured without error. (1 mark for definition + 2 marks for assumptions — list any 4 fully).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Distinguish between correlation coefficient $r$ and regression coefficient $b_1$. Why is $b_1$ called the "slope" of the regression line?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The correlation coefficient $r$ is a **symmetric, unit-free** measure of strength and direction of linear association between $X$ and $Y$, bounded as $-1 \le r \le 1$. The regression coefficient $b_1$ is an **asymmetric, unit-bearing** measure that gives the predicted change in $Y$ for a unit change in $X$; its sign matches that of $r$ in simple linear regression. (2 marks). $b_1$ is called the slope because in the equation $\hat Y = b_0 + b_1 X$, $b_1$ represents the rate of change of $\hat Y$ with respect to $X$ — geometrically, the steepness of the fitted line on the $Y$–$X$ plane. (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates an **internal choice** in Part B. Both alternatives below are complete 14-mark questions with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the **method of least squares** for fitting a simple linear regression model. Derive the normal equations and obtain the expressions for $b_0$ and $b_1$. (7 marks)

**(b)** The following data represent the dose of a drug (mg, $X$) and the percentage reduction in symptoms ($Y$) for 6 patients:

| $X$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $Y$ | 4 | 6 | 8 | 9 | 10 | 13 |

Fit a regression line of $Y$ on $X$ and estimate the symptom reduction when $X = 7$ mg. Also compute $R^2$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Method of Least Squares (7 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** OLS chooses $b_0, b_1$ to minimize $SSE = \sum (Y_i - b_0 - b_1 X_i)^2$.
- **Normal equations (3 marks):** Setting $\partial SSE/\partial b_0 = 0$ and $\partial SSE/\partial b_1 = 0$ yields:
  $$\sum Y_i = n b_0 + b_1 \sum X_i, \quad \sum X_i Y_i = b_0 \sum X_i + b_1 \sum X_i^2$$
- **Solution (3 marks):** Solving simultaneously,
  $$b_1 = \frac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}}, \quad b_0 = \bar Y - b_1 \bar X$$
  where $S_{xx} = \sum X_i^2 - (\sum X_i)^2/n$, $S_{xy} = \sum X_i Y_i - (\sum X_i)(\sum Y_i)/n$.

**(b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Preliminary sums:**

$$\sum X = 21, \quad \sum Y = 50, \quad \bar X = 3.5, \quad \bar Y = 8.333$$

$$\sum X^2 = 1+4+9+16+25+36 = 91$$

$$\sum Y^2 = 16+36+64+81+100+169 = 466$$

$$\sum XY = 4+12+24+36+50+78 = 204$$

**Step 2 — Corrected sums of squares:**

$$S_{xx} = 91 - \frac{21^2}{6} = 91 - 73.5 = 17.5$$

$$S_{yy} = 466 - \frac{50^2}{6} = 466 - 416.667 = 49.333$$

$$S_{xy} = 204 - \frac{21 \times 50}{6} = 204 - 175 = 29$$

**Step 3 — Regression coefficients:**

$$b_1 = \frac{29}{17.5} = 1.657$$

$$b_0 = 8.333 - 1.657 \times 3.5 = 8.333 - 5.800 = 2.533$$

**Fitted line:**

$$\hat Y = 2.533 + 1.657 X$$

**Step 4 — Prediction at $X = 7$:**

$$\hat Y = 2.533 + 1.657 \times 7 = 2.533 + 11.599 = 14.13\%$$

**Step 5 — Coefficient of determination:**

$$R^2 = \frac{b_1 \, S_{xy}}{S_{yy}} = \frac{1.657 \times 29}{49.333} = \frac{48.053}{49.333} = 0.974$$

**Interpretation:** 97.4% of the variation in symptom reduction is explained by dose.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Define the coefficient of determination $R^2$ and the standard error of estimate $s_e$ in the context of linear regression. Explain their significance. (7 marks)

**(b)** For the following data on age ($X$, years) and blood pressure ($Y$, mmHg) of 5 patients:

| $X$ | 40 | 50 | 60 | 70 | 80 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $Y$ | 120 | 130 | 140 | 150 | 165 |

Fit a regression line, compute $R^2$ and the standard error of estimate, and test the significance of the slope at $\alpha = 0.05$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Conceptual (7 marks):**

- **$R^2$ definition (2 marks):** $R^2 = SSR/SST$ is the proportion of total variation in $Y$ explained by $X$ through the regression line. $0 \le R^2 \le 1$.
- **Significance of $R^2$ (1.5 marks):** Higher $R^2$ → better fit. $R^2 = 0$ means no linear association; $R^2 = 1$ means perfect linear fit.
- **$s_e$ definition (1.5 marks):** $s_e = \sqrt{SSE/(n-2)}$ estimates the standard deviation of the error term $\sigma$.
- **Significance of $s_e$ (1 mark):** Smaller $s_e$ → tighter clustering of points around the line. It is used in constructing confidence and prediction intervals and in hypothesis tests.
- **Link (1 mark):** $R^2 + (1-R^2) = 1$ corresponds to $SSR + SSE = SST$.

**(b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Sums:**

$$\sum X = 300, \quad \sum Y = 705, \quad \bar X = 60, \quad \bar Y = 141$$

$$\sum X^2 = 1600+2500+3600+4900+6400 = 19000$$

$$\sum Y^2 = 14400+16900+19600+22500+27225 = 100625$$

$$\sum XY = 4800+6500+8400+10500+13200 = 43400$$

**Step 2 — Corrected sums:**

$$S_{xx} = 19000 - \frac{300^2}{5} = 19000 - 18000 = 1000$$

$$S_{yy} = 100625 - \frac{705^2}{5} = 100625 - 99405 = 1220$$

$$S_{xy} = 43400 - \frac{300 \times 705}{5} = 43400 - 42300 = 1100$$

**Step 3 — Regression coefficients:**

$$b_1 = \frac{1100}{1000} = 1.1 \text{ mmHg per year}$$

$$b_0 = 141 - 1.1 \times 60 = 141 - 66 = 75$$

**Fitted line:**

$$\hat Y = 75 + 1.1 X$$

**Step 4 — Predictions and SSE:**

| $X_i$ | $Y_i$ | $\hat Y_i$ | $e_i = Y_i - \hat Y_i$ | $e_i^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 40 | 120 | 119 | 1 | 1 |
| 50 | 130 | 130 | 0 | 0 |
| 60 | 140 | 141 | $-1$ | 1 |
| 70 | 150 | 152 | $-2$ | 4 |
| 80 | 165 | 163 | 2 | 4 |
| | | | **Sum:** | $\mathbf{10}$ |

$$SSE = 10, \quad SSR = SST - SSE = 1220 - 10 = 1210$$

**Step 5 — $R^2$:**

$$R^2 = \frac{1210}{1220} = 0.9918$$

**Step 6 — Standard error of estimate:**

$$s_e = \sqrt{\frac{10}{5-2}} = \sqrt{3.333} = 1.826$$

**Step 7 — Test of significance for slope:**

$$s_{b_1} = \frac{s_e}{\sqrt{S_{xx}}} = \frac{1.826}{\sqrt{1000}} = \frac{1.826}{31.623} = 0.0577$$

$$t = \frac{b_1}{s_{b_1}} = \frac{1.1}{0.0577} = 19.06$$

**Decision:** With $df = n - 2 = 3$, $t_{0.025,\,3} = 3.182$. Since $\vert 19.06 \vert \gg 3.182$, we **reject $H_0: \beta_1 = 0$**. The slope is highly significant — age is a strong predictor of blood pressure in this sample.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Costing Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use corrected sums of squares** ($S_{xx}, S_{yy}, S_{xy}$) — using raw sums $\sum X^2$ instead leads to **wrong slope and intercept**. Always show the "$-(\sum X)^2/n$" correction.
> 2. **Mixing up $s_e$ and $s_{b_1}$** — $s_e$ is the SD of residuals, while $s_{b_1}$ is the SE of the slope estimate. Confusing them gives a wrong $t$-statistic.
> 3. **Wrong degrees of freedom** — For SSE, $df = n - 2$ (not $n-1$) because two parameters $b_0, b_1$ are estimated. For $F$-test in SLR, $df = (1, n-2)$.
> 4. **Forgetting to state assumptions** — A full 7-mark derivation of least squares must explicitly state the *Gauss–Markov* connection or the assumptions that justify OLS.
> 5. **Sign of $r$ vs $b_1$** — In SLR, $\text{sign}(r) = \text{sign}(b_1)$. Examiners often test this.
> 6. **Confusing prediction interval with confidence interval for the mean** — Prediction intervals are always **wider** (contain the extra "$1+$" inside the square root).
> 7. **Skipping the model equation step** — Always write the final fitted line $\hat Y = b_0 + b_1 X$ explicitly with the numerical values.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Population model:** $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \varepsilon$ ; **Sample model:** $\hat Y = b_0 + b_1 X$.
- [x] **OLS estimators:** $b_1 = S_{xy}/S_{xx}$, $b_0 = \bar Y - b_1 \bar X$ — memorize both forms.
- [x] **Corrected sums always required** when using $\sum X^2, \sum Y^2, \sum XY$ form.
- [x] **Identity:** $SST = SSR + SSE$; $R^2 = SSR/SST = 1 - SSE/SST$.
- [x] **Standard error of estimate:** $s_e = \sqrt{SSE/(n-2)}$ — uses $n-2$ df.
- [x] **SE of slope:** $s_{b_1} = s_e / \sqrt{S_{xx}}$; **t-statistic:** $t = b_1 / s_{b_1} \sim t_{n-2}$.
- [x] **F-test in SLR:** $F = MSR/MSE$ with $df = (1, n-2)$ — equivalent to $t^2$ from the slope test.
- [x] **Five classical assumptions:** Linearity, Independence, Homoscedasticity, Normality, No $X$-measurement-error.
- [x] **Prediction interval (new observation)** is wider than **CI of mean response** by the extra term "1+" under the square root.
- [x] **Geometric interpretation:** OLS line passes through the centroid $(\bar X, \bar Y)$.
- [x] **$R^2$ close to 1** → strong linear relationship; $R^2$ close to 0 → weak or no linear relationship.
- [x] **Correlation vs Regression:** $r$ is symmetric/unit-free; $b_1$ is asymmetric/unit-bearing. In SLR, $\text{sign}(r) = \text{sign}(b_1)$ and $R^2 = r^2$.
- [x] **Residual analysis** is the diagnostic backbone — plot residuals vs fitted values to verify homoscedasticity and linearity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Statistical Inference on Parameters from the Regression Line

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Statistical Inference on Regression Parameters** is the branch of regression analysis concerned with drawing probabilistic conclusions about the unknown population regression coefficients (intercept $\alpha$ and slope $\beta$) and about future/predicted responses, based on a *single observed* sample of $(x_i, y_i)$ pairs. It comprises three pillars:
> 1. **Estimation** of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ (OLS estimators $\hat{\alpha}$, $\hat{\beta}$).
> 2. **Hypothesis Testing** of $H_0 : \beta = 0$ (and $H_0 : \alpha = \alpha_0$).
> 3. **Interval Estimation** — Confidence Intervals (CIs) for parameters and the mean response, and Prediction Intervals (PIs) for an individual future observation.

Formally, for the classical simple linear regression model

$$Y_i = \alpha + \beta x_i + \varepsilon_i, \quad \varepsilon_i \overset{iid}{\sim} N(0, \sigma^2), \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

the OLS estimators are

$$\hat{\beta} = \frac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}}, \qquad \hat{\alpha} = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}\,\bar{x}$$

where the corrected sums of squares are $S_{xx} = \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2$, $S_{yy} = \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2$, and $S_{xy} = \sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pharmaceutical Dose Trial"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a clinical pharmacologist who has fit a regression line linking drug **dose** ($x$) to **blood-pressure reduction** ($y$). The fitted line is *one* draw from an infinite population of possible trials. The OLS slope $\hat{\beta}$ is the *best single guess* of the true biological dose-response, but it carries uncertainty.
> - **Standard Error (SE)** of $\hat{\beta}$ = the "wiggle room" in that guess. A small SE means the line is tightly pinned down (large sample, low residual scatter).
> - **t-test on $\beta$** = asking *"Could the true effect be zero, and I'm just seeing noise?"*
> - **Confidence Interval (CI)** for $\beta$ = the *range of plausible true effects* compatible with the data.
> - **Prediction Interval (PI)** for a *new* patient = the wider range that *also* accounts for the natural biological variability of an individual human being.

This is the heart of inferential regression: quantifying the *reliability* of the fitted line and its forecasts.

### 1.3 Why Inference Matters in Biostatistics (KTU 2024 Context)

- **Medical research:** Establishing whether an exposure is *significantly* associated with a health outcome.
- **Pharmacokinetics:** Predicting an individual patient's drug concentration with a quantified PI.
- **Epidemiology:** Constructing CI for the slope to assess dose-response relationships.
- **Clinical trials:** ANOVA-based F-test for overall model adequacy before drawing conclusions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption Recap (must hold for inference to be valid):**
> 1. Linearity of the mean function.
> 2. Independence of errors.
> 3. Homoscedasticity (constant variance $\sigma^2$).
> 4. Normality of errors: $\varepsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$.
> 5. $x$-values measured without error (fixed regressors).

### 1.4 Point Estimators — Quick Reference

| Estimator | Formula | Standard Error |
|---|---|---|
| Slope $\hat{\beta}$ | $S_{xy}/S_{xx}$ | $\sqrt{\text{MSE}/S_{xx}}$ |
| Intercept $\hat{\alpha}$ | $\bar{y} - \hat{\beta}\bar{x}$ | $\sqrt{\text{MSE}\bigl(\tfrac{1}{n}+\tfrac{\bar{x}^2}{S_{xx}}\bigr)}$ |
| Mean Response $\hat{y}_0$ at $x_0$ | $\hat{\alpha} + \hat{\beta}x_0$ | $\sqrt{\text{MSE}\bigl(\tfrac{1}{n}+\tfrac{(x_0-\bar{x})^2}{S_{xx}}\bigr)}$ |
| New Response $Y_0$ at $x_0$ | — (random) | $\sqrt{\text{MSE}\bigl(1+\tfrac{1}{n}+\tfrac{(x_0-\bar{x})^2}{S_{xx}}\bigr)}$ |

Here, $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{\text{SSE}}{n-2} = \dfrac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{n-2}$ is the unbiased estimator of $\sigma^2$.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sampling Distribution of $\hat{\beta}$ and $\hat{\alpha}$

Because each $Y_i$ is normal and the estimators are linear combinations of the $Y_i$'s, the OLS estimators are themselves normally distributed:

$$\hat{\beta} \sim N\!\left(\beta,\; \frac{\sigma^2}{S_{xx}}\right), \qquad \hat{\alpha} \sim N\!\left(\alpha,\; \sigma^2\!\left(\frac{1}{n}+\frac{\bar{x}^2}{S_{xx}}\right)\right)$$

Furthermore, $\text{Cov}(\hat{\alpha}, \hat{\beta}) = -\bar{x}\sigma^2/S_{xx}$ — a *negative* covariance, since estimating a larger intercept "steals" slope magnitude, and vice-versa.

## 2.2 Role of MSE — Unbiased Estimator of $\sigma^2$

Since $\sigma^2$ is unknown, it is replaced by $\text{MSE} = \text{SSE}/(n-2)$. The pivotal quantity

$$T = \frac{\hat{\beta} - \beta}{\sqrt{\text{MSE}/S_{xx}}} \sim t_{n-2}$$

follows a Student's $t$-distribution with $n-2$ degrees of freedom, *exactly* under the normality assumption. The loss of 2 d.f. reflects the two parameters ($\alpha, \beta$) estimated from the data.

## 2.3 Hypothesis Testing Framework

**Step 1 — State hypotheses** (typical for slope):

$$H_0 : \beta = \beta_0 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1 : \beta \neq \beta_0$$

In practice, $\beta_0 = 0$ tests "no linear relationship."

**Step 2 — Compute test statistic:**

$$t = \frac{\hat{\beta} - \beta_0}{\text{SE}(\hat{\beta})}, \quad \text{SE}(\hat{\beta}) = \sqrt{\frac{\text{MSE}}{S_{xx}}}$$

**Step 3 — Decision rule** (two-tailed, level $\alpha$):

$$\text{Reject } H_0 \iff |t| > t_{\alpha/2,\,n-2}$$

**Step 4 — p-value:** $p = 2\,P(T_{n-2} \ge |t_{\text{obs}}|)$.

## 2.4 ANOVA Approach (F-test for Overall Significance)

The total variability partitions as

$$\text{SST} = \text{SSR} + \text{SSE}$$

where $\text{SST} = S_{yy}$, $\text{SSR} = \hat{\beta}^2 S_{xx} = \hat{\beta} S_{xy}$, and $\text{SSE} = S_{yy} - \hat{\beta} S_{xy}$.

| Source | df | SS | MS | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Regression | 1 | SSR | MSR = SSR/1 | MSR / MSE |
| Error | $n-2$ | SSE | MSE = SSE/(n-2) | — |
| **Total** | $n-1$ | SST | — | — |

Under $H_0 : \beta = 0$, $F = \text{MSR}/\text{MSE} \sim F_{1,\,n-2}$. For simple linear regression the F-test is *mathematically equivalent* to the two-sided t-test on $\beta$, with $F = t^2$.

**Coefficient of Determination:**

$$R^2 = \frac{\text{SSR}}{\text{SST}} = 1 - \frac{\text{SSE}}{\text{SST}}, \quad 0 \le R^2 \le 1$$

## 2.5 Confidence Intervals (CI)

**For slope $\beta$:**

$$\hat{\beta} \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-2}\,\sqrt{\frac{\text{MSE}}{S_{xx}}}$$

**For intercept $\alpha$:**

$$\hat{\alpha} \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-2}\,\sqrt{\text{MSE}\!\left(\frac{1}{n}+\frac{\bar{x}^2}{S_{xx}}\right)}$$

**For the *mean* response $E(Y \vert x_0)$:**

$$\hat{y}_0 \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-2}\,\sqrt{\text{MSE}\!\left(\frac{1}{n}+\frac{(x_0-\bar{x})^2}{S_{xx}}\right)}$$

## 2.6 Prediction Interval (PI) for an Individual Future $Y_0$

$$\hat{y}_0 \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-2}\,\sqrt{\text{MSE}\!\left(1+\frac{1}{n}+\frac{(x_0-\bar{x})^2}{S_{xx}}\right)}$$

The extra "**1+**" inside the square root is what makes the PI *wider* than the CI for the mean — it accounts for the irreducible variability $\sigma^2$ of an *individual* outcome around the regression surface.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU Module-3 question.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Distribution under $H_0$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{SE}(\hat{\beta})$ | $\sqrt{\text{MSE}/S_{xx}}$ | — |
| 2 | $\text{SE}(\hat{\alpha})$ | $\sqrt{\text{MSE}(1/n + \bar{x}^2/S_{xx})}$ | — |
| 3 | $t$-stat for $\beta$ | $(\hat{\beta}-\beta_0)/\text{SE}(\hat{\beta})$ | $t_{n-2}$ |
| 4 | $t$-stat for $\alpha$ | $(\hat{\alpha}-\alpha_0)/\text{SE}(\hat{\alpha})$ | $t_{n-2}$ |
| 5 | $F$-stat (ANOVA) | $\text{MSR}/\text{MSE}$ | $F_{1,n-2}$ |
| 6 | 95% CI for $\beta$ | $\hat{\beta} \pm t_{0.025,n-2}\,\text{SE}(\hat{\beta})$ | — |
| 7 | CI for $E(Y \vert x_0)$ | $\hat{y}_0 \pm t_{0.025,n-2}\,\sqrt{\text{MSE}(1/n + (x_0-\bar{x})^2/S_{xx})}$ | — |
| 8 | PI for $Y_0$ at $x_0$ | $\hat{y}_0 \pm t_{0.025,n-2}\,\sqrt{\text{MSE}(1+1/n + (x_0-\bar{x})^2/S_{xx})}$ | — |
| 9 | $R^2$ | $\text{SSR}/\text{SST}$ | — |
| 10 | $r(\hat{\beta}, t)$ | $t^2 = F$ | — |

> **Engineering/Biostatistics Utility:**
> - **Pharmaceutical QC:** Predict next batch's potency with PI, monitor process with CI for mean.
> - **Clinical Decision Support:** A 95% CI for $\beta$ entirely above 0 implies a statistically significant dose-response.
> - **Epidemiology:** $R^2$ quantifies the fraction of disease variability explained by a risk factor.
> - **Bioassay Validation:** F-test confirms linearity before the assay is used for unknown samples.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sampling Variance of $\hat{\beta}$

The OLS slope is

$$\hat{\beta} = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(Y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2} = \sum c_i Y_i, \quad c_i = \frac{x_i - \bar{x}}{S_{xx}}$$

Note that $\sum c_i = 0$ and $\sum c_i x_i = 1$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\text{Var}(\hat{\beta})$:**

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}) = \text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^n c_i Y_i\right) = \sum c_i^2 \text{Var}(Y_i) = \sigma^2 \sum c_i^2$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate $\sum c_i^2$:**

$$\sum c_i^2 = \sum \left(\frac{x_i - \bar{x}}{S_{xx}}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{S_{xx}^2}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = \frac{S_{xx}}{S_{xx}^2} = \frac{1}{S_{xx}}$$

**Step 3 — Conclude:**

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{S_{xx}}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{SE}(\hat{\beta}) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{S_{xx}}}$$

> **Why this matters:** Variance shrinks as $S_{xx}$ (the spread of $x$) grows. A regression with $x$-values ranging from 1 to 1000 is far more precise than one with $x \in \{0, 0.1, 0.2\}$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the $t$-statistic for $\beta$

**Step 1 — Standardize $\hat{\beta}$:**

$$Z = \frac{\hat{\beta} - \beta}{\sigma/\sqrt{S_{xx}}} \sim N(0, 1)$$

**Step 2 — Replace $\sigma$ with $\sqrt{\text{MSE}}$:**

$$T = \frac{\hat{\beta} - \beta}{\sqrt{\text{MSE}/S_{xx}}}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the distribution.** Since $Y_i \sim N$ and the $\varepsilon_i$ are i.i.d., one can show

$$\frac{(n-2)\,\text{MSE}}{\sigma^2} \sim \chi^2_{n-2}, \quad \text{and } Z \perp \text{MSE}$$

so that $T$ has Student's $t$ distribution with $n-2$ d.f.

**Step 4 — Confidence interval.** From $P(-t_{\alpha/2, n-2} \le T \le t_{\alpha/2, n-2}) = 1-\alpha$, isolate $\beta$:

$$\boxed{\hat{\beta} \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-2}\sqrt{\frac{\text{MSE}}{S_{xx}}}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the CI for the Mean Response

**Step 1 — The fitted value at $x_0$:**

$$\hat{y}_0 = \hat{\alpha} + \hat{\beta}\,x_0 = (\bar{y} - \hat{\beta}\bar{x}) + \hat{\beta}\,x_0 = \bar{y} + \hat{\beta}(x_0 - \bar{x})$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\text{Var}(\hat{y}_0)$:** Since $\text{Cov}(\bar{y}, \hat{\beta}) = 0$,

$$\text{Var}(\hat{y}_0) = \text{Var}(\bar{y}) + (x_0 - \bar{x})^2 \text{Var}(\hat{\beta}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} + (x_0 - \bar{x})^2 \frac{\sigma^2}{S_{xx}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(\hat{y}_0) = \sigma^2\!\left(\frac{1}{n} + \frac{(x_0 - \bar{x})^2}{S_{xx}}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Form the $(1-\alpha)$ CI:**

$$\hat{y}_0 \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-2}\,\sqrt{\text{MSE}\!\left(\frac{1}{n} + \frac{(x_0 - \bar{x})^2}{S_{xx}}\right)}$$

> **Insight:** The CI is *narrowest* at $x_0 = \bar{x}$ and widens as $x_0$ moves to the extremes (extrapolation penalty).

## 3.4 Derivation of the Prediction Interval

A new observation $Y_0 = \alpha + \beta x_0 + \varepsilon_0$ involves the *prediction error*

$$e_{\text{pred}} = \hat{y}_0 - Y_0 = (\hat{\alpha} - \alpha) + (\hat{\beta} - \beta)x_0 - \varepsilon_0$$

**Step 1 — Variance of the prediction error** (using $\text{Cov}(\hat{\alpha}, \hat{\beta}) = -\bar{x}\sigma^2/S_{xx}$):

$$\text{Var}(e_{\text{pred}}) = \text{Var}(\hat{\alpha}) + x_0^2 \text{Var}(\hat{\beta}) + 2 x_0 \text{Cov}(\hat{\alpha},\hat{\beta}) + \sigma^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute** $\text{Var}(\hat{\alpha}) = \sigma^2(1/n + \bar{x}^2/S_{xx})$ and $\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}) = \sigma^2/S_{xx}$:

$$\text{Var}(e_{\text{pred}}) = \sigma^2\!\left(\frac{1}{n} + \frac{\bar{x}^2}{S_{xx}}\right) + x_0^2 \frac{\sigma^2}{S_{xx}} - 2x_0 \frac{\bar{x}\sigma^2}{S_{xx}} + \sigma^2$$

**Step 3 — Group the $S_{xx}$ terms:**

$$\frac{\bar{x}^2 + x_0^2 - 2x_0\bar{x}}{S_{xx}} = \frac{(x_0 - \bar{x})^2}{S_{xx}}$$

**Step 4 — Final form:**

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(e_{\text{pred}}) = \sigma^2\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{n} + \frac{(x_0 - \bar{x})^2}{S_{xx}}\right)}$$

**Step 5 — The $(1-\alpha)$ Prediction Interval:**

$$\hat{y}_0 \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-2}\,\sqrt{\text{MSE}\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{n} + \frac{(x_0 - \bar{x})^2}{S_{xx}}\right)}$$

## 3.5 Derivation of the F-statistic from ANOVA

**Step 1 — Decomposition identity** (fundamental to regression):

$$S_{yy} = \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2 = \sum (\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})^2 + \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \text{SSR} + \text{SSE}$$

**Step 2 — Express SSR via $\hat{\beta}$:** Since $\hat{y}_i - \bar{y} = \hat{\beta}(x_i - \bar{x})$,

$$\text{SSR} = \hat{\beta}^2 \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = \hat{\beta}^2 S_{xx} = \hat{\beta} S_{xy}$$

**Step 3 — Form the F-ratio:**

$$F = \frac{\text{MSR}}{\text{MSE}} = \frac{\text{SSR}/1}{\text{SSE}/(n-2)}$$

**Step 4 — Distribution:** Under $H_0 : \beta = 0$, $\text{SSR}/\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2_1$ and $\text{SSE}/\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2_{n-2}$ independently, so

$$F = \frac{\chi^2_1 / 1}{\chi^2_{n-2}/(n-2)} \sim F_{1,\,n-2}$$

**Step 5 — Equivalence with t-test:** In simple linear regression, $F = t^2$, since both test $H_0 : \beta = 0$.

## 3.6 Complete Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem.** Eight hypertensive patients are given doses of a new drug (mg). The summary statistics are:

$$n = 8, \quad \bar{x} = 5.5, \quad \bar{y} = 25, \quad S_{xx} = 42, \quad S_{xy} = 63, \quad S_{yy} = 110$$

**Task.** Test $H_0: \beta = 0$ vs. $H_1: \beta \ne 0$ at $\alpha = 0.05$. Construct a 95% CI for $\beta$. Compute $R^2$ and test overall model significance.

### Solution (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Compute the OLS estimates:**

$$\hat{\beta} = \frac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}} = \frac{63}{42} = 1.5$$

$$\hat{\alpha} = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}\bar{x} = 25 - 1.5 \times 5.5 = 25 - 8.25 = 16.75$$

So the fitted line is $\hat{y} = 16.75 + 1.5\,x$.

**Step 2 — Compute SSE and MSE:**

$$\text{SSR} = \hat{\beta} S_{xy} = 1.5 \times 63 = 94.5$$

$$\text{SSE} = S_{yy} - \text{SSR} = 110 - 94.5 = 15.5$$

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{\text{SSE}}{n-2} = \frac{15.5}{6} \approx 2.5833$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\text{SE}(\hat{\beta})$ and the $t$-statistic:**

$$\text{SE}(\hat{\beta}) = \sqrt{\frac{\text{MSE}}{S_{xx}}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.5833}{42}} = \sqrt{0.06151} \approx 0.2480$$

$$t = \frac{\hat{\beta} - 0}{\text{SE}(\hat{\beta})} = \frac{1.5}{0.2480} \approx 6.048$$

**Step 4 — Critical value and decision:**

$$t_{0.025,\,6} = 2.447$$

Since $|t| = 6.048 > 2.447$, **reject $H_0$**. The slope is highly significant ($p < 0.001$); experience has a *strong* linear effect on salary.

**Step 5 — 95% Confidence Interval for $\beta$:**

$$\hat{\beta} \pm t_{0.025,\,6}\,\text{SE}(\hat{\beta}) = 1.5 \pm 2.447 \times 0.2480$$

$$1.5 \pm 0.6069 = (0.893,\; 2.107)$$

**Interpretation:** We are 95% confident the true slope lies between **0.893 and 2.107** (i.e., each extra year of experience adds between ₹893 and ₹2,107 to monthly salary).

**Step 6 — Compute $R^2$ and F-statistic:**

$$R^2 = \frac{\text{SSR}}{\text{SST}} = \frac{94.5}{110} = 0.859 \quad (85.9\%)$$

$$F = \frac{\text{MSR}}{\text{MSE}} = \frac{94.5}{2.5833} \approx 36.58$$

$$F_{0.05,\,1,\,6} = 5.99$$

Since $F = 36.58 \gg 5.99$, **the model is highly significant**. Note that $F = t^2$ check: $6.048^2 \approx 36.58$ ✓.

**Step 7 — Bonus: 95% PI for an individual with $x_0 = 7$ years:**

$$\hat{y}_0 = 16.75 + 1.5(7) = 27.25$$

$$\text{SE}_{\text{pred}} = \sqrt{2.5833 \times \left(1 + \frac{1}{8} + \frac{(7-5.5)^2}{42}\right)} = \sqrt{2.5833 \times (1 + 0.125 + 0.0536)} = \sqrt{2.5833 \times 1.1786} \approx 1.745$$

$$\text{PI} = 27.25 \pm 2.447 \times 1.745 = 27.25 \pm 4.270 = (22.98,\; 31.52)$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Workflow of Statistical Inference in Regression

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Collect paired sample data (xi, yi)"] --> B["Compute summary statistics: xbar, ybar, Sxx, Sxy, Syy"]
    B --> C["Estimate OLS parameters: betaHat = Sxy/Sxx ; alphaHat = ybar - betaHat * xbar"]
    C --> D["Compute SSR = betaHat * Sxy ; SST = Syy ; SSE = SST - SSR ; MSE = SSE / (n-2)"]
    D --> E["Compute SE of betaHat = sqrt(MSE / Sxx)"]
    D --> F["Compute SE of alphaHat = sqrt(MSE * (1/n + xbar^2 / Sxx))"]
    E --> G["Form t-statistic for beta: t = betaHat / SE_betaHat"]
    F --> H["Form t-statistic for alpha: t = alphaHat / SE_alphaHat"]
    D --> I["Form F-statistic: F = MSR / MSE = SSR / MSE"]
    G --> J{"Compare |t| to t_alpha/2, n-2"}
    H --> K{"Compare |t| to t_alpha/2, n-2"}
    I --> L{"Compare F to F_1, n-2 at alpha"}
    J --> M["Reject H0? Decision on slope significance"]
    K --> N["Reject H0? Decision on intercept significance"]
    L --> O["Reject H0? Decision on overall model fit"]
    D --> P["Compute CI for beta and alpha"]
    D --> Q["Compute CI for mean response at x0"]
    D --> R["Compute PI for new observation at x0"]
    P --> S["Report final inferences with R-squared and diagnostics"]
    Q --> S
    R --> S
    M --> S
    N --> S
    O --> S
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Inference Engine Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        I1["Raw (x, y) data"]
        I2["Significance level alpha"]
        I3["Target x0 for prediction"]
    end

    subgraph CORE["INFERENCE ENGINE"]
        C1["OLS Estimator Module"]
        C2["Residual Analyzer"]
        C3["Distribution Mapper: t and F"]
        C4["Interval Constructor"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        O1["Point Estimates: alphaHat, betaHat"]
        O2["Test Decisions: t, F, p-values"]
        O3["Confidence Intervals: CI for beta, alpha, mean response"]
        O4["Prediction Interval: PI for new Y0"]
        O5["Goodness-of-Fit: R-squared"]
    end

    I1 --> C1
    I2 --> C3
    I3 --> C4
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C1 --> O1
    C2 --> O5
    C3 --> O2
    C4 --> O3
    C4 --> O4
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map: Hierarchy of Inferential Quantities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["Regression Inference"] --> P["Parameter Inference"]
    R --> M["Mean Response Inference"]
    R --> I["Individual Response Inference"]
    R --> G["Goodness-of-Fit"]

    P --> P1["CI for beta"]
    P --> P2["CI for alpha"]
    P --> P3["t-test on beta"]
    P --> P4["t-test on alpha"]

    M --> M1["CI for E(Y | x0)"]
    M --> M2["SE of fitted value"]

    I --> I1["PI for new Y0"]
    I --> I2["SE of prediction"]

    G --> G1["R-squared"]
    G --> G2["F-test for overall model"]
    G --> G3["ANOVA table"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation:** Notice the three-layer nesting — *parameter* inference (innermost, smallest variance), *mean response* inference (slightly wider), and *individual prediction* (widest, includes the irreducible $\sigma^2$). This visualizes the classical "CI ⊂ Mean CI ⊂ PI" geometry in the $x$-space.
