## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]`
> **Define Biostatistics. List any four major areas where statistics is applied in biomedical data analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Biostatistics is the branch of applied statistics that involves the collection, organisation, analysis, interpretation, and presentation of quantitative data relating to biology, medicine, and public health.

**Four application areas (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
1. **Clinical Trials** — design and analysis of Phase I–IV trials of new drugs and devices.
2. **Epidemiology** — studying distribution and determinants of disease in populations (e.g., COVID-19 incidence in Kerala).
3. **Diagnostic Test Evaluation** — calculating sensitivity, specificity, ROC curves for new diagnostic kits.
4. **Public Health Surveillance** — monitoring vaccination coverage, infant mortality rates, and disease outbreaks.

*(Acceptable alternatives: genomic data analysis, survival analysis, pharmacovigilance, health-economics modelling, medical-imaging statistics.)*

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]`
> **Differentiate between descriptive and inferential statistics with one biomedical example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Descriptive Statistics | Inferential Statistics |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Summarise the *observed* sample | Make *predictions/inferences* about the unobserved population |
| **Tools** | Mean, median, SD, frequency, percentage | t-test, chi-square, regression, CI, ANOVA |
| **Output** | Numerical snapshot of the cohort | Probability statements, hypothesis decisions |
| **Biomedical example** | "Mean systolic BP of 50 hypertensive patients in Thrissur = 142 mmHg" | "With 95% confidence, the true mean systolic BP of *all* hypertensives in Kerala lies between 138 and 146 mmHg" |

**(1 mark for clear distinction, 1 mark for each biomedical example with correct statistical concept.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Module 4, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]`

> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **scope of statistics in clinical trials**, covering the role of statistics in design, randomisation, sample-size determination, blinding, and analysis. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A pharmaceutical company is conducting a Phase-III RCT to compare a new anti-hypertensive drug *A* with the standard drug *B*. The primary endpoint is reduction in systolic BP at 12 weeks. The expected effect size (difference in means) is 5 mmHg, with a common standard deviation of 12 mmHg. Using a two-sided $\alpha = 0.05$ and power $1 - \beta = 0.80$, compute the required sample size per group. State your assumptions clearly. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a) — 7 Marks**

**1. Role of statistics in study design (2 marks):** Statistics informs *every* design choice — choice of parallel-group vs crossover, allocation ratio, stratification factors, choice of primary/secondary endpoints, and definition of analysis populations (ITT, per-protocol, safety).

**2. Randomisation (1 mark):** Randomisation eliminates selection bias and balances known and unknown confounders. Statistical methods include simple randomisation, block randomisation, stratified randomisation, and minimisation.

**3. Sample-size determination (2 marks):** Statistics quantifies the trade-off between Type-I error $\alpha$, Type-II error $\beta$, expected effect size, and variability. Formulas (for two-sample t-test at equal allocation):

$$
n_{\text{per group}} = \frac{2 \sigma^{2} (z_{\alpha/2} + z_{\beta})^{2}}{\Delta^{2}}
$$

**4. Blinding (1 mark):** Single-, double-, and triple-blinding reduce measurement and performance bias. Statistical analysis must respect the blinding hierarchy (e.g., data analysts blinded until database lock).

**5. Analysis (1 mark):** Intention-to-treat (ITT) is the gold-standard analysis population. Missing data handled via multiple imputation, mixed-model repeated measures (MMRM), or last-observation-carried-forward (LOCF). Reporting follows CONSORT 2010 guidelines.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters (1 mark).**
- Significance level (two-sided): $\alpha = 0.05 \Rightarrow z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96$
- Power: $1 - \beta = 0.80 \Rightarrow z_{\beta} = 0.84$
- Effect size: $\Delta = 5$ mmHg
- Common SD: $\sigma = 12$ mmHg

**Step 2 — Apply sample-size formula (3 marks).**

$$
n_{\text{per group}} = \frac{2 \sigma^{2} (z_{\alpha/2} + z_{\beta})^{2}}{\Delta^{2}}
$$

Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{per group}} &= \frac{2 \times 12^{2} \times (1.96 + 0.84)^{2}}{5^{2}} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 144 \times (2.80)^{2}}{25} \\
&= \frac{288 \times 7.84}{25} \\
&= \frac{2257.92}{25} \\
&= 90.32
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Round up (1 mark).** $n_{\text{per group}} = 91$ patients. **Total sample = 182.**

**Step 4 — Adjust for attrition (1 mark).** Assuming 10% dropout, inflate:

$$
n_{\text{adjusted}} = \frac{91}{1 - 0.10} = \frac{91}{0.90} = 101.11
$$

Round up → **102 per group, 204 total.**

**Step 5 — State assumptions (1 mark).**
- Two-sided test.
- Equal allocation 1:1.
- Independent samples, approximate normality.
- Common variance $\sigma^{2}$ in both groups.
- No interim analysis (would require alpha-spending adjustment).

**Final Answer:** A minimum of **102 patients per group, 204 patients in total**, are required to detect a 5 mmHg difference with 80% power at 5% significance, accounting for 10% dropout.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Module 4, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]`

> **(a)** Explain the **scope and importance of statistics in epidemiology and public health**, with reference to measures of disease frequency (prevalence, incidence) and measures of association (relative risk, odds ratio). *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A case-control study on lung cancer and smoking yields the following 2 × 2 table. Compute the **Odds Ratio** and interpret it. Also compute the **95% confidence interval** for the OR using Woolf's approximation.

| | Lung Cancer (Cases) | No Lung Cancer (Controls) | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Smokers** | 80 | 30 | 110 |
| **Non-smokers** | 20 | 70 | 90 |
| **Total** | 100 | 100 | 200 |

*Use $z_{0.025} = 1.96$.* *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a) — 7 Marks**

**1. Prevalence (1 mark):** Proportion of population having the disease at a given point in time.

$$
P = \frac{\text{Existing cases at time } t}{\text{Total population at time } t}
$$

*Example:* If 60 of 1000 screened Keralite adults have diabetes, $P = 6\%$.

**2. Incidence (1 mark):** Rate of *new* cases per unit time.

$$
I = \frac{\text{New cases in period}}{\text{Population at risk} \times \text{time}}
$$

*Example:* 25 new TB cases per 100 000 person-years in Ernakulam district.

**3. Relative Risk (Cohort study) (2 marks):**

$$
RR = \frac{\text{Incidence in exposed}}{\text{Incidence in unexposed}}
$$

- $RR = 1$ → no association.
- $RR > 1$ → exposure increases risk.
- $RR < 1$ → exposure is protective.

**4. Odds Ratio (Case-control) (2 marks):**

$$
OR = \frac{a \cdot d}{b \cdot c}
$$

where $a, b, c, d$ are the four cells of the 2 × 2 table. For rare diseases $OR \approx RR$.

**5. Importance in Public Health (1 mark):** Statistics underpins outbreak investigation (R-nought estimation), screening-programme design, vaccine-efficacy trials, and resource allocation — all central to Keralas health-policy decisions (e.g., Nipah containment, COVID-19 vaccination).

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Identify cells (1 mark).**
$a = 80$, $b = 30$, $c = 20$, $d = 70$.

**Step 2 — Compute OR (2 marks).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
OR &= \frac{a \cdot d}{b \cdot c} \\
&= \frac{80 \times 70}{30 \times 20} \\
&= \frac{5600}{600} \\
&= 9.333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (1 mark).** The odds of lung cancer among smokers are approximately **9.33 times** the odds among non-smokers — a very strong positive association.

**Step 4 — Compute log-OR and its SE (1 mark).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(OR) &= \ln(9.333) = 2.2336 \\
SE(\ln OR) &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b} + \frac{1}{c} + \frac{1}{d}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{1}{80} + \frac{1}{30} + \frac{1}{20} + \frac{1}{70}} \\
&= \sqrt{0.01250 + 0.03333 + 0.05000 + 0.01429} \\
&= \sqrt{0.11012} \\
&= 0.3318
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Construct 95% CI for ln(OR) (1 mark).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CI_{\ln OR} &= 2.2336 \pm 1.96 \times 0.3318 \\
&= 2.2336 \pm 0.6503 \\
&= (1.5833, \ 2.8839)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Back-transform to OR scale (1 mark).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CI_{OR} &= \left(e^{1.5833}, \ e^{2.8839}\right) \\
&= (4.87, \ 17.90)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $OR = 9.33$; **95% CI = (4.87, 17.90)**. Since the CI does *not* include 1, the association is statistically significant at the 5% level. Smokers have approximately 9.3-fold higher odds of lung cancer than non-smokers, with the true population OR plausibly lying between 4.9 and 17.9.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Module-4 Answers:**
> 1. **Confusing prevalence with incidence.** Prevalence = existing cases; Incidence = *new* cases per unit time. Examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this mix-up.
> 2. **Applying SE = $\sigma / \sqrt{n}$ but then using $s$ without explicitly stating the substitution** — examiners expect the line "since $\sigma$ is unknown, we use sample $s$ as its estimate".
> 3. **In case-control studies, students often compute RR instead of OR** — case-control studies cannot yield true incidence, so only OR is valid.
> 4. **Forgetting to back-transform the CI from log-OR to OR** — a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Failing to round sample size UP** — $n = 90.3$ must become $n = 91$, not $90$.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** for the chosen test (normality, independence, equal variance) — at least 1 mark reserved for assumptions.
> 7. **In sensitivity/specificity problems, mixing up rows and columns of the 2×2 table** — always put the *true disease status* in the columns and the *test result* in the rows.
> 8. **Omitting clinical interpretation** — KTU 2024 scheme requires *clinical* as well as *statistical* interpretation; pure numbers alone fetch at most 60% of marks for that sub-question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Scope of Statistics in Biomedical Data Analysis**

- **Biostatistics** is the application of statistical methods to biological, medical, and health data.
- **Scope spans five stages**: Question → Design → Collection → Analysis → Interpretation.
- **Descriptive stats** summarise; **inferential stats** generalise.
- **Sensitivity** = TP / (TP + FN) → ability to detect disease when present.
- **Specificity** = TN / (TN + FP) → ability to rule out disease when absent.
- **PPV depends on prevalence** via Bayes' theorem — low prevalence ⇒ low PPV even with excellent tests.
- **Odds Ratio** is the *only valid measure of association* in case-control studies.
- **Relative Risk** is valid in cohort and RCT settings.
- **95% CI for a mean** = $\bar{x} \pm 1.96 \, (s/\sqrt{n})$; for difference of means replace 1.96 with $t_{0.025, \nu}$.
- **SE of mean** = $s / \sqrt{n}$ — derived from the variance-addition property of independent samples.
- **Sample size for two-sample t-test** at equal allocation:

$$
n_{\text{per group}} = \frac{2\sigma^{2}(z_{\alpha/2} + z_{\beta})^{2}}{\Delta^{2}}
$$

- Always **round up** the calculated $n$.
- **p-value < 0.05** is *not* the only criterion of importance — always report **effect size and CI**.
- **CONSORT** for trials, **STROBE** for observational studies — KTU 2024 scheme expects awareness of reporting standards.
- **Survival analysis** uses Kaplan–Meier (non-parametric) and Cox PH (semi-parametric) methods; never use ordinary regression on time-to-event data.
- **Confidence interval interpretation**: "If we repeated the study 100 times, approximately 95 of the constructed CIs would contain the true population parameter" — *NOT* "there is a 95% probability that the parameter lies in this particular interval".
- **Recent scope expansion**: AI/ML in radiology, genomics GWAS, mHealth analytics, federated learning across hospitals.
- **Bayesian vs Frequentist**: KTU syllabus emphasises frequentist; recognise that Bayesian methods are increasingly used in adaptive-trial design.
- **Always check assumptions** before applying a parametric test: normality (Shapiro–Wilk), equal variance (Levene/F-test), independence (design-level).
- **For paired data** use paired t-test / Wilcoxon signed-rank; for independent groups use two-sample t / Mann–Whitney.
- **Multiple testing** inflates Type-I error — apply Bonferroni (divide $\alpha$ by number of tests) or Benjamini–Hochberg FDR control.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Statistical Design of Experiments for Clinical and Laboratory Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT416 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Statistical Design of Experiments for Clinical and Laboratory Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the Statistical Design of Experiments (DOE)?

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
Statistical Design of Experiments (DOE) is a formal, structured, and mathematically rigorous framework for planning, conducting, analyzing, and interpreting controlled investigations. In the biomedical context, it provides a systematic methodology to determine the causal effect of one or more *treatments*, *drugs*, *interventions*, or *environmental factors* on a measured biological response, while simultaneously controlling for nuisance variation, bias, and confounders.

In simple terms: **DOE is the science of planning experiments so that the data collected can be analyzed using statistical methods to draw valid and objective conclusions.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus Areas:**
> 1. Completely Randomized Design (CRD)
> 2. Randomized Block Design (RBD)
> 3. Latin Square Design (LSD)
> 4. Factorial Designs (2² and 2³)
> 5. Clinical Trial Phases & Bioassay
> 6. Sample Size Determination

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pharmaceutical Kitchen"

Imagine you are a chef trying to perfect a new therapeutic drug formulation. You have three "ingredients" you can vary: **Dosage (mg)**, **Frequency (times/day)**, and **Diet Type (with food/empty stomach)**. If you randomly change one thing at a time, you may never discover the magical *combination* — and your kitchen (lab) conditions (temperature, humidity) might secretly be ruining your results.

**Statistical DOE is your Master Recipe Book.** It tells you:
- **Which combinations to try** (Treatment combinations)
- **How many times to repeat each trial** (Replication)
- **In what order to run them** (Randomization)
- **How to neutralize kitchen noise** (Blocking)

Without DOE, you are just "guessing." With DOE, every experimental run gives you *maximum information for minimum effort* — this is the **Principle of Parsimony** in experimental research.

---

## 1.3 The Three Foundational Principles of DOE (R.A. Fisher's Legacy)

Every valid experimental design is built upon **three sacred pillars**:

### Pillar 1: **Randomization** 🎲
Every experimental unit (patient, mouse, petri dish) has an equal and independent probability of being assigned to *any* treatment group. This eliminates **selection bias** and ensures that the effects we observe are due to the treatment, not pre-existing differences.

### Pillar 2: **Replication** 🔁
Each treatment is applied to **multiple** experimental units. Replication accomplishes two things:
- Provides an estimate of the *experimental error* (σ²)
- Increases the **statistical power** (1 − β) of the test

### Pillar 3: **Blocking** 🧱
Homogeneous subgroups (blocks) are created to control for known sources of variability. For example, grouping mice by *litter* or patients by *age group* eliminates the noise caused by these nuisance factors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context:** Sir Ronald Aylmer Fisher introduced these principles in the 1920s at Rothamsted Agricultural Station, England. He is considered the *Father of Modern Experimental Design*. His book *"The Design of Experiments"* (1935) remains a classic.

---

## 1.4 Why is DOE Critical in Biomedical/Clinical Research?

| Application Area | Role of DOE |
|------------------|-------------|
| **Clinical Drug Trials** | Comparing efficacy of Drug A vs Drug B vs Placebo |
| **Laboratory Bioassays** | Determining dose-response relationships |
| **Animal Studies** | Controlling for genetic/environmental variation |
| **Epidemiology** | Designing case-control and cohort studies |
| **Quality Control in Hospitals** | Optimizing assay procedures and diagnostic kits |
| **Microbiology** | Comparing growth media and culture conditions |

---

## 1.5 KTU 2024 Classification of Experimental Designs

```
                        EXPERIMENTAL DESIGNS
                                  │
        ┌─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┐
        │                         │                         │
  SINGLE FACTOR             MULTI-FACTOR             SPECIAL
  DESIGNS                   DESIGNS                  DESIGNS
        │                         │                         │
   ┌────┼────┐              ┌─────┼─────┐              ┌────┼────┐
   │         │              │           │              │         │
  CRD     RBD            LSD     Factorial         Crossover   Bioassay
                  (Latin Square)    (2^k)            Designs    Designs
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** A *Factor* is the independent variable (e.g., Drug Type), *Levels* are the values it takes (e.g., Placebo, 5mg, 10mg), and *Response* is the dependent variable measured (e.g., Blood Pressure Reduction in mmHg).

---

## 1.6 Key Terminology for Module 4

- **Experimental Unit (EU):** The smallest entity to which a treatment is independently applied (a single patient, one animal, one aliquot of serum).
- **Treatment:** The specific experimental condition applied to a unit.
- **Factor:** A categorical or quantitative explanatory variable under the researcher's control.
- **Response Variable (Y):** The quantitative outcome measured.
- **Nuisance Variable:** An uncontrollable factor that adds noise (e.g., ambient lab temperature).
- **Main Effect:** The average effect of one factor averaged over all levels of the other factors.
- **Interaction Effect:** The effect of one factor that *depends* on the level of another factor.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Completely Randomized Design (CRD)

### 2.1.1 Definition and Structure
The CRD is the **simplest** experimental design. It involves **one factor** with `t` treatments, and the experimental units are *randomly assigned* to treatments with **no blocking**. Every unit has an equal chance of receiving any treatment.

### 2.1.2 Statistical Model

$$Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}$$

Where:
- $Y_{ij}$ = Response from the $j$-th unit in the $i$-th treatment
- $\mu$ = Overall (grand) mean
- $\tau_i$ = Effect of the $i$-th treatment (deviation from $\mu$)
- $\epsilon_{ij}$ = Random error, assumed $\sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ and i.i.d.

**Constraints:** $\sum_{i=1}^{t} \tau_i = 0$ (for identifiability)

### 2.1.3 Hypotheses

$$H_0: \tau_1 = \tau_2 = \cdots = \tau_t = 0 \quad \text{(No treatment effect)}$$

$$H_1: \text{At least one } \tau_i \neq 0$$

### 2.1.4 Partition of Total Sum of Squares (SS_Total)

The fundamental decomposition is:

$$SS_{Total} = SS_{Treatment} + SS_{Error}$$

Where:

$$SS_{Total} = \sum_{i=1}^{t}\sum_{j=1}^{r_i}(Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2$$

$$SS_{Treatment} = \sum_{i=1}^{t} r_i (\bar{Y}_{i.} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2$$

$$SS_{Error} = \sum_{i=1}^{t}\sum_{j=1}^{r_i}(Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i.})^2$$

**Correction Factor (CF):** Used for computational simplification:

$$CF = \frac{(\sum Y_{ij})^2}{N}, \quad N = \sum r_i$$

### 2.1.5 CRD ANOVA Table

| Source | df | SS | MS | F-ratio |
|--------|-----|----|----|---------|
| Between Treatments | $t-1$ | $SS_{Tr}$ | $MS_{Tr} = SS_{Tr}/(t-1)$ | $F = MS_{Tr}/MS_E$ |
| Within (Error) | $N-t$ | $SS_E$ | $MS_E = SS_E/(N-t)$ | — |
| **Total** | $N-1$ | $SS_T$ | — | — |

**Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $F_{calc} > F_{(t-1, \, N-t, \, \alpha)}$

---

## 2.2 Randomized Block Design (RBD)

### 2.2.1 Motivation
When the experimental units are **heterogeneous** (e.g., patients of different ages), CRD loses power because the error variance inflates. RBD groups units into *homogeneous blocks* and assigns all treatments within each block.

### 2.2.2 Statistical Model

$$Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + \epsilon_{ij}$$

Where:
- $\tau_i$ = Effect of $i$-th treatment ($i = 1, \ldots, t$)
- $\beta_j$ = Effect of $j$-th block ($j = 1, \ldots, b$)
- $\epsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$

**Constraints:** $\sum \tau_i = 0$ and $\sum \beta_j = 0$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** In RBD, the number of blocks equals the number of treatments (each block receives all treatments — this is a *complete* block design).

### 2.2.3 Partition of Total Sum of Squares

$$SS_{Total} = SS_{Treatment} + SS_{Block} + SS_{Error}$$

### 2.2.4 RBD ANOVA Table

| Source | df | SS | MS | F |
|--------|----|----|----|---|
| Treatments | $t-1$ | $SS_{Tr}$ | $MS_{Tr}$ | $F_1 = MS_{Tr}/MS_E$ |
| Blocks | $b-1$ | $SS_{Bl}$ | $MS_{Bl}$ | $F_2 = MS_{Bl}/MS_E$ |
| Error | $(t-1)(b-1)$ | $SS_E$ | $MS_E$ | — |
| **Total** | $tb - 1$ | $SS_T$ | — | — |

---

## 2.3 Latin Square Design (LSD)

### 2.3.1 Motivation
When **two** nuisance factors exist (e.g., Operator + Day, or Lab Technician + Instrument), the LSD allows us to "control" both simultaneously by arranging treatments in a `t × t` square.

### 2.3.2 Statistical Model

$$Y_{ijk} = \mu + \tau_k + R_i + C_j + \epsilon_{ijk}$$

Where:
- $\tau_k$ = Effect of $k$-th treatment
- $R_i$ = Effect of $i$-th row
- $C_j$ = Effect of $j$-th column
- $\epsilon_{ijk} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Rule:** In a Latin Square of size `t`, each treatment appears exactly **once** in each row and exactly **once** in each column. So $N = t^2$ total observations.

### 2.3.3 LSD ANOVA Table

| Source | df | SS | MS | F |
|--------|----|----|----|---|
| Rows | $t-1$ | $SS_R$ | $MS_R$ | $MS_R/MS_E$ |
| Columns | $t-1$ | $SS_C$ | $MS_C$ | $MS_C/MS_E$ |
| Treatments | $t-1$ | $SS_{Tr}$ | $MS_{Tr}$ | $MS_{Tr}/MS_E$ |
| Error | $(t-1)(t-2)$ | $SS_E$ | $MS_E$ | — |
| **Total** | $t^2 - 1$ | $SS_T$ | — | — |

---

## 2.4 Factorial Designs (2^k Designs)

### 2.4.1 Concept
A **factorial design** studies *two or more factors simultaneously*, allowing estimation of:
1. **Main effects** of each factor
2. **Interaction effects** between factors

A **2² factorial** has 2 factors (A, B), each at 2 levels (low/high), giving **4 treatment combinations**: (1), a, b, ab.

A **2³ factorial** has 3 factors (A, B, C), each at 2 levels, giving **8 combinations**: (1), a, b, ab, c, ac, bc, abc.

### 2.4.2 Statistical Model for 2² Design

$$Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_A + \tau_B + (\tau\tau)_{AB} + \epsilon_{ij}$$

### 2.4.3 Effect Estimation (Yates' Algorithm — KTU High-Yield)

For 2² design with `n` replicates:

**Main Effect of A:**
$$A = \frac{1}{2n}\big[(-Y_{(1)} + Y_a - Y_b + Y_{ab})\big]$$

**Main Effect of B:**
$$B = \frac{1}{2n}\big[(-Y_{(1)} - Y_a + Y_b + Y_{ab})\big]$$

**Interaction AB:**
$$AB = \frac{1}{2n}\big[(Y_{(1)} - Y_a - Y_b + Y_{ab})\big]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention (Yates):** The standard order is (1), a, b, ab. The contrast coefficients follow the pattern: $(-1, +1)$ for A, $(-1, +1)$ for B in the corresponding dimension.

### 2.4.4 2² Factorial ANOVA Table (with `n` replicates)

| Source | df | SS | MS | F |
|--------|-----|-----|----|---|
| A | 1 | $SS_A$ | $MS_A$ | $MS_A/MS_E$ |
| B | 1 | $SS_B$ | $MS_B$ | $MS_B/MS_E$ |
| AB | 1 | $SS_{AB}$ | $MS_{AB}$ | $MS_{AB}/MS_E$ |
| Error | $4(n-1)$ | $SS_E$ | $MS_E$ | — |
| **Total** | $4n-1$ | $SS_T$ | — | — |

---

## 2.5 Clinical Trials — Phase-wise Design

| Phase | Purpose | Sample Size | DOE Aspect |
|-------|---------|-------------|------------|
| **Phase I** | Safety, dosage, tolerability | 20–100 healthy volunteers | Dose-escalation design |
| **Phase II** | Efficacy, side effects | 100–300 patients | RBD or CRD |
| **Phase III** | Confirm efficacy, compare to standard | 1,000–3,000 patients | Multi-center RBD |
| **Phase IV** | Post-marketing surveillance | Thousands | Observational |

---

## 2.6 Sample Size Determination (High-Yield for KTU)

For comparing **two means**:

$$n = \frac{2\sigma^2 (Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^2}{( \mu_1 - \mu_2 )^2}$$

For comparing **two proportions**:

$$n = \frac{(Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{2\bar{p}\bar{q}} + Z_{\beta}\sqrt{p_1 q_1 + p_2 q_2})^2}{(p_1 - p_2)^2}$$

Where:
- $\bar{p} = (p_1 + p_2)/2$, $\bar{q} = 1 - \bar{p}$
- $Z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96$ for $\alpha = 0.05$
- $Z_{\beta} = 0.84$ for power $= 0.80$

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Table)

| Design | Model | df Error | F-Test Formula | Replicates |
|--------|-------|----------|----------------|------------|
| **CRD** | $Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}$ | $N - t$ | $MS_{Tr}/MS_E$ | $r_i$ per treatment |
| **RBD** | $Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + \epsilon_{ij}$ | $(t-1)(b-1)$ | $MS_{Tr}/MS_E$ | One per block |
| **LSD** | $Y_{ijk} = \mu + \tau_k + R_i + C_j + \epsilon_{ij}$ | $(t-1)(t-2)$ | $MS_{Tr}/MS_E$ | $t \times t$ square |
| **2² Factorial** | $Y = \mu + A + B + AB + \epsilon$ | $4(n-1)$ | $MS_{effect}/MS_E$ | $n$ replicates |
| **Sample Size (means)** | Two-sample formula | — | $n = 2\sigma^2(Z_{\alpha/2}+Z_{\beta})^2 / \Delta^2$ | — |
| **Sample Size (proportions)** | Two-proportion formula | — | Complex ratio formula | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering/Clinical Utility:** DOE is the engine behind FDA-approved drug testing, ISO-certified laboratory procedures, Six Sigma quality control in hospital biochemistry labs, and evidence-based clinical guidelines. Without DOE, biomedical research would be guesswork.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Completely Randomized Design (CRD)

### Problem Statement
A pharmacologist compares **3 drugs** (A, B, C) for lowering blood pressure. The systolic BP reduction (mmHg) is recorded for **5 patients per group** (not necessarily equal in general CRD, but here equal). Test whether drugs differ at $\alpha = 0.05$.

| Drug A | Drug B | Drug C |
|--------|--------|--------|
| 12 | 15 | 8 |
| 14 | 18 | 10 |
| 11 | 16 | 9 |
| 13 | 17 | 7 |
| 10 | 14 | 6 |

### Solution

**Step 1: Set up Hypotheses**

$$H_0: \mu_A = \mu_B = \mu_C \quad \text{(All drugs equally effective)}$$

$$H_1: \text{At least one mean differs}$$

**Step 2: Compute Totals**

$$\sum_A = 12+14+11+13+10 = 60$$

$$\sum_B = 15+18+16+17+14 = 80$$

$$\sum_C = 8+10+9+7+6 = 40$$

$$G = 60 + 80 + 40 = 180$$

$$N = 15, \quad t = 3, \quad r_i = 5$$

**Step 3: Correction Factor**

$$CF = \frac{G^2}{N} = \frac{(180)^2}{15} = \frac{32400}{15} = 2160$$

**Step 4: Compute Raw Sum of Squares**

$$SS_{Raw} = \sum_{i,j} Y_{ij}^2$$

For Drug A: $12^2 + 14^2 + 11^2 + 13^2 + 10^2 = 144+196+121+169+100 = 730$

For Drug B: $15^2 + 18^2 + 16^2 + 17^2 + 14^2 = 225+324+256+289+196 = 1290$

For Drug C: $8^2 + 10^2 + 9^2 + 7^2 + 6^2 = 64+100+81+49+36 = 330$

$$SS_{Raw} = 730 + 1290 + 330 = 2350$$

**Step 5: Total Sum of Squares**

$$SS_{Total} = SS_{Raw} - CF = 2350 - 2160 = 190$$

**Step 6: Treatment Sum of Squares**

$$SS_{Tr} = \frac{\sum_{i}(\sum_j Y_{ij})^2}{r_i} - CF = \frac{60^2 + 80^2 + 40^2}{5} - 2160$$

$$SS_{Tr} = \frac{3600 + 6400 + 1600}{5} - 2160 = \frac{11600}{5} - 2160 = 2320 - 2160 = 160$$

**Step 7: Error Sum of Squares**

$$SS_{Error} = SS_{Total} - SS_{Tr} = 190 - 160 = 30$$

**Step 8: Degrees of Freedom**

- $df_{Tr} = t - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2$
- $df_{Error} = N - t = 15 - 3 = 12$
- $df_{Total} = N - 1 = 14$

**Step 9: Mean Squares**

$$MS_{Tr} = \frac{160}{2} = 80$$

$$MS_E = \frac{30}{12} = 2.5$$

**Step 10: F-statistic**

$$F_{calc} = \frac{MS_{Tr}}{MS_E} = \frac{80}{2.5} = 32.0$$

**Step 11: Critical Value**

$$F_{table} = F_{(2, 12, 0.05)} = 3.89$$

**Step 12: Decision**

Since $F_{calc} = 32.0 > F_{crit} = 3.89$, **reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** The three drugs differ significantly in their BP-lowering effect at the 5% level.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Randomized Block Design (RBD)

### Problem Statement
A clinical biochemist tests **4 diets** (D1, D2, D3, D4) on weight loss (kg). Because initial weights vary widely, **5 blocks** (BMI groups) are created. Each patient within a block is randomly assigned to a diet.

| Block | D1 | D2 | D3 | D4 |
|-------|----|----|----|-----|
| B1 | 8 | 10 | 12 | 9 |
| B2 | 7 | 9 | 11 | 8 |
| B3 | 9 | 11 | 13 | 10 |
| B4 | 6 | 8 | 10 | 7 |
| B5 | 8 | 10 | 12 | 9 |

### Solution

**Step 1: Row (Block) and Column (Treatment) Totals**

| Block | Total |
|-------|-------|
| B1 | 8+10+12+9 = 39 |
| B2 | 7+9+11+8 = 35 |
| B3 | 9+11+13+10 = 43 |
| B4 | 6+8+10+7 = 31 |
| B5 | 8+10+12+9 = 39 |
| **G** | **187** |

| Diet | Total |
|------|-------|
| D1 | 8+7+9+6+8 = 38 |
| D2 | 10+9+11+8+10 = 48 |
| D3 | 12+11+13+10+12 = 58 |
| D4 | 9+8+10+7+9 = 43 |

**Step 2: Correction Factor**

$$CF = \frac{G^2}{N} = \frac{(187)^2}{20} = \frac{34969}{20} = 1748.45$$

**Step 3: Raw Sum of Squares**

$$SS_{Raw} = \sum Y_{ij}^2 = (8^2+10^2+12^2+9^2) + (7^2+9^2+11^2+8^2) + \cdots$$

Let me compute per block:
- B1: $64+100+144+81 = 389$
- B2: $49+81+121+64 = 315$
- B3: $81+121+169+100 = 471$
- B4: $36+64+100+49 = 249$
- B5: $64+100+144+81 = 389$

$$SS_{Raw} = 389+315+471+249+389 = 1813$$

**Step 4: Total SS**

$$SS_{Total} = 1813 - 1748.45 = 64.55$$

**Step 5: Block SS**

$$SS_{Block} = \frac{39^2 + 35^2 + 43^2 + 31^2 + 39^2}{4} - CF$$

$$SS_{Block} = \frac{1521 + 1225 + 1849 + 961 + 1521}{4} - 1748.45$$

$$SS_{Block} = \frac{7077}{4} - 1748.45 = 1769.25 - 1748.45 = 20.80$$

**Step 6: Treatment SS**

$$SS_{Tr} = \frac{38^2 + 48^2 + 58^2 + 43^2}{5} - CF$$

$$SS_{Tr} = \frac{1444 + 2304 + 3364 + 1849}{5} - 1748.45 = \frac{8961}{5} - 1748.45$$

$$SS_{Tr} = 1792.20 - 1748.45 = 43.75$$

**Step 7: Error SS**

$$SS_{Error} = SS_{Total} - SS_{Tr} - SS_{Block} = 64.55 - 43.75 - 20.80 = 0.00$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** In this specific example, the Error SS came to near zero, suggesting a perfectly additive model (no interaction between Block × Treatment). In practice, this rarely happens exactly.

**Step 8: Degrees of Freedom**

- $df_{Tr} = t - 1 = 4 - 1 = 3$
- $df_{Block} = b - 1 = 5 - 1 = 4$
- $df_{Error} = (t-1)(b-1) = 3 \times 4 = 12$
- $df_{Total} = 19$

**Step 9: Mean Squares and F-ratios**

$$MS_{Tr} = \frac{43.75}{3} = 14.58$$

$$MS_{Block} = \frac{20.80}{4} = 5.20$$

$$MS_E = \frac{0.00}{12} = 0.00 \quad (\text{not computable for F})$$

**Step 10: Decision**

If we compute (assuming slight non-zero error from rounding):

$$F_{Tr} = \frac{14.58}{0.00} \to \infty \implies \text{Significant at } \alpha = 0.05$$

Conclusion: The 4 diets differ significantly in average weight loss.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — 2² Factorial Design (Two-Factor)

### Problem Statement
A microbiologist investigates the effect of **Temperature (A: 30°C, 37°C)** and **pH (B: 6, 8)** on bacterial growth (CFU × 10⁶). Each combination is replicated **twice** (n = 2). The data:

| Combination | Replicate 1 | Replicate 2 | Total |
|-------------|-------------|-------------|-------|
| (1) [Low A, Low B] | 20 | 22 | 42 |
| a [High A, Low B] | 30 | 28 | 58 |
| b [Low A, High B] | 25 | 27 | 52 |
| ab [High A, High B] | 40 | 38 | 78 |

### Solution

**Step 1: Identify Totals**

- $Y_{(1)} = 42$, $Y_a = 58$, $Y_b = 52$, $Y_{ab} = 78$
- $G = 42 + 58 + 52 + 78 = 230$
- $N = 8$, $n = 2$

**Step 2: Correction Factor**

$$CF = \frac{(230)^2}{8} = \frac{52900}{8} = 6612.5$$

**Step 3: Total SS**

Compute $\sum Y^2$:

- (1): $20^2 + 22^2 = 400 + 484 = 884$
- a: $30^2 + 28^2 = 900 + 784 = 1684$
- b: $25^2 + 27^2 = 625 + 729 = 1354$
- ab: $40^2 + 38^2 = 1600 + 1444 = 3044$

$$SS_{Raw} = 884 + 1684 + 1354 + 3044 = 6966$$

$$SS_{Total} = 6966 - 6612.5 = 353.5$$

**Step 4: Effect Calculations (Yates' Method)**

**Main Effect of A (Temperature):**

$$A = \frac{-Y_{(1)} + Y_a - Y_b + Y_{ab}}{2n} = \frac{-42 + 58 - 52 + 78}{4}$$

$$A = \frac{42}{4} = 10.5$$

**Main Effect of B (pH):**

$$B = \frac{-Y_{(1)} - Y_a + Y_b + Y_{ab}}{2n} = \frac{-42 - 58 + 52 + 78}{4}$$

$$B = \frac{30}{4} = 7.5$$

**Interaction AB:**

$$AB = \frac{Y_{(1)} - Y_a - Y_b + Y_{ab}}{2n} = \frac{42 - 58 - 52 + 78}{4}$$

$$AB = \frac{10}{4} = 2.5$$

**Step 5: Sums of Squares**

$$SS_A = \frac{[-Y_{(1)} + Y_a - Y_b + Y_{ab}]^2}{4n} = \frac{42^2}{8} = \frac{1764}{8} = 220.5$$

$$SS_B = \frac{[-Y_{(1)} - Y_a + Y_b + Y_{ab}]^2}{4n} = \frac{30^2}{8} = \frac{900}{8} = 112.5$$

$$SS_{AB} = \frac{[Y_{(1)} - Y_a - Y_b + Y_{ab}]^2}{4n} = \frac{10^2}{8} = \frac{100}{8} = 12.5$$

**Step 6: Error SS**

$$SS_{Error} = SS_{Total} - (SS_A + SS_B + SS_{AB}) = 353.5 - (220.5 + 112.5 + 12.5)$$

$$SS_{Error} = 353.5 - 345.5 = 8.0$$

**Step 7: ANOVA Table**

| Source | df | SS | MS | F |
|--------|-----|-----|----|----|
| A (Temp) | 1 | 220.5 | 220.5 | 110.25 |
| B (pH) | 1 | 112.5 | 112.5 | 56.25 |
| AB | 1 | 12.5 | 12.5 | 6.25 |
| Error | $4(2-1)=4$ | 8.0 | 2.0 | — |
| **Total** | 7 | 353.5 | — | — |

**Step 8: Critical Values**

- $F_{A} = 110.25 > F_{(1,4,0.05)} = 7.71$ → **Significant**
- $F_{B} = 56.25 > F_{(1,4,0.05)} = 7.71$ → **Significant**
- $F_{AB} = 6.25 < F_{(1,4,0.05)} = 7.71$ → **Not Significant**

**Conclusion:** Both Temperature (A) and pH (B) significantly affect bacterial growth, but their *interaction* is not significant — the factors act **independently**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Sample Size Determination

### Problem
A clinical trial plans to compare a new antihypertensive drug vs. standard. Past data: $\sigma = 8$ mmHg. They wish to detect a difference of $\Delta = 5$ mmHg with $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-sided) and power = 80%.

### Solution

$$n = \frac{2\sigma^2(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^2}{\Delta^2}$$

$$n = \frac{2(8)^2(1.96 + 0.84)^2}{5^2} = \frac{2 \cdot 64 \cdot (2.80)^2}{25}$$

$$n = \frac{128 \cdot 7.84}{25} = \frac{1003.52}{25} \approx 40.14$$

$$\boxed{n \approx 41 \text{ per group (round up)}}$$

Total sample = **82 patients**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Design to Use?

| Situation | Recommended Design |
|-----------|---------------------|
| Homogeneous units, 1 factor, quick pilot study | **CRD** |
| Heterogeneous units, 1 factor, 1 nuisance variable | **RBD** |
| Heterogeneous units, 1 factor, 2 nuisance variables | **LSD** |
| Want to study interactions between 2+ factors | **Factorial (2^k)** |
| Long-term clinical trial with patient dropouts | **Crossover** |
| Dose-response estimation | **Bioassay / Parallel-line assay** |
| Need to optimize conditions | **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)** |

---

## 3.6 Computational Verification — Latin Square (3×3)

### Problem
A 3×3 LSD with treatments A, B, C arranged as:

| | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|-------|-------|-------|
| Row 1 | A | B | C |
| Row 2 | B | C | A |
| Row 3 | C | A | B |

Data (response Y):

| | C1 | C2 | C3 |
|---|----|----|----|
| R1 | 10 | 12 | 8 |
| R2 | 14 | 11 | 13 |
| R3 | 9 | 15 | 12 |

### Solution Summary

- $G = 10+12+8+14+11+13+9+15+12 = 104$
- $N = 9$, $CF = 104^2/9 = 10816/9 = 1201.78$
- Row totals: R1=30, R2=38, R3=36
- Column totals: C1=33, C2=38, C3=33
- Treatment totals: A=(10+13+15)=38, B=(12+14+12)=38, C=(8+11+9)=28

$$SS_{Total} = \sum Y^2 - CF = (100+144+64+196+121+169+81+225+144) - 1201.78$$

$$SS_{Total} = 1244 - 1201.78 = 42.22$$

$$SS_{Row} = \frac{30^2+38^2+36^2}{3} - 1201.78 = \frac{900+1444+1296}{3} - 1201.78 = 1213.33 - 1201.78 = 11.55$$

$$SS_{Col} = \frac{33^2+38^2+33^2}{3} - 1201.78 = \frac{1089+1444+1089}{3} - 1201.78 = 1207.33 - 1201.78 = 5.55$$

$$SS_{Tr} = \frac{38^2+38^2+28^2}{3} - 1201.78 = \frac{1444+1444+784}{3} - 1201.78 = 1224 - 1201.78 = 22.22$$

$$SS_{Error} = 42.22 - 11.55 - 5.55 - 22.22 = 2.90$$

**ANOVA Table:**

| Source | df | SS | MS | F |
|--------|----|----|----|----|
| Rows | 2 | 11.55 | 5.775 | 7.97 |
| Columns | 2 | 5.55 | 2.775 | 3.83 |
| Treatments | 2 | 22.22 | 11.11 | 15.32 |
| Error | 2 | 2.90 | 1.45 | — |
| **Total** | 8 | 42.22 | — | — |

Critical values at $\alpha = 0.05$, $df_1 = 2$, $df_2 = 2$: $F_{crit} = 19.00$.

Since $F_{Tr} = 15.32 < 19.00$, treatments are **not significant** at 5% (borderline). Row effect is also marginally significant.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Architecture of DOE (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Research Objective] --> B[Identify Factors and Levels]
    B --> C[Select Experimental Design]
    C --> D{Count of Nuisance Factors}
    D -- 0 nuisance --> E1[Completely Randomized Design CRD]
    D -- 1 nuisance --> E2[Randomized Block Design RBD]
    D -- 2 nuisance --> E3[Latin Square Design LSD]
    D -- Multiple factors --> E4[Factorial Design 2k]
    E1 --> F[Randomize Units to Treatments]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Conduct Experiment and Record Data]
    G --> H[Construct ANOVA Table]
    H --> I{Compute F statistic}
    I --> J[Compare with F critical value]
    J -- Reject H0 --> K[Conclude Significant Effect]
    J -- Fail to reject --> L[Conclude No Significant Effect]
    K --> M[Post hoc Tests if applicable]
    L --> N[Report Findings]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Clinical or Laboratory Decision]
```

## 4.2 Logic Flow — Selection of Appropriate Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Number of Factors] --> Q1{More than 1?}
    Q1 -- No --> R1{Nuisance Factors?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> S1[Factorial Design 2k]
    R1 -- None --> T1[CRD]
    R1 -- One --> T2[RBD]
    R1 -- Two --> T3[LSD]
    R1 -- Many --> T4[Incomplete Block Design]
    S1 --> U1{Replication Available?}
    U1 -- Yes --> V1[Full Factorial]
    U1 -- No --> W1[Fractional Factorial]
```

## 4.3 ANOVA Computational Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X1[Raw Data Yij] --> Y1[Compute Grand Total G]
    Y1 --> Z1[Correction Factor CF = G2 over N]
    Z1 --> AA1[Compute SS Raw = sum of Yij squared]
    AA1 --> AB1[SS Total = SS Raw minus CF]
    AB1 --> AC1[SS Treatment = sum of Ti squared over r minus CF]
    AC1 --> AD1[SS Error = SS Total minus SS Treatment]
    AD1 --> AE1[Compute Mean Squares]
    AE1 --> AF1[Compute F statistic]
    AF1 --> AG1[Look up F table]
    AG1 --> AH1{Decision}
    AH1 -- Fcalc greater Fcrit --> AI1[Reject H0]
    AH1 -- Fcalc less Fcrit --> AJ1[Fail to reject H0]
```

## 4.4 Factorial Design — Treatment Combination Structure (2²)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FactorA[Temperature: A_low and A_high] --> Combo1
    FactorB[pH: B_low and B_high] --> Combo1
    Combo1[Define Treatment Grid] --> T1[Combination 1: A_low B_low = label 1]
    Combo1 --> T2[Combination 2: A_high B_low = label a]
    Combo1 --> T3[Combination 3: A_low B_high = label b]
    Combo1 --> T4[Combination 4: A_high B_high = label ab]
    T1 --> Rep[Apply n Replicates per Cell]
    T2 --> Rep
    T3 --> Rep
    T4 --> Rep
    Rep --> Estim[Estimate Main Effects and Interaction]
    Estim --> ANOVA2x2[Build 2 squared ANOVA Table]
```

## 4.5 Clinical Trial Phase Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase I: Safety] --> P2[Phase II: Efficacy]
    P2 --> P3[Phase III: Confirmatory RCT]
    P3 --> P4[Phase IV: Post Marketing Surveillance]
    P1 --> DOE1[CRD or Dose Escalation]
    P2 --> DOE2[RBD or Parallel Group]
    P3 --> DOE3[Multi Center RBD]
    P4 --> DOE4[Observational Cohort]
```

## 4.6 Randomization Logic Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Recruit[Recruit N Experimental Units] --> Homog{Units Homogeneous?}
    Homog -- Yes --> A[Assign via Simple Randomization to t Treatments]
    Homog -- No --> B[Group into Blocks by Nuisance Factor]
    B --> C[Randomize within each Block to all t Treatments]
    A --> Collect[Collect Response Data]
    C --> Collect
    Collect --> Analyze[Run ANOVA F test]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Completely Randomized Design (CRD). State its statistical model.**

**Model Answer:**

A Completely Randomized Design is a basic experimental design in which experimental units are randomly allocated to treatments without any blocking or grouping. It is used when the experimental units are homogeneous and there is only one factor under study.

The statistical model is:

$$Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}$$

where $Y_{ij}$ is the response of the $j$-th observation under the $i$-th treatment, $\mu$ is the overall mean, $\tau_i$ is the effect of the $i$-th treatment, and $\epsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ is the random error.

The total degrees of freedom is $N-1$, partitioned into $t-1$ for treatments and $N-t$ for error. The treatment effect is tested using the F-ratio $F = MS_{Tr}/MS_E$ with degrees of freedom $(t-1, N-t)$.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is Replication in DOE? Why is it essential?**

**Model Answer:**

Replication refers to the independent repetition of each treatment on multiple experimental units. It is one of the three fundamental principles of experimental design proposed by R.A. Fisher.

**Importance of Replication:**
1. **Provides an estimate of experimental error** ($\sigma^2$) — without replication, error cannot be estimated and no F-test is possible.
2. **Increases the precision** of treatment mean estimates. The standard error of a mean is $\sigma/\sqrt{r}$, which decreases as $r$ (replications) increase.
3. **Increases statistical power** — the ability to detect a true treatment effect when it exists.
4. **Permits generalization** of results to a wider population.

In clinical trials, replication is achieved by enrolling multiple patients per treatment arm. In laboratory bioassays, it is achieved by running multiple aliquots per condition.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the Randomized Block Design (RBD) with its statistical model and ANOVA table. Under what conditions is RBD preferred over CRD? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** The following data give the yields (kg/plot) of 4 varieties of rice tested in 5 blocks. Analyze the data using RBD and state your conclusions at 5% level. **[7 Marks]**

| Block | V1 | V2 | V3 | V4 |
|-------|----|----|----|-----|
| I | 22 | 28 | 30 | 25 |
| II | 20 | 26 | 28 | 23 |
| III | 24 | 30 | 32 | 27 |
| IV | 21 | 27 | 29 | 24 |
| V | 23 | 29 | 31 | 26 |

(F-table at 5%: $F_{3,12} = 3.49$, $F_{4,12} = 3.26$)

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** RBD is used when experimental material is heterogeneous due to one nuisance factor. Units are grouped into homogeneous blocks, and within each block, treatments are randomly assigned.

**Model:** $Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + \epsilon_{ij}$ with $\sum \tau_i = \sum \beta_j = 0$.

**Conditions for RBD over CRD:**
- Known source of variation among experimental units.
- Each block can accommodate all treatments.
- The number of treatments is moderate (typically ≤ 10).

**ANOVA Table:**

| Source | df | SS | MS | F |
|--------|----|----|----|---|
| Treatments | $t-1$ | $SS_{Tr}$ | $MS_{Tr}$ | $MS_{Tr}/MS_E$ |
| Blocks | $b-1$ | $SS_{Bl}$ | $MS_{Bl}$ | $MS_{Bl}/MS_E$ |
| Error | $(t-1)(b-1)$ | $SS_E$ | $MS_E$ | — |
| **Total** | $tb - 1$ | $SS_T$ | — | — |

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

- $G = (22+28+30+25) + (20+26+28+23) + (24+30+32+27) + (21+27+29+24) + (23+29+31+26)$
- $G = 105 + 97 + 113 + 101 + 109 = 525$
- $N = 20$
- $CF = 525^2/20 = 275625/20 = 13781.25$

**Raw SS:**
- V1: $22^2+20^2+24^2+21^2+23^2 = 484+400+576+441+529 = 2430$
- V2: $28^2+26^2+30^2+27^2+29^2 = 784+676+900+729+841 = 3930$
- V3: $30^2+28^2+32^2+29^2+31^2 = 900+784+1024+841+961 = 4510$
- V4: $25^2+23^2+27^2+24^2+26^2 = 625+529+729+576+676 = 3135$

$SS_{Raw} = 2430 + 3930 + 4510 + 3135 = 14005$

$SS_{Total} = 14005 - 13781.25 = 223.75$

**Variety totals:** V1=110, V2=140, V3=150, V4=125

$SS_{Tr} = \frac{110^2 + 140^2 + 150^2 + 125^2}{5} - 13781.25$

$= \frac{12100 + 19600 + 22500 + 15625}{5} - 13781.25 = \frac{69825}{5} - 13781.25 = 13965 - 13781.25 = 183.75$

**Block totals:** I=105, II=97, III=113, IV=101, V=109

$SS_{Bl} = \frac{105^2 + 97^2 + 113^2 + 101^2 + 109^2}{4} - 13781.25$

$= \frac{11025 + 9409 + 12769 + 10201 + 11881}{4} - 13781.25 = \frac{55285}{4} - 13781.25 = 13821.25 - 13781.25 = 40$

$SS_E = 223.75 - 183.75 - 40 = 0$

**ANOVA Table:**

| Source | df | SS | MS | F |
|--------|----|----|----|----|
| Treatments | 3 | 183.75 | 61.25 | very high (>>3.49) |
| Blocks | 4 | 40 | 10 | very high (>>3.26) |
| Error | 12 | 0 | 0 | — |
| **Total** | 19 | 223.75 | — | — |

**Conclusion:** Both variety effect and block effect are significant. The 4 rice varieties differ significantly in mean yield.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the 2² factorial design. How are the main effects and interaction effect computed? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** In a 2² factorial experiment to study the effect of two factors A (concentration) and B (catalyst) on the yield of a chemical reaction, the following data (in grams) were obtained. Replicates = 2. Analyze the data. **[7 Marks]**

| Treatment Combination | Replicate 1 | Replicate 2 | Total |
|----------------------|-------------|-------------|-------|
| (1) [A_low B_low] | 25 | 27 | 52 |
| a [A_high B_low] | 35 | 33 | 68 |
| b [A_low B_high] | 30 | 32 | 62 |
| ab [A_high B_high] | 40 | 42 | 82 |

(F-table: $F_{1,4, 0.05} = 7.71$)

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** A 2² factorial design is one in which 2 factors A and B are each investigated at 2 levels, giving 4 treatment combinations: (1), a, b, ab. With $n$ replicates per combination, the total number of observations is $4n$.

**Computation of Effects (Yates' Method):**

**Main Effect of A:**

$$A = \frac{-T_{(1)} + T_a - T_b + T_{ab}}{2n}$$

**Main Effect of B:**

$$B = \frac{-T_{(1)} - T_a + T_b + T_{ab}}{2n}$$

**Interaction AB:**

$$AB = \frac{T_{(1)} - T_a - T_b + T_{ab}}{2n}$$

The corresponding sums of squares are:

$$SS_A = \frac{(-T_{(1)} + T_a - T_b + T_{ab})^2}{4n}$$

and similarly for B and AB.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

- $T_{(1)} = 52$, $T_a = 68$, $T_b = 62$, $T_{ab} = 82$
- $G = 52 + 68 + 62 + 82 = 264$, $N = 8$, $n = 2$
- $CF = 264^2/8 = 69696/8 = 8712$

**Raw SS:**
- (1): $25^2+27^2 = 625+729 = 1354$
- a: $35^2+33^2 = 1225+1089 = 2314$
- b: $30^2+32^2 = 900+1024 = 1924$
- ab: $40^2+42^2 = 1600+1764 = 3364$

$SS_{Raw} = 1354 + 2314 + 1924 + 3364 = 8956$

$SS_{Total} = 8956 - 8712 = 244$

**Contrast computations:**
- $-T_{(1)} + T_a - T_b + T_{ab} = -52 + 68 - 62 + 82 = 36$
- $-T_{(1)} - T_a + T_b + T_{ab} = -52 - 68 + 62 + 82 = 24$
- $T_{(1)} - T_a - T_b + T_{ab} = 52 - 68 - 62 + 82 = 4$

**Effects:**
- $A = 36/4 = 9$
- $B = 24/4 = 6$
- $AB = 4/4 = 1$

**Sums of Squares:**
- $SS_A = 36^2/8 = 1296/8 = 162$
- $SS_B = 24^2/8 = 576/8 = 72$
- $SS_{AB} = 4^2/8 = 16/8 = 2$
- $SS_E = 244 - 162 - 72 - 2 = 8$

**ANOVA Table:**

| Source | df | SS | MS | F |
|--------|-----|-----|----|----|
| A (Concentration) | 1 | 162 | 162 | 81.0 |
| B (Catalyst) | 1 | 72 | 72 | 36.0 |
| AB (Interaction) | 1 | 2 | 2 | 1.0 |
| Error | 4 | 8 | 2 | — |
| **Total** | 7 | 244 | — | — |

**Conclusion:** 
- $F_A = 81.0 > 7.71$ → Concentration has **highly significant** effect.
- $F_B = 36.0 > 7.71$ → Catalyst has **highly significant** effect.
- $F_{AB} = 1.0 < 7.71$ → Interaction is **not significant** — A and B act independently.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Correction Factor (CF):** Many students compute $SS_{Total}$ without subtracting CF — KTU examiners deduct 1 full mark.
> 2. **Wrong degrees of freedom:** For RBD, $df_{Error} = (t-1)(b-1)$, **NOT** $tb - 2$. For LSD, $df_{Error} = (t-1)(t-2)$.
> 3. **Sign errors in Yates' contrasts:** Memorize the contrast signs carefully:
>    - A: $(-1, +1, -1, +1)$
>    - B: $(-1, -1, +1, +1)$
>    - AB: $(+1, -1, -1, +1)$
> 4. **Misinterpreting "Not Significant":** Saying "we proved the treatments are equal" is WRONG. Correct phrasing: *"We fail to reject $H_0$; there is insufficient evidence of a difference."*
> 5. **Confusing LSD (Least Significant Difference) with Latin Square Design.** These are two different concepts! Always write the full name.
> 6. **Skipping normality assumption:** The ANOVA F-test assumes $\epsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$. Always mention this in your model statement.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Three Principles of DOE:** Randomization, Replication, Blocking (Fisher).
- ✅ **CRD Model:** $Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}$; used when units are homogeneous.
- ✅ **RBD Model:** $Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + \epsilon_{ij}$; controls 1 nuisance factor.
- ✅ **LSD Model:** $Y_{ijk} = \mu + \tau_k + R_i + C_j + \epsilon_{ij}$; controls 2 nuisance factors in $t \times t$ square.
- ✅ **Factorial Design:** Studies multiple factors + their interactions. 2² has 4 combinations; 2³ has 8.
- ✅ **Yates' Algorithm:** Compute main and interaction effects using contrasts.
- ✅ **Correction Factor:** $CF = G^2/N$ — subtract from raw SS to get corrected SS.
- ✅ **F-statistic:** Always ratio of treatment (or factor) MS to error MS.
- ✅ **Sample Size Formula (means):** $n = 2\sigma^2(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^2/\Delta^2$.
- ✅ **Clinical Trial Phases:** I (safety), II (efficacy), III (RCT confirmatory), IV (surveillance).
- ✅ **Standard Z-values:** $Z_{0.025} = 1.96$ (for 95% CI), $Z_{0.05} = 0.84$ (for 80% power).
- ✅ **Assumption of ANOVA:** Independence, Normality, Homoscedasticity (equal variances).
- ✅ **KTU Favorite Questions:** Setting up ANOVA tables, interpreting F-values, computing main effects using Yates' method, sample size calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT416 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Study Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Study Design** is the structured, pre-specified plan, protocol, and framework that dictates the systematic collection, measurement, and analysis of data in a biomedical or epidemiological investigation. It is the architectural blueprint that defines the **population**, the **sampling strategy**, the **variables of interest**, the **method of observation/intervention**, and the **statistical tests** to be applied — all calibrated to a specific research hypothesis.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Course: PEEBT416 – Biostatistics), study design is the cornerstone of Module 4 because it dictates the *type* of evidence a study can generate, the *validity* of its conclusions, and the *causal inferences* permissible from its results.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A well-designed study minimizes the three classical threats to validity — **Bias**, **Confounding**, and **Chance (random error)** — while maximizing **Precision** and **Statistical Power**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe for a Medical Investigation"

Imagine you are a **detective** (the researcher) trying to solve a **mystery** (the research question: *"Does smoking cause lung cancer?"*).

- The **Study Design** is your *investigation strategy*. Will you go door-to-door interviewing victims (case-control)? Will you follow a group of suspects over 20 years (cohort)? Or will you set up a controlled experiment in a lab (RCT)?
- The **2×2 contingency table** is your *evidence board* — a 2×2 grid where you pin the data: who was exposed, who was diseased, who was both, and who was neither.
- The **Odds Ratio** and **Relative Risk** are your *quantitative verdict* — they tell you *how strongly* the suspect (exposure) is linked to the crime (disease).

> [!TIP]
> Think of study design as the **"lens"** through which biostatisticians view reality. A poor lens (flawed design) gives a blurry picture, no matter how good your statistical software is. **"Garbage In = Garbage Out"** — a phrase examiners love to quote.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology (KTU-Standard)

| Term | Standard Symbol | Engineering/Precision Value |
|------|----------------|----------------------------|
| Statistical Significance Threshold | **α (alpha)** | **0.05** (95% confidence) |
| Statistical Power | **1 − β** | **0.80** (80% by convention) |
| Type I Error Rate | **α** | Probability of False Positive |
| Type II Error Rate | **β** | Probability of False Negative |
| Confidence Level | **1 − α** | **95%** or **99%** standard |
| 2×2 Table Cell Labels | **a, b, c, d** | **Universal** in epidemiology |

---

## 1.4 Hierarchical Classification of Study Designs

All study designs in biomedical research fall under **two grand divisions**:

1. **Observational Studies** — the researcher only *observes and records*; no active intervention.
2. **Experimental Studies (Interventional)** — the researcher *actively assigns* an exposure/intervention to participants.

> [!NOTE]
> Within **Observational Studies**, there are three principal types:
> 1. **Cross-Sectional Studies** (snapshot in time)
> 2. **Cohort Studies** (follow forward/backward in time)
> 3. **Case-Control Studies** (start from outcome, look backward)

> [!NOTE]
> Within **Experimental Studies**, the gold standard is the **Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 2×2 Contingency Table — The Heart of Study Design Analysis

Every association study (whether case-control, cohort, or cross-sectional) is summarized in a **2×2 table**:

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
 & \text{Disease (D+)} & \text{No Disease (D-)} \\
\hline
\text{Exposed (E+)} & a & b \\
\hline
\text{Not Exposed (E-)} & c & d \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

Where:
- **a** = Exposed & Diseased (Cases with exposure)
- **b** = Exposed & Not Diseased (Exposed controls)
- **c** = Not Exposed & Diseased (Unexposed cases)
- **d** = Not Exposed & Not Diseased (Unexposed controls)

**Row totals:** (a + b) = Total Exposed ; (c + d) = Total Not Exposed
**Column totals:** (a + c) = Total Diseased ; (b + d) = Total Not Diseased
**Grand Total:** N = a + b + c + d

---

## 2.2 The Three Core Measures of Association

### 2.2.1 Relative Risk (RR) — Also called Risk Ratio

**Definition:** The ratio of the probability (incidence) of disease in the **exposed group** to the probability in the **unexposed group**. RR is the *natural* measure in **Cohort Studies** and **RCTs**.

$$
RR = \frac{\text{Incidence in Exposed}}{\text{Incidence in Unexposed}} = \frac{a/(a+b)}{c/(c+d)}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- **RR = 1** → No association (exposure is neutral)
- **RR > 1** → Positive association (exposure increases risk)
- **RR < 1** → Negative/protective association (exposure reduces risk)

### 2.2.2 Odds Ratio (OR)

**Definition:** The ratio of the **odds of exposure among diseased** to the **odds of exposure among non-diseased**. OR is the *natural* measure in **Case-Control Studies** (where true incidence cannot be calculated because the researcher fixes the number of cases and controls).

$$
OR = \frac{a \times d}{b \times c}
$$

**Key Property:** When the disease is **rare** (rare disease assumption), **OR ≈ RR**.

### 2.2.3 Attributable Risk (AR) — Risk Difference

**Definition:** The **absolute difference** in incidence rates between exposed and unexposed groups. AR tells us the *excess risk* in the exposed group that is attributable to the exposure.

$$
AR = I_e - I_u = \frac{a}{a+b} - \frac{c}{c+d}
$$

- **Attributable Risk in the Exposed (ARₑ)**: the proportion of disease among exposed that is *due to* the exposure.

$$
AR_e = \frac{RR - 1}{RR} \times 100\%
$$

- **Population Attributable Risk (PAR)**: the proportion of disease in the *total population* that is due to the exposure.

$$
PAR = \frac{I_{pop} - I_u}{I_{pop}} \times 100\%
$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is HIGH-YIELD for KTU University Exams — memorize the column "Used In" carefully.**

| Formula | Mathematical Expression | Used In (Study Type) | Range / Interpretation |
|---------|------------------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Relative Risk (RR)** | $\dfrac{a/(a+b)}{c/(c+d)}$ | **Cohort Study / RCT** | RR = 1: No effect ; RR > 1: Harmful ; RR < 1: Protective |
| **Odds Ratio (OR)** | $\dfrac{a \cdot d}{b \cdot c}$ | **Case-Control Study** | OR > 1: Exposure raises odds of disease |
| **Attributable Risk (AR)** | $\dfrac{a}{a+b} - \dfrac{c}{c+d}$ | **Cohort Study / RCT** | AR > 0: Excess risk in exposed |
| **AR in Exposed (ARₑ)** | $\dfrac{RR - 1}{RR} \times 100\%$ | Cohort / RCT | % of disease in exposed due to exposure |
| **Population AR (PAR)** | $\dfrac{I_{pop} - I_u}{I_{pop}} \times 100\%$ | Cohort / RCT | % of disease in population due to exposure |
| **Relative Risk Reduction (RRR)** | $\dfrac{I_u - I_e}{I_u} = 1 - RR$ | **RCT (clinical trials)** | Positive: treatment is effective |
| **Absolute Risk Reduction (ARR)** | $I_u - I_e$ | RCT | Direct clinical impact |
| **Number Needed to Treat (NNT)** | $\dfrac{1}{ARR}$ | RCT | Patients to treat to prevent 1 event |
| **Chi-Square Statistic** | $\chi^2 = \sum \dfrac{(O - E)^2}{E}$ | All categorical comparisons | df = (rows − 1)(cols − 1) |
| **Sample Size (Comparing 2 Means)** | $n = \dfrac{2\sigma^2(z_{\alpha/2} + z_\beta)^2}{(μ_1 - μ_2)^2}$ | RCT / Experimental | Per group |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** In a **2×2 table for 2×2**, degrees of freedom = **(2−1) × (2−1) = 1** — this is a frequent exam question!

---

## 2.4 Why Each Design? — The Engineering / Real-World Utility

| Design | Real-World Utility | Engineering Analogy |
|--------|--------------------|---------------------|
| **Case-Control** | Quick study of rare diseases (e.g., rare cancers) | Reverse-engineering a failed machine to find the cause |
| **Cohort (Prospective)** | Establishes temporal/causal sequence; calculates true incidence | Tracking a new component through its lifecycle |
| **Cohort (Retrospective)** | Fast cohort design using historical records | Forensic audit of past records |
| **Cross-Sectional** | Prevalence snapshots; hypothesis generation | A single still photograph |
| **RCT** | Gold standard for testing new drugs/treatments | Controlled lab experiment in a physics lab |

---

## 2.5 Bias, Confounding, Chance — The Three Threats

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Focus Point:** Every study design question expects you to *identify the type of bias* the design minimizes or suffers from.

| Threat | Definition | Design That Best Controls It |
|--------|------------|------------------------------|
| **Selection Bias** | Systematic error in *who* is selected | Randomization (in RCT) |
| **Information Bias** (Recall / Interviewer) | Systematic error in *how* data is measured | Blinding (in RCT) |
| **Confounding** | Distortion by a third variable linked to both exposure and outcome | Randomization, Matching, Stratification |
| **Chance (Random Error)** | Sampling variability | Larger sample size, replication |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Cohort Study (Deriving RR and AR)

**Problem:** A cohort study follows 1,000 smokers and 1,000 non-smokers for 10 years to track lung cancer incidence.

| Group | Lung Cancer (D+) | No Lung Cancer (D−) | Total |
|-------|------------------|----------------------|-------|
| **Smokers (E+)** | 100 (a) | 900 (b) | 1,000 (a+b) |
| **Non-Smokers (E−)** | 10 (c) | 990 (d) | 1,000 (c+d) |

**Step 1 — Identify cells:**
- a = 100, b = 900, c = 10, d = 990

**Step 2 — Incidence in Exposed (Smokers):**

$$
I_e = \frac{a}{a+b} = \frac{100}{1000} = 0.10 \; (10\%)
$$

**Step 3 — Incidence in Unexposed (Non-Smokers):**

$$
I_u = \frac{c}{c+d} = \frac{10}{1000} = 0.01 \; (1\%)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Relative Risk (RR):**

$$
RR = \frac{I_e}{I_u} = \frac{0.10}{0.10/0.01 \text{ ratio}} = \frac{0.10}{0.01} = 10
$$

**Interpretation:** Smokers are **10 times** more likely to develop lung cancer than non-smokers.

**Step 5 — Compute Attributable Risk (AR):**

$$
AR = I_e - I_u = 0.10 - 0.01 = 0.09 \; (9\%)
$$

**Interpretation:** **9%** of lung cancer cases in smokers are *excess* — attributable to smoking.

**Step 6 — Compute Attributable Risk in Exposed (ARₑ):**

$$
AR_e = \frac{RR - 1}{RR} \times 100\% = \frac{10 - 1}{10} \times 100\% = 90\%
$$

**Interpretation:** **90%** of lung cancer in smokers is *due to* smoking (only 10% would have occurred anyway).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Case-Control Study (Deriving OR)

**Problem:** A case-control study identifies 200 lung cancer patients and 200 controls, then assesses smoking history.

| Group | Smokers (E+) | Non-Smokers (E−) | Total |
|-------|--------------|------------------|-------|
| **Cases (D+)** | 180 (a) | 20 (b) | 200 (a+b) |
| **Controls (D−)** | 60 (c) | 140 (d) | 200 (c+d) |

**Step 1 — Identify cells:**
- a = 180, b = 20, c = 60, d = 140

**Step 2 — Verify the table is internally consistent:**
- Row totals: 180 + 20 = 200 ✓ ; 60 + 140 = 200 ✓
- Column totals: 180 + 60 = 240 (smokers) ; 20 + 140 = 160 (non-smokers)
- Grand total: 180 + 20 + 60 + 140 = 400 ✓

**Step 3 — Compute Odds Ratio (OR):**

$$
OR = \frac{a \cdot d}{b \cdot c} = \frac{180 \times 140}{20 \times 60}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate numerator:**

$$
180 \times 140 = 25200
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate denominator:**

$$
20 \times 60 = 1200
$$

**Step 6 — Final division:**

$$
OR = \frac{25200}{1200} = 21
$$

**Interpretation:** The **odds of being a smoker** are **21 times higher** among lung cancer cases than among controls. This is a very strong association.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-check with RR estimate (rare disease assumption):** Because lung cancer is relatively rare, OR ≈ RR, so we estimate the risk in smokers is ~21× higher than in non-smokers. (Note: in this case, because cases and controls are sampled in equal numbers, OR will be larger than the true RR.)

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Randomized Controlled Trial (Deriving NNT)

**Problem:** A clinical trial tests a new drug versus placebo. Over 5 years:
- Placebo group: 200/1000 had a myocardial infarction (MI)
- Drug group: 100/1000 had an MI

**Step 1 — Incidence in Placebo (Control):**

$$
I_u = \frac{200}{1000} = 0.20
$$

**Step 2 — Incidence in Drug (Exposed):**

$$
I_e = \frac{100}{1000} = 0.10
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Relative Risk Reduction (RRR):**

$$
RRR = \frac{I_u - I_e}{I_u} = \frac{0.20 - 0.10}{0.20} = \frac{0.10}{0.20} = 0.50 \; (50\%)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Absolute Risk Reduction (ARR):**

$$
ARR = I_u - I_e = 0.20 - 0.10 = 0.10 \; (10\%)
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Number Needed to Treat (NNT):**

$$
NNT = \frac{1}{ARR} = \frac{1}{0.10} = 10
$$

**Interpretation:** You must treat **10 patients** with the new drug to prevent **1 myocardial infarction**. Lower NNT = more effective treatment.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Sample Size Calculation for a Cohort Study

**Problem:** Estimate the sample size per group for a cohort study comparing mean systolic BP between two groups.
- Expected mean difference: μ₁ − μ₂ = **10 mmHg**
- Common standard deviation: σ = **20 mmHg**
- Significance level: α = 0.05 → z_{α/2} = 1.96
- Power: 1 − β = 0.80 → z_β = 0.84

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$
n = \frac{2\sigma^2(z_{\alpha/2} + z_\beta)^2}{(\mu_1 - \mu_2)^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
n = \frac{2 \times (20)^2 \times (1.96 + 0.84)^2}{(10)^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify stepwise:**

$$
n = \frac{2 \times 400 \times (2.80)^2}{100}
$$

$$
n = \frac{800 \times 7.84}{100}
$$

$$
n = \frac{6272}{100} = 62.72
$$

**Step 4 — Round up:**

$$
n \approx 63 \text{ per group}
$$

**Step 5 — Add 10% for attrition:**

$$
n_{final} = 63 \times 1.10 \approx 70 \text{ per group}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Study Design Selection Guide

> [!TIP]
> **Use this matrix in exam answers to justify your choice of study design:**

| Research Question Type | Best Design | Why? | Measure of Association |
|------------------------|-------------|------|------------------------|
| "Does the *exposure* cause *disease*?" (causal) | **RCT** (gold standard) | Randomization controls confounding | RR, ARR, NNT |
| "Is exposure linked to disease in *real-world* settings?" | **Prospective Cohort** | Temporal sequence clear | RR, AR |
| "What *risk factors* are linked to a *rare* disease?" | **Case-Control** | Efficient for rare outcomes | OR |
| "What is the *prevalence* of a disease?" | **Cross-Sectional** | Single-time-point snapshot | Prevalence ratio |
| "What is the *incidence* of disease in exposed?" | **Cohort Study** | Calculates true incidence | RR, AR |
| "Can we *describe* characteristics of a population?" | **Descriptive / Cross-Sectional** | Hypothesis-generating | Proportions, means |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
