<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [KTU University Exam - Model Question 1, May 2024]

**Q1.** Define **Relative Risk (RR)** and state the type of study in which it is the appropriate measure of association.

**Model Answer:**
Relative Risk (RR) is defined as the ratio of the incidence of disease in the exposed group to the incidence in the unexposed group.

$$
RR = \frac{\text{Incidence in Exposed}}{\text{Incidence in Unexposed}} = \frac{a/(a+b)}{c/(c+d)}
$$

It is the appropriate measure of association in **Cohort Studies** and **Randomized Controlled Trials (RCTs)**, where the incidence of disease in both exposed and unexposed groups can be directly calculated.

- RR = 1 → No association
- RR > 1 → Exposure is a risk factor
- RR < 1 → Exposure is protective

---

> [KTU University Exam - Model Question 2, Dec 2023]

**Q2.** What is an **Odds Ratio (OR)**? Why is it the preferred measure in a case-control study?

**Model Answer:**
The **Odds Ratio (OR)** is the ratio of the odds of exposure among cases to the odds of exposure among controls:

$$
OR = \frac{a \times d}{b \times c}
$$

In a **case-control study**, the researcher *fixes* the number of cases and controls (e.g., 200 cases, 200 controls). Because the proportion of diseased individuals is set by design and not by nature, the true **incidence** of disease in the exposed and unexposed groups cannot be calculated. Hence, RR cannot be computed, and **OR is the only valid measure** of association. Under the **rare disease assumption**, OR is a good approximation of RR.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read This Before You Write!**
>
> 1. **DO NOT confuse RR with OR.** In exam answers, if the question says *case-control*, you MUST compute **OR**, not RR. Examiners deduct **3–4 marks** for this common mistake.
> 2. **Always interpret the measure.** A bare numerical answer (e.g., "RR = 10") without interpretation gets only **partial credit**. Always state what the number *means clinically*.
> 3. **Show all 2×2 table steps.** Neatly label cells **a, b, c, d** and show substitution — this earns **step-marking** credit.
> 4. **Mention assumptions** (rare disease assumption for OR ≈ RR, randomization for unbiased RR in RCT).
> 5. **Units and percentages** must be present in your final answer.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Cohort / RCT Focus

> [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 4]

**A. (a)** Define a **Cohort Study**. Explain the difference between a **prospective** and **retrospective** cohort study with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **Cohort Study** is an observational analytical study in which subjects are grouped based on their exposure status and followed over time (forward or backward) to assess the development of the outcome/disease. It allows direct calculation of **incidence** and **relative risk**.

| Feature | Prospective Cohort | Retrospective Cohort |
|---------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Direction of inquiry** | Exposure → Future outcome | Exposure → Past outcome (using historical records) |
| **Time frame** | Starts now, follows into the future | Starts now, looks backward |
| **Duration** | Long (often years/decades) | Short (data already exists) |
| **Cost** | High | Lower |
| **Loss to follow-up** | Significant risk | Minimal (records-based) |
| **Example** | Following smokers vs non-smokers for 20 years to track lung cancer | Using past employment records to compare asbestos exposure in workers with/without mesothelioma today |

Both designs share the same strength: they establish a **temporal (cause → effect) sequence**, allowing inference of causality. Their main weakness is confounding (controlled via matching, stratification, or multivariable regression).

---

**A. (b)** A prospective cohort study tracked 5,000 individuals exposed to a chemical and 10,000 unexposed individuals for 5 years. The number of new cancer cases was: **200 in exposed** and **100 in unexposed**. Calculate and interpret: **(i) Relative Risk (RR), (ii) Attributable Risk (AR), and (iii) Attributable Risk in the Exposed (ARₑ)**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Build the 2×2 table:**

| Group | Cancer (D+) | No Cancer (D−) | Total |
|-------|-------------|----------------|-------|
| **Exposed (E+)** | a = 200 | b = 4,800 | a+b = 5,000 |
| **Unexposed (E−)** | c = 100 | d = 9,900 | c+d = 10,000 |

**Step 2 — Compute Incidence in Exposed:**

$$
I_e = \frac{a}{a+b} = \frac{200}{5000} = 0.04 \; (4\%)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Incidence in Unexposed:**

$$
I_u = \frac{c}{c+d} = \frac{100}{10000} = 0.01 \; (1\%)
$$

**(i) Relative Risk (RR):**

$$
RR = \frac{I_e}{I_u} = \frac{0.04}{0.01} = 4
$$

**Interpretation:** The exposed group is **4 times** more likely to develop cancer than the unexposed group — a strong positive association.

**(ii) Attributable Risk (AR):**

$$
AR = I_e - I_u = 0.04 - 0.01 = 0.03 \; (3\%)
$$

**Interpretation:** There are **3 excess cancer cases per 100 exposed individuals** per 5 years that are attributable to the chemical exposure.

**(iii) Attributable Risk in the Exposed (ARₑ):**

$$
AR_e = \frac{RR - 1}{RR} \times 100\% = \frac{4 - 1}{4} \times 100\% = 75\%
$$

**Interpretation:** **75%** of the cancer cases occurring in the exposed group can be attributed to the chemical exposure; the remaining 25% would have occurred regardless of exposure.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Case-Control / OR Focus

> [KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 4]

**B. (a)** Compare and contrast **Case-Control Studies** and **Cohort Studies** under the following heads: **direction, temporal sequence, suitable disease type, measure of association, and key bias controlled.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Case-Control Study | Cohort Study |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------|
| **Direction** | Outcome → Past exposure | Exposure → Outcome (forward or backward) |
| **Temporal sequence** | Cannot establish directly | Clearly establishes (cause precedes effect) |
| **Suitable disease** | **Rare diseases** (e.g., rare cancers) | Common diseases with long latency |
| **Subject selection** | Based on **disease status** (cases vs controls) | Based on **exposure status** (exposed vs unexposed) |
| **Measure of association** | **Odds Ratio (OR)** | **Relative Risk (RR), Attributable Risk (AR)** |
| **Key bias** | **Recall bias** (cases remember exposure better) | **Loss to follow-up bias** |
| **Time required** | Short (retrospective look) | Long (especially prospective) |
| **Cost** | Low | High |
| **Can compute incidence?** | **No** | **Yes** |
| **Causal inference** | Weak (hypothesis-generating) | Strong (supports causality) |

---

**B. (b)** A case-control study was conducted to assess the association between **oral contraceptive (OC) use** and **breast cancer**. 300 breast cancer cases and 600 controls were enrolled. Among cases, 90 had used OCs; among controls, 120 had used OCs. Compute and interpret the **Odds Ratio (OR)**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Build the 2×2 table:**

| Group | OC Use (E+) | No OC Use (E−) | Total |
|-------|-------------|----------------|-------|
| **Cases (D+)** | a = 90 | b = 210 | a+b = 300 |
| **Controls (D−)** | c = 120 | d = 480 | c+d = 600 |

*(Note: b = 300 − 90 = 210 ; d = 600 − 120 = 480)*

**Step 2 — Apply the Odds Ratio formula:**

$$
OR = \frac{a \cdot d}{b \cdot c}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$
OR = \frac{90 \times 480}{210 \times 120}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator:**

$$
90 \times 480 = 43{,}200
$$

**Step 5 — Compute denominator:**

$$
210 \times 120 = 25{,}200
$$

**Step 6 — Final division:**

$$
OR = \frac{43{,}200}{25{,}200} = 1.714
$$

**Interpretation:** The **odds of OC use** among breast cancer cases are approximately **1.71 times** the odds among controls. This suggests a **moderate positive association** between oral contraceptive use and breast cancer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rare disease assumption check:** Breast cancer is moderately common, so the OR (1.71) **slightly overestimates** the true relative risk. The actual RR is closer to 1 but still elevated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Study Design**

- ✅ **Study designs** are classified into **Observational** (Case-Control, Cohort, Cross-Sectional) and **Experimental** (RCT).
- ✅ The **2×2 contingency table** is the universal framework — with cells **a, b, c, d**.
- ✅ **Relative Risk (RR)** = I_e / I_u → used in **Cohort Studies and RCTs**.
- ✅ **Odds Ratio (OR)** = (a × d) / (b × c) → used in **Case-Control Studies**.
- ✅ **OR ≈ RR** only under the **rare disease assumption**.
- ✅ **Attributable Risk (AR)** = I_e − I_u → the *excess* risk in exposed.
- ✅ **AR in Exposed (ARₑ)** = ((RR − 1) / RR) × 100% → % of disease in exposed due to exposure.
- ✅ **Population AR (PAR)** uses I_pop instead of I_e.
- ✅ **NNT** = 1 / ARR → lower NNT = more effective treatment.
- ✅ **RCT is the gold standard** — it controls confounding via **randomization** and information bias via **blinding**.
- ✅ **Bias types to memorize**: Selection bias, Information (Recall/Interviewer) bias, Confounding.
- ✅ **Confounding** is controlled by **randomization, matching, stratification, and multivariable regression**.
- ✅ **Sample size formula** for comparing two means: n = 2σ²(z_{α/2} + z_β)² / (μ₁ − μ₂)².
- ✅ **Degrees of freedom for 2×2 chi-square** = **(2−1) × (2−1) = 1** — memorize this!
- ✅ **Standard significance level α = 0.05**, **standard power 1 − β = 0.80** for KTU problems.
- ✅ **Always interpret RR/OR**: = 1 (no effect), > 1 (harmful), < 1 (protective).
- ✅ **Evidence hierarchy** (top to bottom): Meta-analysis → RCT → Cohort → Case-Control → Cross-Sectional → Case reports.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** "**Design first, data later.**" Choosing the right study design is the single most important decision a researcher makes — get it right, and statistics will follow.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measure of Effect for Categorical Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT416 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Measure of Effect for Categorical Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In biostatistics, **Measures of Effect** are quantitative indices that capture the **strength, direction, and magnitude** of the association between an exposure (risk factor / treatment) and a binary health outcome (disease present / absent). When the outcome variable is **categorical** (e.g., Diseased vs. Non-diseased, Cured vs. Not cured), the data are summarized in a **2 × 2 contingency table**, and effect measures are computed directly from the cell frequencies.

The two broad families are:

1. **Relative Measures** (dimensionless ratios) — *Relative Risk (RR)*, *Odds Ratio (OR)*, *Relative Risk Reduction (RRR)*.
2. **Absolute Measures** (carry units of risk) — *Risk Difference (RD) / Attributable Risk (AR)*, *Number Needed to Treat (NNT)*, *Population Attributable Risk (PAR)*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For PEEBT416 – Module 4, the examiner expects students to *derive*, *interpret*, and *select* the correct measure depending on the **study design** — RR for cohort / RCT, OR for case-control studies.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you walk into a hospital lobby. Two hundred people are sitting in the waiting area:
- 100 of them **smoke** (exposed group).
- 100 of them **do not smoke** (unexposed group).

You count the lung-cancer patients in each group:
- Among smokers: 30 patients have lung cancer.
- Among non-smokers: 6 patients have lung cancer.

Now, the questions you naturally ask are:

- *"How many times more likely is a smoker to get cancer compared to a non-smoker?"* → This is **Relative Risk**.
- *"If a smoker has cancer, what are the odds of being a smoker?"* → This is **Odds Ratio**.
- *"If we eliminate smoking, how many cases can we prevent in the exposed?"* → This is **Attributable Risk**.
- *"How many people must quit smoking to prevent one cancer case?"* → This is **Number Needed to Treat (NNT)**.

The numbers you compute are the **Measures of Effect for Categorical Data**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction to Remember**
> - **Risk** = Probability of an event occurring (ranges from 0 to 1).
> - **Odds** = Ratio of the probability of an event occurring to the probability of it *not* occurring (ranges from 0 to ∞).
> - **Risk** is intuitive; **Odds** is mathematically more convenient for case-control studies because the sampling is done on outcome, not exposure.

## 1.3 The 2 × 2 Contingency Table — Foundation of All Measures

| | Disease (D⁺) | Disease (D⁻) | Total |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Exposed (E⁺)** | a | b | a + b |
| **Unexposed (E⁻)** | c | d | c + d |
| **Total** | a + c | b + d | N |

Where:
- **a** = Exposed & Diseased (true positives in epidemiology)
- **b** = Exposed & Non-diseased
- **c** = Unexposed & Diseased
- **d** = Unexposed & Non-diseased
- **N = a + b + c + d** (total sample size)

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Board Exams:** Think **"a = at-risk diseased"**, **"b = benign exposed"**, **"c = control diseased"**, **"d = definitely disease-free"**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Risk-Based Measures (For Cohort Studies & RCTs)

### 2.1.1 Risk in the Exposed Group (R₁)
The probability (incidence proportion) of disease among those who were exposed.

$$R_1 = \frac{a}{a+b}$$

### 2.1.2 Risk in the Unexposed Group (R₀)
The probability of disease among those who were not exposed.

$$R_0 = \frac{c}{c+d}$$

### 2.1.3 Relative Risk (RR) — The "How-Many-Times-More-Likely" Index
RR quantifies the ratio of the risk in the exposed to the risk in the unexposed.

$$RR = \frac{R_1}{R_0} = \frac{a/(a+b)}{c/(c+d)}$$

**Interpretation:**
- **RR = 1** → No association (null value).
- **RR > 1** → Exposure is a *risk factor* (harmful).
- **RR < 1** → Exposure is *protective* (beneficial, e.g., vaccine efficacy).

### 2.1.4 Attributable Risk (AR) / Risk Difference (RD)
The absolute difference in incidence rates between exposed and unexposed groups.

$$AR = RD = R_1 - R_0 = \frac{a}{a+b} - \frac{c}{c+d}$$

**Interpretation:** The *excess risk* in the exposed group that can be attributed to the exposure.

### 2.1.5 Attributable Risk Percent (AR%)
The proportion of disease in the exposed group that is *attributable* to the exposure.

$$AR\% = \frac{R_1 - R_0}{R_1} \times 100 = \frac{RR - 1}{RR} \times 100$$

### 2.1.6 Relative Risk Reduction (RRR) — For Treatment Studies
The proportional reduction in risk produced by a treatment compared to a control.

$$RRR = \frac{R_0 - R_1}{R_0} \times 100 = (1 - RR) \times 100$$

### 2.1.7 Absolute Risk Reduction (ARR)
The absolute arithmetic difference in risk between control and treatment groups.

$$ARR = R_0 - R_1$$

### 2.1.8 Number Needed to Treat (NNT)
The number of patients that must be treated to prevent **one** additional adverse event.

$$NNT = \frac{1}{ARR} = \frac{1}{\vert R_0 - R_1 \vert}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always **round NNT upward** to the next whole number (since you cannot treat a fraction of a patient). This is a frequent KTU valuation step.

### 2.1.9 Population Attributable Risk (PAR)
The reduction in incidence that would occur in the **whole population** if the exposure were eliminated.

$$PAR = R_t - R_0$$

Where **Rₜ = (a + c) / N** is the total population risk.

$$PAR\% = \frac{R_t - R_0}{R_t} \times 100$$

## 2.2 Odds-Based Measures (For Case-Control Studies)

### 2.2.1 Odds of Disease in the Exposed (O₁)

$$O_1 = \frac{a}{b}$$

### 2.2.2 Odds of Disease in the Unexposed (O₀)

$$O_0 = \frac{c}{d}$$

### 2.2.3 Odds Ratio (OR)
The cross-product ratio of the odds of exposure among diseased vs. non-diseased.

$$OR = \frac{O_1}{O_0} = \frac{a/b}{c/d} = \frac{a \cdot d}{b \cdot c}$$

**Interpretation:**
- **OR = 1** → No association.
- **OR > 1** → Exposure increases odds of disease.
- **OR < 1** → Exposure decreases odds of disease (protective).
- **OR = RR** when the disease is *rare* (Rare Disease Assumption: prevalence < 10%).

### 2.2.4 Log-Odds Ratio Approximation
For large samples, **ln(OR)** is approximately normally distributed with:

$$SE[\ln(OR)] = \sqrt{\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b} + \frac{1}{c} + \frac{1}{d}}$$

## 2.3 Standard Error & Confidence Intervals

| Measure | Standard Error (SE) | 95% Confidence Interval (CI) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **ln(RR)** | $\sqrt{\dfrac{b}{a(a+b)} + \dfrac{d}{c(c+d)}}$ | $e^{\ln(RR) \pm 1.96 \cdot SE[\ln(RR)]}$ |
| **ln(OR)** | $\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{a} + \dfrac{1}{b} + \dfrac{1}{c} + \dfrac{1}{d}}$ | $e^{\ln(OR) \pm 1.96 \cdot SE[\ln(OR)]}$ |
| **RD** | $\sqrt{\dfrac{R_1(1-R_1)}{a+b} + \dfrac{R_0(1-R_0)}{c+d}}$ | $RD \pm 1.96 \cdot SE[RD]$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Statistical Significance Rule:** If the **95% CI excludes the null value** (1 for RR/OR, 0 for RD), the association is statistically significant at α = 0.05.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Measure | Formula | Study Design | Null Value |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Risk in Exposed | $R_1 = a/(a+b)$ | Cohort / RCT | — |
| 2 | Risk in Unexposed | $R_0 = c/(c+d)$ | Cohort / RCT | — |
| 3 | Relative Risk (RR) | $RR = R_1/R_0$ | Cohort / RCT | 1 |
| 4 | Risk Difference (RD) | $RD = R_1 - R_0$ | Cohort / RCT | 0 |
| 5 | Attributable Risk % | $(RR-1)/RR \times 100$ | Cohort / RCT | 0% |
| 6 | RRR (Vaccine) | $(1-RR)\times 100$ | RCT | 0% |
| 7 | NNT | $1/\vert RD \vert$ | RCT | — |
| 8 | PAR % | $(R_t - R_0)/R_t \times 100$ | Cross-sectional | 0% |
| 9 | Odds in Exposed | $O_1 = a/b$ | Any | — |
| 10 | Odds in Unexposed | $O_0 = c/d$ | Any | — |
| 11 | Odds Ratio (OR) | $OR = ad/bc$ | Case-control | 1 |
| 12 | SE[ln(OR)] | $\sqrt{1/a+1/b+1/c+1/d}$ | Case-control | — |
| 13 | SE[ln(RR)] | $\sqrt{b/[a(a+b)]+d/[c(c+d)]}$ | Cohort | — |
| 14 | 95% CI for OR | $e^{\ln(OR)\pm 1.96 \cdot SE}$ | Case-control | excludes 1? |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always state the **direction** (harmful/protective) and the **magnitude** (e.g., "smokers have 5 times the risk") in your final answer. KTU examiners award 1 mark each for **calculation, direction, and interpretation**.

## 2.5 Why These Measures Matter in Engineering & Public Health

In **clinical trials** of a new drug, RR and NNT directly guide regulatory approval. In **epidemiology**, OR is the workhorse for case-control studies (e.g., linking smoking to lung cancer). In **health economics**, NNT and PAR% inform cost-effectiveness analyses and policy decisions. Even in **engineering risk analysis** (e.g., failure rates of components), the same RR/OR framework is applied to binary outcome data.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Setup of the Master Worked Example

A prospective cohort study followed **1,000 smokers** and **1,000 non-smokers** for 10 years to assess the incidence of lung cancer. Results:

| | Lung Cancer (D⁺) | No Lung Cancer (D⁻) | Total |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Smokers (E⁺)** | a = 200 | b = 800 | 1000 |
| **Non-Smokers (E⁻)** | c = 40 | d = 960 | 1000 |
| **Total** | 240 | 1760 | 2000 |

We will now compute **every** measure of effect exhaustively.

## 3.2 Calculation of Risk in Each Group

**Risk in Smokers (R₁):**
The number of diseased among the exposed is **a = 200**, total exposed = **a + b = 1000**.

$$R_1 = \frac{a}{a+b} = \frac{200}{1000} = 0.20$$

**Risk in Non-Smokers (R₀):**
The number of diseased among the unexposed is **c = 40**, total unexposed = **c + d = 1000**.

$$R_0 = \frac{c}{c+d} = \frac{40}{1000} = 0.04$$

**Interpretation:** 20% of smokers developed lung cancer vs. 4% of non-smokers.

## 3.3 Relative Risk (RR) — Full Derivation

$$RR = \frac{R_1}{R_0} = \frac{0.20}{0.04} = 5.00$$

**Step-by-step algebra:**

$$\begin{aligned}
RR &= \frac{a/(a+b)}{c/(c+d)} \\
   &= \frac{a}{a+b} \times \frac{c+d}{c} \\
   &= \frac{200 \times 1000}{1000 \times 40} \\
   &= \frac{200000}{40000} \\
   &= 5.00
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** Smokers are **5 times more likely** to develop lung cancer compared to non-smokers. Since RR > 1, smoking is a *risk factor*.

## 3.4 Attributable Risk (AR) / Risk Difference (RD)

$$AR = R_1 - R_0 = 0.20 - 0.04 = 0.16 \; \text{(or 16 cases per 100 persons)}$$

**Interpretation:** Of every 100 smokers, **16 extra lung cancer cases** are attributable to smoking.

## 3.5 Attributable Risk Percent (AR%)

$$AR\% = \frac{R_1 - R_0}{R_1} \times 100 = \frac{0.16}{0.20} \times 100 = 80\%$$

**Interpretation:** **80%** of the lung cancer cases occurring in smokers can be attributed to smoking itself.

## 3.6 Relative Risk Reduction (RRR) — *Hypothetical Vaccine Interpretation*

If we treat this exposure as a *harmful factor* and imagine a vaccine that eliminates the risk in 80% of cases, the RRR would equal AR%. Here:

$$RRR = (1 - RR) \times 100 = (1 - 5) \times 100$$

Wait — for RRR we always take **control − treatment**, so conceptually the "control" is the lower-risk group. Rewriting with the protective frame:

$$RRR = (1 - RR_{\text{treatment}}) \times 100$$

If we flip the question and ask: *By what percentage does smoking increase risk?*

$$RRR_{\text{smoking}} = (RR - 1) \times 100 = (5 - 1) \times 100 = 400\%$$

**Interpretation:** Smoking increases the risk of lung cancer by **400%** over the baseline non-smoker risk.

## 3.7 Number Needed to Treat (NNT) — Hypothetical Anti-Smoking Therapy

Suppose an anti-smoking intervention reduces a smoker's risk from 0.20 to 0.04 (i.e., makes them statistically equivalent to a non-smoker). Then:

$$ARR = R_{\text{untreated}} - R_{\text{treated}} = 0.20 - 0.04 = 0.16$$

$$NNT = \frac{1}{ARR} = \frac{1}{0.16} = 6.25$$

**Rounding up** (KTU-required convention):

$$NNT = 7 \text{ patients}$$

**Interpretation:** We must treat **7 smokers** with the intervention to prevent **one** case of lung cancer.

## 3.8 Population Attributable Risk (PAR)

Total population risk:

$$R_t = \frac{a+c}{N} = \frac{200+40}{2000} = \frac{240}{2000} = 0.12$$

Population Attributable Risk:

$$PAR = R_t - R_0 = 0.12 - 0.04 = 0.08$$

$$PAR\% = \frac{PAR}{R_t} \times 100 = \frac{0.08}{0.12} \times 100 = 66.67\%$$

**Interpretation:** If smoking were eliminated from the entire population, lung cancer incidence would drop by **66.67%**.

## 3.9 Odds Ratio (OR) — Full Derivation

$$\begin{aligned}
O_1 &= \frac{a}{b} = \frac{200}{800} = 0.25 \\
O_0 &= \frac{c}{d} = \frac{40}{960} = 0.0417 \\
OR  &= \frac{O_1}{O_0} = \frac{0.25}{0.0417} = 6.00
\end{aligned}$$

**Cross-product verification:**

$$OR = \frac{a \cdot d}{b \cdot c} = \frac{200 \times 960}{800 \times 40} = \frac{192000}{32000} = 6.00$$

**Interpretation:** The odds of lung cancer among smokers are **6 times** the odds among non-smokers. Note that OR (6.00) > RR (5.00) because lung cancer is not rare in this sample (12% prevalence) — this violates the *rare disease assumption*.

## 3.10 95% Confidence Interval for the Odds Ratio

**Step 1 — Compute ln(OR):**

$$\ln(6.00) = 1.7918$$

**Step 2 — Compute Standard Error of ln(OR):**

$$\begin{aligned}
SE[\ln(OR)] &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b} + \frac{1}{c} + \frac{1}{d}} \\
            &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{200} + \frac{1}{800} + \frac{1}{40} + \frac{1}{960}} \\
            &= \sqrt{0.00500 + 0.00125 + 0.02500 + 0.00104} \\
            &= \sqrt{0.03229} \\
            &= 0.1797
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute 95% CI:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Lower limit of ln(OR)} &= 1.7918 - (1.96 \times 0.1797) \\
                             &= 1.7918 - 0.3522 \\
                             &= 1.4396
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Upper limit of ln(OR)} &= 1.7918 + (1.96 \times 0.1797) \\
                             &= 1.7918 + 0.3522 \\
                             &= 2.1440
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate to get CI for OR:**

$$OR_{lower} = e^{1.4396} = 4.22$$

$$OR_{upper} = e^{2.1440} = 8.53$$

**Final 95% CI for OR:** **(4.22, 8.53)**

**Interpretation:** Since the interval **excludes 1**, the association between smoking and lung cancer is **statistically significant** at α = 0.05.

## 3.11 Statistical Test — Z-test for OR

$$Z = \frac{\ln(OR)}{SE[\ln(OR)]} = \frac{1.7918}{0.1797} = 9.97$$

Since |Z| = 9.97 > 1.96, we **reject H₀** (no association).

## 3.12 Analytical Matrix — Master Summary Table

| Measure | Symbol | Computed Value | Interpretation |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Risk in Exposed | R₁ | 0.20 | 20% of smokers develop cancer |
| Risk in Unexposed | R₀ | 0.04 | 4% of non-smokers develop cancer |
| Relative Risk | RR | **5.00** | 5× more likely in smokers |
| Risk Difference | RD | 0.16 | 16 extra cases per 100 smokers |
| Attributable Risk % | AR% | 80% | 80% of cases in smokers due to smoking |
| NNT (intervention) | NNT | 7 | Treat 7 to prevent 1 case |
| Population Attributable Risk % | PAR% | 66.67% | Eliminating smoking cuts incidence by 66.67% |
| Odds Ratio | OR | **6.00** | Odds 6× higher in smokers |
| 95% CI for OR | — | (4.22, 8.53) | Excludes 1 → significant |
| Z-statistic (OR) | Z | 9.97 | Highly significant |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Study-Design-to-Effect-Measure Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Binary Outcome Data Collected"] --> B{"Sampling Strategy?"}
    B -- "Sampling by EXPOSURE" --> C["Cohort Study or RCT"]
    B -- "Sampling by OUTCOME" --> D["Case-Control Study"]
    B -- "Sampling by POPULATION" --> E["Cross-Sectional Study"]

    C --> F["Compute R1 and R0 from cell frequencies"]
    F --> G["Primary Measure: Relative Risk RR"]
    F --> H["Secondary: Risk Difference RD"]
    F --> I["Secondary: RRR and NNT"]

    D --> J["Odds cannot be converted to Risk without prevalence"]
    J --> K["Primary Measure: Odds Ratio OR"]
    K --> L["Use cross product formula ad divided by bc"]
    L --> M["Compute 95 percent CI using log OR method"]

    E --> N["Compute prevalence in exposed vs unexposed"]
    N --> O["Primary Measure: Prevalence Ratio or PAR percent"]

    G --> P["Clinical Decision: Drug Approval or Risk Factor Identification"]
    I --> P
    K --> Q["Etiological Inference: Cause of Disease"]
    O --> R["Public Health Policy: Population-Level Intervention"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Effect-Measure Computation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Raw Categorical Data 2x2 Table"] --> B["Step 1: Compute Cell Margins a plus b and c plus d"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Compute Risks R1 and R0"]
    C --> D{"Study Type?"}
    D -- "Cohort or RCT" --> E["Step 3a: Compute RR and RD"]
    D -- "Case Control" --> F["Step 3b: Compute OR using ad over bc"]

    E --> G["Step 4a: Derive RRR and NNT from RR and RD"]
    F --> H["Step 4b: Log transform OR for CI calculation"]
    H --> I["Step 5b: Exponentiate CI bounds"]
    G --> J["Step 5a: Apply log method for RR CI"]
    I --> K["Step 6: Statistical Hypothesis Test Z test"]
    J --> K
    K --> L["Step 7: Interpret Significance and Direction"]
    L --> M["Final Report: Effect Size plus 95 percent CI plus P value"]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Which Measure Should I Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Choose Effect Measure for Categorical Data"] --> Q1{"Is disease incidence available?"}
    Q1 -- "Yes: Cohort or RCT" --> RR["Use Relative Risk RR"]
    Q1 -- "No: Case-Control" --> OR["Use Odds Ratio OR"]
    Q1 -- "Prevalence only" --> PR["Use Prevalence Ratio"]

    RR --> RQ1{"Need absolute impact?"}
    RQ1 -- "Yes" --> RD["Compute RD and NNT"]
    RQ1 -- "No" --> RRR["Report RRR for treatment efficacy"]

    OR --> OQ1{"Disease is rare under 10 percent?"}
    OQ1 -- "Yes" --> Approx["OR approximates RR"]
    OQ1 -- "No" --> Direct["Report OR directly, do not approximate"]

    RD --> Final["Report RD with 95 percent CI"]
    RRR --> Final
    Approx --> Final
    Direct --> Final
    PR --> Final
```
