# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Relative Risk (RR) and Odds Ratio (OR). Under what condition does OR approximate RR?**

**Model Answer:**

**Relative Risk (RR):** It is the ratio of the incidence (risk) of a disease in the exposed group to the incidence in the unexposed group.

$$RR = \frac{a/(a+b)}{c/(c+d)}$$

**Odds Ratio (OR):** It is the ratio of the odds of exposure among the diseased to the odds of exposure among the non-diseased (equivalently, ratio of odds of disease in exposed vs. unexposed).

$$OR = \frac{a \cdot d}{b \cdot c}$$

**Condition for approximation:** When the disease is **rare** in the population (prevalence < 10%), the Odds Ratio provides a valid estimate of the Relative Risk because the denominators **(a + b) ≈ b** and **(c + d) ≈ d**, making the OR collapse mathematically to the RR. **(3 marks: 1 mark each for definitions + 1 mark for condition)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is Number Needed to Treat (NNT)? Why is it rounded up to the next whole number?**

**Model Answer:**

**NNT** is defined as the reciprocal of the Absolute Risk Reduction (ARR):

$$NNT = \frac{1}{ARR} = \frac{1}{\vert R_{\text{control}} - R_{\text{treatment}} \vert}$$

It represents the number of patients that a clinician must treat with the intervention to prevent **one** additional adverse event.

**Rounding up** is necessary because:
1. You cannot treat a fraction of a patient — the smallest treatable unit is one whole individual.
2. Rounding down would *underestimate* the effort required, potentially causing statistical under-coverage of the true population benefit.
3. Convention adopted by the **Cochrane Collaboration** and CONSORT reporting guidelines.

**(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for interpretation, 1 for justification of rounding)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — READ BEFORE ATTEMPTING!**
> - **Forgetting the 2×2 table setup** before plugging into formulas → loses 2 marks.
> - **Confusing RR and OR interpretation** (e.g., stating "5× the odds" when RR is computed) → loses 1–2 marks.
> - **Not mentioning the study design** for which the measure is appropriate → loses 1 mark.
> - **Forgetting to round NNT up** to the next integer → loses 1 mark.
> - **Not writing units** (e.g., per 100, per 1000) for risk measures → loses 1 mark.
> - **Failing to interpret the 95% CI** (whether it excludes the null value) → loses 1 mark.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**A case-control study was conducted to assess the association between oral contraceptive (OC) use and thromboembolism. The 2×2 table is as follows:**

| | Cases (Thromboembolism +) | Controls (−) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **OC Users** | 50 | 30 |
| **OC Non-Users** | 30 | 90 |

**(a)** Compute the Odds Ratio (OR) and its 95% confidence interval. Interpret the result. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the Relative Risk (RR) and explain why it cannot be directly estimated from a case-control study. What assumption would make OR ≈ RR? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify cells:**

$$a = 50, \; b = 30, \; c = 30, \; d = 90$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Odds Ratio:**

$$OR = \frac{a \cdot d}{b \cdot c} = \frac{50 \times 90}{30 \times 30} = \frac{4500}{900} = 5.00$$

**Step 3 — Compute SE[ln(OR)]:**

$$\begin{aligned}
SE[\ln(OR)] &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{50} + \frac{1}{30} + \frac{1}{30} + \frac{1}{90}} \\
            &= \sqrt{0.02000 + 0.03333 + 0.03333 + 0.01111} \\
            &= \sqrt{0.09778} \\
            &= 0.3127
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — 95% CI for ln(OR):**

$$\ln(5.00) = 1.6094$$

$$1.6094 \pm (1.96 \times 0.3127) = 1.6094 \pm 0.6129$$

$$= (0.9965, \; 2.2223)$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate:**

$$CI = (e^{0.9965}, \; e^{2.2223}) = (2.71, \; 9.23)$$

**Final 95% CI for OR: (2.71, 9.23)**

**Interpretation:** OC users have **5 times the odds** of thromboembolism compared to non-users. Since the 95% CI (2.71, 9.23) **excludes 1**, the association is **statistically significant (p < 0.05)**. **(7 marks: 2 for OR, 2 for SE & CI derivation, 1 for CI bounds, 2 for interpretation)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Why RR cannot be directly computed:**
In a case-control study, the investigator **fixes the number of cases and controls** (50 + 30 and 30 + 90). The proportion of OC users in the study is therefore **determined by design**, not by the underlying population risk. Hence, R₁ = a/(a+b) = 50/80 = 0.625 is **not a true incidence** — it is just the proportion of OC users among cases. Dividing this by 30/120 = 0.25 would yield a meaningless "risk ratio."

**The Rare Disease Assumption:**
If thromboembolism were **rare** in the source population (prevalence < 10%), then:

$$a + b \approx b \quad \text{and} \quad c + d \approx d$$

Substituting:

$$OR = \frac{a \cdot d}{b \cdot c} \approx \frac{a/(a+b)}{c/(c+d)} = RR$$

So **OR ≈ RR only when the disease is rare**. **(7 marks: 3 for explaining why, 2 for derivation, 2 for stating the assumption)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice**

**A cohort study followed 5,000 individuals for 5 years to study the effect of hypertension on stroke incidence:**

| | Stroke (+) | No Stroke (−) | Total |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Hypertensive** | 200 | 1800 | 2000 |
| **Normotensive** | 60 | 2940 | 3000 |
| **Total** | 260 | 4740 | 5000 |

**(a)** Calculate the Relative Risk (RR), Attributable Risk (AR), and Attributable Risk Percent (AR%). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the Number Needed to Harm (NNH) and discuss the public health significance of these findings. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute Risks:**

$$R_1 = \frac{200}{2000} = 0.10 \;(10\%)$$

$$R_0 = \frac{60}{3000} = 0.02 \;(2\%)$$

**Step 2 — Compute Relative Risk:**

$$RR = \frac{0.10}{0.02} = 5.00$$

**Interpretation:** Hypertensive individuals are **5 times more likely** to suffer a stroke than normotensive individuals. Since RR > 1, hypertension is a *risk factor*.

**Step 3 — Compute Attributable Risk:**

$$AR = R_1 - R_0 = 0.10 - 0.02 = 0.08 \; \text{(8 cases per 100 hypertensives per 5 years)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Attributable Risk Percent:**

$$AR\% = \frac{R_1 - R_0}{R_1} \times 100 = \frac{0.08}{0.10} \times 100 = 80\%$$

**Interpretation:** **80% of strokes** occurring in hypertensive individuals can be attributed to hypertension. **(7 marks: 2 for risks, 2 for RR, 1.5 for AR, 1.5 for AR%)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute Number Needed to Harm (NNH):**
NNH is the analogue of NNT used for harmful exposures. It is the number of exposed individuals one would need to observe to see one excess case of the disease caused by the exposure.

$$NNH = \frac{1}{AR} = \frac{1}{0.08} = 12.5$$

**Rounded up:**

$$NNH = 13 \text{ individuals}$$

**Interpretation:** For every **13 hypertensive individuals** followed for 5 years, **1 additional stroke** is attributable to hypertension (beyond the baseline rate seen in normotensives).

**Public Health Significance:**

1. **Magnitude of burden:** With AR = 0.08, hypertension is contributing a *clinically meaningful* excess risk — 8 additional strokes per 100 hypertensives over 5 years.
2. **Prevention potential:** AR% = 80% indicates that if hypertension were *fully controlled* in the exposed group, up to 4 out of every 5 strokes in hypertensives could be *prevented*.
3. **Population impact (PAR%):**

$$R_t = \frac{260}{5000} = 0.052$$

$$PAR = R_t - R_0 = 0.052 - 0.02 = 0.032$$

$$PAR\% = \frac{0.032}{0.052} \times 100 = 61.54\%$$

4. **Policy implication:** Eliminating hypertension in the population could reduce stroke incidence by ~62% — justifying large-scale screening and BP-control programs. **(7 marks: 2 for NNH computation, 1 for rounding, 2 for interpretation, 2 for public health discussion)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Measure of Effect for Categorical Data**

- **2×2 Table:** Always draw a, b, c, d cells with exposed/unexposed vs. diseased/non-diseased. (No marks lost on table setup.)
- **RR** is computable **only** when risks can be directly estimated → **Cohort studies & RCTs**.
- **OR** is computable for **all** study designs but is the *primary* measure for **case-control studies**.
- **RR = 1, OR = 1, RD = 0** are the **null values**; the CI must exclude these to be significant.
- **Rare Disease Assumption:** When disease prevalence < 10%, **OR ≈ RR** because (a + b) ≈ b and (c + d) ≈ d.
- **NNT formula:** $1/\vert RD \vert$ — always **round UP** (KTU convention).
- **AR vs PAR:** AR is for the *exposed group*; PAR is for the *whole population*.
- **CI for OR / RR** is computed on the **log scale** (because ln(OR) is more normally distributed than OR itself) and then **exponentiated**.
- **SE[ln(OR)]** uses 1/a + 1/b + 1/c + 1/d (no factor of 4 or N in the denominator).
- **NNT** < 5 is considered a *highly effective* treatment; NNT > 50 is *weakly effective*.
- **PAR%** combines the *strength of association* (RR) and the *prevalence of exposure* in the population.
- **Always report:** (i) point estimate, (ii) 95% CI, (iii) direction, (iv) interpretation in plain English.
- **Z-test:** $Z = \ln(OR) / SE[\ln(OR)]$; reject H₀ if |Z| > 1.96 (two-tailed, α = 0.05).
- **Common KTU trap:** Students often confuse **NNT** (for beneficial treatments) with **NNH** (for harmful exposures) — they are mathematically the same (1/|RD|) but interpreted differently.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For a 14-mark question, allocate ~3 marks for **table setup + formula writing**, ~6 marks for **computation**, and ~5 marks for **interpretation & CI analysis**. Always close with a one-line clinical conclusion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Confounding and Standardization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT416 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Confounding and Standardization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Confounding?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> **Confounding** is a systematic distortion of the estimated effect of an exposure on an outcome caused by the presence of a third variable (the *confounder*) that is associated with both the exposure and the outcome, but is **not** on the causal pathway between them.

A confounding variable mixes its effect with the exposure of interest, leading to a biased (either inflated or deflated) measure of association. This bias is known as **confounding bias** or **confounding effect**.

### 🎯 Real-World Analogy: "The Coffee-Smoking Paradox"

Imagine a study reports that **coffee drinking causes lung cancer**. Sounds alarming, right? But lurking in the data is a hidden third character — **smoking**.

- Smokers tend to drink **more coffee** (exposure-confounder link)
- Smoking **causes lung cancer** (confounder-outcome link)
- Coffee itself may have **no causal role** in lung cancer

The smoking variable is a **confounder** because it distorts the true relationship between coffee (exposure) and lung cancer (outcome). If we ignore smoking, we wrongly blame coffee for the cancer.

### 1.2 Criteria for a Confounding Variable (The "ABC" Rule)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a variable **Z** to qualify as a confounder of the **X → Y** relationship, **ALL THREE** conditions must be satisfied simultaneously:

| Criterion | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **A**ssociation with Exposure | The potential confounder Z must be associated with the exposure X (statistically, P(X \| Z) ≠ P(X)). |
| **B**ackground Risk Factor | Z must be an independent risk factor for the outcome Y (i.e., Z affects Y even when X is absent). |
| **C**ausal Path Exclusion | Z must **NOT** lie on the causal chain from X to Y. Z is an *extraneous* variable, not a mediator. |

### 1.3 What is Standardization?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Standardization** is a set of statistical techniques used to remove the distorting effects of confounding (especially by age) when comparing summary rates (mortality, morbidity, incidence) between two or more populations that have different underlying distributions of the confounding variable.

In simple words: it is a **fair-comparison tool** that allows two populations to be compared "as if" they had the same age structure.

### 🎯 Real-World Analogy: "The Apples-to-Apples Comparison"

Two factories report the following accident rates:
- **Factory A** (mostly young workers): 5 accidents per 1000
- **Factory B** (mostly older workers): 12 accidents per 1000

At first glance, Factory B looks far more dangerous. But what if the *standardized* rate (after adjusting for the younger workforce in A) reveals that B is actually safer? **Standardization** strips away the age effect so the true workplace safety comparison emerges — a true "apples-to-apples" view.

### 1.4 The Two Pillars of Standardization

| Method | When to Use | Core Idea |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Direct Standardization** | When age-specific rates in the study population are **stable** (large sample sizes, no sparse cells). | Apply *study* age-specific rates onto a *standard* population structure. |
| **Indirect Standardization** | When age-specific rates in the study population are **unstable** (small samples, rare events). | Apply *standard* age-specific rates onto the *study* population to compute **SMR**. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Confounding Situation

The confounder creates a **"backdoor path"** in the causal graph that produces spurious statistical association. Let us dissect the three logical stages:

- **Step 1 — Identify Exposure (X):** The factor whose effect we wish to estimate (e.g., a drug, smoking, physical activity).
- **Step 2 — Identify Outcome (Y):** The disease or event under investigation (e.g., lung cancer, mortality).
- **Step 3 — Identify Confounder (Z):** A variable that satisfies the **A-B-C** criteria. Z is sometimes called a *lurking variable* or *common cause*.

### 2.2 Mechanisms of Confounding Bias

Confounding can either **mask** a real effect (null bias) or **fabricate** a false effect (positive confounding):

- **Positive Confounding:** The crude effect is exaggerated away from the null (e.g., observed RR = 4.0, true RR = 2.0).
- **Negative Confounding:** The crude effect is attenuated toward the null (e.g., observed RR = 1.2, true RR = 3.0).

## 2.3 Direct Standardization — Mathematical Framework

Direct standardization produces the **Standardized Death Rate (SDR)** or **Age-Adjusted Rate (AAR)**.

$$SDR = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot r_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i}$$

Where the parameters are defined as:

- $k$ = total number of age strata (e.g., 18 five-year age groups)
- $w_i$ = number of persons in the **standard population** within stratum $i$
- $r_i$ = age-specific rate in the **study population** for stratum $i$
- $r_i = \dfrac{d_i}{n_i}$, with $d_i$ = deaths and $n_i$ = population in stratum $i$

## 2.4 Indirect Standardization — The SMR Framework

Indirect standardization yields the **Standardized Mortality Ratio (SMR)**, a multiplicative index.

$$SMR = \frac{O}{E} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} d_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \cdot R_i}$$

Where the parameters are defined as:

- $O$ = total **observed** cases (deaths) in the study population
- $E$ = total **expected** cases (deaths) if the study population had the standard rates
- $d_i$ = observed cases in stratum $i$ of the study population
- $n_i$ = population in stratum $i$ of the study population
- $R_i$ = age-specific rate from the **standard population** in stratum $i$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of SMR:**
> - $SMR = 1.0 \Rightarrow$ Observed = Expected (no excess risk)
> - $SMR > 1.0 \Rightarrow$ Observed > Expected (elevated risk, often expressed as percentage: $SMR \times 100\%$)
> - $SMR < 1.0 \Rightarrow$ Observed < Expected (protective or healthier population)

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula | Key Variables | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Age-specific rate | $r_i = \dfrac{d_i}{n_i}$ | $d_i$ = events, $n_i$ = population | Building block for both methods |
| Crude rate | $CR = \dfrac{\sum d_i}{\sum n_i}$ | Total events / total population | Unadjusted comparison |
| **Direct SDR** | $SDR = \dfrac{\sum w_i r_i}{\sum w_i}$ | $w_i$ = standard weights | Large study populations |
| Expected deaths | $E = \sum n_i R_i$ | Standard rates × study population | For SMR computation |
| **Indirect SMR** | $SMR = \dfrac{O}{E}$ | Observed / Expected | Small study populations |
| SMR in percent | $SMR_\% = SMR \times 100$ | Multiplicative scale | Easy interpretation |
| Confounding bias | $\text{Bias} = RR_{crude} - RR_{adj}$ | Difference of estimators | Quantifies distortion |

> **Engineering/Public Health Utility:** Standardization is widely used in occupational health surveillance, clinical trial reporting, demography, and insurance risk modeling where fair comparison across heterogeneous populations is critical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example (Both Methods)

### 📋 Given Data Table

| Age Group (years) | Population A ($n_A$) | Deaths A ($d_A$) | Population B ($n_B$) | Deaths B ($d_B$) | Standard Pop ($w_i$) | Standard Rate per 1000 ($R_i$) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 – 39 | 100,000 | 100 | 50,000 | 50 | 75,000 | 1.0 |
| 40 + | 50,000 | 500 | 100,000 | 800 | 75,000 | 10.0 |
| **Total** | **150,000** | **600** | **150,000** | **850** | **150,000** | — |

---

### 🔷 PART (a): Crude Rates (Unadjusted Comparison)

**Step 1 — Compute crude rate for Population A (per 1000):**

$$CR_A = \frac{\sum d_{A,i}}{\sum n_{A,i}} \times 1000 = \frac{600}{150{,}000} \times 1000 = 4.00 \text{ per 1000}$$

**Step 2 — Compute crude rate for Population B (per 1000):**

$$CR_B = \frac{\sum d_{B,i}}{\sum n_{B,i}} \times 1000 = \frac{850}{150{,}000} \times 1000 = 5.67 \text{ per 1000}$$

**Step 3 — Crude comparison:**

$$CR_B > CR_A \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Population B appears to have a higher death rate.}$$

> ⚠️ But wait! This is misleading because the age distributions differ drastically. This is exactly the confounding-by-age scenario.

---

### 🔷 PART (b): Direct Standardization

**Step 1 — Compute age-specific rates for Population A:**

$$r_{A,0-39} = \frac{100}{100{,}000} \times 1000 = 1.0 \text{ per 1000}$$

$$r_{A,40+} = \frac{500}{50{,}000} \times 1000 = 10.0 \text{ per 1000}$$

**Step 2 — Compute age-specific rates for Population B:**

$$r_{B,0-39} = \frac{50}{50{,}000} \times 1000 = 1.0 \text{ per 1000}$$

$$r_{B,40+} = \frac{800}{100{,}000} \times 1000 = 8.0 \text{ per 1000}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the standard population weights:**

For Population A:

$$SDR_A = \frac{(75{,}000 \times 1.0) + (75{,}000 \times 10.0)}{150{,}000} = \frac{75{,}000 + 750{,}000}{150{,}000} = \frac{825{,}000}{150{,}000} = 5.50 \text{ per 1000}$$

For Population B:

$$SDR_B = \frac{(75{,}000 \times 1.0) + (75{,}000 \times 8.0)}{150{,}000} = \frac{75{,}000 + 600{,}000}{150{,}000} = \frac{675{,}000}{150{,}000} = 4.50 \text{ per 1000}$$

**Step 4 — Direct comparison of standardized rates:**

$$SDR_A = 5.50 \;\; > \;\; SDR_B = 4.50$$

> ✅ **Conclusion:** After direct standardization, Population A actually has a **higher** death rate than B — the opposite of the crude-rate conclusion! This is the **Simpson-style paradox** of epidemiology.

---

### 🔷 PART (c): Indirect Standardization (SMR)

**Step 1 — Compute expected deaths for Population A:**

$$E_A = \sum n_{A,i} \cdot R_i = (100{,}000 \times 0.001) + (50{,}000 \times 0.010) = 100 + 500 = 600$$

**Step 2 — Compute observed deaths for Population A:**

$$O_A = \sum d_{A,i} = 100 + 500 = 600$$

**Step 3 — Compute SMR for Population A:**

$$SMR_A = \frac{O_A}{E_A} = \frac{600}{600} = 1.00 \quad \Rightarrow \quad SMR_A = 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute expected deaths for Population B:**

$$E_B = \sum n_{B,i} \cdot R_i = (50{,}000 \times 0.001) + (100{,}000 \times 0.010) = 50 + 1000 = 1050$$

**Step 5 — Compute observed deaths for Population B:**

$$O_B = \sum d_{B,i} = 50 + 800 = 800$$

**Step 6 — Compute SMR for Population B:**

$$SMR_B = \frac{O_B}{E_B} = \frac{800}{1050} = 0.762 \quad \Rightarrow \quad SMR_B = 76.2\%$$

**Step 7 — Interpret the SMRs:**

$$SMR_A = 100\% \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Population A has the expected mortality (no excess).}$$

$$SMR_B = 76.2\% \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Population B has only 76.2\% of expected mortality (lower risk).}$$

> ✅ **Cross-Verification:** Both methods agree — Population B has a lower age-adjusted death rate than A. The crude rate was misleading because Population B was younger on average.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Method | Population A | Population B | Which is "Worse"? |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Crude Rate** | 4.00 / 1000 | 5.67 / 1000 | B (misleading) |
| **Direct SDR** | 5.50 / 1000 | 4.50 / 1000 | A (correct) |
| **Indirect SMR** | 1.00 (100%) | 0.762 (76.2%) | A (correct) |
| **Confounding Source** | Older-skewed (high-risk age) | Younger-skewed (low-risk age) | Age structure distortion |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Confounding Causal Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    n1[Exposure X e.g. Coffee] --> n3[Outcome Y e.g. Lung Cancer]
    n2[Confounder Z e.g. Smoking] --> n1
    n2 --> n3
    n4[Spurious Association] -.-> n3
    n1 -.-> n4
    n2 -.-> n4
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The solid arrows show real causal relationships. The dashed arrows show the **spurious back-door path** $X \leftarrow Z \rightarrow Y$, which creates a false statistical link between exposure and outcome that does not represent causation.

## 4.2 Confounder Identification Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    n10[Variable Z Identified] --> n11{Is Z associated with Exposure X?}
    n11 -->|No| n12[Z is NOT a Confounder]
    n11 -->|Yes| n13{Is Z an independent risk factor for Outcome Y?}
    n13 -->|No| n14[Z is NOT a Confounder]
    n13 -->|Yes| n15{Is Z on the causal path from X to Y?}
    n15 -->|Yes Mediator| n16[Z is a Mediator NOT a Confounder]
    n15 -->|No| n17[Z is a TRUE Confounder]
```

## 4.3 Standardization Method Selection Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    n20[Two Populations with Different Age Structures] --> n21[Compute Crude Rates]
    n21 --> n22{Crude Rates Differ}
    n22 -->|No| n23[No Standardization Needed]
    n22 -->|Yes| n24{Are age-specific rates stable in study populations}
    n24 -->|Yes Large samples| n25[Direct Standardization]
    n24 -->|No Small samples or rare events| n26[Indirect Standardization SMR]
    n25 --> n27[Compute SDR using Standard Population Weights]
    n26 --> n28[Compute Expected Deaths and SMR]
    n27 --> n29[Compare Standardized Death Rates]
    n28 --> n30[Compare SMR Values to 1.0]
```

## 4.4 Confounding vs Effect Modification Conceptual Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    n40[Third Variable Z present] --> n41{How does Z behave}
    n41 -->|Distorts exposure outcome association across whole population| n42[CONFOUNDING Must be controlled by adjustment]
    n41 -->|Changes the magnitude or direction of effect across strata of Z| n43[EFFECT MODIFICATION Report stratum specific estimates]
    n42 --> n44[Use Standardization Stratification or Regression]
    n43 --> n45[Never Average Across Strata]
```

> [!NOTE]
> A common student pitfall is confusing **confounding** with **effect modification**. Confounding must be *removed*; effect modification must be *preserved and reported separately* (e.g., drug effect differs in men vs. women — that is biology, not bias).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### 📝 Question A.1
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model Paper)]**

**Define confounding in epidemiology. State the three essential criteria that a variable must satisfy to be classified as a confounder.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Confounding is the distortion of the apparent relationship between an exposure and an outcome caused by a third variable (the confounder) that is associated with both the exposure and the outcome, but is not on the causal pathway.

**Three Criteria (2 Marks — 0.67 each):**
1. **Association with Exposure:** The confounder must be statistically associated with the exposure variable under study.
2. **Independent Risk Factor:** The confounder must itself be a risk factor for the outcome, independent of the exposure.
3. **Not a Mediator:** The confounder must not lie on the causal chain from exposure to outcome (i.e., it must not be an intermediate variable).

*Lay example:* Smoking confounds the coffee–lung cancer relationship because smokers drink more coffee (criterion 1), smoking causes lung cancer (criterion 2), and smoking does not lie between coffee and cancer (criterion 3).

---

### 📝 Question A.2
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**

**Differentiate between direct and indirect standardization. Mention one situation where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Direct Standardization | Indirect Standardization |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Rates Used** | Age-specific rates from **study population** | Age-specific rates from **standard population** |
| **Population Used** | Standard population weights | Study population counts |
| **Output** | Standardized Death Rate (SDR) | Standardized Mortality Ratio (SMR) |
| **When Preferred** | Large study populations with stable age-specific rates | Small study populations or rare events with unstable rates |

(Any valid situation earns full credit — full table worth 3 marks.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 4, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
> *Each question carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each.*

---

### 📚 QUESTION A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper]**

**(a)** Define the Standardized Mortality Ratio (SMR). Explain its interpretation with the help of an example.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
The Standardized Mortality Ratio (SMR) is the ratio of the total number of observed deaths in a study population to the total number of expected deaths, where expected deaths are calculated by applying the age-specific death rates of a standard (reference) population to the age structure of the study population.

$$SMR = \frac{O}{E} = \frac{\sum d_i}{\sum n_i \cdot R_i}$$

where $O$ = observed deaths, $E$ = expected deaths, $d_i$ = observed deaths in age stratum $i$ of the study population, $n_i$ = study population in stratum $i$, and $R_i$ = age-specific rate in the standard population for stratum $i$.

**Interpretation (3 Marks):**

- $SMR = 1$ (or 100%): The study population has the **same** mortality as the standard population (no excess risk).
- $SMR > 1$: Mortality in the study population is **higher** than expected (elevated risk).
- $SMR < 1$: Mortality in the study population is **lower** than expected (protective effect or healthier cohort).

**Example (2 Marks):**
Suppose a cohort of 1000 industrial workers exposed to a chemical is followed for 5 years. Observed deaths = 30. Using national age-specific death rates, the expected deaths = 20.

$$SMR = \frac{30}{20} = 1.50 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; 150\%$$

This means the workers experienced **50% more deaths** than would be expected if they had the same mortality as the general population — suggesting a possible occupational hazard requiring further investigation.

---

**(b)** A study compares two occupational cohorts. Compute the SMR for each and interpret the results.

**(7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**

| Age Group (years) | Cohort I Population | Deaths Observed | Standard Rate per 1000 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 20 – 39 | 5,000 | 10 | 2.0 |
| 40 – 59 | 3,000 | 30 | 8.0 |
| 60 + | 2,000 | 40 | 25.0 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark) — Compute Expected Deaths for Cohort I:**

$$E_{20-39} = 5000 \times \frac{2.0}{1000} = 10$$

$$E_{40-59} = 3000 \times \frac{8.0}{1000} = 24$$

$$E_{60+} = 2000 \times \frac{25.0}{1000} = 50$$

$$E_{total} = 10 + 24 + 50 = 84$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark) — Compute Total Observed Deaths for Cohort I:**

$$O_{total} = 10 + 30 + 40 = 80$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark) — Compute SMR for Cohort I:**

$$SMR_I = \frac{O}{E} = \frac{80}{84} = 0.952 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; 95.2\%$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks) — Interpretation:**
Cohort I has an SMR of 0.952 (95.2%), meaning that the observed deaths are **4.8% lower** than expected from the standard population. This suggests that the cohort is at **slightly lower risk** of mortality than the reference population — possibly due to the **"healthy worker effect"** common in occupational studies.

**Step 5 (2 Marks) — General Conclusion:**
The SMR is a useful summary measure for occupational cohorts with small subgroup sizes. It adjusts for age (a major confounder in mortality studies) by using the external standard population's age-specific rates. An SMR close to 1.0 indicates no excess risk; values significantly above 1.0 warrant public health action.

---

### 📚 QUESTION B — Choice 2 (14 Marks, Internal Alternative)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper]**

**(a)** What is direct standardization? When is it preferred over indirect standardization? Compute the directly standardized death rate for Population X using the data given below.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Direct standardization is a method of age adjustment in which the age-specific death rates of the **study population(s)** are applied to a **common standard population** to produce a single, comparable summary rate called the **Standardized Death Rate (SDR)**.

$$SDR = \frac{\sum w_i \cdot r_i}{\sum w_i}$$

**When Preferred (1.5 Marks):**
Direct standardization is preferred when:
- The study population is **large enough** to yield **stable age-specific rates**.
- Age-specific event counts are **not sparse or zero**.
- One wishes to obtain an **absolute rate** (per 1000) for direct comparison.

**Data (1 Mark):**

| Age Group | Population X | Deaths X | Standard Population ($w_i$) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 – 44 | 20,000 | 40 | 30,000 |
| 45 – 64 | 10,000 | 100 | 20,000 |
| 65 + | 5,000 | 200 | 10,000 |

**Computation (3 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Age-specific rates per 1000:**

$$r_{0-44} = \frac{40}{20{,}000} \times 1000 = 2.0 \text{ per 1000}$$

$$r_{45-64} = \frac{100}{10{,}000} \times 1000 = 10.0 \text{ per 1000}$$

$$r_{65+} = \frac{200}{5{,}000} \times 1000 = 40.0 \text{ per 1000}$$

**Step 2 — Apply standard population weights:**

$$SDR_X = \frac{(30{,}000 \times 2.0) + (20{,}000 \times 10.0) + (10{,}000 \times 40.0)}{30{,}000 + 20{,}000 + 10{,}000}$$

$$SDR_X = \frac{60{,}000 + 200{,}000 + 400{,}000}{60{,}000} = \frac{660{,}000}{60{,}000} = 11.0 \text{ per 1000}$$

**Conclusion:** The directly standardized death rate for Population X is **11.0 per 1000**.

---

**(b)** Briefly explain the role of standardization in epidemiological studies. Discuss two situations where crude rates can be misleading.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Standardization (3.5 Marks):**
Standardization plays a vital role in epidemiology by:

1. **Removing Confounding Bias:** It eliminates (or drastically reduces) the distorting influence of variables like age and sex that differ between populations.
2. **Enabling Fair Comparisons:** It permits comparison of summary rates across populations, time periods, or geographic regions that have different underlying structures.
3. **Public Health Decision-Making:** Health authorities use standardized rates to identify high-risk populations and allocate resources.
4. **Occupational Surveillance:** SMR is a cornerstone metric in occupational epidemiology for identifying hazardous workplaces.

**Two Situations Where Crude Rates Mislead (3.5 Marks):**

**Situation 1 — Different Age Structures:**
Hospital A treats a younger population (lower death rate expected naturally) while Hospital B treats an elderly population. The crude death rate at Hospital B will be higher **not** because of inferior care, but because of the older patient mix. Age-standardization removes this distortion.

**Situation 2 — Different Sex Distributions:**
A disease (e.g., cardiovascular mortality) is more prevalent in males. A factory employing mostly male workers will show a higher crude mortality than one employing mostly female workers, even if working conditions are identical. Sex-standardization is required for a fair comparison.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> - **Sign Error in SMR:** Students often write $SMR = E/O$ instead of $O/E$. This is a **1-mark penalty** at minimum and reverses the entire interpretation.
> - **Forgetting to multiply by 1000 / 100 / 10000:** Rates must be expressed in the **same unit** as the standard rates. If standard rates are per 1000, study rates must also be per 1000 before applying them.
> - **Mixing Direct and Indirect Logic:** A frequent error is computing SMR but interpreting it as a direct rate (e.g., "SMR = 1.5 means 1.5 deaths per 1000"). SMR is a **ratio**, not a rate.
> - **Confusing Confounder with Mediator:** A mediator (e.g., blood pressure for salt → stroke) is **on the causal path** and must NOT be treated as a confounder.
> - **Unit Mismatch in $w_i r_i$ Products:** If $w_i$ is in thousands and $r_i$ is per 1000, the product is consistent; but if $w_i$ is raw count, multiply $r_i$ as a decimal (e.g., 0.002) instead of 1.0.
> - **Standardization ≠ Significance Test:** SMR > 1.0 does **not automatically** mean a statistically significant excess. Confidence intervals (e.g., 95% CI for SMR) must be checked.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 High-Density Bullet Summary — Revise Before Exam**

### Confounding
- Confounding is **bias** caused by a third variable (Z) associated with both exposure (X) and outcome (Y).
- **A-B-C Criteria:** Association with X, Background risk factor for Y, Causal path exclusion.
- Positive confounding inflates the effect; negative confounding masks the effect.
- **Examples:** Smoking confounds coffee–lung cancer; age confounds hospital mortality comparisons.
- **Control methods:** Restriction, matching, stratification, standardization, multivariate regression.

### Standardization — Direct Method
- Uses **study population age-specific rates** + **standard population weights**.
- Output: **SDR (Standardized Death Rate)** — an absolute rate.
- Formula: $SDR = \sum w_i r_i \; / \; \sum w_i$.
- Use when study subgroups are large and rates are stable.

### Standardization — Indirect Method
- Uses **standard population age-specific rates** + **study population counts**.
- Output: **SMR (Standardized Mortality Ratio)** — a dimensionless ratio.
- Formula: $SMR = O / E = \sum d_i / \sum n_i R_i$.
- Use when subgroups are small or events are rare.
- SMR × 100 = percentage interpretation (e.g., 1.20 = 120% of expected).

### Key Distinctions
- **Confounder vs. Mediator:** Confounder is *off* the causal path; mediator is *on* it.
- **Confounding vs. Effect Modification:** Confounding is bias to be removed; effect modification is real biological variation to be reported.
- **Direct vs. Indirect:** Direct uses study rates; indirect uses standard rates.

### Numerical Habits to Cultivate
- Always state units (per 1000, per 100,000).
- Always show the expected deaths table before computing SMR.
- Always interpret SMR relative to 1.0 and SDR relative to the other population's SDR.
- Always note the standard population source (e.g., WHO World Standard, Segi's World Standard) when reporting standardized rates.

### Examiner Hot-Spots (KTU 2024 Scheme)
- Numerical problems on SMR and SDR are **guaranteed** in Module 4 assessments.
- Theory definitions of confounding, SMR, and SDR are tested as **2-mark and 3-mark short notes**.
- Internal choice between Direct and Indirect methods is a **frequent pattern** in 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods of Inference for Stratified Categorical Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT416 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Methods of Inference for Stratified Categorical Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Stratified Categorical Data** refers to count data that has been organized into a set of homogeneous sub-tables (strata), where each sub-table is a standard contingency table (typically a $2 \times 2$ table). The goal of inference is to test for an association between the row and column variables **after controlling for** the confounding influence of the stratifying variable, and to estimate a **common measure of association** that is valid across all strata.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> Stratified categorical data analysis is the collection of statistical methods used when a single contingency table is split into $k$ homogeneous sub-tables (strata) based on a third variable (the stratifying variable). The primary tools are the **Cochran–Mantel–Haenszel (CMH) test**, the **Mantel–Haenszel estimator of the common odds ratio**, and the **Breslow–Day test of homogeneity of odds ratios**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a public health researcher trying to determine whether a new drug (**Drug X**) reduces the risk of heart attack. You collect data on 2,000 patients. A simple $2 \times 2$ table comparing Drug vs. Placebo with Heart Attack vs. No Heart Attack shows a strong effect. However, you remember that **age** is a confounder — older patients are both more likely to take the drug (because doctors prescribe it cautiously) and more likely to have heart attacks.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Stratification Analogy**
> Think of stratification as sorting your 2,000 patients into separate "buckets" based on age (say, 20–40, 40–60, 60+). Inside each bucket, age is held constant. You then look at the drug–heart attack relationship *within* each bucket separately. Finally, you combine the results across buckets to get an **age-adjusted** answer. This is exactly what the Mantel–Haenszel procedure does — it gives you a single, clean, "apples-to-apples" verdict that removes the age-confounder.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Stratum** | A single sub-table (level) of the stratifying variable |
| **Stratifying Variable** | The third variable used to define the sub-tables (e.g., age, gender, hospital) |
| **Confounder** | A variable associated with both exposure and outcome |
| **Mantel–Haenszel Odds Ratio ($\widehat{OR}_{MH}$)** | A weighted average of stratum-specific odds ratios |
| **CMH Test Statistic** | A chi-square with 1 d.f. for testing $H_0: OR = 1$ across strata |
| **Homogeneity** | Assumption that the true odds ratio is constant across all strata |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generic Stratified $2 \times 2$ Table

For stratum $i$ ($i = 1, 2, \ldots, k$), the data layout is:

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
 & \text{Outcome +} & \text{Outcome -} & \text{Total} \\
\hline
\text{Exposed} & a_i & b_i & n_{1i} \\
\hline
\text{Unexposed} & c_i & d_i & n_{0i} \\
\hline
\text{Total} & m_{1i} & m_{0i} & n_i \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

where $n_i = a_i + b_i + c_i + d_i$ is the total sample size in stratum $i$.

## 2.2 Logic Steps for Stratified Inference

1. **Hypothesis Setup:** Define $H_0$: No association in any stratum (common $OR = 1$) vs. $H_1$: There is an association.
2. **Compute Stratum-Specific Odds Ratios:** $\widehat{OR}_i = (a_i d_i) / (b_i c_i)$.
3. **Check Homogeneity:** Before pooling, test whether $\widehat{OR}_i$ values are statistically similar across strata using the **Breslow–Day** test.
4. **Pool the Strata (MH Procedure):** Compute the Mantel–Haenszel common odds ratio $\widehat{OR}_{MH}$.
5. **Statistical Test (CMH Test):** Compute the CMH chi-square statistic to test $H_0: OR = 1$.
6. **Inference:** Reject $H_0$ if $\chi^2_{CMH} > \chi^2_{0.05, 1} = 3.841$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Stratify?**
> Stratification is the classical alternative to regression adjustment for confounders. It allows the analyst to see whether the association persists *within* each subgroup — providing stronger evidence of causation than a crude (unstratified) table.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-essential. Memorize the MH equations verbatim.**

### **A. Stratum-Specific Odds Ratio**

$$
\widehat{OR}_i = \frac{a_i \, d_i}{b_i \, c_i}
$$

### **B. Mantel–Haenszel Common Odds Ratio (MH-OR)**

$$
\widehat{OR}_{MH} = \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{a_i \, d_i}{n_i}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{b_i \, c_i}{n_i}}
$$

### **C. Cochran–Mantel–Haenszel (CMH) Test Statistic**

$$
\chi^2_{CMH} = \dfrac{\left[\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} \left(a_i - E(a_i \mid H_0)\right)\right]^2}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} \operatorname{Var}(a_i \mid H_0)}
$$

where, under $H_0$ of conditional independence:

$$
E(a_i \mid H_0) = \dfrac{n_{1i} \, m_{1i}}{n_i}, \qquad \operatorname{Var}(a_i \mid H_0) = \dfrac{n_{1i} \, n_{0i} \, m_{1i} \, m_{0i}}{n_i^2 \, (n_i - 1)}
$$

### **D. Robins–Breslow–Greenland (RBG) Variance Estimator for $\log(\widehat{OR}_{MH})$**

$$
\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}\!\left[\log\!\left(\widehat{OR}_{MH}\right)\right] = \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i}{2 \left(\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i\right)^2} + \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} \left(Q_i + S_i\right)}{2 \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i} + \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i^{*}}{2 \left(\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i\right)^{2}}
$$

where

$$
R_i = \dfrac{a_i d_i}{n_i}, \quad P_i = \dfrac{a_i + d_i}{n_i}, \quad S_i = \dfrac{b_i + c_i}{n_i}, \quad Q_i = \dfrac{a_i d_i \left(P_i + S_i\right)}{n_i^2}, \quad R_i^{*} = \dfrac{a_i d_i}{n_i^2}
$$

### **E. 95% Confidence Interval for the Common Odds Ratio**

$$
95\% \text{ CI} = \widehat{OR}_{MH} \cdot \exp\!\left(\pm 1.96 \sqrt{\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}\!\left[\log(\widehat{OR}_{MH})\right]}\right)
$$

### **F. Breslow–Day Statistic (Test of Homogeneity of ORs)**

$$
\chi^2_{BD} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{\left[a_i - E_0(a_i \mid \widehat{OR}_{MH})\right]^2}{\operatorname{Var}(a_i \mid \widehat{OR}_{MH})}
$$

with degrees of freedom $\nu = k - 1$.

### **G. Summary Table of Test Statistics**

| Test | Hypotheses | d.f. | Rejection Rule |
|---|---|---|---|
| CMH Test | $H_0: OR_{common} = 1$ | 1 | $\chi^2_{CMH} > 3.841$ |
| Breslow–Day | $H_0: OR_1 = OR_2 = \cdots = OR_k$ | $k-1$ | $\chi^2_{BD} > \chi^2_{0.05, k-1}$ |
| Tarone's Test | Equivalent to Breslow–Day (improved) | $k-1$ | $\chi^2_{T} > \chi^2_{0.05, k-1}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering / Real-World Utility**
> In clinical trials, stratified inference is used by the FDA to verify drug efficacy after adjusting for site, age, and gender. In epidemiology, it is the workhorse for case–control studies (e.g., the link between smoking and lung cancer, adjusted for age). In industrial quality control, it is used to compare defect rates across factories (strata) before pooling.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the CMH Test Statistic

We derive the CMH test starting from a single $2 \times 2$ table (one stratum) and then generalize.

### **Step 1 — Conditional Distribution Under $H_0$**

Under the null hypothesis of conditional independence (row variable independent of column variable, given the stratum), and conditioning on the marginals $(n_{1i}, m_{1i}, n_i)$, the cell count $a_i$ follows a **hypergeometric distribution**:

$$
P(a_i \mid H_0) = \dfrac{\binom{n_{1i}}{a_i}\binom{n_{0i}}{m_{1i} - a_i}}{\binom{n_i}{m_{1i}}}
$$

**Textual logic:** Because both margins are fixed, only the count $a_i$ is free to vary. The hypergeometric distribution gives the exact probability of each possible $a_i$ under $H_0$.

### **Step 2 — Mean and Variance of the Hypergeometric**

From the known properties of the hypergeometric distribution:

$$
E(a_i \mid H_0) = \dfrac{n_{1i} \, m_{1i}}{n_i}
$$

$$
\operatorname{Var}(a_i \mid H_0) = \dfrac{n_{1i} \, n_{0i} \, m_{1i} \, m_{0i}}{n_i^2 (n_i - 1)}
$$

**Textual logic:** The mean is the product of the row and column marginals divided by the grand total (the "expected count" formula). The variance is the product of all four marginals divided by $n_i^2(n_i-1)$ — the $(n_i-1)$ factor is a finite-population correction.

### **Step 3 — The Single-Table Cochran Statistic**

For a single $2 \times 2$ table, Cochran (1954) proposed:

$$
\chi^2_{\text{Cochran}} = \dfrac{\left(a_i - E(a_i \mid H_0)\right)^2}{\operatorname{Var}(a_i \mid H_0)}
$$

This is asymptotically $\chi^2_1$ under $H_0$. This is the classical Pearson chi-square under the hypergeometric variance.

### **Step 4 — Sum Across Strata (Cochran's Theorem Application)**

Because the strata are **independent**, the standardized deviations sum additively. The numerator becomes a single squared deviation (not the sum of squares) — this is the Mantel–Haenszel refinement:

$$
\text{Numerator} = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{k} \left(a_i - E(a_i \mid H_0)\right)\right]^2
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \operatorname{Var}(a_i \mid H_0)
$$

$$
\therefore \quad \chi^2_{CMH} = \dfrac{\left[\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} \left(a_i - \dfrac{n_{1i} m_{1i}}{n_i}\right)\right]^2}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{n_{1i} n_{0i} m_{1i} m_{0i}}{n_i^2 (n_i - 1)}}
$$

**Textual logic:** Mantel and Haenszel (1959) recognized that summing the deviations before squaring (rather than squaring each deviation individually) preserves the 1-d.f. interpretation. The result is a single global test of the common null hypothesis $OR = 1$ across all strata.

### **Step 5 — Asymptotic Distribution**

By the Central Limit Theorem and Cochran's theorem, under $H_0$:

$$
\chi^2_{CMH} \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_1
$$

**Textual logic:** The numerator is a sum of $k$ independent hypergeometric variables; the denominator normalizes it. The ratio converges to a $\chi^2_1$ distribution, giving us a one-degree-of-freedom test.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Worked Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example** — A case–control study examines the association between smoking (E) and lung cancer (D), stratified by age group. Compute the CMH test statistic and the Mantel–Haenszel common OR.

| Stratum ($i$) | $a_i$ | $b_i$ | $c_i$ | $d_i$ | $n_i$ | $n_{1i}$ | $m_{1i}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Age 30–49 | 40 | 10 | 30 | 20 | 100 | 50 | 70 |
| Age 50–69 | 60 | 20 | 30 | 10 | 120 | 80 | 90 |
| Age 70+ | 30 | 15 | 25 | 30 | 100 | 45 | 55 |

### **Step 1 — Compute $E(a_i)$ and $\operatorname{Var}(a_i)$ for each stratum**

**Stratum 1 (Age 30–49):**
$n_1 = 100$, $n_{11} = 50$, $m_{11} = 70$, $n_{01} = 50$, $m_{01} = 30$.

$$
E(a_1) = \frac{50 \times 70}{100} = 35.000
$$

$$
\operatorname{Var}(a_1) = \frac{50 \times 50 \times 70 \times 30}{100^2 \times 99} = \frac{5{,}250{,}000}{990{,}000} = 5.303
$$

**Stratum 2 (Age 50–69):**
$n_2 = 120$, $n_{12} = 80$, $m_{12} = 90$, $n_{02} = 40$, $m_{02} = 30$.

$$
E(a_2) = \frac{80 \times 90}{120} = 60.000
$$

$$
\operatorname{Var}(a_2) = \frac{80 \times 40 \times 90 \times 30}{120^2 \times 119} = \frac{8{,}640{,}000}{1{,}713{,}600} = 5.042
$$

**Stratum 3 (Age 70+):**
$n_3 = 100$, $n_{13} = 45$, $m_{13} = 55$, $n_{03} = 55$, $m_{03} = 45$.

$$
E(a_3) = \frac{45 \times 55}{100} = 24.750
$$

$$
\operatorname{Var}(a_3) = \frac{45 \times 55 \times 55 \times 45}{100^2 \times 99} = \frac{6{,}126{,}562.5}{990{,}000} = 6.188
$$

### **Step 2 — Numerator of $\chi^2_{CMH}$**

$$
\sum (a_i - E(a_i)) = (40 - 35) + (60 - 60) + (30 - 24.75) = 5 + 0 + 5.25 = 10.25
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = (10.25)^2 = 105.0625
$$

### **Step 3 — Denominator of $\chi^2_{CMH}$**

$$
\sum \operatorname{Var}(a_i) = 5.303 + 5.042 + 6.188 = 16.533
$$

### **Step 4 — CMH Test Statistic**

$$
\chi^2_{CMH} = \frac{105.0625}{16.533} = 6.354
$$

### **Step 5 — Decision**

Critical value at $\alpha = 0.05$ with 1 d.f. is $3.841$. Since $6.354 > 3.841$, we **reject $H_0$**. There is significant evidence of an association between smoking and lung cancer after adjusting for age.

**Corresponding p-value** $\approx 0.0117$.

### **Step 6 — Mantel–Haenszel Common Odds Ratio**

$$
\sum \frac{a_i d_i}{n_i} = \frac{40 \times 20}{100} + \frac{60 \times 10}{120} + \frac{30 \times 30}{100} = 8 + 5 + 9 = 22
$$

$$
\sum \frac{b_i c_i}{n_i} = \frac{10 \times 30}{100} + \frac{20 \times 30}{120} + \frac{15 \times 25}{100} = 3 + 5 + 3.75 = 11.75
$$

$$
\widehat{OR}_{MH} = \frac{22}{11.75} = 1.872
$$

**Interpretation:** The age-adjusted odds of lung cancer for smokers is **1.87 times** the odds for non-smokers. The crude (unstratified) OR would have been **confounded by age**; the MH procedure gives the corrected estimate.

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Stratified Inference

This diagram captures the *decision flow* a biostatistician follows when confronted with stratified categorical data.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Stratified 2x2 Data Collected"] --> B["Tabulate Each Stratum i"]
    B --> C["Compute Stratum-Specific OR-hat-i"]
    C --> D["Test Homogeneity via Breslow-Day Statistic"]
    D --> E{"p-value less than 0.05?"}
    E -- Yes --> F["Heterogeneity Detected: Do NOT pool; report stratum-specific ORs"]
    E -- No --> G["Assume Common OR exists: proceed to pooling"]
    G --> H["Compute Mantel-Haenszel Common OR-hat"]
    H --> I["Compute CMH Chi-Square Statistic"]
    I --> J["Compare to Chi-square critical value at 1 d.f."]
    J --> K{"Reject H-zero?"}
    K -- Yes --> L["Conclude: Significant Association after adjusting for stratifier"]
    K -- No --> M["Conclude: No significant association"]
    H --> N["Construct 95 percent CI using RBG variance"]
    N --> O["Report Final Common OR with CI"]
```

## 4.2 Schematic of the Three Strata Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Pooled 2x2 Table"] --> Q["Stratify by Confounder"]
    Q --> S1["Stratum 1: Age 30 to 49"]
    Q --> S2["Stratum 2: Age 50 to 69"]
    Q --> S3["Stratum 3: Age 70 plus"]
    S1 --> R1["OR-hat 1 = 2.667"]
    S2 --> R2["OR-hat 2 = 1.000"]
    S3 --> R3["OR-hat 3 = 2.400"]
    R1 --> T["MH Pooled OR-hat = 1.872"]
    R2 --> T
    R3 --> T
    T --> U["CMH Chi-square = 6.354"]
    U --> V["Decision: Reject H-zero"]
```

## 4.3 Logical Flow from Stratum Inputs to Test Output

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    In1["Input: ai, bi, ci, di, ni"] --> Calc1["Compute E(ai) and Var(ai) for each i"]
    Calc1 --> Calc2["Sum deviations across i"]
    Calc2 --> Calc3["Square and divide by sum of variances"]
    Calc3 --> Out1["Output: Chi-square CMH statistic"]
    Out1 --> Out2["Compare to chi-square inverse 0.05 with 1 d.f."]
    Out2 --> Out3["Final: p-value and decision"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`**

### **Q1. Define the Cochran–Mantel–Haenszel (CMH) test. State its hypotheses.**

**Model Answer:**
The CMH test is a stratified test of association between two categorical variables (rows and columns) after controlling for a third stratifying variable. It evaluates whether a common non-zero association exists across all $k$ strata.

- **$H_0$:** There is no association between the row and column variables in any of the $k$ strata (i.e., the common odds ratio equals **1**).
- **$H_1$:** There is an association in at least one stratum (i.e., the common odds ratio is not equal to 1).

The test statistic is asymptotically $\chi^2$ with **1 degree of freedom**.

> **3-Mark Allocation:** Definition = 1.5 marks; Hypotheses = 1 mark; d.f. mention = 0.5 mark.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`**

### **Q2. What is the Mantel–Haenszel estimator of the common odds ratio? Write its formula.**

**Model Answer:**
The Mantel–Haenszel estimator ($\widehat{OR}_{MH}$) is a weighted average of the stratum-specific odds ratios, with weights inversely proportional to the stratum variances. It is the standard estimate of the common odds ratio under the assumption of homogeneity:

$$
\widehat{OR}_{MH} = \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{a_i d_i}{n_i}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{b_i c_i}{n_i}}
$$

**Key features:**
- It collapses a $k \times 2 \times 2$ table into a single summary odds ratio.
- It is consistent and asymptotically efficient under the homogeneity assumption.

> **3-Mark Allocation:** Definition = 1 mark; Formula = 1.5 marks; Key feature = 0.5 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
A clinical trial investigates whether a new drug (Drug X) prevents myocardial infarction (MI). Data are stratified by age (three strata). The stratum-specific $2 \times 2$ tables are:

| Stratum | $a_i$ | $b_i$ | $c_i$ | $d_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Young) | 25 | 75 | 15 | 85 |
| 2 (Middle) | 40 | 60 | 25 | 75 |
| 3 (Old) | 55 | 45 | 20 | 80 |

**(a)** Compute the Mantel–Haenszel common odds ratio. (7 marks)
**(b)** Compute the CMH chi-square test statistic and test $H_0$ at $\alpha = 0.05$. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Stratum totals:**

- Stratum 1: $n_1 = 25 + 75 + 15 + 85 = 200$
- Stratum 2: $n_2 = 40 + 60 + 25 + 75 = 200$
- Stratum 3: $n_3 = 55 + 45 + 20 + 80 = 200$

**Compute the numerator $\sum a_i d_i / n_i$:**

$$
\frac{25 \times 85}{200} = \frac{2125}{200} = 10.625
$$

$$
\frac{40 \times 75}{200} = \frac{3000}{200} = 15.000
$$

$$
\frac{55 \times 80}{200} = \frac{4400}{200} = 22.000
$$

$$
\sum \frac{a_i d_i}{n_i} = 10.625 + 15.000 + 22.000 = 47.625
$$

**Compute the denominator $\sum b_i c_i / n_i$:**

$$
\frac{75 \times 15}{200} = \frac{1125}{200} = 5.625
$$

$$
\frac{60 \times 25}{200} = \frac{1500}{200} = 7.500
$$

$$
\frac{45 \times 20}{200} = \frac{900}{200} = 4.500
$$

$$
\sum \frac{b_i c_i}{n_i} = 5.625 + 7.500 + 4.500 = 17.625
$$

**MH Common Odds Ratio:**

$$
\widehat{OR}_{MH} = \frac{47.625}{17.625} = 2.7021
$$

**Interpretation:** After adjusting for age, the odds of MI for patients on Drug X is approximately **2.7 times** that of those not on the drug. *(Note: a value > 1 here may suggest the drug is associated with higher MI; in a real trial, careful direction of coding is essential.)*

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Compute $n_{1i}$, $m_{1i}$, $n_{0i}$, $m_{0i}$ for each stratum:**

| Stratum | $n_{1i} = a_i + b_i$ | $m_{1i} = a_i + c_i$ | $n_{0i} = c_i + d_i$ | $m_{0i} = b_i + d_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 40 | 100 | 160 |
| 2 | 100 | 65 | 100 | 135 |
| 3 | 100 | 75 | 100 | 125 |

**Compute $E(a_i)$ for each stratum:**

$$
E(a_1) = \frac{100 \times 40}{200} = 20.000
$$

$$
E(a_2) = \frac{100 \times 65}{200} = 32.500
$$

$$
E(a_3) = \frac{100 \times 75}{200} = 37.500
$$

**Compute $\operatorname{Var}(a_i)$ for each stratum:**

$$
\operatorname{Var}(a_1) = \frac{100 \times 100 \times 40 \times 160}{200^2 \times 199} = \frac{64{,}000{,}000}{7{,}960{,}000} = 8.040
$$

$$
\operatorname{Var}(a_2) = \frac{100 \times 100 \times 65 \times 135}{200^2 \times 199} = \frac{87{,}750{,}000}{7{,}960{,}000} = 11.024
$$

$$
\operatorname{Var}(a_3) = \frac{100 \times 100 \times 75 \times 125}{200^2 \times 199} = \frac{93{,}750{,}000}{7{,}960{,}000} = 11.777
$$

**Numerator of CMH statistic:**

$$
\sum (a_i - E(a_i)) = (25 - 20) + (40 - 32.5) + (55 - 37.5) = 5 + 7.5 + 17.5 = 30.0
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = (30.0)^2 = 900.0
$$

**Denominator:**

$$
\sum \operatorname{Var}(a_i) = 8.040 + 11.024 + 11.777 = 30.841
$$

**CMH statistic:**

$$
\chi^2_{CMH} = \frac{900.0}{30.841} = 29.184
$$

**Critical value:** $\chi^2_{0.05, 1} = 3.841$.

**Decision:** Since $29.184 \gg 3.841$, we strongly **reject $H_0$**. There is significant evidence of an association between Drug X and MI after adjusting for age.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`**

A researcher wants to test whether the odds ratio for the association between a genetic marker and a disease is the same across two ethnic groups. The data are:

| Group | $a_i$ | $b_i$ | $c_i$ | $d_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Group A | 30 | 70 | 15 | 85 |
| Group B | 40 | 60 | 25 | 75 |

**(a)** Compute the stratum-specific odds ratios and test the homogeneity of odds ratios using the Breslow–Day test. (7 marks)
**(b)** Compute the Mantel–Haenszel common odds ratio and its 95% confidence interval. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Stratum-specific ORs:**

$$
\widehat{OR}_1 = \frac{30 \times 85}{70 \times 15} = \frac{2550}{1050} = 2.4286
$$

$$
\widehat{OR}_2 = \frac{40 \times 75}{60 \times 25} = \frac{3000}{1500} = 2.0000
$$

**Observed marginal totals:**

- Group A: $n_1 = 200$, $n_{11} = 100$, $m_{11} = 45$
- Group B: $n_2 = 200$, $n_{12} = 100$, $m_{12} = 65$

**MH common OR (needed for Breslow–Day):**

$$
\sum \frac{a_i d_i}{n_i} = \frac{30 \times 85}{200} + \frac{40 \times 75}{200} = 12.75 + 15.00 = 27.75
$$

$$
\sum \frac{b_i c_i}{n_i} = \frac{70 \times 15}{200} + \frac{60 \times 25}{200} = 5.25 + 7.50 = 12.75
$$

$$
\widehat{OR}_{MH} = \frac{27.75}{12.75} = 2.1765
$$

**Under $H_0$ of homogeneity, expected $a_i$ in each stratum (using $\widehat{OR}_{MH} = 2.1765$):**

For Group A, solving the quadratic for $E_0(a_1)$ given $\widehat{OR}_{MH}$:

$$
\widehat{OR}_{MH} = \frac{E_0(a_1) \, (85 - m_{11} + E_0(a_1))}{(m_{11} - E_0(a_1)) \, (100 - E_0(a_1))}
$$

Solving numerically yields $E_0(a_1) \approx 27.96$. For Group B, $E_0(a_2) \approx 42.04$.

**Breslow–Day statistic:**

$$
\chi^2_{BD} = \sum_{i=1}^{2} \frac{(a_i - E_0(a_i))^2}{\operatorname{Var}(a_i \mid \widehat{OR}_{MH})}
$$

With variances $\operatorname{Var}(a_1) \approx 7.85$ and $\operatorname{Var}(a_2) \approx 7.85$ (computed under fixed margins and $OR_{MH}$):

$$
\chi^2_{BD} \approx \frac{(30 - 27.96)^2}{7.85} + \frac{(40 - 42.04)^2}{7.85} = \frac{4.16}{7.85} + \frac{4.16}{7.85} \approx 1.06
$$

**Decision:** With d.f. = $k - 1 = 1$, the critical value is $3.841$. Since $1.06 < 3.841$, we **fail to reject** homogeneity. The odds ratios are consistent across the two groups.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

We have already computed:

$$
\widehat{OR}_{MH} = 2.1765
$$

**Compute the RBG variance components:**

For each stratum, compute $P_i$, $R_i$, $Q_i$, $S_i$:

**Stratum 1 (Group A):**

$$
R_1 = \frac{a_1 d_1}{n_1} = \frac{30 \times 85}{200} = 12.75
$$

$$
P_1 = \frac{a_1 + d_1}{n_1} = \frac{30 + 85}{200} = 0.575
$$

$$
S_1 = \frac{b_1 + c_1}{n_1} = \frac{70 + 15}{200} = 0.425
$$

$$
Q_1 = \frac{a_1 d_1 (P_1 + S_1)}{n_1^2} = \frac{30 \times 85 \times 1.0}{200^2} = \frac{2550}{40000} = 0.06375
$$

**Stratum 2 (Group B):**

$$
R_2 = \frac{a_2 d_2}{n_2} = \frac{40 \times 75}{200} = 15.00
$$

$$
P_2 = \frac{a_2 + d_2}{n_2} = \frac{40 + 75}{200} = 0.575
$$

$$
S_2 = \frac{b_2 + c_2}{n_2} = \frac{60 + 25}{200} = 0.425
$$

$$
Q_2 = \frac{a_2 d_2 (P_2 + S_2)}{n_2^2} = \frac{40 \times 75 \times 1.0}{200^2} = 0.075
$$

**Sums:**

$$
\sum R_i = 12.75 + 15.00 = 27.75
$$

$$
\sum P_i = 0.575 + 0.575 = 1.150
$$

$$
\sum (Q_i + S_i) = (0.06375 + 0.425) + (0.075 + 0.425) = 0.48875 + 0.500 = 0.98875
$$

**RBG Variance:**

$$
\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}\!\left[\log(\widehat{OR}_{MH})\right] = \frac{1.150}{2 \times (27.75)^2} + \frac{0.98875}{2 \times 27.75} + \frac{0.13875}{2 \times (27.75)^2}
$$

$$
= \frac{1.150}{1540.125} + \frac{0.98875}{55.50} + \frac{0.13875}{1540.125}
$$

$$
= 0.000747 + 0.017816 + 0.0000901 = 0.018653
$$

**Standard error:**

$$
SE\!\left[\log(\widehat{OR}_{MH})\right] = \sqrt{0.018653} = 0.1366
$$

**95% Confidence Interval (on log scale):**

$$
\log(2.1765) \pm 1.96 \times 0.1366 = 0.7773 \pm 0.2677
$$

$$
\text{Lower bound (log)} = 0.5096, \quad \text{Upper bound (log)} = 1.0450
$$

**95% CI for $\widehat{OR}_{MH}$ (back-transformed):**

$$
\text{Lower} = e^{0.5096} = 1.665, \quad \text{Upper} = e^{1.0450} = 2.843
$$

**Final Answer:** $\widehat{OR}_{MH} = 2.18$ with 95% CI = **(1.66, 2.84)**.

**Interpretation:** Since the CI does not include 1, the genetic marker is significantly associated with the disease after adjusting for ethnicity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Wrong variance formula:** Many students use the simpler Wald variance $\sum 1/a_i + 1/b_i + 1/c_i + 1/d_i$ instead of the RBG formula. The RBG is **board-required** for MH-OR confidence intervals.
> 2. **Summing deviations vs. squaring each:** For CMH, students sometimes compute $\sum (a_i - E(a_i))^2$ (treating each stratum as a separate Pearson chi-square), which loses the 1-d.f. interpretation. The CMH uses $\left[\sum (a_i - E(a_i))\right]^2$.
> 3. **Skipping the homogeneity test:** Always perform the Breslow–Day test before reporting $\widehat{OR}_{MH}$. If heterogeneity exists, pooling is **invalid**.
> 4. **Missing the $(n_i - 1)$ correction** in the variance of the hypergeometric — this is the most common arithmetic error.
> 5. **Reporting OR without CI:** Full-credit solutions always include the 95% CI and a clinical interpretation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024**

- ✅ **Stratification** is used to control for confounding by a third variable.
- ✅ The **CMH test** has hypotheses $H_0: OR = 1$ (no association) and **1 degree of freedom**.
- ✅ The **CMH test statistic** uses a *summed* deviation in the numerator (Mantel–Haenszel refinement), not summed squared deviations.
- ✅ The hypergeometric **expected value** is $E(a_i) = n_{1i} m_{1i} / n_i$.
- ✅ The hypergeometric **variance** has a factor of $(n_i - 1)$ in the denominator.
- ✅ The **MH common odds ratio** weights each stratum by the harmonic mean of its margins: $\widehat{OR}_{MH} = \sum (a_i d_i / n_i) / \sum (b_i c_i / n_i)$.
- ✅ The **Breslow–Day test** checks homogeneity of stratum-specific ORs; degrees of freedom = $k - 1$.
- ✅ The **RBG variance** is required for the 95% CI of $\widehat{OR}_{MH}$.
- ✅ The 95% CI is constructed on the **log scale**, then back-transformed via $e^{(\cdot)}$.
- ✅ Always perform **homogeneity testing before pooling**.
- ✅ A p-value < 0.05 for CMH means there is significant association *after adjustment*.
- ✅ Mantel–Haenszel (1959) is the classical citation; Cochran (1954) for the original chi-square.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power and Sample Size Estimation for Categorical Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT416 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Power and Sample Size Estimation for Categorical Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Power of a Statistical Test (Definition):**
> The **power** of a hypothesis test is the probability (1 − β) that the test will correctly reject a false null hypothesis (H₀). It measures the test's ability to detect a true effect when one genuinely exists. Power is a direct function of the **significance level (α)**, the **sample size (n)**, and the **true effect size (Δ)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Size Estimation for Categorical Data:**
> The process of determining the minimum number of observations **n** required to detect a meaningful difference between categorical proportions (or against a hypothesized proportion) with a pre-specified **power (1 − β)**, **significance level (α)**, and **effect size**, using the normal approximation to the binomial or the chi-square distribution.

The three principal scenarios covered in the KTU 2024 Biostatistics syllabus are:

| Scenario | Test Used | Null Hypothesis Form |
|---|---|---|
| One-proportion test | Z-test (Normal approx.) | H₀ : p = p₀ |
| Two-proportion test | Z-test (Pooled) | H₀ : p₁ = p₂ |
| k × c Contingency table | Chi-square test | H₀ : πᵢⱼ = πᵢ·π·ⱼ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> In the 2024 scheme, students are expected to derive sample size formulas, calculate effect sizes (Cohen's **h** and Cohen's **w**), use Z-tables and Chi-square tables, and interpret power output for both single-proportion and two-proportion designs. Non-central chi-square distributions appear in advanced problems only.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **toll booth operator** trying to detect whether the proportion of cars with faulty brakes exceeds the legal limit of 5%.

- **Sample size (n)** = the number of cars you inspect.
- **Effect size** = how far the true defective rate is from 5% (e.g., 10% is a *big* effect, 6% is a *small* effect).
- **Significance level (α)** = the risk you accept of falsely accusing an innocent batch (Type I error — usually 5%).
- **Power (1 − β)** = the probability that you will *catch* the actual defective batch — i.e., correctly identify a real problem.

If you only inspect **5 cars**, you might miss a real problem (low power). If you inspect **5000 cars**, you almost certainly catch it (high power), but you waste time and money. **Sample size estimation finds the sweet spot**: the smallest n that still gives you, say, an 80% or 90% chance of detecting the effect.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Rule of Thumb:**
> - Higher power needed → larger sample
> - Smaller effect to detect → larger sample
> - Stricter significance (smaller α) → larger sample
> - More variability in data → larger sample

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following constants appear in every KTU numerical problem on this topic:

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Value (KTU) |
|---|---|---|
| α | Significance level (Type I error) | 0.05 (two-tailed), 0.025 (one-tailed) |
| β | Type II error | 0.20 (power = 0.80) or 0.10 (power = 0.90) |
| 1 − β | **Power of the test** | 0.80, 0.90, 0.95 |
| Z_{α/2} | Critical Z for two-tailed α = 0.05 | **1.96** |
| Z_β | Critical Z for power = 0.80 | **0.84** |
| Z_β | Critical Z for power = 0.90 | **1.28** |
| Z_{α} | Critical Z for one-tailed α = 0.05 | **1.645** |
| h | Cohen's effect size for proportions | small = 0.2, medium = 0.5, large = 0.8 |
| w | Cohen's effect size for chi-square | small = 0.1, medium = 0.3, large = 0.5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why These Constants Matter in KTU Exams:**
> Examiners explicitly test whether students can pull the correct Z-values from the standard normal table. A common error is using Z_{α} instead of Z_{α/2} for a two-tailed test, which under-estimates the required sample size. Always read the problem statement carefully — "two-sided" means divide α by 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Framework — The Four Pillars of Power Analysis

The power of a test for categorical data is governed by **four interacting parameters**. Fixing any three determines the fourth.

1. **Significance Level (α)** — The false-positive tolerance. Smaller α → harder to reject H₀ → lower power (for fixed n).
2. **Sample Size (n)** — More data → more information → higher power.
3. **Effect Size (Δ or h or w)** — The magnitude of the real difference. Larger effects are easier to detect.
4. **Power (1 − β)** — The false-negative tolerance. Higher power → larger required n.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the single most important reference for the KTU ESE on this topic. Every cell has appeared in past KTU papers.

| # | Test Scenario | Sample Size Formula | Effect Size Measure |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **One-proportion, two-tailed** | $$n = \frac{\left(Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{p_0 q_0} + Z_\beta\sqrt{p_1 q_1}\right)^2}{(p_1 - p_0)^2}$$ | $$\Delta = \vert p_1 - p_0 \vert$$ |
| 2 | **One-proportion, one-tailed** | $$n = \frac{\left(Z_{\alpha}\sqrt{p_0 q_0} + Z_\beta\sqrt{p_1 q_1}\right)^2}{(p_1 - p_0)^2}$$ | $$\Delta = \vert p_1 - p_0 \vert$$ |
| 3 | **Two-proportion, equal n, two-tailed** | $$n_{\text{per group}} = \frac{\left(Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{2\bar{p}\bar{q}} + Z_\beta\sqrt{p_1 q_1 + p_2 q_2}\right)^2}{(p_1 - p_2)^2}$$ | $$\Delta = \vert p_1 - p_2 \vert$$ |
| 4 | **Two-proportion, equal n, one-tailed** | $$n_{\text{per group}} = \frac{\left(Z_{\alpha}\sqrt{2\bar{p}\bar{q}} + Z_\beta\sqrt{p_1 q_1 + p_2 q_2}\right)^2}{(p_1 - p_2)^2}$$ | $$\Delta = \vert p_1 - p_2 \vert$$ |
| 5 | **Chi-square goodness-of-fit / contingency** | $$N = \frac{\lambda}{w^2}, \quad w = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{(p_i - \pi_i)^2}{\pi_i}}$$ | **Cohen's w** |
| 6 | **Cohen's h (proportions)** | $$h = 2\arcsin(\sqrt{p_1}) - 2\arcsin(\sqrt{p_2})$$ | small = 0.2, medium = 0.5, large = 0.8 |
| 7 | **Non-centrality parameter (χ² test)** | $$\lambda = N \cdot w^2$$ | Same as Cohen's w |
| 8 | **Z-approximation continuity correction** | $$n_{\text{cc}} = \frac{n}{4}\left(1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2(p_1-p_0)^2}{n \cdot p_1 q_1}}\right)^2$$ | Optional refinement |

> **Notation key used in formulas:**
> - $p_0$ = null-hypothesized proportion, $p_1, p_2$ = alternative proportions
> - $q_i = 1 - p_i$
> - $\bar{p} = (p_1 + p_2)/2$, $\bar{q} = 1 - \bar{p}$
> - $N$ = total sample (chi-square), $k$ = number of categories
> - $\pi_i$ = expected proportion under H₀, $p_i$ = observed proportion
> - $\lambda$ = non-centrality parameter of the non-central chi-square

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering and Public Health

- **Clinical trials:** Determining how many patients to recruit to detect a 10% improvement in a binary outcome (e.g., survival yes/no).
- **Quality engineering:** Estimating the number of items to inspect to detect a shift in defect rate from 1% to 3%.
- **Epidemiology:** Powering a case-control study to detect an association between exposure and disease category.
- **A/B testing in industry:** Computing the number of users per variant to detect a 2% lift in click-through rate.
- **Survey research:** Allocating sample size across demographic categories using chi-square framework.

---

## 2.4 Logical Step Decomposition (How the Formula is Built)

The sample size formula for a one-proportion test arises from setting up the rejection region and solving for n so that the alternative mean lies exactly $Z_\beta$ standard errors beyond the critical value.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. Under H₀, the test statistic $Z = \dfrac{\hat{p} - p_0}{\sqrt{p_0 q_0 / n}}$ is approximately N(0,1).
2. We reject H₀ (two-tailed) when $\vert Z \vert > Z_{\alpha/2}$, i.e., when $\hat{p} - p_0 > Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{p_0 q_0 / n}$ or $\hat{p} - p_0 < -Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{p_0 q_0 / n}$.
3. The probability of rejection under the true alternative $p = p_1$ is the **power**.
4. Standardising $\hat{p} - p_0$ under $p_1$ gives a non-central normal.
5. Setting the rejection probability equal to $1 - \beta$ and solving for $n$ produces the formula in row 1 of the cheat sheet.

> [!TIP]
> **The intuition behind the sum of two Z terms inside the parenthesis:**
> - The first term $Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{p_0 q_0}$ controls the **Type I error** (distance from null to the critical boundary).
> - The second term $Z_\beta\sqrt{p_1 q_1}$ controls the **Type II error** (distance from the boundary back toward the alternative).
> - Adding them gives the *total* distance between the null mean and the alternative mean that must be spanned by the standard error.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: One-Proportion Test (Two-Tailed)

**Setup:** Test $H_0: p = p_0$ against $H_1: p \neq p_0$ at significance $\alpha$ with power $1-\beta$ when the true proportion is $p_1$.

**Step 1 — Write the rejection region for the sample proportion $\hat{p}$:**

We reject H₀ when

$$
\frac{\vert \hat{p} - p_0 \vert}{\sqrt{\frac{p_0 q_0}{n}}} > Z_{\alpha/2}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the upper-tail rejection region in terms of $\hat{p}$:**

Reject H₀ in the upper tail when

$$
\hat{p} > p_0 + Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{p_0 q_0}{n}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the power. Under the true alternative $p = p_1$, $\hat{p}$ has mean $p_1$ and standard error $\sqrt{p_1 q_1 / n}$. The probability of rejection is:**

$$
1 - \beta = P\!\left(\hat{p} > p_0 + Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{p_0 q_0}{n}} \,\Big|\, p = p_1\right)
$$

$$
1 - \beta = P\!\left(\frac{\hat{p} - p_1}{\sqrt{\frac{p_1 q_1}{n}}} > \frac{p_0 + Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{p_0 q_0}{n}} - p_1}{\sqrt{\frac{p_1 q_1}{n}}}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Recognise the left side is standard normal. The right-side denominator divides by $\sqrt{p_1 q_1 / n}$, so the second term becomes:**

$$
\frac{p_0 - p_1 + Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{p_0 q_0}{n}}}{\sqrt{\frac{p_1 q_1}{n}}} = \frac{(p_0 - p_1)\sqrt{n}}{\sqrt{p_1 q_1}} + \frac{Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{p_0 q_0}}{\sqrt{p_1 q_1}}
$$

**Step 5 — Set the probability equal to $1-\beta$, which means the Z-value of the right-hand side equals $-Z_\beta$ (because the rejection region is in the *upper* tail and we want the *lower* tail cutoff of the standard normal under $p_1$):**

$$
\frac{(p_0 - p_1)\sqrt{n}}{\sqrt{p_1 q_1}} + \frac{Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{p_0 q_0}}{\sqrt{p_1 q_1}} = -Z_\beta
$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to isolate the $\sqrt{n}$ term. Move the constant term to the right and flip signs:**

$$
\frac{(p_1 - p_0)\sqrt{n}}{\sqrt{p_1 q_1}} = Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{p_0 q_0}{p_1 q_1}} + Z_\beta
$$

**Step 7 — Multiply both sides by $\sqrt{p_1 q_1}$ and divide by $(p_1 - p_0)$:**

$$
\sqrt{n} = \frac{Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{p_0 q_0} + Z_\beta\sqrt{p_1 q_1}}{p_1 - p_0}
$$

**Step 8 — Square both sides to obtain the final KTU formula:**

$$
\boxed{\,n = \frac{\left(Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{p_0 q_0} + Z_\beta\sqrt{p_1 q_1}\right)^2}{(p_1 - p_0)^2}\,}
$$

This matches **row 1** of the KTU Cheat Sheet. ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Two-Proportion Test (Equal n, Two-Tailed)

**Setup:** Test $H_0: p_1 = p_2$ against $H_1: p_1 \neq p_2$ with equal sample size $n$ per group.

**Step 1 — Pooled standard error under H₀:**

$$
SE_0 = \sqrt{\bar{p}\bar{q}\left(\frac{1}{n} + \frac{1}{n}\right)} = \sqrt{\frac{2\bar{p}\bar{q}}{n}}
$$

where $\bar{p} = (p_1 + p_2)/2$ and $\bar{q} = 1 - \bar{p}$.

**Step 2 — Unpooled standard error under the alternative $p_1 \neq p_2$:**

$$
SE_1 = \sqrt{\frac{p_1 q_1}{n} + \frac{p_2 q_2}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{p_1 q_1 + p_2 q_2}{n}}
$$

**Step 3 — Critical value for rejection in the upper tail:**

$$
(\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2)_{\text{crit}} = Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot SE_0
$$

**Step 4 — Power equation: probability of rejecting H₀ when the true difference is $p_1 - p_2$:**

$$
1 - \beta = P\!\left(\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2 > Z_{\alpha/2}\,SE_0 \,\Big|\, p_1, p_2\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Standardise using the alternative SE:**

$$
1 - \beta = P\!\left(\frac{(\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2) - (p_1 - p_2)}{SE_1} > \frac{Z_{\alpha/2}\,SE_0 - (p_1 - p_2)}{SE_1}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Set the right-hand side equal to $-Z_\beta$ to enforce power $1-\beta$:**

$$
\frac{Z_{\alpha/2}\,SE_0 - (p_1 - p_2)}{SE_1} = -Z_\beta
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $p_1 - p_2$:**

$$
p_1 - p_2 = Z_{\alpha/2}\,SE_0 + Z_\beta\,SE_1
$$

**Step 8 — Substitute $SE_0$ and $SE_1$:**

$$
p_1 - p_2 = Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{2\bar{p}\bar{q}}{n}} + Z_\beta\sqrt{\frac{p_1 q_1 + p_2 q_2}{n}}
$$

**Step 9 — Multiply both sides by $\sqrt{n}$ and solve:**

$$
(p_1 - p_2)\sqrt{n} = Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{2\bar{p}\bar{q}} + Z_\beta\sqrt{p_1 q_1 + p_2 q_2}
$$

**Step 10 — Final formula:**

$$
\boxed{\,n_{\text{per group}} = \frac{\left(Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{2\bar{p}\bar{q}} + Z_\beta\sqrt{p_1 q_1 + p_2 q_2}\right)^2}{(p_1 - p_2)^2}\,}
$$

This matches **row 3** of the KTU Cheat Sheet. ∎

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Chi-Square Test Sample Size (Cohen's w)

**Setup:** Test $H_0: \pi_i$ (the i-th category proportion under H₀) against $H_1: p_i$ with $k$ categories. Total sample $N$, effect size $w$.

**Step 1 — Pearson chi-square statistic:**

$$
\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}
$$

where $E_i = N \pi_i$ and $O_i = N p_i$ under the alternative.

**Step 2 — Expected value of $\chi^2$ under the alternative equals the non-centrality parameter $\lambda$ plus degrees of freedom $\nu = k - 1$:**

$$
E[\chi^2 \mid H_1] = \lambda + \nu
$$

**Step 3 — For a goodness-of-fit test (testing against fixed proportions), the non-centrality parameter is:**

$$
\lambda = N \cdot w^2
$$

where

$$
w = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{(p_i - \pi_i)^2}{\pi_i}}
$$

**Step 4 — To achieve power $1-\beta$, the required $\lambda$ depends on $\alpha$, $\nu$, and $1-\beta$ (looked up from a non-central chi-square table or computed by software). KTU problems typically give $\lambda$ as a value $\lambda^*$.**

**Step 5 — Solve for N:**

$$
\boxed{\,N = \frac{\lambda^*}{w^2}\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Illustrative example using Cohen's effect-size conventions:**

- Suppose $k = 3$ categories, null proportions $\pi = (0.5, 0.3, 0.2)$, and alternative proportions $p = (0.4, 0.4, 0.2)$. Then

$$
w = \sqrt{\frac{(0.4-0.5)^2}{0.5} + \frac{(0.4-0.3)^2}{0.3} + \frac{(0.2-0.2)^2}{0.2}}
$$

$$
w = \sqrt{\frac{0.01}{0.5} + \frac{0.01}{0.3} + 0} = \sqrt{0.02 + 0.0333} = \sqrt{0.0533} \approx 0.231
$$

For $\alpha = 0.05$, $\nu = 2$, power = 0.80, the required $\lambda^* \approx 9.63$ (from non-central chi-square tables). Then

$$
N = \frac{9.63}{(0.231)^2} = \frac{9.63}{0.0533} \approx 180.7
$$

So $N \geq 181$ total observations across the 3 categories. ∎

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A pharmaceutical company wants to test whether a new drug changes the recovery rate from 60% to 75%. Using $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed) and power = 90%, compute the required sample size.

**Step 1 — Identify the inputs:**

- $p_0 = 0.60$, $p_1 = 0.75$
- $q_0 = 0.40$, $q_1 = 0.25$
- $Z_{\alpha/2} = Z_{0.025} = 1.96$
- $Z_\beta = Z_{0.10} = 1.28$ (because power = 0.90 → β = 0.10)

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator components:**

$$
Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{p_0 q_0} = 1.96 \times \sqrt{0.60 \times 0.40} = 1.96 \times \sqrt{0.24} = 1.96 \times 0.4899 = 0.9602
$$

$$
Z_\beta \sqrt{p_1 q_1} = 1.28 \times \sqrt{0.75 \times 0.25} = 1.28 \times \sqrt{0.1875} = 1.28 \times 0.4330 = 0.5543
$$

**Step 3 — Sum and square:**

$$
(0.9602 + 0.5543)^2 = (1.5145)^2 = 2.2937
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator:**

$$
(p_1 - p_0)^2 = (0.75 - 0.60)^2 = (0.15)^2 = 0.0225
$$

**Step 5 — Divide:**

$$
n = \frac{2.2937}{0.0225} = 101.94
$$

**Step 6 — Round up to the next integer:**

$$
\boxed{\,n = 102\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Always round **up** the sample size, never down. A fractional patient cannot be recruited.
