# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flow — Power Analysis for Categorical Data

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the logical decision sequence a KTU student must follow when faced with a power/sample size problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Power or Sample Size Problem] --> B{Identify Scenario}
    B -->|One proportion| C[Use Z-test formula]
    B -->|Two proportions| D[Use pooled Z-test formula]
    B -->|k categories| E[Use chi-square / Cohen w formula]

    C --> F[Identify p0, p1, alpha, power]
    D --> G[Identify p1, p2, alpha, power]
    E --> H[Identify pi, p, k, alpha, power]

    F --> I{One-tailed or two-tailed?}
    G --> I
    H --> J[Look up lambda from non-central chi-square]

    I -->|Two-tailed| K[Use Z alpha/2 = 1.96]
    I -->|One-tailed| L[Use Z alpha = 1.645]

    K --> M[Plug into formula]
    L --> M
    G --> M
    J --> N[Compute Cohen w]
    N --> O[Compute N = lambda / w squared]
    M --> P[Round up to integer n]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Report final sample size]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Inputs and Outputs of Power Analysis

This diagram emphasises the four-pillar structure of any power calculation, mapping each input to the output it determines.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Parameters]
        A1[Significance Level alpha]
        A2[Desired Power 1 minus beta]
        A3[Effect Size h or w]
        A4[Baseline Proportion p0]
    end

    subgraph SOLVER[Power Solver Engine]
        B1[Z-table Lookup Module]
        B2[Normal Approximation Module]
        B3[Non-central Chi-square Module]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output]
        C1[Required Sample Size n]
        C2[Achieved Power for given n]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
    A4 --> B2
    A1 --> B3
    A2 --> B3
    A3 --> B3

    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B1 --> C2
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C2
```

---

## 4.3 Effect-Size Classification Map (Cohen's Conventions)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compute Effect Size] --> B{Which Type?}
    B -->|Two proportions| C[Compute Cohen h]
    B -->|k categories| D[Compute Cohen w]

    C --> E{h value?}
    E -->|0 to 0.2| F[Negligible]
    E -->|0.2 to 0.5| G[Small effect]
    E -->|0.5 to 0.8| H[Medium effect]
    E -->|above 0.8| I[Large effect]

    D --> J{w value?}
    J -->|0 to 0.1| K[Negligible]
    J -->|0.1 to 0.3| L[Small effect]
    J -->|0.3 to 0.5| M[Medium effect]
    J -->|above 0.5| N[Large effect]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a problem does not specify the effect size, you are expected to use Cohen's medium convention (h = 0.5 or w = 0.3) unless stated otherwise. The phrase "clinically meaningful difference" usually implies the medium threshold.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "power of a statistical test" and list the four factors on which it depends.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Power** of a statistical test is the probability of correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis, i.e., $1 - \beta$, where $\beta$ is the Type II error probability.
>
> The four factors on which power depends are:
> 1. **Significance level (α)** — higher α increases power.
> 2. **Sample size (n)** — larger n increases power.
> 3. **Effect size (Δ, h, or w)** — larger true effect increases power.
> 4. **Population variability** — lower variability increases power (for proportions this is captured by $p(1-p)$).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is Cohen's h? How is it computed for two proportions 0.40 and 0.60?**

**Model Answer:**

> Cohen's **h** is a standardised effect size for the difference between two proportions, defined as
>
> $$h = 2\arcsin(\sqrt{p_1}) - 2\arcsin(\sqrt{p_2})$$
>
> For $p_1 = 0.60$ and $p_2 = 0.40$:
>
> $$h = 2\arcsin(\sqrt{0.60}) - 2\arcsin(\sqrt{0.40})$$
>
> $$h = 2(0.8870) - 2(0.6847) = 1.7740 - 1.3694 = 0.4046$$
>
> Since $0.2 \le h \le 0.5$, this is a **small-to-medium** effect.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **Module 4 Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Derive the sample size formula for testing a single proportion against a hypothesised value at significance level $\alpha$ and power $1 - \beta$. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A public health researcher claims that the prevalence of a disease in a district has increased from the national average of 12% to 18%. How many subjects must be sampled to test this claim at $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed) with 80% power? Use $Z_{0.025} = 1.96$ and $Z_{0.20} = 0.84$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

1. State null and alternative: $H_0: p = p_0$ vs $H_1: p = p_1 \neq p_0$.
2. Test statistic: $Z = \dfrac{\hat{p} - p_0}{\sqrt{p_0 q_0 / n}}$, standard normal under H₀.
3. Reject H₀ when $\vert Z \vert > Z_{\alpha/2}$, equivalent to $\hat{p} > p_0 + Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{p_0 q_0 / n}$.
4. Compute power: under true $p = p_1$, $\hat{p} \sim N(p_1, p_1 q_1 / n)$. The rejection probability is
$$1 - \beta = P\!\left(\hat{p} > p_0 + Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{p_0 q_0 / n}\right)$$
5. Standardise:
$$1 - \beta = P\!\left(Z > \frac{p_0 - p_1 + Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{p_0 q_0 / n}}{\sqrt{p_1 q_1 / n}}\right) = P(Z > -Z_\beta)$$
6. Equate the inner expression to $-Z_\beta$ and solve for $n$:
$$n = \frac{\left(Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{p_0 q_0} + Z_\beta\sqrt{p_1 q_1}\right)^2}{(p_1 - p_0)^2}$$
7. **Assumptions:** (i) binomial distribution approximated by normal, (ii) $n p \ge 5$ and $n(1-p) \ge 5$, (iii) independent observations, (iv) random sampling.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters:**

- $p_0 = 0.12$, $p_1 = 0.18$
- $q_0 = 0.88$, $q_1 = 0.82$
- $Z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96$, $Z_\beta = 0.84$ (power = 0.80 → β = 0.20)
- Two-tailed test

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator terms:**

$$
Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{p_0 q_0} = 1.96 \times \sqrt{0.12 \times 0.88} = 1.96 \times \sqrt{0.1056} = 1.96 \times 0.3250 = 0.6370
$$

$$
Z_\beta\sqrt{p_1 q_1} = 0.84 \times \sqrt{0.18 \times 0.82} = 0.84 \times \sqrt{0.1476} = 0.84 \times 0.3842 = 0.3227
$$

**Step 3 — Sum and square:**

$$
(0.6370 + 0.3227)^2 = (0.9597)^2 = 0.9210
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator:**

$$
(p_1 - p_0)^2 = (0.06)^2 = 0.0036
$$

**Step 5 — Divide and round up:**

$$
n = \frac{0.9210}{0.0036} = 255.83 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{n = 256}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify normal approximation assumption:** $n p_0 = 256 \times 0.12 = 30.72 \ge 5$ ✓ and $n p_1 = 256 \times 0.18 = 46.08 \ge 5$ ✓. The approximation is valid.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Two teaching methods are compared. Method A has a pass rate of 65% and Method B has a pass rate of 80%. Derive the sample size formula for a two-proportion test with equal allocation, and compute the sample size **per group** at $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed) and 90% power. Use $Z_{0.025} = 1.96$ and $Z_{0.10} = 1.28$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A chi-square goodness-of-fit test is to be performed on 4 categories with null proportions $\pi = (0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25)$ and alternative proportions $p = (0.40, 0.30, 0.20, 0.10)$. Compute Cohen's w. If the required non-centrality parameter is $\lambda^* = 13.0$ for 80% power at $\alpha = 0.05$, find the total sample size N. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Derivation outline (key steps):**

1. Test statistic: $Z = \dfrac{(\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2) - 0}{\sqrt{2\bar{p}\bar{q}/n}}$ where $\bar{p} = (p_1 + p_2)/2$.
2. Reject H₀ when $\vert Z \vert > Z_{\alpha/2}$.
3. Under alternative, the standard error is $\sqrt{(p_1 q_1 + p_2 q_2)/n}$.
4. Setting the rejection probability to $1-\beta$ and solving gives
$$n_{\text{per group}} = \frac{\left(Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{2\bar{p}\bar{q}} + Z_\beta\sqrt{p_1 q_1 + p_2 q_2}\right)^2}{(p_1 - p_2)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Numerical computation:**

- $p_1 = 0.80$ (Method B), $p_2 = 0.65$ (Method A)
- $q_1 = 0.20$, $q_2 = 0.35$
- $\bar{p} = (0.80 + 0.65)/2 = 0.725$, $\bar{q} = 0.275$
- $Z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96$, $Z_\beta = 1.28$ (power = 0.90 → β = 0.10)

**Step 3 — Numerator components:**

$$
Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{2\bar{p}\bar{q}} = 1.96 \times \sqrt{2 \times 0.725 \times 0.275} = 1.96 \times \sqrt{0.3988} = 1.96 \times 0.6315 = 1.2377
$$

$$
Z_\beta\sqrt{p_1 q_1 + p_2 q_2} = 1.28 \times \sqrt{0.80 \times 0.20 + 0.65 \times 0.35} = 1.28 \times \sqrt{0.16 + 0.2275} = 1.28 \times \sqrt{0.3875} = 1.28 \times 0.6225 = 0.7968
$$

**Step 4 — Sum and square:**

$$
(1.2377 + 0.7968)^2 = (2.0345)^2 = 4.1392
$$

**Step 5 — Denominator:**

$$
(p_1 - p_2)^2 = (0.15)^2 = 0.0225
$$

**Step 6 — Final result:**

$$
n_{\text{per group}} = \frac{4.1392}{0.0225} = 183.96 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{n = 184 \text{ per group}}
$$

**Step 7 — Total sample:** $N = 2 \times 184 = 368$.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute Cohen's w:**

$$
w = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{4}\frac{(p_i - \pi_i)^2}{\pi_i}}
$$

$$
w = \sqrt{\frac{(0.40-0.25)^2}{0.25} + \frac{(0.30-0.25)^2}{0.25} + \frac{(0.20-0.25)^2}{0.25} + \frac{(0.10-0.25)^2}{0.25}}
$$

$$
w = \sqrt{\frac{0.0225}{0.25} + \frac{0.0025}{0.25} + \frac{0.0025}{0.25} + \frac{0.0225}{0.25}}
$$

$$
w = \sqrt{0.09 + 0.01 + 0.01 + 0.09} = \sqrt{0.20} \approx 0.4472
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total sample size:**

$$
N = \frac{\lambda^*}{w^2} = \frac{13.0}{0.20} = 65
$$

**Step 3 — Round up:**

$$
\boxed{N = 65}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** A total of 65 subjects distributed across 4 categories is required to detect the departure from uniformity specified by $p = (0.40, 0.30, 0.20, 0.10)$ with 80% power at $\alpha = 0.05$. Since $w = 0.4472$ falls in the medium-to-large range, a moderate sample is sufficient.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Wrong Z-value for two-tailed tests** — Using $Z_{0.05} = 1.645$ instead of $Z_{0.025} = 1.96$ loses 1–2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting to round up** — Reporting $n = 183.96$ as $n = 183$ instead of $184$ per group loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing pooled and unpooled variances** — In two-proportion tests, the term under $Z_{\alpha/2}$ uses **pooled** $\bar{p}\bar{q}$, but the term under $Z_\beta$ uses **unpooled** $p_1 q_1 + p_2 q_2$. Reversing this leads to a wrong answer.
> 4. **Ignoring the normal approximation assumption** — Always verify $np \ge 5$ and $n(1-p) \ge 5$. If violated, mention the limitation.
> 5. **Reporting total n instead of per-group n** — In two-proportion tests, the formula gives **n per group**, not total. The examiner expects you to clarify this.
> 6. **Confusing Cohen's h with raw difference** — Cohen's h uses the arcsin transformation; the raw difference $\vert p_1 - p_2 \vert$ is **not** the standardised effect size.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Power and Sample Size for Categorical Data**

- [x] **Power** = $1 - \beta$ = probability of rejecting a false H₀. Standard values: 0.80, 0.90, 0.95.
- [x] **Four pillars of power:** α, n, effect size, variability. Fixing three determines the fourth.
- [x] **Standard Z-values to memorise:** $Z_{0.05} = 1.645$, $Z_{0.025} = 1.96$, $Z_{0.10} = 1.28$, $Z_{0.20} = 0.84$.
- [x] **One-proportion formula:** $n = \left(Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{p_0 q_0} + Z_\beta\sqrt{p_1 q_1}\right)^2 / (p_1 - p_0)^2$.
- [x] **Two-proportion formula:** $n_{\text{per group}} = \left(Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{2\bar{p}\bar{q}} + Z_\beta\sqrt{p_1 q_1 + p_2 q_2}\right)^2 / (p_1 - p_2)^2$.
- [x] **Chi-square formula:** $N = \lambda^* / w^2$, where $w = \sqrt{\sum(p_i - \pi_i)^2 / \pi_i}$.
- [x] **Cohen's h:** $h = 2\arcsin(\sqrt{p_1}) - 2\arcsin(\sqrt{p_2})$. Thresholds: 0.2 (small), 0.5 (medium), 0.8 (large).
- [x] **Cohen's w thresholds:** 0.1 (small), 0.3 (medium), 0.5 (large).
- [x] **Always round up** the final sample size to the next integer.
- [x] **Verify normal approximation:** $n p \ge 5$ and $n(1-p) \ge 5$.
- [x] **In two-proportion tests:** pooled variance under $Z_{\alpha/2}$, unpooled under $Z_\beta$.
- [x] **Type of test matters:** one-tailed uses $Z_\alpha$, two-tailed uses $Z_{\alpha/2}$.
- [x] **Higher desired power → larger n.** Doubling power from 80% to 95% increases n by approximately 60%.
- [x] **Smaller effect size → larger n.** Halving the effect size quadruples the required sample size.
- [x] **Continuity correction** (Casagrande-Pike) is used when $n$ is small (< 30) — rarely required in KTU problems.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Power and Sample Size Estimation for Categorical Data**
